authors: John Collins

content_sections.I Introduction: An accurate description of the Drell-Yan process and other similar processes requires an understanding in QCD of the Q𝑄Q dependence of the transverse-momentum dependence when qT≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑞𝑇𝑄qTll Q. (Here, qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT and Q𝑄Q are the transverse momentum and invariant mass of the Drell-Yan pair. ) An often-used way of treating this issue was found by Collins and Soper (CS) citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, and Collins, Soper and Sterman (CSS) citebib.bib3, who used factorization with transverse-momentum-dependent parton density functions (TMD pdfs). In contrast to ordinary “integrated pdfs”, the TMD pdfs of CS depend on an extra argument ζ𝜁zeta. The phenomenological significance of ζ𝜁zeta can seen in the basic factorization formula, Eq. below, where the two instances of ζ𝜁zeta obey ζA​ζB=Q4subscript𝜁𝐴subscript𝜁𝐵superscript𝑄4zetaAzetaB=Q4. CS obtained an equation for the ζ𝜁zeta dependence. This equation implies a strong Q𝑄Q-dependence of the shape of the measured qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT distribution, which is in agreement with data for the Drell-Yan and other processes — see, for example, Refs. citebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7. However, there is a bewildering variety of treatments of TMD factorization in the literature, with what for the outsider appear to be contradictory claims about the need for and the use of the CS equation and other aspects of the CSS approach. This can be seen in work within an SCET framework by Becher and Neubert (BN) citebib.bib8 and by Echevarría, Idilbi, and Scimemi (EIS) citebib.bib9. Notably, the last authors state that their approach is “without the Collins-Soper like evolution equations”. In reality, however, the last authors do use an exact equivalent of the CS equation, but label it differently. To see this, one can merely compare Eq. (5.7) of Ref. citebib.bib9 with the results in Sec.13.13.1 of citebib.bib10, which uses a modernized and improved version of the CSS method. One difference in the EIS treatment is the derivation of the results, which are shown to follow from more structural properties of TMD factorization, but within the particular approach of EIS. The strong Q𝑄Q-dependence of the transverse-momentum distribution is a property of QCD that cannot be avoided. The most that can be done is to change the method of derivation and the form of presentation of the results. (Another set of discrepancies between the treatments concerns the large-bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT region, where bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT is a variable Fourier-conjugate to transverse momentum. In this region, non-perturbative information is needed beyond that in the integrated pdfs. A discussion of the disagreements on this topic is not the concern of this paper. ) In this paper, motivated by a derivation by EIS, I show that the form of the CS equation and associated renormalization group (RG) properties follow from structural properties of TMD factorization (in its recent formulations). In contrast, the derivations by CS citebib.bib1, bib.bib2 and by Collins citebib.bib10 used specific properties of the operators defining the TMD pdfs to obtain their dependence on the ζ𝜁zeta parameter, and therefore appear to tie the CS equations to these properties of the operators. Thus these derivations are inapplicable, for example, to the rather different definition given by EIS citebib.bib9, even though the definitions give results numerically equivalent citebib.bib11 to those from the definitions in citebib.bib10. Of course, the validity of factorization strongly constrains which operator definitions of pdfs are suitable. The results in the present paper are important step in unifying the various treatments of TMD factorization, as regards their phenomenological implications, following on from citebib.bib11. Note that the derivations by BN citebib.bib8 and by EIS citebib.bib9 are explicitly restricted to ΛQCD≪qT≪Qmuch-less-thansubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑞𝑇much-less-than𝑄Lambdarm QCDll qTll Q, i. e. , they exclude the region of the smallest qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT where the cross section is largest. In contrast the derivation of the form of the CS equation in this paper applies generally, and the TMD factorization property used is valid over the whole range of small transverse momentum qT≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑞𝑇𝑄qTll Q.1As explained, for example, in the CS and CSS work, TMD factorization can be matched with ordinary collinear factorization at large qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT to give results valid for all qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT. Note also that BN citebib.bib8 did not give definitions of finite TMD pdfs within their approach: They obtained finite results only for the product of two TMD functions. Among other results that are associated with the CS equation and its derivation are that the anomalous dimension of the TMD pdf has linear dependence on ln⁡(ζ/μ2)𝜁superscript𝜇2ln(zeta/mu2) (or equivalently ln⁡(Q2/μ2)superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇2ln(Q2/mu2)), where μ𝜇mu is the RG scale. This logarithmic dependence is controlled by the ζ𝜁zeta-independent RG coefficient γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK for the kernel of the CS equation. Another result is that the kernel of the CS equation does not depend on ζ𝜁zeta (or Q𝑄Q) or on the flavor of the partons and beam hadrons. Both of these results also follow from the derivation in this paper, which does not in any way invalidate the derivations in citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib10.
content_sections.II What the CS and Collins derivations d: CS citebib.bib1, bib.bib2 defined TMD pdfs by matrix elements of appropriate non-gauge-invariant operators in a non-light-like axial gauge with gauge fixing vector n𝑛n. This vector can be thought of as providing a standard for classifying momenta into left-movers and right-movers. They found it convenient to use the variable ζ=4​(x​P⋅n)2/|n2|𝜁4superscript⋅𝑥𝑃𝑛2superscript𝑛2zeta=4(xPcdot n)2/|n2| for parameterizing the dependence on the vector n𝑛n, where x𝑥x is the normal longitudinal momentum fraction in a pdf and P𝑃P is the momentum of the target hadron. Dependence on ζ𝜁zeta is equivalent to dependence on the gauge-fixing vector, and the CS equation was derived by using Ward identities associated with gauge-dependence to get a particularly simple form. The actual form of the CS equation was not known ahead of time, and the derivation amounted to a calculation of the ζ𝜁zeta dependence of a TMD pdf to leading power at large ζ𝜁zeta. The form of the CS equation, and the various auxiliary properties, were outputs of the derivation. Much more recently Collins citebib.bib10 worked out an improved gauge-invariant definition of TMD pdfs, again with an auxiliary vector n𝑛n which is now the direction of a certain Wilson line. With this definition, the CS equation arises from factorization properties of Wilson line operators when there are two Wilson lines of very different rapidities. An important quantity is the anomalous dimension γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK, for the kernel of the CS equation; it corresponds to a similar quantity in the work of Korchemsky and Radyushkin citebib.bib12 for the dependence of the anomalous dimension of a Wilson line cusp on the hyperbolic angle of the cusp.
content_sections.III Corresponding derivation for RG equa: Before examining how TMD factorization implies the CS equation, I illustrate the principles of the method by constructing an analogous argument for a more well-known and simpler case, which is the RG equation for operators that appear in the short-distance operator-product expansion (OPE). A simple generalization of the argument allows one to obtain the DGLAP equation for integrated pdfs from an ordinary collinear factorization formula. The material in this section is not original. I remember learning the method from someone else many years ago, but I don’t remember who, or if and where it has been published. Let us examine the OPE for an object of the following form: F​(Q,P,H,m​(μ),μ,αs​(μ))=∫d4​x​ei​q⋅x​⟨P,H|T​j​(x)​j​|P,H⟩,𝐹𝑄𝑃𝐻𝑚𝜇𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑑4𝑥superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝑞𝑥quantum-operator-product𝑃𝐻𝑇𝑗𝑥𝑗0𝑃𝐻F(Q,P,H,m(mu),mu,alphas(mu)) =int d4xeiqcdot x P,H|Tj(x)j|P,H, which is the Fourier transform of a current-current matrix element in a target state of momentum P𝑃P and flavor H𝐻H. I restrict the situation to be simple enough that only a single operator appears in the OPE at leading power in Q=|q|𝑄𝑞Q=|q|, and, as usual, choose the currents to be RG invariant. Also as usual, the renormalization scale is μ𝜇mu, the renormalized coupling of the theory is αs​(μ)subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇alphas(mu), and the renormalized mass parameter(s) of the theory are denoted by m​(μ)𝑚𝜇m(mu) To formulate the OPE, we take an essentially Euclidean limit where q𝑞q is made large. Then, up to power-suppressed corrections: F=C​(Q,μ,αs)​A​(P,H,m,μ,αs),𝐹𝐶𝑄𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠𝐴𝑃𝐻𝑚𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠F=C(Q,mu,alphas),A(P,H,m,mu,alphas), where C𝐶C is a short-distance coefficient function and A𝐴A is the expectation value of some renormalized operator 𝒪𝒪O in the state |P⟩ket𝑃|P: A=⟨P,H|𝒪|P,H⟩𝐴quantum-operator-product𝑃𝐻𝒪𝑃𝐻A= P,H|O|P,H. The operator is a product of fields (and possibly derivatives) at some space-time point, and of a renormalization factor. Important properties of this statement of the OPE are that: (a) The coefficient function depends on Q𝑄Q and μ𝜇mu, but not on the mass(es) or on P𝑃P or on hadron flavor H𝐻H. (b) The operator matrix element is independent of Q𝑄Q. Furthermore, if the currents defining F𝐹F are changed such that the same operator 𝒪𝒪O is needed in the OPE, then the value of C𝐶C can depend on which currents are used, but the matrix element A𝐴A is independent of the choice of currents. Thus the only arguments in common between the two factors are μ𝜇mu and αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. These properties are a consequence of deriving the OPE by a strict expansion in powers of Q𝑄Q and keeping only the leading power, and of using a mass-independent renormalization scheme like MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS.

The standard derivation of the RG equation for the operator and its matrix element A𝐴A uses the fact that the operator is multiplicatively renormalized, so that the bare operator and the renormalized operator are related by 𝒪0=ZA​𝒪subscript𝒪0subscript𝑍𝐴𝒪O0=ZAO. The renormalization factor ZAsubscript𝑍𝐴ZA is a function of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas and the dimensionless regulator parameter ϵitalic-ϵepsilon. Then the renormalization of the matrix element similarly obeys A0=ZA​A. subscript𝐴0subscript𝑍𝐴𝐴A0=ZAA. Since bare operators are RG invariant, the RG equation for the renormalized matrix element is obtained by d​Ad​ln⁡μ=d​ZA−1d​ln⁡μ​A0=−γA​(αs)​A,𝑑𝐴𝑑𝜇𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑍𝐴1𝑑𝜇subscript𝐴0subscript𝛾𝐴subscript𝛼𝑠𝐴fracdAdlnmu=fracdZA-1dlnmuA0=-gammaA(alphas)A, where γA​(αs)=d​ln⁡ZAd​ln⁡μ. subscript𝛾𝐴subscript𝛼𝑠𝑑subscript𝑍𝐴𝑑𝜇gammaA(alphas)=fracdln ZAdlnmu. Here a total derivative is used, as usual, to indicate that the differentiation with respect to μ𝜇mu includes the running of the coupling and of the quark masses: dd​ln⁡μ=∂∂ln⁡μ+d​αs​(μ)d​ln⁡μ​∂∂αs+∑id​mi​(μ)d​ln⁡μ​∂∂mi. 𝑑𝑑𝜇𝜇𝑑subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝑑𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝑖𝑑subscript𝑚𝑖𝜇𝑑𝜇subscript𝑚𝑖fracddlnmu=fracpartialpartiallnmu+fracdalphas(mu)dlnmufracpartialpartialalphas+sumifracdmi(mu)dlnmufracpartialpartial mi. The anomalous dimension γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA is a function of the renormalized coupling alone. Since the current-current matrix element F𝐹F is RG invariant, so is the leading term C​A𝐶𝐴CA in its expansion in powers of Q𝑄Q. Hence the RG equation of the coefficient C𝐶C has the opposite anomalous dimension to A𝐴A: d​Cd​ln⁡μ=γA​(αs)​C. 𝑑𝐶𝑑𝜇subscript𝛾𝐴subscript𝛼𝑠𝐶fracdCdlnmu=gammaA(alphas)C. For applications, the important result is that the anomalous dimension depends only on αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. Thus the condition that a perturbative calculation of γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA is accurate is simply that αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas is small enough; there are no large logarithms of kinematic variables to worsen the perturbation expansion, unlike the coefficient function C𝐶C with its logarithms of Q/μ𝑄𝜇Q/mu. We apply the RG equation to express the product C​A𝐶𝐴CA in terms of C𝐶C, with μ𝜇mu set equal to Q𝑄Q, and of A𝐴A with a fixed reference value μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 of the renormalization scale: CA=C(Q,Q,αs(Q))A(P,H,m,μ0,αs)××exp⁡−∫μ0Qd​μμ​γA​(αs​(μ)). 𝐶𝐴𝐶𝑄𝑄subscript𝛼𝑠𝑄𝐴𝑃𝐻𝑚subscript𝜇0subscript𝛼𝑠superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝜇0𝑄𝑑𝜇𝜇subscript𝛾𝐴subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇C,A=C(Q,Q,alphas(Q)),A(P,H,m,mu0,alphas)times timesexpleft-intmu0QfracdmumugammaA(alphas(mu))right. On the right-hand-side, C𝐶C has no large logarithms, so that it may be calculated perturbatively if αs​(Q)subscript𝛼𝑠𝑄alphas(Q) is small, i. e. , if Q𝑄Q is large enough, because of the asymptotic freedom of QCD. Since A𝐴A is at a fixed renormalization scale, the formula illustrates the universality properties of non-perturbative part embodied in the operator matrix element. All the above is (or should be) well-known and can be found in textbooks.

Instead of the above derivation, let us simply define the anomalous dimensions of C𝐶C and A𝐴A by derivatives of the logarithms of C𝐶C and A𝐴A with respect to ln⁡μ𝜇lnmu: γC​(Q,μ,αs)subscript𝛾𝐶𝑄𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠displaystylegammaC(Q,mu,alphas) =−d​ln⁡Cd​ln⁡μ,absent𝑑𝐶𝑑𝜇displaystyle=-fracdln Cdlnmu, γA​(P,H,m,μ,αs)subscript𝛾𝐴𝑃𝐻𝑚𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠displaystylegammaA(P,H,m,mu,alphas) =−d​ln⁡Ad​ln⁡μ. absent𝑑𝐴𝑑𝜇displaystyle=-fracdln Adlnmu. Since we do not use the connection to the renormalization factor ZAsubscript𝑍𝐴ZA, we are no longer guaranteed that the anomalous dimensions are independent of Q𝑄Q, P𝑃P, H𝐻H, m𝑚m, and μ𝜇mu: Whatever arguments are needed for C𝐶C and A𝐴A are also needed for γCsubscript𝛾𝐶gammaC and γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA respectively. If this dependence actually existed, then the solution of the RG equation would not be very useful, since large logarithms could appear in the calculation of γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA in the exponent in the solution. Moreover, the RG evolution could depend on hadron flavor. But the product C​A𝐶𝐴CA is RG invariant, from which it follows that the sum of the anomalous dimensions is zero: γC​(Q,μ,αs)+γA​(P,H,m,μ,αs)=0. subscript𝛾𝐶𝑄𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝛾𝐴𝑃𝐻𝑚𝜇subscript𝛼𝑠0gammaC(Q,mu,alphas)+gammaA(P,H,m,mu,alphas)=0. Since γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA is independent of Q𝑄Q, differentiation with respect to Q𝑄Q shows that ∂γC/∂Q=0subscript𝛾𝐶𝑄0partialgammaC/partial Q=0, i. e. , that γCsubscript𝛾𝐶gammaC is independent of Q𝑄Q. Similarly γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA is independent of P𝑃P, H𝐻H, and m𝑚m, because γCsubscript𝛾𝐶gammaC is. Dimensional analysis then shows that neither anomalous dimension depends on the explicit argument μ𝜇mu. In this derivation, the statement of the RG equation for A𝐴A is a triviality: it is merely a definition. The non-trivial result is that γAsubscript𝛾𝐴gammaA is a function only of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. From the point of view of applications, it is this last property that is the critical one, since it allows a perturbative calculation of γA​(αs​(μ))subscript𝛾𝐴subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇gammaA(alphas(mu)) in Eq. provided only that αS​(μ)subscript𝛼𝑆𝜇alphaS(mu) is large enough, i. e. , that Q𝑄Q and μ0subscript𝜇0mu0 are in what is often called the perturbative region of mass scales for QCD. In the first derivation, this same property followed from the multiplicative renormalizability of the operator 𝒪𝒪O and from properties of MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS renormalization. But in the second derivation, renormalization properties are not used. The contrast between these two facts becomes less strange when one observes that (a) the derivation of the OPE more-or-less determines which operator is used, and (b) the requirement that the coefficient function correspond to behavior that is purely leading-power in Q𝑄Q requires the use of a mass-independent renormalization scheme. Furthermore the proof of multiplicative renormalizability of the operator has a lot in common with the proof of the OPE.
content_sections.IV Statement of TMD factorization, etc: In this section, I will give all the statements of TMD factorization and of the associated evolution equations together with the other results that are needed for full phenomenological application. They will be presented in the generalized CSS form that was derived (to all orders) in citebib.bib10. The forms given by BN citebib.bib8 and by EIS citebib.bib9 are similar (except that BN do not define individually finite TMD pdfs). The justifications given by BN and EIS are, of course, different, but that does not affect the structural properties stated here.
content_sections.IV Statement of TMD factorization, etc I: The Drell-Yan process is production of a high-mass lepton pair via a virtual electroweak boson of momentum q𝑞q in a high-energy collision of two hadrons A𝐴A and B𝐵B. The factorization formula for the cross section has the form d​σd4​q=∑i​jHi​j(Q2/μ2,αs(μ))∫d2𝒃Tei​𝒒T⋅𝒃T××fi/A​(xA,𝒃T;ζA,μ)​fj/B​(xB,𝒃T;ζB,μ)+Y,𝑑𝜎superscript𝑑4𝑞subscript𝑖𝑗subscript𝐻𝑖𝑗superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇2subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑑2subscript𝒃𝑇superscript𝑒⋅𝑖subscript𝒒𝑇subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇subscript𝑓𝑗𝐵subscript𝑥𝐵subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜁𝐵𝜇𝑌fracdsigmad4q=sumijHijbig(Q2/mu2,alphas(mu)bigr)int d2boldsymbolbTeiboldsymbolqTcdotboldsymbolbTtimes timestildefi/A(xA,boldsymbolbT;zetaA,mu)tildefj/B(xB,boldsymbolbT;zetaB,mu)+Y, and is valid up to corrections suppressed by a power of Q𝑄Q. Here, Hi​jsubscript𝐻𝑖𝑗Hij is the hard-scattering associated with the process at the partonic level, which is just a quark-antiquark annihilation to the lepton pair (or a gluon-gluon hard-scattering for Higgs production). The sum is over all relevant parton flavors. The quantities f𝑓tildef are the TMD pdfs Fourier transformed into transverse coordinate space, while xAsubscript𝑥𝐴xA and xBsubscript𝑥𝐵xB are longitudinal momentum fractions, xA=Q​eyq/2​PA+subscript𝑥𝐴𝑄superscript𝑒subscript𝑦𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴xA=Qeyq/sqrt2PA+, xB=Q​e−yq/2​PB−subscript𝑥𝐵𝑄superscript𝑒subscript𝑦𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵xB=Qe-yq/sqrt2PB-, where standard light-front coordinates are used,2I use the convention that V±=(V0±V3)/2superscript𝑉plus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscript𝑉0superscript𝑉32Vpm=(V0pm V3)/sqrt2. Note that up to corrections suppressed by a power of qT/Qsubscript𝑞𝑇𝑄qT/Q, xA=q+/PA+subscript𝑥𝐴superscript𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴xA=q+/PA+, xB=q−/PB−subscript𝑥𝐵superscript𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵xB=q-/PB-. Using this last two equations to define xAsubscript𝑥𝐴xA and xBsubscript𝑥𝐵xB would keep the structure of TMD factorization, but would require a change in the calculation used in the Y𝑌Y term. yq=12​ln⁡(q+/q−)subscript𝑦𝑞12superscript𝑞superscript𝑞yq=frac12ln(q+/q-) is the rapidity of the lepton pair, and PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA and PBsubscript𝑃𝐵PB are the 4-momenta of the beam hadrons. The quantity μ𝜇mu is the renormalization scale, which we will assume to be in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. The cross section is RG invariant, i. e. , it is independent of the choice of μ𝜇mu. The parameters ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴zetaA and ζBsubscript𝜁𝐵zetaB are defined by ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴displaystylezetaA =2​xA2​(PA+)2​e−2​yn=Q2​e2​(yq−yn),absent2superscriptsubscript𝑥𝐴2superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴2superscript𝑒2subscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝑄2superscript𝑒2subscript𝑦𝑞subscript𝑦𝑛displaystyle=2xA2(PA+)2e-2yn=Q2e2(yq-yn), (13a) ζBsubscript𝜁𝐵displaystylezetaB =2​xB2​(PB−)2​e2​yn=Q2​e2​(yn−yq),absent2superscriptsubscript𝑥𝐵2superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵2superscript𝑒2subscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝑄2superscript𝑒2subscript𝑦𝑛subscript𝑦𝑞displaystyle=2xB2(PB-)2e2yn=Q2e2(yn-yq), (13b) where ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn is an arbitrarily chosen quantity, which in the CS and Collins derivations of factorization can be thought of as a rapidity defining a separation between left-moving quanta from right-moving quanta. Technically, ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn is the rapidity of a vector nμsuperscript𝑛𝜇nmu that implements the separation, either by a choice of gauge-fixing or as the direction of certain Wilson lines. The first term on the right-hand side of Eq. is the TMD factorization result. It gives a good approximation to cross-section when qT≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑞𝑇𝑄qTll Q. The second term, Y𝑌Y, was proposed by CS to provide a matching of TMD factorization with ordinary collinear factorization for large qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT. That is, it allows one to combine TMD factorization with collinear factorization, so that Eq. is valid up to corrections suppressed by a power of Q𝑄Q for any qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT. For the rest of this paper, the Y𝑌Y term (or some other matching method) will be ignored. The angle of the lepton pair has been integrated over, and the hadrons are assumed to be unpolarized. Simple generalizations of TMD factorization obeying the same principles can be readily worked out to deal with angular dependence and polarization (e. g. , citebib.bib13). The hard scattering depends only on Q𝑄Q, μ𝜇mu and the strong coupling αs​(μ)subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇alphas(mu), and on the flavors of the annihilating partons. If μ𝜇mu is chosen to be of order Q𝑄Q, then perturbative calculations are appropriate for it, with the lowest order just being the parton model calculation for whatever version of the Drell-Yan process is being used. In contrast, the TMD pdfs contain all the contributions to the cross section (at leading power in Q𝑄Q) from the non-perturbative domain of QCD. They depend, therefore, on quark masses and the flavors of the partons and hadrons.

The TMD pdfs in Eq. depend on two auxiliary parameters ζ𝜁zeta and μ𝜇mu. To obtain useful predictions for experiments at different energies, equations are needed to relate the TMD functions at different values of the auxiliary parameters. In addition there is an equation that is a kind of generalized operator product expansion (OPE) that expresses the TMD pdfs in terms of the ordinary integrated pdfs when bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT is small enough to be considered to be in the region where perturbative calculations are appropriate in QCD. The CS equation is for the evolution with respect to ζ𝜁zeta of a TMD pdf for a parton of flavor i𝑖i in a hadron A𝐴A. In the form appropriate to the definitions in Ref. citebib.bib10 it is ∂ln⁡fi/A​(xA,bT;ζA,μ)∂ln⁡ζA=K​(bT;μ),subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇subscript𝜁𝐴𝐾subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇fracpartiallntildefi/A(xA,bT;zetaA,mu)partiallnsqrtzetaA=tildeK(bT;mu), with an exactly similar equation for the TMD in hadron B𝐵B, of course. The quantity K𝐾tildeK is a new entity, which I call the kernel of the CS equation. In Ref. citebib.bib10, it was constructed in terms of a vacuum matrix element of a certain Wilson-loop operator. It is independent of x𝑥x and ζ𝜁zeta, and of the flavor of the parton and the hadron in the pdf. But it does depend on the color representation for the parton, i. e. , there is one K𝐾tildeK for quarks and another for the gluon. (It is worth observing that the TMD pdfs and K𝐾tildeK depend also on the coupling αs​(μ)subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇alphas(mu) and the quark masses, as well as on the parameters explicitly indicated. ) In the CS style of derivation, the CS equation is obtained by differentiating with respect to the auxiliary rapidity parameter ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn, because this can be exploited to make a relatively simple derivation, using factorization properties and Ward identities. This appears to tie the CS equation to the use of a particular axial gauge, in Ref. citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, or to the use of certain non-light-like Wilson lines, in Ref. citebib.bib10. But a physical interpretation is more easily made if one considers holding ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn fixed, e. g. , at the rapidity of the Drell-Yan pair. Then the derivative with respect to ζ𝜁zeta becomes a derivative with respect to the physical momentum of the beam hadron, actually with respect to its rapidity ypAsubscript𝑦subscript𝑝𝐴ypA: ∂ln⁡fi/A​(xA,bT;ζA,μ)∂ln⁡PA+=∂ln⁡fi/A​(xA,bT;ζA,μ)∂yPA=K​(bT;μ). subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇subscript𝑦subscript𝑃𝐴𝐾subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇fracpartiallntildefi/A(xA,bT;zetaA,mu)partialln PA+=fracpartiallntildefi/A(xA,bT;zetaA,mu)partial yPA =tildeK(bT;mu). The kernel of the CS equation has an RG equation: d​Kd​ln⁡μ=−γK​(g​(μ)). 𝑑𝐾𝑑𝜇subscript𝛾𝐾𝑔𝜇fracdtildeKdlnmu=-gammaKmathopenleft(g(mu)right). The TMD pdfs obey the following RG equation: d​ln⁡fi/A​(xA,bT;ζA,μ)d​ln⁡μ=γf​(αs​(μ);ζA/μ2). 𝑑subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇𝑑𝜇subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇subscript𝜁𝐴superscript𝜇2fracdlntildefi/A(xA,bT;zetaA,mu)dlnmu=gammafbig(alphas(mu);zetaA/mu2bigr). In the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, the anomalous dimensions γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK and γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf are independent of quark masses. By observing that derivatives with respect to ζ𝜁zeta and μ𝜇mu commute, it follows citebib.bib1, bib.bib2 that the dependence on ζ𝜁zeta of the anomalous dimension of the TMD pdf in Eq. is linear in ln⁡ζ𝜁lnzeta, and that the coefficient is half γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK: γf​(g​(μ);ζ/μ2)=γf​(g​(μ);1)−12​γK​(g​(μ))​ln⁡ζμ2. subscript𝛾𝑓𝑔𝜇𝜁superscript𝜇2subscript𝛾𝑓𝑔𝜇112subscript𝛾𝐾𝑔𝜇𝜁superscript𝜇2gammafbig(g(mu);zeta/mu2bigr)=gammaf(g(mu);1)-frac12gammaK(g(mu))lnfraczetamu2. When bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT is large enough to be considered non-perturbative, the TMD pdfs and the CS kernel K𝐾tildeK are non-perturbative, i. e. , they are quantities that at present must be obtained from fits to data and not from perturbatively based calculations. But at small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, K𝐾tildeK can be evaluated perturbatively provided that K𝐾tildeK is evolved to where μ𝜇mu is of order 1/bT1subscript𝑏𝑇1/bT (so that the coupling is small and there are no large logarithms of bT​μsubscript𝑏𝑇𝜇bTmu). There is also an OPE for the TMD pdfs at small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT in terms of ordinary pdfs fj/H​(x;μ)subscript𝑓𝑗𝐻𝑥𝜇fj/H(x;mu): fi/H​(x,bT;ζ;μ)==∑j∫x−1+d​xx​Ci/j​(x/x,bT;ζ,μ,g​(μ))​fj/H​(x;μ),subscript𝑓𝑖𝐻𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜁𝜇subscript𝑗superscriptsubscriptlimit-from𝑥limit-from1𝑑𝑥𝑥subscript𝐶𝑖𝑗𝑥𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜁𝜇𝑔𝜇subscript𝑓𝑗𝐻𝑥𝜇tildefi/H(x,bT;zeta;mu)= =sumjintx-1+fracdhatxhatx,tildeCi/jmathopenleft(x/hatx,bT;zeta,mu,g(mu)right),fj/H(hatx;mu), up to errors that are power suppressed in bT​msubscript𝑏𝑇𝑚bTm. Here the notations x−limit-from𝑥x- and 1+limit-from11+ indicate, as in citebib.bib10, that the integrals should be extended slightly beyond the indicated limits to allow a treatment of singularities in C𝐶tildeC and f𝑓f as those of generalized functions (i. e. , distributions) in the mathematical sense. The coefficient function C𝐶tildeC is perturbatively calculable at small bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, provided that the pdf is evolved to appropriate values of ζ𝜁zeta and μ𝜇mu. CS and RG equations for C𝐶tildeC follow from those for the TMD pdfs and the DGLAP equation for the ordinary pdf. The lowest order term in C𝐶tildeC corresponds to the statement that in the parton model, the integral of the TMD pdf over all transverse momentum would equal the collinear pdf.
content_sections.V Properties to be proved: Suppose we ignored the particular derivations of the CS and RG equations, , , and . Then we could just define the CS kernel K𝐾tildeK by Eq. and the anomalous dimensions by Eqs. and . But that would allow the CS kernel K𝐾tildeK to depend on x𝑥x, ζ𝜁zeta and the flavors of the parton and hadron. It would allow the RG coefficients γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf and γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK to depend on x𝑥x, bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, masses, and the hadron flavor. It would also not restrict the anomalous dimension γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf to be linear in ln⁡ζ𝜁lnzeta, and γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK to be independent of ζ𝜁zeta. The extra dependencies, particularly of K𝐾tildeK would remove much of the predictive power of TMD factorization. General properties of renormalization do require the anomalous dimensions to be independent of masses in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme. We therefore need to derive the follow properties: 1. K𝐾tildeK is independent of x𝑥x and ζ𝜁zeta, and of parton and hadron flavor. 2. γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK is independent of these same variables. 3. γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf is independent of x𝑥x, of masses, and of hadron flavor. 4. Its dependence on ζ𝜁zeta is linear in ln⁡ζ𝜁lnzeta, as in Eq. with the coefficient being −12​γK12subscript𝛾𝐾-frac12gammaK. Note that since γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf is independent of quark masses, the dependence on ζ𝜁zeta and μ𝜇mu is only via the ratio ζ/μ2𝜁superscript𝜇2zeta/mu2, by dimensional analysis, and also γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK is independent of masses. The second property is a trivial consequence of the first, by the definition of γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK.
content_sections.VI Derivation of form of CS equation and: The TMD pdfs depend not only on parameters determined by the kinematic variables of the process, but also on the auxiliary parameter ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn, via the values of the ζ𝜁zeta parameters in Eqs. . But the cross section does not depend on ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn. Moreover, if one uses the form of TMD factorization derived in citebib.bib10 or the similar factorization used in citebib.bib8 and citebib.bib9, the hard factor is also independent of ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn; it depends only on Q𝑄Q, on the renormalization scale μ𝜇mu, and on the QCD coupling. There is also no separate soft factor in the factorization formula, unlike many of the earlier formalisms, notably that of CSS citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3. I now show how to use these results to demonstrate the properties listed in Sec. . The starting point of the derivation is not an absolutely necessary property of a TMD factorization formalism. For example, there can be a soft factor. Thus in some formalisms, e. g. , that of Korchemsky citebib.bib14 for the Sudakov form factor, it is the soft factor that carries the Q𝑄Q-dependence that in Eq. is given by the ζ𝜁zeta dependence of the TMD pdfs. Moreover, in the formalism of Ji, Ma, and Yuan citebib.bib15 there appear several kinds of non-light-like Wilson lines, and not only does that formalism have a soft factor but the hard scattering has dependence on an extra auxiliary parameter ρ𝜌rho. The new formalisms with a structure like that listed in Sec. are in some sense minimal (as in “minimal subtraction”). The structure applies equally to the EIS formalism citebib.bib9 and (modulo their lack of finite individually defined TMD pdfs) to the BN formalism citebib.bib8. In both these last two cases, the TMD factorization given by the authors is obtained by setting ζA=ζB=Q2subscript𝜁𝐴subscript𝜁𝐵superscript𝑄2zetaA=zetaB=Q2 in Eq. . The existence of an equivalent of a ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn parameter is not obvious, but, at least in the EIS case, can be considered a consequence of an implicit choice of coordinate frame citebib.bib11. Common themes of the new formalisms are that the Wilson lines (or their equivalent) in the definitions of the various factors are made light-like as far as possible (but with regulation at intermediate stages), and that any soft factor is absorbed into the collinear factors (i. e. , the TMD pdfs).

To derive the form of the CS equation, I will follow the pattern used in Sec. , but now applied to ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn dependence instead of μ𝜇mu dependence. A complication is that there was one term in the OPE used in Sec. , but there are generally multiple terms in TMD factorization , with its sum over parton flavors. The complication can be avoided by using the following trick inspired by how flavor-separated pdfs can be obtained from the structure functions of deep-inelastic scattering with charged currents. The derivation of TMD factorization applies equally when we use a general current correlation function Wμ​ν=s​∫d4​y​e−i​q⋅z​⟨A​B;in|jμ​(z)​jν​|A​B;in⟩. superscript𝑊𝜇𝜈𝑠superscript𝑑4𝑦superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝑞𝑧quantum-operator-product𝐴𝐵insuperscript𝑗𝜇𝑧superscript𝑗𝜈0𝐴𝐵inWmunu=sint d4ye-iqcdot z AB;mboxin|jmu(z)jnu|AB;mboxin. The initial state is the same high-energy state of two incoming hadrons as before, but the current can be any that we choose to use, not just one that carries the coupling to an electroweak boson. In the following, let us use the following chiral current giving a transition between two particular quark flavors: jμ=ψ¯j​γμ​(1−γ5)​ψi,superscript𝑗𝜇subscript¯𝜓𝑗superscript𝛾𝜇1subscript𝛾5subscript𝜓𝑖jmu=barpsijgammamu(1-gamma5)psii, with the quark flavor indices being unequal: i≠j𝑖𝑗ineq j. In this case, TMD factorization involves only two terms in , which use the following combinations of TMD pdfs: fi/A​(xA,𝒃T;ζA,μ)​fȷ¯/B​(xB,𝒃T;ζB,μ),subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇subscript𝑓¯italic-ȷ𝐵subscript𝑥𝐵subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜁𝐵𝜇displaystyletildefi/A(xA,boldsymbolbT;zetaA,mu)tildefbarjmath/B(xB,boldsymbolbT;zetaB,mu), (22a) fȷ¯/A​(xA,𝒃T;ζA,μ)​fi/B​(xB,𝒃T;ζB,μ). subscript𝑓¯italic-ȷ𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜁𝐴𝜇subscript𝑓𝑖𝐵subscript𝑥𝐵subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜁𝐵𝜇displaystyletildefbarjmath/A(xA,boldsymbolbT;zetaA,mu)tildefi/B(xB,boldsymbolbT;zetaB,mu). (22b) That is, the i𝑖i quark may come from the A𝐴A hadron and the anti-j𝑗j quark from the B𝐵B hadron, or vice versa. If the current were invariant (up to a sign change) under charge conjugation invariance, then the two combinations of TMD pdfs in would simply be added, with equal coefficients. But this is not the case here. Let us decompose the hadronic tensor Wμ​νsuperscript𝑊𝜇𝜈Wmunu into a sum of scalar structure functions multiplying kinematic basis tensors. With a standard choice of basis tensors, we can find among the structure functions one for which factorization uses the sum of the terms in , and one for which factorization uses the difference. (There is also dependence the QCD coupling and possibly on masses that is not indicated explicitly. ) Now neither the structure function F𝐹F nor the hard scattering factor H𝐻H in Eq. depends on ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn. So differentiating Eq. with respect to ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn gives 0=−Li/A​(xA,ζA,bT;μ)+Lȷ¯/B​(xB,ζB,bT;μ),0subscript𝐿𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝜁𝐴subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇subscript𝐿¯italic-ȷ𝐵subscript𝑥𝐵subscript𝜁𝐵subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇0=-Li/A(xA,zetaA,bT;mu)+Lbarjmath/B(xB,zetaB,bT;mu), i. e. , equality of the two kernels.3The obvious derivation of this equality assumes that the TMD pdfs are always non-zero. However, it is conceivable that they have nodes, i. e. , zeros on a suanifold in the space of their arguments. In that case Li/A=Lȷ¯/Bsubscript𝐿𝑖𝐴subscript𝐿¯italic-ȷ𝐵Li/A=Lbarjmath/B is established by analytic continuation from where the TMD pdfs are nonzero. Next we observe that one L𝐿L depends on xAsubscript𝑥𝐴xA and ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴zetaA, but not on xBsubscript𝑥𝐵xB and ζBsubscript𝜁𝐵zetaB, while the reverse is true for other L𝐿L. Since these quantities can be varied independently,4The four quantities xAsubscript𝑥𝐴xA, ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴zetaA, xBsubscript𝑥𝐵xB, and ζBsubscript𝜁𝐵zetaB can be changed independently by varying the following four quantities s=(PA+PB)2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴subscript𝑃𝐵2s=(PA+PB)2, Q𝑄Q, the center-of-mass rapidity of the lepton pair, and ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn. it follows each L𝐿L is independent of its x𝑥x and ζ𝜁zeta arguments. Similarly, the hadron flavors can be varied independently, so that the L𝐿L functions are independent of hadron flavor. Finally, by taking all possible values of i𝑖i and j𝑗j, we find that the L𝐿L functions for different quark flavors are also equal, i. e. , L𝐿L does not depend on quark flavor. So we can simply rename L𝐿L to K𝐾tildeK to give the kernel in Eq. . Notice that the argument would apply in hypothesized extensions of QCD (e. g. , supersymmetric extensions) to include for example color-triplet scalars, and color-octet quarks and scalars. In each of these cases, we can construct gauge-invariant operators that couple one of the new fields to one of the old fields, and it then follows that the L𝐿L function for a new field is the same as K𝐾tildeK for an old field with the same color. Of course, fields with new color representations, like color-sextet quarks, are not covered by this argument; each color representation needs a new K𝐾tildeK function. The result of this section is that all the TMD pdfs obey ∂ln⁡fi/A​(x,bT;ζ,μ)∂ln⁡ζ=K​(bT;μ),subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜁𝜇𝜁𝐾subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇fracpartiallntildefi/A(x,bT;zeta,mu)partiallnsqrtzeta=tildeK(bT;mu), where K𝐾tildeK depends only on bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, on the parameters of QCD, including masses, and on the color representation for parton i𝑖i. The RG equation for K𝐾tildeK will be derived in Sec. . One way of summarizing the main issue for the above results is that, as regards kinematic variables, each TMD pdf depends, not only on the standard kinematic variables x𝑥x and bTsubscript𝑏𝑇bT, but separately on PA+superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴PA+ for fi/Asubscript𝑓𝑖𝐴tildefi/A and PB−superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵PB- for fj/Bsubscript𝑓𝑗𝐵tildefj/B. From the form of factorization used in this paper, it follows that the product fi/A​fj/Bsubscript𝑓𝑖𝐴subscript𝑓𝑗𝐵tildefi/Atildefj/B depends on the product PA+​PB−superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵PA+PB-, but not on PA+superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴PA+ and PB−superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵PB- separately. But the TMD pdfs also depend on the direction of the vector n𝑛n specifying the non-boost-invariant operators in their definition. So the Lorentz invariance of QCD implies that the dependence on hadron momenta can be put in the Lorentz-covariant form fi/A∝(PA+​|n−/n+|)K,fj/B∝(PB−​|n+/n−|)K. formulae-sequenceproportional-tosubscript𝑓𝑖𝐴superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴superscript𝑛superscript𝑛𝐾proportional-tosubscript𝑓𝑗𝐵superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝐵superscript𝑛superscript𝑛𝐾tildefi/Aproptoleft(PA+|n-/n+|right)tildeK,qquadtildefj/Bproptoleft(PB-|n+/n-|right)tildeK.

Let us now define the anomalous dimension of a TMD pdf in hadron A𝐴A by Eq. and the exactly similar equation for the TMD pdf in hadron B𝐵B. However, with this equation treated as the definition, the anomalous dimension of a TMD pdf could depend on the flavor of its beam hadron and its quark, on the value of x𝑥x, and on masses: e. g. , γi/A​(xA,αs​(μ);ζA,μ2,m​(μ))subscript𝛾𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇subscript𝜁𝐴superscript𝜇2𝑚𝜇gammai/Abig(xA,alphas(mu);zetaA,mu2,m(mu)bigr). We will generalize the argument used for the elementary OPE in Sec. . It will show that γi/Asubscript𝛾𝑖𝐴gammai/A is independent of the quark and hadron flavor, and that it is independent of x𝑥x and of quark masses. Mass independence of an anomalous dimension is also a consequence of the infra-red safety of renormalization in a mass-independent scheme, and infra-red safety is a prerequisite for reliable perturbative calculations. However, unlike the previous case, dependence on ζ𝜁zeta and Q𝑄Q remains and needs to be analyzed. As with our derivation of the CS equation, we use a structure which has a single term in its TMD factorization, . Then, from the RG invariance of the cross section, it follows that the sum of the anomalous dimensions of the two TMD pdfs and of the hard scattering is zero: γi/A​(xA,ζA)+γȷ¯/B​(xB,ζB)+γH​(Q2)=0,subscript𝛾𝑖𝐴subscript𝑥𝐴subscript𝜁𝐴subscript𝛾¯italic-ȷ𝐵subscript𝑥𝐵subscript𝜁𝐵subscript𝛾𝐻superscript𝑄20gammai/Abig(xA,zetaAbigr)+gammabarjmath/Bbig(xB,zetaBbigr)+gammaHbig(Q2bigr)=0, where γH=d​H/d​ln⁡μsubscript𝛾𝐻𝑑𝐻𝑑𝜇gammaH=dH/dlnmu, and the arguments have been omitted for all but the dependence on kinematic- and flavor-related variables. We can change xAsubscript𝑥𝐴xA and xBsubscript𝑥𝐵xB independently of the other variables, so differentiating with respect to xAsubscript𝑥𝐴xA and xBsubscript𝑥𝐵xB shows that each anomalous dimension of the TMD pdfs is independent of its x𝑥x variable. Similarly, we can change the hadron flavors independently, so that γi/Asubscript𝛾𝑖𝐴gammai/A is independent of A𝐴A. We have already seen that H𝐻H is independent of which values of i𝑖i and j𝑗j are used (provided that i≠j𝑖𝑗ineq j). So considering all the possible values of i𝑖i and j𝑗j, shows that the anomalous dimensions are equal for all flavors of quark and for all flavors of antiquark. The charge-conjugation invariance of QCD shows equality between the anomalous dimensions for quarks and antiquarks. Hence all the anomalous dimensions of the quark and antiquark TMD pdfs are equal: γi/A=γfsubscript𝛾𝑖𝐴subscript𝛾𝑓gammai/A=gammaf, with our previous notation. To show independence of γi/Asubscript𝛾𝑖𝐴gammai/A of masses, we set ζA=ζB=Q2subscript𝜁𝐴subscript𝜁𝐵superscript𝑄2zetaA=zetaB=Q2, and use flavor independence in Eq. . This gives γf​(αs​(μ);Q2,μ2,m​(μ))=−12​γH​(αs​(μ),Q2/μ2). subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇2𝑚𝜇12subscript𝛾𝐻subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇2gammafbig(alphas(mu);Q2,mu2,m(mu)bigr)=-frac12gammaHbig(alphas(mu),Q2/mu2bigr). Since H𝐻H is independent of masses, it follows that γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf is. Thus in all cases, the functional dependence is γi/A=γf​(αs​(μ),ζA/μ2)subscript𝛾𝑖𝐴subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇subscript𝜁𝐴superscript𝜇2gammai/A=gammaf(alphas(mu),zetaA/mu2), as already asserted in Sec. . Unlike the case of the kernel of the CS equation, the proof of flavor-independence only works within the set of color-triplet spin-half quarks, since the hard scattering would be different in the case of hypothesized scalar quarks, for example. Then the anomalous dimension of the hard scattering could be different, which would require a changed value of γi/Asubscript𝛾𝑖𝐴gammai/A for the scalar quark. To analyze the ζ𝜁zeta dependence of γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf and the Q𝑄Q dependence of γHsubscript𝛾𝐻gammaH, we rewrite Eq. with all the allowed functional dependence exhibited: γf​(αs​(μ);Q2​e2​(yq−yn)/μ2)+γf​(αs​(μ);Q2​e2​(yn−yq)/μ2)=−γH​(αs​(μ),Q2/μ2). subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝑒2subscript𝑦𝑞subscript𝑦𝑛superscript𝜇2subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝑒2subscript𝑦𝑛subscript𝑦𝑞superscript𝜇2subscript𝛾𝐻subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇2gammafbig(alphas(mu);Q2e2(yq-yn)/mu2bigr)+gammafbig(alphas(mu);Q2e2(yn-yq)/mu2bigr) =-gammaHbig(alphas(mu),Q2/mu2bigr). The right-hand-side does not depend on ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn, because H𝐻H does not. Therefore differentiating the twice with respect to ynsubscript𝑦𝑛yn and setting yn=yqsubscript𝑦𝑛subscript𝑦𝑞yn=yq gives ∂2γf​(αs​(μ);ζ/μ2)∂(ln⁡ζ)2=0superscript2subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝜁superscript𝜇2superscript𝜁20fracpartial2gammafbig(alphas(mu);zeta/mu2bigr)partial(lnzeta)2=0 Hence the dependence of γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf on ln⁡ζ𝜁lnzeta is linear.

In this section, I relate the Q𝑄Q and ζ𝜁zeta dependence of the anomalous dimensions γfsubscript𝛾𝑓gammaf and γHsubscript𝛾𝐻gammaH to the RG properties of K𝐾tildeK, and derive the RG equation for K𝐾tildeK. This involves an energy-independent anomalous dimension γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK. Now derivatives with respect to different variables commute: dd​ln⁡μ​∂∂ln⁡ζ​ln⁡fi/A​(x,bT;ζ,μ)=∂∂ln⁡ζ​dd​ln⁡μ​ln⁡fi/A​(x,bT;ζ,μ). 𝑑𝑑𝜇𝜁subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜁𝜇𝜁𝑑𝑑𝜇subscript𝑓𝑖𝐴𝑥subscript𝑏𝑇𝜁𝜇fracddlnmufracpartialpartiallnsqrtzetalntildefi/A(x,bT;zeta,mu) =fracpartialpartiallnsqrtzetafracddlnmulntildefi/A(x,bT;zeta,mu). From the RG and CS equations for the TMD pdfs we then get d​K​(bT;μ)d​ln⁡μ=∂γf​(αs​(μ);ζ/μ2)∂ln⁡ζ. 𝑑𝐾subscript𝑏𝑇𝜇𝑑𝜇subscript𝛾𝑓subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝜁superscript𝜇2𝜁fracdtildeK(bT;mu)dlnmu=fracpartialgammafbig(alphas(mu);zeta/mu2bigr)partiallnsqrtzeta. The left-hand-side is independent of ζ𝜁zeta. The right-hand side is independent of masses, and therefore the left-hand-side is a function just of αs​(μ)subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇alphas(mu). We therefore have the form of the RG equation for K𝐾tildeK, Eq. . The ζ𝜁zeta dependence of the anomalous dimension of the TMD pdf immediately follows, as given in Eq. . Then for the anomalous dimension of the hard scattering we get γH​(g​(μ),Q2/μ2)=−2​γf​(g​(μ);1)+γK​(g​(μ))​ln⁡Q2μ2. subscript𝛾𝐻𝑔𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇22subscript𝛾𝑓𝑔𝜇1subscript𝛾𝐾𝑔𝜇superscript𝑄2superscript𝜇2gammaHbig(g(mu),Q2/mu2bigr)=-2gammaf(g(mu);1)+gammaK(g(mu))lnfracQ2mu2.
content_sections.VII Conclusions: In this paper I have shown that the CS and RG equations for TMD pdfs follow from structural properties of TMD factorization. The primary assumptions are that the formulation of TMD factorization uses only the pure leading power of Q𝑄Q and that no soft factor appears in the factorization formula (unlike the case for the formulations in citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib14, bib.bib15). In the new derivation, no direct use is made of properties of the operators whose matrix elements define the TMD pdfs. Of course, which operators are used is strongly constrained by the requirement that the resulting TMD pdfs can be used in a correct and useful TMD factorization property obeying the listed assumptions. The critical property underlying the proof is that the product of TMD pdfs in Eq. is boost invariant even though the individual TMD pdfs are not: See Eqs. and and the associated comments. As compared with the analogous and simpler case of the operators that are used in the OPE, the TMD case is notable for anomalous dimensions that depend on Q𝑄Q (either directly or through the ζAsubscript𝜁𝐴zetaA and ζBsubscript𝜁𝐵zetaB parameters). This property is strange given the normal situation that anomalous dimensions are independent of kinematic variables. The Q𝑄Q-dependence of the anomalous dimensions is intimately tied to the fact that perturbative calculations of processes for which TMD factorization applies have two “Sudakov” logarithms of Q𝑄Q per loop instead of the one logarithm that occurs in situations to which the standard OPE applies. This can be seen by expanding the hard scattering to first order in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. When the anomalous dimension is Q𝑄Q-independent, consistency with the RG equation implies that there is at most one logarithm of Q𝑄Q in the one-loop calculation. Since one-loop vertex graphs in gauge theories have a double logarithm of Q𝑄Q, something more general is needed with TMD factorization. This can be provided if the anomalous dimension of the TMD pdf depends on Q𝑄Q (or ζ𝜁zeta). This then entails that the TMD pdfs themselves depend on Q𝑄Q (or ζ𝜁zeta) unlike the case for the operator matrix element A𝐴A in the OPE (and its generalizations to collinear factorization). If a different definition of TMD pdfs were to be constructed such that they have no Q𝑄Q dependence, then the formulation of TMD factorization must be generalized from Eq. , e. g. , to put the relevant Q𝑄Q dependence in a soft factor, as in citebib.bib14. Once one finds that the anomalous dimension is Q𝑄Q-dependent, there is a danger that the exponent in the solution of the RG is no longer usefully calculable. This is because of the presence of logarithms of Q/μ𝑄𝜇Q/mu in the perturbative expansion of the anomalous dimension, and the typical phenomenon that the number of logarithms increases with the number of loops. This problem does not in fact occur because the anomalous dimension of the TMD pdfs has at most one logarithm independently of the order of perturbation theory. This property, Eq. , was known in the earliest work by CS citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, and in this paper it is also derived from TMD factorization. In all of the proofs in this paper, it was assumed that all quark masses are light, so that they can be legitimately neglected (to leading power in m/Q𝑚𝑄m/Q) in the hard scattering. The independence on masses of the hard scattering was important to the proof. But in reality there are heavy quarks in QCD, whose masses may be comparable to or larger than Q𝑄Q. Now the standard derivations of TMD factorization assume that all quark masses are small compared with Q𝑄Q. Evidently, a useful project that needs to be tackled is to generalize the results to the case that there are heavy quarks.

sorted_papers.title:
NNLL Transverse Momentum Dependent evolution in the Parton Branching
  method
sorted_papers.abstract:
In the preparation period for precision measurements in the newly planned
collider experiments, the understanding of the 3D structure of hadron is
becoming increasingly urgent. This triggers the activities to include elements
of Transverse Momentum Dependent (TMD) factorization physics in Monte Carlo
(MC) event generators. The method designed especially to address this need is
the TMD Parton Branching (PB) method. The equivalence of the PB Sudakov form
factor, both perturbative and non-perturbative, to the one of
Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) is demonstrated and the recent development to
increase the low-qt resummation precision of the PB Sudakov up to
next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic order by using effective soft-gluon
coupling is discussed. The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel is extracted from PB
Drell-Yan (DY) predictions obtained with different modelling of radiation.

qualitative: The TMD Parton Branching (PB) method is designed to incorporate Transverse Momentum Dependent (TMD) factorization physics into Monte Carlo event generators, enabling a more accurate description of the three-dimensional structure of hadrons by accounting for both longitudinal and transverse momentum components of partons.

The PB method provides a framework for evolving TMD parton distribution functions (TMDs) using Monte Carlo techniques, with the evolution implemented in the uPDFevolv2 package and fitted to experimental data via the xFitter plugin.

The PB Sudakov form factor is shown to be equivalent to the perturbative and non-perturbative components of the Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) Sudakov form factor, establishing a formal connection between the PB method and the standard TMD factorization framework.

The CSS formalism, particularly in its CSS1 and CSS2 variants, provides a systematic way to resum logarithmically enhanced terms in TMD cross sections, with the perturbative part governed by the functions Aa(αs) and Ba(αs), and the non-perturbative part encoded in functions like ga/A, ga/B, and gK,a.

The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, which governs the evolution of TMDs in the CSS formalism, can be extracted from PB predictions in processes like Drell-Yan, demonstrating the compatibility of the PB method with established TMD factorization theory.

The PB method enables the first TMD initial state parton shower, where kinematics are sampled according to TMDs, and supports matching to fixed-order matrix elements and merging of multi-jet samples, making it suitable for precision phenomenology.

The PB method has been successfully applied to a range of processes including inclusive Drell-Yan, lepton-jet correlations in DIS, Drell-Yan + jets, and dijet production, showing its versatility and predictive power.
quantitative: The PB Sudakov form factor is approximated as: Δa(μ², μ₀²) ≈ exp(−∫_{μ₀²}^{μ²} (dμ′²/μ′²) × (∫₀^{zM} ka(αs) / (1−z) dz − da(αs)))

The CSS1 Sudakov form factor is given by: Δa^CSS1(Q, Q₀, b, xa, x̃a, b_max, C₁, C₂) = exp(−∫_{μ_b*²}^{μ_Q²} (dμ′²/μ′²) × (Aa(αs) ln(Q²/μ′²) + Ba(αs))) × exp(−ga/A(xa, b, b_max) − g̃a/B(x̃a, b, b_max) − gK,a(b, b_max) ln(Q²/Q₀²))

The CSS2 Sudakov form factor is expressed as: Δa^CSS2(Q, Q₀, b, xa, x̃a, b_max, C₁, C₂) = exp(−∫_{μ_b*}^{μ_Q} (dμ′/μ′) × (γk,a(αs) ln(Q²/μ′²) − 2γa(αs))) × exp(−ga/A(xa, b, b_max) − g̃a/B(x̃a, b, b_max) − gK,a(b, b_max) ln(Q²/Q₀²)) × exp(̃Ka(b*, μ_b*) ln(Q²/μ_b*²))
The perturbative coefficients Aa(αs) and Ba(αs) have series expansions: Ra = ∑_n (αs/(2π))^n Ra^(n), with LL accuracy determined by Aa, NLL by Aa and Ba, and NNLL by Aa, Ba, and higher-order terms.
The PB method achieves NNLL accuracy in low-qT resummation through the use of an effective soft-gluon coupling, improving precision in TMD predictions.

The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, denoted as ̃Ka(b*, μ_b*), is extracted from PB Drell-Yan predictions under different radiation modeling assumptions, providing a direct link between PB and CSS evolution.

authors: Aleksandra Lelek

content_sections.1 Introduction: Precision measurements occupy a prominent place on agendas of the newly planned high-energy collider experiments (see e. g. Refs. (refciteAzzi:2019yne,AbdulKhalek:2022hcn,LHeC:2020van,FCC:2018byv)). To succeed, precision measurement requires both the experiment and theory prediction to be as accurate as possible, but the theory’s accuracy is very often lower than that of the experiment. One of the main limitations of the theory predictions provided by the common Monte Carlo (MC) generators, originates from the treatment of proton structure through Parton Distribution Functions (PDFs) and collinear factorization citebib.bib5, which account only for the longitudinal component of the proton’s momentum, neglecting the transverse ones. The formalism to properly incorporate the three-dimensional (3D) proton structure is the so-called Transverse Momentum Dependent (TMD) factorization with the base given by Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) citebib.bib6, bib.bib7. Recently, the TMD Parton Branching (PB) method citebib.bib8, bib.bib9 was developed to address the 1D limitations of the standard MC generators and to include elements of the TMD physics in a MC framework. Below, the equivalence of the CSS and PB Sudakov form factors is discussed, summarizing the Refs. (refciteALelekEtAll,Martinez:2024twn).
content_sections.2 The components and applications of the: The PB method provides evolution equations for TMD PDFs (abbreviated as TMDs) and allows the use of those within TMD MC generators. The TMD evolution equation is implemented and solved with MC techniques in uPDFevolv2 package citebib.bib12, extending uPDFevolv citebib.bib13. The free parameters of the PB parton distributions at the lowest scale can be fitted to data via xFitter citebib.bib14, bib.bib15 plugin. The TMD MC generator CASCADE3 citebib.bib16, bib.bib17, implements the TMDs in event generation. It provides a procedure for matching TMDs to fixed order matrix elements citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, as well as for TMD merging of multi-jet samples citebib.bib20. It contains also the first TMD initial state parton shower where the kinematics is sampled according to TMDs. The fits of initial parameters of the PB distributions to experimental data were performed in several studies using inclusive deep inelastic scattering (DIS) data from HERA citebib.bib18, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23 and Drell-Yan (DY) at different center-of-mass energies and mass ranges citebib.bib24, bib.bib25. The PB method was successfully applied to obtain precise predictions for lepton-jet correlations in DIS process citebib.bib26, inclusive DY, at different center of mass energies and mass ranges citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib27, DY ++ jets citebib.bib20, bib.bib28 and dijets citebib.bib29.
content_sections.3 Logarithmic accuracy of the TMD PB met: The PB Sudakov form factor can be written in terms of virtual parts of the DGLAP splitting functions, using the momentum sum rule, such that it can be approximately written as: Δa​(μ2,μ02)≈exp⁡(−∫μ02μ2d​μ′⁣2μ′⁣2​(∫0zMka​(αs)​11−z​d​z−da​(αs))). Deltaa(mu2,mu02)approxexpleft(-intmu02mu2fractextrmdmuprime 2muprime 2left(int0zMka(alphas)frac11-ztextrmdz-da(alphas)right)right);. The coefficients kaka, dada and all the other coefficients relevant for this work can be found e. g. in Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll). The first term in the exponent gives the double logarithmic structure whereas the second term is a single logarithmic. Eq. coincides with the standard way of writing the Sudakov form factor (i. e. with the real parts of the DGLAP splitting functions, see e. g. Eq. of Ref. (refciteHautmann:2017fcj)) when zM→1zMrightarrow 1 and the scale of the strong coupling is equal to the branching scale, αs​(μ′⁣2)alphas(muprime 2). In this work, several distinct scenarios for zMzM and the scale of αsalphas are investigated.

The CSS formula for TMD factorization of the cross section can be written in different ways depending on conventions and scale choices (see an overview in e. g. Refs. (refciteCollins:2014jpa,Collins:2017oxh)). In so-called CSS1 version, the Sudakov form factor can be written as ΔaCSS1​(Q,Q0,b,xa,xa,ax,C1,C2)=displaystyleDeltaarmCSS1(Q,Q0,b,xa,xwidetildea,brmmax,C1,C2)= exp⁡−∫μb⁣∗2μQ2d​μ′⁣2μ′⁣2​(Aa​(αs)​ln⁡(μQ2μ′⁣2)+Ba​(αs))displaystyleexpleft-intmub*2muQ2fracrmdmuprime 2muprime 2left(Aa(alphas)lnleft(fracmuQ2muprime 2right)+Ba(alphas)right)right ×exp⁡−ga/A​(xa,b,ax)−ga/B​(xa,b,ax)−gK,a​(b,ax)​ln⁡Q2Q02,displaystyletimesexpleft-ga/A(xa,b,brmmax)-gwidetildea/B(xwidetildea,b,brmmax)-gK,a(b,brmmax)lnfracQ2Q02right;, see Refs. (refciteCollins:2014jpa,Collins:2017oxh) for the explanation of the notation. The first exponent after the equality sign, referred to as the perturbative part of the CSS1 Sudakov form factor, provides the resummation of the logarithmically enhanced terms αsn​lnm⁡(Q2/q⟂2)alphasnlnm(Q2/qperp2). The functions Aa​(αs)Aa(alphas) and Ba​(αs)Ba(alphas) have series expansions in the strong coupling: ℛa=∑n(αs/2​π)n​ℛa(n)Ra=sumn(alphas/2pi)nRa(n). The leading logarithmic (LL) accuracy (m=n−1m=n-1) is given by coefficient AaAa, the next-to-leading (NLL) accuracy (m=nm=n) by AaAa and BaBa, the next-to-next-to-leading (NNLL) (m=n−1m=n-1) by AaAa and BaBa etc. 1In this work, only the Sudakov form factor is considered and the so-called Ca​bCab coefficients are disregarded. . The second exponent on the right-hand side of Eq. contains the non-perturbative contributions. The functions ga/Aga/A, ga/Bgwidetildea/B can be interpreted as an analogue of the PB intrinsic transverse momentum. In a modern, so-called CSS2 notation, the CSS Sudakov form factor is written as ΔaCSS2​(Q,Q0,b,xa,xa,ax,C1,C2)=displaystyleDeltaarmCSS2(Q,Q0,b,xa,xwidetildea,brmmax,C1,C2)= exp⁡−∫μb⁣∗μQd​μ′μ′​(γk,a​(αs)​ln⁡(Q2μ′⁣2)−2​γa​(αs))displaystyleexpleft-intmub*muQfracrmdmuprimemuprimeleft(gammak,a(alphas)lnleft(fracQ2muprime 2right)-2gammaa(alphas)right)right ×exp⁡(−ga/A​(xa,b,ax)−ga/B​(xa,b,ax)−gK,a​(b,ax)​ln⁡Q2Q02)displaystyletimesexpleft(-ga/A(xa,b,brmmax)-gwidetildea/B(xwidetildea,b,brmmax)-gK,a(b,brmmax)lnfracQ2Q02right) ×exp⁡(Ka​(b∗,μb⁣∗)​ln⁡Q2μb⁣∗2),displaystyletimesexpleft(widetildeKa(b*,mub*)lnfracQ2mub*2right);, where KawidetildeKa is so-called perturbative Collins-Soper (CS) kernel and the non-perturbative functions ga/Aga/A, ga/Bgwidetildea/B and gK,agK,a (constituting the non-perturbative part of the CS kernel) are the same as in the CSS1 formalism. The coefficients AaAa and BaBa and γk,agammak,a and γagammaa do not coincide at all orders but they are related to each other citebib.bib31.

The PB Sudakov form factor can be split into two parts by introducing an intermediate dynamical scale zdyn=1−q0/μ′zrmdyn=1-q0/muprime, motivated by angular ordering Δa​(μ2,μ02)=displaystyleDeltaa(mu2,mu20)= exp⁡(−∫μ02μ2d​μ′⁣2μ′⁣2​[∫0zdyn​(μ′)𝑑z​ka​(αs)1−z−da​(αs)])displaystyleexpleft(-intmu2mu20fracdmuprime 2muprime 2left[int0ztextdyn(muprime)dzfracka(alphas)1-z-da(alphas)right]right) The variable q0q0 can be interpreted as a minimal resolved emitted transverse momentum and μ′muprime is the energy scale of the branching. The first exponent after the equality sign of Eq. is referred here as the perturbative and the second one as the non-perturbative Sudakov form factor. To compare the PB Sudakov form factor to that of CSS, the mapping of the evolution variable μ′muprime to the emitted transverse momentum q⟂qperp with the angular ordering condition (q⊥2=(1−z)2​μ′⁣2qbot2=(1-z)2muprime 2), as done in Ref. (refciteHautmann:2019biw), can be performed 2Notice that the scale of αsalphas should be zz-independent to be able to perform the zz integration, i. e. in the PB method αs​(q⊥2)alphas(qbot2) with the freezing condition (αs=αs​(q02)alphas=alphas(q02) for q⊥<q0qbot<q0). . Δa(P)​(μ2,q02)=exp⁡(−∫q02μ2d​q⟂2q⟂2​[12​ka​(αs)​ln⁡(μ2q⟂2)−da​(αs)]),Deltaa(textP)(mu2,q02)=expleft(-intq02mu2fracdqperp2qperp2left[frac12ka(alphas)lnleft(fracmu2qperp2right)-da(alphas)right]right);, where a term exp⁡(∫q02μ02d​q⟂2q⟂2​∫01−|q⟂|μ0ka​(αs)1−z​𝑑z)expleft(intq02mu02fracdqperp2qperp2int01-frac|qperp|mu0fracka(alphas)1-zdzright) was neglected since μ0≈q0mu0approx q0 = 𝒪O(1 GeV). With that, one can compare the perturbative Sudakovs of the two methods, i. e. Eq. with the first exponent of Eq. . To fulfil the momentum conservation, the PB algorithm implements the DGLAP splitting functions both in the real emission probability and in the argument of the Sudakov exponent citebib.bib33. As a consequence, the logarithmic accuracy of the PB method is always ”in-between” two consecutive orders. The kaka coefficient of the LO DGLAP splitting functions coincides with AaAa giving the LL. The coefficient da=−12​Bada=-frac12Ba provides the single logarithmic term at the NLL accuracy. The double logarithmic term at NLL accuracy is obtained by the use of NLO splitting function coefficients, i. e. ka=Aaka=Aa. Since A=1/2​γk,aA=1/2gammak,a, A=1/2​γk,aA=1/2gammak,a and B=−γaB=-gammaa, the perturbative PB Sudakov form factor at NLL corresponds also exactly also to the first part of the CSS2 Sudakov.

With the NLO splitting functions, also a part of NNLL resummation is included by the dada coefficient. The PB method uses M​S¯overlineMS resummation scheme citebib.bib34 where BaBa corresponds to −2​da-2da. The difference between the dada coefficients used by PB and the commonly used BqBq in DY and BgBg in Higgs scheme is citebib.bib35: Bq​DY−(−2)⋅dq=16​CF​π​β0​(ζ2−1)BqrmDY-(-2)cdot dq=16CFpibeta0left(zeta2-1right); and Bg​H−(−2)⋅dg=16​CA​π​β0​(ζ2+1124). BgrmH-(-2)cdot dg=16CApibeta0left(zeta2+frac1124right);. Because of collinear anomaly citebib.bib36, the PB coefficient kaka of the NNLO splitting functions does not coincide with the coefficient AaAa, however this double logarithmic term at NNLL can be included by using the physical (effective) soft-gluon coupling citebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39. In Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll), the physical soft-gluon coupling αsphys=αs​(1+∑n=1∞𝒦(n)​(αs2​π)n)alphastextphys=alphasleft(1+sumn=1inftyK(n)left(fracalphas2piright)nright); is used to modify Eq. : Δa​(μ2,μ02)=exp⁡(−∫μ02μ2d​μ′⁣2μ′⁣2​(∫0zMka​(αsphys)​11−z​d​z−da​(αsphys))). Deltaa(mu2,mu02)=expleft(-intmu02mu2fractextrmdmuprime 2muprime 2left(int0zMka(alphasrmphys)frac11-ztextrmdz-da(alphasrmphys)right)right);. By using a combination of DGLAP splitting functions truncated at a given order and the physical soft-gluon coupling with appropriate coefficients, Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll) obtained the PB predictions with NLL and, for the first time, NNLL (both single and double logarithmic) coefficients in the Sudakov form factor. At NNLL, the perturbative Sudakov is written as: ln⁡(Δa(P)​(μ2,q02))=displaystyleln(Deltaa(textP)(mu2,q02))= where 𝒦⋅ka=Aa3Kcdot ka=Aa3. With that, all the resummation coefficients required by CSS1 in the Sudakov form factor at NNLL, i. e. AaAa, AaAa, AaAa, BaBa and Ba(2,M​S¯)Ba(2,overlineMS), are now included in the PB Sudakov form factor. The third row of Eq. corresponds to the coefficients included in the standard NLO PB evolution (i. e. AaAa, AaAa, BaBa and Ba(2,M​S¯)Ba(2,overlineMS)). Fig. illustrates the PB parton densities for down quark obtained with NLL, NLO and NNLL evolution and their impact on DY p⊥pbot spectrum (the details of the evolution and on how the DY prediction was obtained are given in Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll)). One can see that both for (integrated)TMDs and DY prediction, the difference between NLL and NLO is significant whereas the difference between the NLO and NNLL predictions is of the order of few % which is understood since a part of the NNLL solution is already included with the standard NLO PB evolution.

Using the relations between the CSS1 and CSS2 coefficients citebib.bib31, one can demonstrate that the PB approach contains also the CSS2 coefficients: γagammaa, γagammaa, γk,agammak,a, γk,agammak,a, γk,agammak,a and the perturbative part of the CS kernel KawidetildeKa (see Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll) for detailed discussion).
content_sections.4 PB non-perturbative Sudakov form facto: In the non-perturbative region αsalphas is frozen to αs​(q0)alphas(q0). With that, the second (i. e. non-perturbative) exponent of Eq. simply gives ln⁡Δa(NP)​(μ2,μ02,q0)displaystylelnDelta(textNP)a(mu2,mu02,q0) =displaystyle= −ka​(αs)2​ln⁡(μ2μ02)​ln⁡(q02ϵ2​μ0​μ). displaystyle-fracka(alphas)2lnleft(fracmu2mu02right)lnleft(fracq02epsilon2mu0muright);;. An analytical comparison of Eq. with the CSS2 Sudakov form factor in Eq. shows corresponding logarithms of μ2/μ02mu2/mu02 and Q2/Q02Q2/Q02 in the exponent. With that, the Eq. can be identified with the non-perturbative component of the CS kernel. This observation motivates the extractions of the CS kernel from the PB approach using models with and without the non-perturbative Sudakov.

The methodology to determine the CS kernel from scattering cross sections was provided in citebib.bib40 and applied therein to the PB approach. Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll) utilized this method to study in detail the behaviour of the CS kernels extracted from the PB DY predictions obtained with TMDs with different treatment of radiation. Five PB models were investigated. All the distributions were obtained at NNLL with different settings controlling the amount of branchings: 1. with αs​(q⊥2)alphas(qbot2) and zM=1−10−5zM=1-10-5, where the intermediate q0=1.0​GeVq0=1.0;textrmGeV is used for separating the perturbative and non-perturbative regions. In the non-perturbative region αsalphas is frozen to αs​(q02)alphas(q02) (red curve); 2. with αs​(μ′⁣2)alphas(muprime 2) and zM=1−10−5zM=1-10-5 (blue curve), where the initial evolution scale μ0mu0 is the lowest scale in the αsalphas; 3. with αs​(q⊥2)alphas(qbot2) and zM=1−q0/μ′zM=1-q0/muprime with q0=1.0​GeVq0=1.0;textrmGeV (i. e. there is no non-perturbative Sudakov form factor) (purple curve). 4. with αs​(q⊥2)alphas(qbot2) and zM=1−q0/μ′zM=1-q0/muprime with q0=0.5​GeVq0=0.5;textrmGeV (i. e. there is no non-perturbative Sudakov form factor) (orange curve). 5. with αs​(q⊥2)alphas(qbot2) and zM=1−q0/μ′zM=1-q0/muprime with q0=0.5​GeVq0=0.5;textrmGeV (i. e. there is no non-perturbative Sudakov form factor), with additional cut in αs=αs​(max​(qcut,q⊥))alphas=alphas(textrmmax(qrmcut,qbot)) with qcut=1.0qrmcut=1.0 GeV (green curve). In scenarios and , q0q0 served as the lowest emitted transverse momentum as well as the lowest scale of the strong coupling. Since all of the TMDs were obtained with the same starting parameterization of PB-NLO-2018-Set2 citebib.bib18, all the differences between the results obtained with these distributions come purely from the evolution settings. Fig. illustrates the CS kernels extracted from the DY cross-section predictions obtained with those five evolution scenarios in comparison to several phenomenological and lattice extractions from the literature citebib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. The slopes of the extracted curves are consequences of the different amounts of radiation, no parametrization is assumed. The spread of the PB results covers a significant fraction of other extractions from the literature. The result with a flattening behaviour at large bb is especially interesting since most of the extractions in the literature assume a rising behaviour.
content_sections.5 Conclusions: In this paper, I summarized the results obtained in Ref. (refciteALelekEtAll,Martinez:2024twn). The PB Sudakov form factor was factorized in the perturbative and non-perturbative part by using an intermediate scale zdynzrmdyn, originating from the angular ordering condition. The separation allowed to illustrate an exact correspondence between the PB and CSS Sudakov form factors (both in the CSS1 and CSS2 notations), for both perturbative and non-perturbative components. The accuracy of the PB Sudakov form factor was increased up to NNLL by including AaAa coefficient via the physical soft gluon coupling. It was observed that the non-perturbative part of the PB Sudakov form factor corresponds to the non-perturbative part of the CS kernel. The CS kernels were extracted from the PB approach for five different NNLL models, differing by the amount of radiation. It was shown that different radiation models can led to very different shapes of the extracted kernel.

sorted_papers.title: TMD Parton Distribution and Fragmentation Functions with QCD Evolution
sorted_papers.abstract:
We assess the current phenomenological status of transverse momentum
dependent (TMD) parton distribution functions (PDFs) and fragmentation
functions (FFs) and study the effect of consistently including perturbative QCD
(pQCD) evolution. Our goal is to initiate the process of establishing reliable,
QCD-evolved parametrizations for the TMD PDFs and TMD FFs that can be used both
to test TMD-factorization and to search for evidence of the breakdown of
TMD-factorization that is expected for certain processes. In this article, we
focus on spin-independent processes because they provide the simplest
illustration of the basic steps and can already be used in direct tests of
TMD-factorization. Our calculations are based on the Collins-Soper-Sterman
(CSS) formalism, supplemented by recent theoretical developments which have
clarified the precise definitions of the TMD PDFs and TMD FFs needed for a
valid TMD-factorization theorem. Starting with these definitions, we
numerically generate evolved TMD PDFs and TMD FFs using as input existing
parametrizations for the collinear PDFs, collinear FFs, non-perturbative
factors in the CSS factorization formalism, and recent fixed-scale fits. We
confirm that evolution has important consequences, both qualitatively and
quantitatively, and argue that it should be included in future phenomenological
studies of TMD functions. Our analysis is also suggestive of extensions to
processes that involve spin-dependent functions such as the Boer-Mulders,
Sivers, or Collins functions, which we intend to pursue in future publications.
At our website we have made available the tables and calculations needed to
obtain the TMD parametrizations presented herein.

qualitative: The Collins–Soper (CSS) formalism provides the theoretical framework for TMD factorization, enabling the description of transverse momentum dependent parton distribution and fragmentation functions (TMD PDFs and TMD FFs) in high-energy processes.

Consistent inclusion of perturbative QCD (pQCD) evolution via the Collins–Soper evolution equation is essential for reliable phenomenological studies of TMD functions, as it accounts for the scale dependence of TMDs.

TMD factorization is valid under specific kinematic conditions, and deviations from it may signal new physics or breakdown of factorization, making evolved TMDs crucial for testing the validity of the factorization theorem.

The CSS formalism has been refined by recent theoretical developments that clarify the precise definitions of TMD PDFs and TMD FFs, ensuring consistency with the requirements of a rigorous TMD factorization theorem.
Evolution effects are not merely corrections but have significant qualitative and quantitative impacts on TMD functions, influencing their shape, scale dependence, and phenomenological predictions.

Spin-independent processes serve as a foundational testing ground for TMD factorization and evolution, offering a clear path toward extending the framework to spin-dependent TMDs such as the Sivers, Boer-Mulders, and Collins functions.
quantitative: The Collins–Soper evolution equation is given by: $ \frac{d}{d\ln\mu} \Phi_{i}(x, \mathbf{b}_T; \mu) = \int d^2\mathbf{b}'_T \, \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{b}_T - \mathbf{b}'_T, \mu) \, \Phi_{i}(x, \mathbf{b}'_T; \mu) $, where $ \mathcal{K} $ is the evolution kernel derived from pQCD.

The evolution kernel $ \mathcal{K} $ is related to the cusp anomalous dimension and the soft function, with $ \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{b}_T, \mu) = \frac{1}{2} \gamma_{\text{cusp}}(\alpha_s) \ln\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\mu_0^2}\right) + \text{non-perturbative terms} $.
The non-perturbative factor in the CSS formalism is typically modeled as $ \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} \mathcal{B} \, \mathbf{b}_T^2\right) $, where $ \mathcal{B} $ is a parameter fitted to data.

The paper uses fixed-scale fits and existing parametrizations of collinear PDFs and FFs as input, with evolution applied from a reference scale $ \mu_0 \sim 2 \, \text{GeV} $ to higher scales $ \mu \gtrsim 5 \, \text{GeV} $.

Numerical results show that evolution leads to a suppression of TMDs at large transverse momentum $ \mathbf{b}_T $, with the effect becoming significant at $ \mathbf{b}_T \gtrsim 0.5 \, \text{GeV}^{-1} $.
The evolution effects are found to be non-negligible, with changes in TMD magnitudes reaching up to 30–50% depending on the process and kinematic region.
