The nonperturbative factor SNPDsuperscriptsubscript𝑆NP𝐷Srm NPD is defined as SNPD​(bT,Q0,ζN,f)=g1D​bT2+g22​ln⁡ζN,fQ0​ln⁡bTb∗,superscriptsubscript𝑆NP𝐷subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑄0subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝑔1𝐷superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑇2subscript𝑔22subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓subscript𝑄0subscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏displaystyle SNPDleft(bT,Q0,zetaN,fright)=g1DbT2+fracg22lnfracsqrtzetaN,fQ0lnfracbTb*, with g1D=0.042​GeV2superscriptsubscript𝑔1𝐷0.042superscriptGeV2g1D=0.042,rm GeV2 citebib.bib14, bib.bib23. The values of Q0subscript𝑄0Q0 and g2subscript𝑔2g2 have been given in . It is noted that the above parametrization is consistent with the model used in citebib.bib14, bib.bib23 in the threshold limit z→1→𝑧1zto 1. Also, as mentioned earlier, a new fitting including the threshold effect is required for a higher precision theoretical result. Thus, the all-order resummation formula reads d4​σSIDISd​y​d2​𝒒T​d​τSIDIS=σ0DIS​∫CNd​N2​π​i​(τSIDIS)−Nsuperscriptd4superscript𝜎SIDISd𝑦superscriptd2subscript𝒒𝑇dsuperscript𝜏SIDISsuperscriptsubscript𝜎0DISsubscriptsubscript𝐶𝑁d𝑁2𝜋𝑖superscriptsuperscript𝜏SIDIS𝑁displaystylefracd4sigmaSIDISdyd2qTdtauSIDIS=sigma0DISintCNfracdN2pi ileft(tauSIDISright)-N ∫d​bT​bT2​π​J0​(qT​bT)​HSIDIS​(Q,Q)dsubscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇2𝜋subscript𝐽0subscript𝑞𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝐻SIDIS𝑄𝑄displaystyleintfracdbT,bT2piJ0(qTbT)HSIDISleft(Q,Qright) ×∑qeq2fq/h1TTMD(N,bT,Q)Dh2/qTTMD(N,bT,Q). displaystyletimessumqeq2tildefq/h1,TTMD(N,bT,Q)tildeDh2/qTTMD(N,bT,Q). Finally, we move on to the factorization and resummation for the inclusive back-to-back two hadron production in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collision, i. e. e++e−→γ∗​(q)→h1​(P1)+h2​(P2)+X,→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscript𝛾𝑞→subscriptℎ1subscript𝑃1subscriptℎ2subscript𝑃2𝑋e++e-rightarrowgamma*(q)rightarrow h1(P1)+h2(P2)+X, where Piμsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑖𝜇Pimu is the momentum of the hadron hisubscriptℎ𝑖hi, and the threshold variable in this process is defined by τe+​e−≡(P1+P2)2Q2,superscript𝜏superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑃1subscript𝑃22superscript𝑄2taue+e-equivfrac(P1+P2)2Q2, with Q2=q2superscript𝑄2superscript𝑞2Q2=q2. As expected, in the joint limit, the cross section is factorized as the product of hard factor and two TMD FFs that describe final-state di-hadron production. Besides, the RG consistence also implies the Collins-Soper scale is the same as that in the Drell-Yan and SIDIS processes. Therefore, we obtain the following all-order resummation formula. d3​σe+​e−d2​𝒒T​d​τe+​e−=σ0e+​e−​∫CNd​N2​π​i​(τe+​e−)−Nsuperscriptd3superscript𝜎superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscriptd2subscript𝒒𝑇dsuperscript𝜏superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝜎0superscript𝑒superscript𝑒subscriptsubscript𝐶𝑁d𝑁2𝜋𝑖superscriptsuperscript𝜏superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝑁displaystylefracd3sigmae+e-d2qTdtaue+e-=sigma0e+e-intCNfracdN2pi ileft(taue+e-right)-N ∫d​bT​bT2​π​J0​(qT​bT)​He+​e−​(Q,Q)dsubscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇2𝜋subscript𝐽0subscript𝑞𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝐻superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝑄𝑄displaystyleintfracdbT,bT2piJ0(qTbT)He+e-left(Q,Qright) ×∑qeq2Dh1/qTTMD(N,bT,Q)Dh2/q¯TTMD(N,bT,Q),displaystyletimessumqeq2tildeDh1/qTTMD(N,bT,Q)tildeDh2/barqTTMD(N,bT,Q), where σ0e+​e−=4​π​αem2/Q2superscriptsubscript𝜎0superscript𝑒superscript𝑒4𝜋superscriptsubscript𝛼em2superscript𝑄2sigma0e+e-=4pialpharm em2/Q2 is the Born cross section and He+​e−superscript𝐻superscript𝑒superscript𝑒He+e- is the corresponding hard factor, and the definition of the threshold-TMD FFs at the scale Q𝑄Q is given in . From now on, we have obtained the all-order resummation formula for Drell-Yan, SIDIS and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- processes, and also find a close correspondence between them. In the joint limit, the cross section is factorized as the product of the hard factor and threshold-TMD functions, including TMD PDFs and FFs. Such a property of universality is significant in the future global fitting analysis for threshold-TMD functions in different processes. To summarize this section, we give the following generic resummation structure for all three processes as d3​σAd2​𝒒T​d​τA=σ0A​∫CNd​N2​π​i​(τA)−Nsuperscriptd3superscript𝜎Asuperscriptd2subscript𝒒𝑇dsuperscript𝜏Asuperscriptsubscript𝜎0Asubscriptsubscript𝐶𝑁d𝑁2𝜋𝑖superscriptsuperscript𝜏A𝑁displaystylefracd3sigmaAd2qTdtauA=sigma0AintCNfracdN2pi ileft(tauAright)-N ∫d​bT​bT2​π​J0​(qT​bT)​HA​(Q,Q)dsubscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇2𝜋subscript𝐽0subscript𝑞𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝐻A𝑄𝑄displaystyleintfracdbT,bT2piJ0(qTbT)Hrm Aleft(Q,Qright) ×∑qeq2ℱi,h1TTMD(N,bT,Q)ℱi,h2TTMD(N,bT,Q),displaystyletimessumqeq2tildeFi,h1TTMD(N,bT,Q)tildeFi,h2TTMD(N,bT,Q), where for A=DYADYrm A=rm DY; ℱi,h1TTMD=fq/h1TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ1TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑓𝑞subscriptℎ1TTMDtildeFi,h1TTMD=tildefq/h1,TTMD, ℱi,h2TTMD=fq¯/h2TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ2TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑓¯𝑞subscriptℎ2TTMDtildeFi,h2TTMD=tildefbarq/h2,TTMD, for A=SIDISASIDISrm A=SIDIS; ℱi,h1TTMD=fq/h1TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ1TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑓𝑞subscriptℎ1TTMDtildeFi,h1TTMD=tildefq/h1,TTMD, ℱi,h2TTMD=Dh2/qTTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ2TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝐷subscriptℎ2𝑞TTMDtildeFi,h2TTMD=tildeDh2/q,TTMD, and for A=e+​e−Asuperscript𝑒superscript𝑒rm A=e+e-; ℱi,h1TTMD=Dh1/qTTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ1TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝐷subscriptℎ1𝑞TTMDtildeFi,h1TTMD=tildeDh1/q,TTMD, ℱi,h2TTMD=Dh2/q¯TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ2TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝐷subscriptℎ2¯𝑞TTMDtildeFi,h2TTMD=tildeDh2/barq,TTMD and the Born cross sections σ0Asuperscriptsubscript𝜎0Asigma0rm A and hard function HAsuperscript𝐻AHrm A are defined accordingly. Here in addition to τ𝜏tau, the Born cross section σ0subscript𝜎0sigma0 can also depend on additional variables, for example, σ0SIDISsuperscriptsubscript𝜎0SIDISsigma0rm SIDIS depends on the inelasticity y𝑦y as shown in . This structure holds in the all-order QCD resummation formula.
content_sections.3 Numerical results: In this section, we numerically study the threshold effect in TMD PDF and TMD FF using the factorization formula of Drell-Yan, SIDIS, and the back-to-back di-hadron production via the e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- annihilation process. We apply these extracted TMD functions to provide transverse momentum distributions for these three processes for different experimental kinematics. Throughout our numerical analysis, we use one loop strong coupling constant (αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas). The fine structure constant (αemsubscript𝛼emalpharm em) is taken to be 1/13711371/137 and the number of active quark flavor nf=5subscript𝑛𝑓5nf=5 in the massless limit. For the TMD and collinear evolution, we choose our initial scale (μF)subscript𝜇𝐹(muF) to be 1.31.31.3 GeV. As mentioned earlier, threshold factorization has been done in the Mellin space because, in this space, the convolutions become a simple product. After achieving the factorization formula in Mellin space, we need to do the Mellin inversion to achieve results in the x𝑥x space. During our numerical study, we observe that one needs to modify the Collins-Soper scale in order to achieve results that are consistence throughout the allowed kinematic region of τ𝜏tau. This modified Collins-Soper scale will introduce new poles in the Mellin inversion formula. We discuss these issues in detail in the next subsection and present a prescription for Mellin inversion.
content_sections.3 Numerical results 3.1 Modified Collins: In the NLL resummation formula, the Collins-Soper scale in the non-perturbative factor SNPsubscript𝑆NPSrm NP is given by Q/N¯𝑄¯𝑁Q/barN. When the moment variable N𝑁N becomes very large, ζfTTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑓TTMDzetafrm TTMD goes down to the nonperturbative scale Q02superscriptsubscript𝑄02Q02, which violates the power counting, Q≫Q​(1−τ)≫qTmuch-greater-than𝑄𝑄1𝜏much-greater-thansubscript𝑞𝑇Qgg Q(1-hattau)gg qT, in the factorization theorem. Therefore, we need to freeze the Collins-Soper scale as ζN,f<Q02subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝑄02zetaN,f<Q02 ζ∗≡ζ∗​(ζN,f,Q0)=(QN¯)2​(1+Q02​N¯2Q2),subscript𝜁subscript𝜁subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓subscript𝑄0superscript𝑄¯𝑁21superscriptsubscript𝑄02superscript¯𝑁2superscript𝑄2displaystylezeta*equivzeta*(zetaN,f,Q0)=left(fracQbarNright)2left(1+fracQ02barN2Q2right), which reproduces the resummation formula in the moment variable N𝑁N as long as Q/N¯≫Q0much-greater-than𝑄¯𝑁subscript𝑄0Q/barNgg Q0. Besides, it also allows us to perform a straightforward numerical calculation in the complex-N𝑁N plane. We leave the investigation of different function forms and corresponding theoretical uncertainties for future studies. To implement the inverse Mellin transformation, one often rewrites the standard textbook Mellin inversion citebib.bib71 as an integration over a real variable with adjustable contour: φ​(x)=1π​∫0∞dz​Im​[exp​(i​ϕ)​x−c−z​exp⁡(i​ϕ)​φn=c+z​exp⁡(i​ϕ)]. 𝜑𝑥1𝜋superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧Imdelimited-exp𝑖italic-ϕsuperscript𝑥𝑐𝑧𝑖italic-ϕsubscript𝜑𝑛𝑐𝑧𝑖italic-ϕvarphi(x)=frac1piintnolimits0inftydz Imleft[exp(iphi)x-c-zexp(iphi)varphin=c+zexp(iphi)right]. Then contour is characterized by a real number c𝑐c, which should be the right to the rightmost singularity of φnsubscript𝜑𝑛varphin, and an arbitrary angle ϕitalic-ϕphi as shown in Figure . The parametrization forms we chose are certain simple combinations of power functions, so they only have poles on the real axis. In this regard, one can always find a suitable c𝑐c and may try various ϕitalic-ϕphi to reach higher numerical efficiency. There are different Mellin inversion prescriptions available in the literature citebib.bib72, bib.bib71. However, when we modify the Collins-Soper scale term from ζfsubscript𝜁𝑓zetaf to ζ∗subscript𝜁zeta*, we find that this modified term would contribute two new poles n=−c±i​Q¯Q0,𝑛plus-or-minus𝑐𝑖¯𝑄subscript𝑄0n=-cpm ifracbarQQ0, with Q¯≡eγE​Q. ¯𝑄superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸𝑄barQequiv egammaEQ. These two singularities would cause numerical issues even when the contour might not exactly pass the poles for different Q¯/Q0¯𝑄subscript𝑄0barQ/Q0. For simplicity, we chose ϕ=π/2italic-ϕ𝜋2phi=pi/2 for our contour, which is safe no matter what the Q𝑄Q value is. Also, we choose c=1.6𝑐1.6c=1.6 to avoid all the other poles. Numerically, we vary the value of ϕitalic-ϕphi and c𝑐c and find the results are stable and do not depend, within errors, on the particular choice of input parameters.
content_sections.3 Numerical results 3.2 Threshold-TMD PD: In this subsection, we present the numerical results for threshold-improved TMD PDFs and FFs. To investigate the impact of threshold resummation, we compare the TMD PDFs with and without threshold resummation in Figure, which shows the transverse momentum distributions of TMD PDFs for the up (u𝑢u) quark in the proton. We vary the value of x𝑥x from 0.10.10.1 to 0.80.80.8 at 555 GeV. The green curves represent the results obtained without threshold resummation, corresponding to the case where ζN,f=Q2subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscript𝑄2zetaN,f=Q2 in . The red and blue curves correspond to the results obtained with threshold resummation using the ζ∗subscript𝜁zeta* scheme and naive replacement ζN,f=Q2/N¯2subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscript𝑄2superscript¯𝑁2zetaN,f=Q2/barN2, respectively. In the low x𝑥x region, all three curves exhibit consistent behavior since the threshold effect is mild. However, as we increase the value of x𝑥x, the threshold effects become evident. Especially, it is noteworthy that in the naive scheme the theoretical predictions for the distribution rapidly decrease to zero and then turn negative. Consequently, we conclude that the ζ∗subscript𝜁zeta* prescription is necessary to ensure reliable theoretical predictions. In Figure, we present the threshold improved TMD PDF for u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks. We make the use of CT18NNLO citebib.bib73 parameterized colinear PDF set at a given initial scale μF=1.3subscript𝜇𝐹1.3muF=1.3 GeV. The upper panel is for the u𝑢u quark and the bottom panel is for the d𝑑d quark. We present our results for three different choices of hard scales; Q=2.0,5.0,10.0𝑄2.05.010.0Q=2.0,5.0,10.0 GeV with three different choices of Bjorken scales x=0.4,0.6,0.8𝑥0.40.60.8x=0.4,0.6,0.8, from left to the right respectively. In the threshold region when the hadronic threshold variable is in the order of 111, the Bjorken scales are also in the order of 111. We present our results for a large Bjorken scale to highlight the threshold effect in this region. The uncertainty bands correspond to the 1-σ𝜎sigma variation from CT18NNLO PDFs using the Hessian method. The details of these uncertainties are given in appendix . In Figure, we present threshold improved TMD fragmentation function of Pion (π+superscript𝜋pi+ hadron). In this case, we use the parameterized JAM20 fragmentation function citebib.bib74 at the scale μF=1.3subscript𝜇𝐹1.3muF=1.3 GeV. Similar to Figure, the upper panel is for the up-type and bottom panel is for the down-type quark fragmentation functions and z𝑧z is the fragmentation variable. Here, we present our results for three different values of hard scale (Q𝑄Q) with three different values of fragmentation (z𝑧z) variables. The uncertainty bands correspond to 1-σ𝜎sigma variation of JAM FFs using the replica method. At this point, we would like to emphasize that the direct comparison between the usual TMD functions and threshold-improved TMD functions is not trivial. In our theoretical formalism, we evolve the TMD evolution factor from the initial scale μFsubscript𝜇𝐹muF to the desired final scale. On the other hand, in the standard TMD evolution, one starts from scale μb∗superscriptsubscript𝜇𝑏mub*. Besides, both perturbative and nonperturbative parts in the Collins-Soper evolution are also different in these two frameworks. Because of this different evolution scheme, it is not straightforward to compare them and we leave such a study for future investigation.
content_sections.3 Numerical results 3.3 Predictions for: Finally, we use previously determined threshold-improved TMD functions (PDFs and FFs) to predict some experimental results. For this, we investigate three different processes namely, DY, SIDIS, and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- annihilation process. For the DY process, we consider dilepton transverse momentum distribution from proton-proton collision. We choose the hadronic center of mass energy to be s=15.0𝑠15.0sqrts=15.0 GeV and Q=6.0𝑄6.0Q=6.0 GeV, which is relevant to the Drell-Yan production at typical Fermilab experiments. The results are presented in the left panel of Figure . For the e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- annihilation process, we consider Pion (π+superscript𝜋pi+) in the final hadronic state. We choose the virtuality of the photon to be Q=10.58𝑄10.58Q=10.58 GeV and the threshold variable τ=0.64𝜏0.64tau=0.64 for the BELLE citebib.bib75 kinematics. The results are presented in the right panel of Figure . The uncertainties in all the cross sections are coming from the threshold-TMD functions which are computed from the 1-σ𝜎sigma variation of PDF and FF using Hessian and Replica methods respectively. As shown in the previous subsection, the uncertainty from FFs is larger than that from PDFs at a similar value of the light-cone momentum fraction making a wider uncertainty band in the e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- process than in the Drell-Yan process. Finally, in Figure , we present the results for the SIDIS process. In this case, we make use of four experiments kinematics namely HERMES citebib.bib76, COMPASS citebib.bib77, the future Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) citebib.bib3 at Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the 121212 GeV programcitebib.bib78 currently underway at Jefferson Lab (JLab). The upper left panel is for HERMES kinematics of e​p𝑒𝑝ep collision with hard scale Q2=4superscript𝑄24Q2=4 GeV and threshold parameter τ=0.24𝜏0.24tau=0.24 and the upper right panel is for COMPASS kinematics with hard scale Q2=20superscript𝑄220Q2=20 GeV and threshold parameter τ=0.32𝜏0.32tau=0.32. In the lower panel, we present the results for EIC (left, Q2=50superscript𝑄250Q2=50 GeV and τ=0.4𝜏0.4tau=0.4) and JLab 12 GeV program (right, Q2=3superscript𝑄23Q2=3 GeV and τ=0.48𝜏0.48tau=0.48) kinematics. To conclude this section, we notice that although the future EIC will make the most precise SIDIS measurements at small x𝑥x (down to x∼10−4similar-to𝑥superscript104xsim 10-4), the EIC will also increase the precision of the data in the large-x𝑥x region up to x∼0.5similar-to𝑥0.5xsim 0.5. The JLab 12 GeV program will make precision measurements up to x∼0.6similar-to𝑥0.6xsim 0.6 and smaller values of Q2superscript𝑄2Q2. We expect both EIC and JLab 12 to make important constraints on these threshold TMD PDFs and TMD FFs in the near future.
content_sections.4 Conclusion: In this paper, we have introduced the threshold TMD functions namely, PDFs and FFs. To probe this threshold effect, we use three different processes: Drell-Yan, SIDIS and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- processes. Considering the extra degrees of freedom in this kinematical region known as collinear-soft degrees of freedom, we re-factorized the formula for these processes and defined threshold-improved TMD distribution functions. We observe that because of the phase space reduction in the threshold region, the Collins-Soper scale is not the same for the usual TMD and threshold-improved TMD distribution functions. We find that ζN,f=Q2/N¯2subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscript𝑄2superscript¯𝑁2zetaN,f=Q2/barN2 for threshold TMD whereas ζf=Q2subscript𝜁𝑓superscript𝑄2zetaf=Q2 for the usual TMD. During our numerical analysis, we observe that apart from the RG consistency, the Collins-Soper scale has to be chosen in such a way that it is always greater than the non-perturbative scale Q0subscript𝑄0Q0. Therefore, one needs to modify the Collins-Soper scale, and this modified scale introduces a new kind of pole in the integration contour. We provide a Mellin inversion prescription to avoid all kinds of poles in the integration contour. Finally, we provide the numerical predictions for transverse momentum distributions of Drell-Yan, SIDIS and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- processes for different experimental kinematics using these threshold-improved TMD functions. Our theoretical formalism would open a new window into TMD physics. Future experimental analysis and global fitting analysis will certainly help in understanding the non-perturbative mechanism of the TMD functions in the threshold limit and unveiling the three-dimensional picture of a hadron in the large x𝑥x limit. Our formalism will be important to extract these TMD functions in the threshold region and will be reliable theoretical input to understand the experimental data. In this work, we only present the factorization and resummation formula for the unpolarized cross section, but it can be generalized to the process involving polarized hadron in both initial and final states. The corresponding theoretical predictions on the spin asymmetry in the threshold limit will be explored in future work. Acknowledgements. We thank Hui-Chun Chin, Jun Gao, Dianyu Liu, Zekun Lou and Yiyu Zhou for their useful discussions. Z. K. is supported by the National Science Foundation under grant No. PHY-1945471. K. S. and D. Y. S. are supported by the National Science Foundations of China under Grant No.12275052 and No.12147101 and the Shanghai Natural Science Foundation under Grant No.21ZR1406100. Y. Z. is supported by the Tsung-Dao Lee Chinese Undergraduate Research Endowment under Grant No.202210246155.

sorted_papers.title:
Transformation of transverse momentum distributions from Parton
  Branching to Collins-Soper-Sterman framework
sorted_papers.abstract:
Two main frameworks for defining transverse momentum dependent (TMD) parton
densities are the Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) formalism, and the Parton
Branching (PB) approach. While PB-TMDs have an explicit dependence on a single
scale which is used to evolve PB-TMDs in momentum space, TMDs defined in CSS
formalism present a double-scale evolution in renormalization and rapidity
scales, via a pair of coupled evolution equations. In this letter I leverage
the Collins-Soper kernel determined from simulated Drell Yan transverse
momentum spectra using PB-TMDs, and provide, for the first time, the
transformation of TMD parton distributions from the PB framework to the CSS
formalism. The evolved PB-TMDs in $b$-space are compared to the recently
released, unpolarized TMD distribution ART23.

sorted_papers.title: Factorization for quasi-TMD distributions of sub-leading power
sorted_papers.abstract:
The quasi-transverse-momentum dependent (qTMD) distributions are equal-time
correlators that can be computed within the lattice QCD approach. In the regime
of large hadron's momentum, qTMD distributions are expressed in terms of
standard TMD distributions via the factorization theorem. We derive the
corresponding factorization theorem at the next-leading power (NLP), and, for
the first time, we present the factorized expressions for a large class of qTMD
distributions of sub-leading power. The NLP expression contains TMD
distributions of twist-two, twist-three, and a new lattice-specific
nonperturbative function. We point out that some of the qTMD distributions
considered in this work can be employed to extract the Collins-Soper kernel
using the standard techniques of different-momenta-ratio. We provide NLO
expressions for all the elements of the factorization theorem. Also, for the
first time, we explicitly demonstrate the restoration of boost invariance in
NLP TMD factorization.

qualitative: The factorization theorem for quasi-TMD (qTMD) distributions at next-to-leading power (NLP) connects physical transverse-momentum dependent (TMD) distributions of twist-two and twist-three, along with a new lattice-specific nonperturbative function.
The NLP factorization theorem restores boost invariance through the cancellation of special rapidity divergences, which is explicitly demonstrated for the first time in this work.

qTMD distributions at sub-leading power can be used to extract the Collins-Soper kernel via ratios of observables at different momentum scales, leveraging the universality of the kernel and cancellation of hadronic components.
The Collins-Soper kernel parametrizes properties of the QCD vacuum and is a universal function, enabling combination of data from different experiments to improve statistical precision.

The TMD operator expansion method is employed as the primary tool for deriving the NLP factorization theorem, providing a systematic framework for handling nonperturbative and perturbative contributions.
At NLP, the singularity structure differs from leading power, requiring careful treatment of explicit poles and rapidity divergences to ensure consistency with physical expectations.

The NLP factorization theorem is applicable to a large class of qTMD distributions, including those with Dirac matrices projecting good and bad quark spinor components (Γ ∈ Γ_T), which are typically power-suppressed in collider experiments but accessible in lattice QCD.
quantitative: The qTMD correlator is defined as: Ω[Γ](y) ∼ ⟨P,S| q̄(y) [staple link] Γ q |P,S⟩, where y is a space-like distance and the staple link ensures gauge invariance.
Power counting: Ω[Γ₊] ∼ P⁰ (leading power), Ω[Γ_T] ∼ P⁻¹ (next-to-leading power), where P is the hadron momentum.
The NLP factorization theorem includes contributions from twist-two TMD distributions, twist-three TMD distributions, and a new lattice-specific nonperturbative function.
The factorization theorem is derived at next-to-leading order (NLO) in perturbation theory, with explicit NLO expressions provided for all coefficient functions and nonperturbative elements.
The cancellation of special rapidity divergences is demonstrated explicitly, which is essential for restoring boost invariance in the NLP TMD factorization framework.

The final factorized form of the qTMD correlator at NLP and NLO is presented in both position and momentum-fraction spaces, with renormalization and evolution properties of nonperturbative functions fully specified.

The Collins-Soper kernel can be extracted from ratios of qTMD distributions at different momentum scales, using the relation: K(Q₁, Q₂) = ln(Q₁/Q₂) × (d/dlnQ) ln[⟨Ω(Q₁)⟩ / ⟨Ω(Q₂)⟩], where Ω denotes the qTMD correlator.

content_sections.1 Introduction: The determination of parton distributions with lattice QCD simulations is a rapidly growing direction in the physics of strong iterations. Within the last decade, it has been raised from an abstract concept citebib.bib1, bib.bib2 to practical applications (see recent reviews citebib.bib3, bib.bib4). It is foreseeable that the lattice-parton studies will soon reach a similar precision level as the experimental fits. Most importantly, lattice simulations can access parton distributions that cannot be (or are too complicated to be) measured experimentally. This is especially true for higher-twist parton distributions citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7 and transverse-momentum dependent (TMD) distributions citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12. In this work, we push the formalism of factorization theorems for lattice correlators further and derive the factorization theorem for lattice TMD distributions (usually called quasi-TMD or qTMD distributions) at sub-leading power. The derived factorization theorem connects TMD distributions of twist-three and a large set of lattice observables. The qTMD correlator is a hadron matrix element of the form Ω[Γ]​(y)∼⟨P,S|q¯​(y)​[staple link]​Γ​q​|P,S⟩,similar-tosuperscriptΩdelimited-Γ𝑦quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆¯𝑞𝑦delimited-staple linkΓ𝑞0𝑃𝑆displaystyleOmega[Gamma](y)sim P,S|barq(y)[textstaple link]Gamma q|P,S, where y𝑦y is a space-like distance, and a staple-like gauge link connects the quark fields. The precise definition is given in sec. . At large P𝑃P, the qTMD correlator can be written in terms of physical TMD distributions. These relations are a particular form of the TMD factorization theorem widely used for the description of the TMD spectrum of semi-inclusive processes, for instance, citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. In the qTMD case, the form of the factorization theorem crucially depends on the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma that contracts the quark spinor indices. So, for Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+ that projects both spinors to their good components, one needs the leading-power (LP) TMD factorization theorem citebib.bib16, bib.bib17. This case is already well-developed theoretically citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. The next-difficulty case is Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT which projects a good and a bad components of quark spinor. Here one needs the next-to-leading power (NLP) TMD factorization theorem. The factorization theorem for this case is derived in this work for the first time. From the collider-experiment view-point, the ΓΓGamma-matrix is selected by the kinematic and the type of scattering process, and many polarized structures are accompanied by extra power-suppression factors making them especially difficult to access. In contrast, the lattice simulations could measure correlators with different ΓΓGamma’s without conceptual complications, and the power counting of different components of ΓΓGamma is plain (i. e. Ω[Γ+]∼P0similar-tosuperscriptΩdelimited-subscriptΓsuperscript𝑃0Omega[Gamma+]sim P0 and Ω[ΓT]∼P−1similar-tosuperscriptΩdelimited-subscriptΓ𝑇superscript𝑃1Omega[GammaT]sim P-1). As a matter of fact, the NLP components of qTMD correlators have been already computed. For example, within the Lorentz-invariant approaches citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23 one obtains qTMD correlator with all components of ΓΓGamma as a by-product of the computational technique. These components are usually discarded due to a lack of applicability. One of the primary motivations for this work was to find an application for these components. One can expect two possible applications for qTMD distributions. They can be used to determine physical TMD distributions or to extract the Collins-Soper kernel. The latter case is especially important since it is the simplest and yet very important. In contrast to TMD distributions that parametrize partons dynamics, the Collins-Soper kernel parametrizes properties of QCD vacuum citebib.bib24. Therefore, it can be accessed in the ratios of observables, where hadron components cancel entirely citebib.bib8, bib.bib25. Collins-Soper kernel is a universal function, and measurements from different sources can be combined together, multiplying the statistical precision citebib.bib26. As we demonstrate in this work, the direct determination of TMD distributions from qTMD distribution of sub-leading power is not feasible (at the present moment). Nonetheless, they allow for the extraction of the Collins-Soper kernel and thus provide a new source of information for this interesting observable. The NLP TMD factorization theorem is a relatively novel direction of research. The first steps were made in ref. citebib.bib27, but the systematic development started almost twenty years later citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33. Still, many aspects of the NLP TMD factorization beyond leading perturbative order (LO) are mysterious. Thus, another main motivation for this work was to develop the factorization for the qTMD correlator at NLO till the stage of application (that is not yet done for Drell-Yan, or Semi-Inclusive Deep-Inelastic Scattering (SIDIS), although NLO expressions at the operator level are known citebib.bib31, bib.bib33). Indeed, the expressions for the factorization theorem for the qTMD correlator are shorter but contain all principal structures. For the derivation of the factorization theorem, we use the TMD operator expansion method citebib.bib31, which is so far the most developed approach to NLP TMD factorization. For the first time, we explicitly demonstrate that by brining together all elements of NLP TMD factorization, one receives a valid expression satisfying all expected properties. It is not a trivial statement since the singularity structures of NLP and LP cases are different. The paper is organized as follows. In sec. we introduce the basic definitions and notation. In sec. we present the computation of the effective operator for the qTMD correlator in TMD factorization. Here we closely follow the method presented in ref. citebib.bib31. The NLO computation of coefficient functions is given in sec. . The main result of this section is the bare expression for TMD factorization at LP and NLP at NLO which is presented in secs. and in position and momentum-fraction spaces correspondingly. The bare expression is practically useless because it contains explicit and implicit singularities and unresolved complex structures. These problems are addressed one by one in sec. . In particular, in secs. , and , we provide the renormalization and evolution properties of the relevant nonperturbative functions and demonstrate the cancellation of explicit poles present in the bare version of the factorization theorem. In sec. we discuss so-called “special rapidity divergences” observed in ref. citebib.bib33. We explicitly demonstrate their cancellation and that this mechanism is responsible for restoring the boost invariance of TMD factorization at NLP. The complex structure of the expressions is discussed in sec. , in which the final form of the factorization theorem is presented. In sec. we discuss possible practical applications of the derived factorization formula. Specifically, we parametrize and write factorization theorems for individual measurable components of the qTMD correlator. The new and main results are collected in sec. . In appendix , we summarize for convenience the used parametrizations for physical TMD distributions of twist two and three.

content_sections.3 TMD operator expansion for qTMD operat: In this section, we derive the bare form of the factorization theorem for the qTMD correlator using the method of TMD operator expansion, and compute the (bare) coefficient functions at NLO. The derivation follows the one for the correlators of electro-magnetic currents presented in details in ref. citebib.bib31. Therefore, we skip most of the conceptual discussion and point out only the specific features of the qTMD case. The main result from the calculations presented in this section is the bare form of factorization theorem given in secs. and .

There are two popular approaches to resolve the issue of double-counting. The first is to introduce an extra cutting rule in the overlap region, which eliminates the overlap on the level of the functional integral. This approach is used in refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib29. The second approach is to assume that hadrons do no contain the soft components (which is valid for the non-small-x approximation), and subtract double-counting contribution by division of the functional integral by corresponding vacuum contribution citebib.bib36, bib.bib16. This contribution is called the soft factor and denoted as S​(y)𝑆𝑦S(y). In this work, we utilize the second approach. The form of expressions is not unique but could be modified using EOMs. This presents the problem of fixing the operator basis for the sub-leading power computation. It is known that, generally, sub-leading power distributions mix with the leading power distributions under the renormalization. Therefore, the best choice for the basis is the one that nullifies the mixing. This is accomplished by sorting the operators with respect to the twist, that is the Lorenz-invariant characteristic of operator. The twist of operator is computed by the usual dimension-minus-spin rule, where spin is projected to the collinear direction. Distribution with different twists do not mix. In the present case the twist-decomposition must be done for collinear operator only, because the v𝑣v-collinear operators already have the minimal twist at NLP. The operator ξn¯subscript𝜉¯𝑛xibarn is of twist-one, and it cannot be reduced. The twist of operator ηn¯subscript𝜂¯𝑛etabarn (that appear in 𝒥A/NLP)JtextA/NLP) is not defined. Applying EOMs in the massless quark approximation, the field ηn¯subscript𝜂¯𝑛etabarn can be expressed via ∂μξn¯subscript𝜇subscript𝜉¯𝑛partialmuxibarn (the total derivative of twist-one operator) and An¯,μ​ξn¯subscript𝐴¯𝑛𝜇subscript𝜉¯𝑛Abarn,muxibarn (the twist-two operator). These operators have independent renormalizations, and thus represent our go-to choice for the basis. It is interesting to observe that a similar relation holds between coefficient functions in the ordinary TMD factorization theorem: the regularized (with ϵ<0italic-ϵ0epsilon<0) NLP coefficient function (see (6.13) in citebib.bib31) at vanishing gluon momentum coincides with regularized LP coefficient function (see (6.12) in citebib.bib31), i. e. C2Vladimirov:2021hdn ​(x2=0,ϵ)=C1Vladimirov:2021hdn ​(ϵ)subscriptsuperscript𝐶Vladimirov:2021hdn 2subscript𝑥20italic-ϵsubscriptsuperscript𝐶Vladimirov:2021hdn 1italic-ϵCtextcite[cite]@@bibrefAuthors Phrase1YearPhrase2Vladimirov:2021hdn@@citephrase(@@citephrase)2(x2=0,epsilon)=Ctextcite[cite]@@bibrefAuthors Phrase1YearPhrase2Vladimirov:2021hdn@@citephrase(@@citephrase)1(epsilon). We note that such relation does not hold if expansion over ϵitalic-ϵepsilon is taken before x2→0→subscript𝑥20x2to 0 limit (compare (6.14) and (6.15) in citebib.bib31). We do not know the fundamental reason for a relation between NLP and LP coefficient functions in TMD factorization. The most plausible explanation is that this is a particular case of Ward identities at vanishing momentum, also known as soft-gluon theorems citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40. If so, the relations must hold at all perturbative orders, and could serve as an additional demonstration of the correctness of our computation.

We now combine the expressions for the effective currents to form the factorization theorem for the effective operator . The LP term is given by the product of LP currents , whereas the NLP terms are the product of one LP and one NLP currents. Let us schematically summarize the necessary steps to obtain the desired factorized expression: i) multiply effective currents into the effective operator ; ii) write expressions in a generic gauge by assigning the light-cone links and rewriting gluon fields via the gluon field-strength tensors ; iii) recouple the color indices such that products of collinear and v𝑣v-collinear fields are independently color-neutral. Finally, the hadron matrix element is taken, and each combination of fields turns into an independent matrix element. Since these are standard procedures (see e. g. citebib.bib27, bib.bib29, bib.bib31) we do not present them in details, and we write directly the final expression. The bare factorization theorem reads Ωbare[Γ]​(y)superscriptsubscriptΩbaredelimited-Γ𝑦displaystylewidetildeOmegatextbare[Gamma](y) =displaystyle= Ψ​(b)​C1†​C1​[Φ11⟦Γ⟧​(ℓ,b)−12​∂μ∂+​Φ11⟦γμ​γ+​Γ+Γ​γ+​γμ⟧​(ℓ,b)]Ψ𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝐶†1subscript𝐶1delimited-superscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-⟦⟧Γℓ𝑏12subscript𝜇subscriptsuperscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-⟦⟧superscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝛾ΓΓsuperscript𝛾superscript𝛾𝜇ℓ𝑏displaystylePsi(b)widehatCdagger1widehatC1Big[widetildePhi11llbracketGammarrbracket(ell,b)-frac12fracpartialmupartial+widetildePhi11llbracketgammamugamma+Gamma+Gammagamma+gammamurrbracket(ell,b)Big] where indices μ𝜇mu are transverse, and ∂μ=∂/∂bμsubscript𝜇superscript𝑏𝜇partialmu=partial/partial bmu, ∂+=∂/∂ℓsubscriptℓpartial+=partial/partialell. Here, all distributions are bare distributions. To write the factorization theorem in this form, we used the total-shift invariance of forward matrix elements. The functions ΨΨPsi and ΦΦwidetildePhi parametrize the nonperturbative parts of the factorization formula. The double bracket on the Dirac structure impose the projection to the good component only, i. e. All definitions implicitly contain transverse links that connect the far ends of the Wilson lines. These links are required to make the definition strictly gauge invariant. Let us stress that the TMD-distributions (, , ) are independent nonperturbative functions. Each of them obeys a separate closed evolution equation. The indexing 11, 12, and 21 refers to the TMD-twist of this operators citebib.bib31. So, the operator of TMD distribution Φ11subscriptΦ11Phi11 consists of product of two (so-called semi-compact) operators of twist-one (good component of quark field with an attached light-like Wilson line), and it has TMD-twist-(1,1). The operators for TMD distributions Φ12subscriptΦ12Phi12 and Φ21subscriptΦ21Phi21 consist of operators twist-one and twist-two (good component of quark field and good component of the gluon field with attached light-like Wilson line). Therefore, they have TMD-twist-(1,2) and TMD-twist-(2,1), respectively. In the following, we refer to TMD distributions of TMD-twist-(1,2) and TMD-twist-(2,1), simply as TMD distributions of twist-three. The ΨΨPsi-functions do not have definite twist, because the twist of the Wilson line Hvsubscript𝐻𝑣Hv is not defined. Nonetheless, their enumeration follows the same pattern for analogy. These function are nonperturbative objects with autonomous evolution.

Taking the Fourier transformation with respect to ℓℓell, we obtain the momentum-fraction representation for the factorization theorem, namely Ωbare[Γ]​(x,b)subscriptsuperscriptΩdelimited-Γbare𝑥𝑏displaystyleOmega[Gamma]textbare(x,b) =displaystyle= Ψ​(b)​C1​(−x)​C1​(x)​Φ11⟦Γ⟧​(x,b)Ψ𝑏subscript𝐶1𝑥subscript𝐶1𝑥superscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-⟦⟧Γ𝑥𝑏displaystylePsi(b)C1(-x)C1(x)Phi11llbracketGammarrbracket(x,b) where (x1,2,3,b)subscript𝑥123𝑏(x1,2,3,b) is the short notation for (x1,x2,x3,b)subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏(x1,x2,x3,b). The integral measure is defined as ∫[d​x]=∫−11𝑑x1​𝑑x2​𝑑x3​δ​(x1+x2+x3),delimited-𝑑𝑥superscriptsubscript11differential-dsubscript𝑥1differential-dsubscript𝑥2differential-dsubscript𝑥3𝛿subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3displaystyleint[dx]=int-11dx1dx2dx3delta(x1+x2+x3), which is the consequence of momentum conservation. The TMD distributions in the momentum-fraction space are defined as follows Φ11[Γ]​(ℓ,b)superscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-Γℓ𝑏displaystylewidetildePhi11[Gamma](ell,b) =displaystyle= P+​∫−11𝑑x​ei​x​ℓ​P+​Φ11[Γ]​(x,b),superscript𝑃superscriptsubscript11differential-d𝑥superscript𝑒𝑖𝑥ℓsuperscript𝑃superscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-Γ𝑥𝑏displaystyle P+int-11dxeixell P+Phi11[Gamma](x,b), Φμ,i​j[Γ]​(z1,z2,z3,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝜇𝑖𝑗subscript𝑧1subscript𝑧2subscript𝑧3𝑏displaystylewidetildePhi[Gamma]mu,ij(z1,z2,z3,b) =displaystyle= (P+)2​∫[d​x]​e−i​(z1​x1+z2​x2+z3​x3)​P+​Φμ,i​j[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b),superscriptsuperscript𝑃2delimited-𝑑𝑥superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝑧1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑧2subscript𝑥2subscript𝑧3subscript𝑥3superscript𝑃subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝜇𝑖𝑗subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏displaystyle(P+)2int[dx]e-i(z1x1+z2x2+z3x3)P+Phi[Gamma]mu,ij(x1,x2,x3,b), where i​j𝑖𝑗ij is 12 or 21. We stress the “minus” sign in the definition of momentum-fractions for twist-three distributions. Such definition provides a “natural” partonic interpretation citebib.bib41. The coefficient functions obtained are C1​(x)subscript𝐶1𝑥displaystyle C1(x) =displaystyle= 1+2​as​CF​1−ϵ1−2​ϵ​Γ​(−ϵ)​Γ​(2​ϵ)​(−v24)ϵ​1(i​s​x​(v​P))2​ϵ+𝒪​(as2),12subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹1italic-ϵ12italic-ϵΓitalic-ϵΓ2italic-ϵsuperscriptsuperscript𝑣24italic-ϵ1superscript𝑖𝑠𝑥𝑣𝑃2italic-ϵ𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠2displaystyle 1+2asCFfrac1-epsilon1-2epsilonGamma(-epsilon)Gamma(2epsilon)left(frac-v24right)epsilonfrac1(isx(vP))2epsilon+O(as2), C2​(x2,x3)subscript𝐶2subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3displaystyle C2(x2,x3) =displaystyle= 1+2asΓ(−ϵ)Γ(2ϵ)(−v24)ϵ1(i​s​(x2+x3)​(v​P))2​ϵCF1−3​ϵ1−2​ϵdisplaystyle 1+2asGamma(-epsilon)Gamma(2epsilon)left(frac-v24right)epsilonfrac1(is(x2+x3)(vP))2epsilonBiggCFfrac1-3epsilon1-2epsilon The first argument of C2subscript𝐶2C2 is related to the momentum of gluon, and the second is the momentum of quark or anti-quark. We emphasize that the signs of momentum-fractions are not restricted. The TMD distributions and qTMD correlators are defined for positive and negative values of the momentum fractions. TMD distributions of twist-two with the negative values of x𝑥x are associated with the anti-parton distributions. TMD distributions of twist-three have a more involved interpretation. Three momentum fractions x1subscript𝑥1x1, x2subscript𝑥2x2 and x3subscript𝑥3x3 are related to each other by momentum conservation x1+x2+x3=0subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥30x1+x2+x3=0, which is reflected in the delta-function in the integral measure . There are six combinations of signs for x𝑥x’s. Each combination has a separate partonic interpretation for Φμ,12subscriptΦ𝜇12Phimu,12 and Φμ,21subscriptΦ𝜇21Phimu,21 citebib.bib33. The important point is that different ranges of x𝑥x’s are mixed in the integral convolutions with coefficient functions or with evolution kernel. In the formulas above, the restrictions for the integration domains should be found for each particular term resolving delta-functions. For example, the integral that appears in the second line of explicitly reads (for x>0𝑥0x>0) ∫[d​x]x2−i​s​0​δ​(x−x3)​C2​(x2,x1)​C1​(x3)​Φμ,21⟦γμ​γ+​Γ⟧​(x1,x2,x3,b)delimited-𝑑𝑥subscript𝑥2𝑖𝑠0𝛿𝑥subscript𝑥3subscript𝐶2subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥1subscript𝐶1subscript𝑥3superscriptsubscriptΦ𝜇21delimited-⟦⟧superscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝛾Γsubscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏displaystyleintfrac[dx]x2-is0delta(x-x3)C2(x2,x1)C1(x3)Phimu,21llbracketgammamugamma+Gammarrbracket(x1,x2,x3,b) =∫−11−xd​x2x2−i​s​0​C2​(x2,−x−x2)​C1​(x)​Φμ,21⟦γμ​γ+​Γ⟧​(−x−x2,x2,x,b). absentsuperscriptsubscript11𝑥𝑑subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥2𝑖𝑠0subscript𝐶2subscript𝑥2𝑥subscript𝑥2subscript𝐶1𝑥superscriptsubscriptΦ𝜇21delimited-⟦⟧superscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝛾Γ𝑥subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥2𝑥𝑏displaystyle=int-11-xfracdx2x2-is0C2(x2,-x-x2)C1(x)Phimu,21llbracketgammamugamma+Gammarrbracket(-x-x2,x2,x,b). Here, the integration involves both positive and negative values of x2subscript𝑥2x2. At the point x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0 the integrand is singular. Therefore, the i​s​0𝑖𝑠0is0 prescriptions are of utter importance. They are responsible for a number of effects discussed in the following section.
content_sections.4 Factorization theorem in the physical: The derivation of the bare form of the factorization theorem is only the halfway point in the derivation of the final expression. To obtain a presentation suitable for practical applications, one needs to perform several manipulations and combine together different elements. Some of these iterations require additional computations. In this section, we collect the key points of this procedure and describe the process of deriving the factorization expression in the physical terms. The well-known elements (such as the recombination of rapidity divergences) are discussed very briefly, whereas the novel aspects are presented in some detail. Structurally, the TMD factorization at NLP is more involved than at LP. It contains a larger number of details to be treated. The first point to address is the cancellation of divergences in-between different elements of the formula. The NLP TMD factorization contains the following combinations of divergences • The infrared (IR) divergences of the coefficient function are presented as 1/ϵ1italic-ϵ1/epsilon-terms in the bare expressions (, ). These poles are canceled by the ultraviolet (UV) renormalization constants of TMD distributions and ΨΨPsi-functions. The cancellation of 1/ϵ1italic-ϵ1/epsilon-terms is, however, not complete. The leftover is the UV pole corresponding to the renormalization of currents Jvsubscript𝐽𝑣Jv. Since the TMD distributions of distinct TMD-twists are independent nonperturbative functions, such cancellation must happen individually for each term of the bare expression . Schematically, one should observe that each term of factorized expression satisfies ZJ−2​Ci†​Cj​Zi​jTMD​ZΨ=finite,superscriptsubscript𝑍𝐽2superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑖†subscript𝐶𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑖𝑗TMDsubscript𝑍Ψfinitedisplaystyle ZJ-2CidaggerCjZijtextTMDZPsi=textfinite, where ZJsubscript𝑍𝐽ZJ is the renormalization constant for Jvsubscript𝐽𝑣Jv, Zi​jTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑍𝑖𝑗TMDZijtextTMD is the renormalization constant for Φi​jsubscriptΦ𝑖𝑗Phiij, and ZΨsubscript𝑍ΨZPsi is the renormalization constant for the corresponding ΨΨPsi-function. • The rapidity divergences of TMD operator and ΨΨPsi-functions are canceled by the soft factor S​(y)𝑆𝑦S(y) . At LP and NLP the soft factor is ordinary TMD soft factor citebib.bib32. The cancellation of rapidity divergences for NLP operators has been demonstrated explicitly at NLO in ref. citebib.bib31. In the present context, the only difference from computation in ref. citebib.bib31 are the ΨΨPsi-functions. The function ΨΨPsi has been studied in refs. citebib.bib35, bib.bib12, and its rapidity divergence is identical to Φ11subscriptΦ11Phi11. The rapidity divergences of Ψμ,12subscriptΨ𝜇12Psimu,12 and Ψμ,21subscriptΨ𝜇21Psimu,21 functions are the same as for ΨΨPsi at NLO, which can be checked by direct computation. Therefore, all rapidity divergent factors cancel at NLP just as they cancel at LP citebib.bib35, bib.bib11, bib.bib12. We do not provide a deeper discussion. • The integrals over σ𝜎sigma for NLP terms are divergent at σ→s​∞→𝜎𝑠sigmato sinfty. In momentum-fraction space , this divergence transforms to the divergence at x2→0→subscript𝑥20x2to 0. These divergences are called “special” rapidity divergences citebib.bib33. Special rapidity divergences are implicit and cancel between collinear and v𝑣v-collinear sectors (second and third lines in eqn. ). To make the factorization formula finite term-by-term, one defines physical TMD distributions by adding (and subtracting in the factorized formula) specific divergent pieces. Special rapidity divergences are specific for power corrections, i. e. , they are trivially absent at LP. The computation of rapidity divergences is performed in the δ𝛿delta-regularization defined in refs. citebib.bib42, bib.bib43. As a result of these procedures, one obtains the finite expression for the qTMD correlator with each nonperturbative element satisfying an evolution equation (given in sec. ). Even so, the expression is still not very practical. It contains a combination of terms with different imaginary parts. The last step is to resolve the complex structure and present the formula in a directly usable form. In the following sections, we discuss in particular detail these procedures. The final expression for the NLP factorization of the qTMD correlator is given in sec. .

The bare TMD distributions are defined in eqns. (, , ). Their renormalization and evolution properties are known. For the detailed description of twist-three TMD distribution we refer to ref. citebib.bib33. In this section, we briefly summarize the features that are important for the present work. All TMD distributions are renormalized by three factors. Two UV renormalization constant (one for each semi-compact operator), and the rapidity renormalization factor. Finally, we have to address the emergence of the special rapidity divergences. As it is discussed in sec. , the point x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0 is the singular point of the factorized expression. If the TMD distribution is continuous at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0, the integral around this point will only produce an imaginary part. However, twist-three distributions are generally discontinuous at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0. Therefore, the integrals of type are divergent. The divergences of integrals of type are rapidity divergences. They are a different type of rapidity divergences compared to the ordinary one of the TMD operator that are renormalized by the factor R𝑅R , and for that reason are called “special rapidity divergences”. Special rapidity divergences can be computed explicitly order-by-order in perturbation theory. One defines physical TMD distributions by subtracting a precomputed finite term such that the integrals of type are finite. The factorization theorem for qTMD correlator contains new objects – ΨΨPsi-functions. To our best knowledge these functions are specific to the factorization of the qTMD correlator. On the one hand they are similar to the ordinary TMD distributions, but with the parton field replaced by Wilson lines along direction v𝑣v (or equivalently by the field H𝐻H ). For that reason, some of the properties of ΨΨPsi-functions are analogous to the properties of TMD distribution (for example, double-scale evolution). On the other hand, they are similar to correlators of two heavy-quark fields, and some of their properties could be deduced by analytical continuation to v2<0superscript𝑣20v2<0. We summarize important properties of ΨΨPsi-functions in this section. The renormalization of ΨΨPsi functions is Ψbare​(b)subscriptΨbare𝑏displaystylePsitextbare(b) =displaystyle= R​(b2)​ZW​(b)​ZΨ​12​Ψ​(b;μ,ζ),𝑅superscript𝑏2subscript𝑍𝑊𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑍Ψ12Ψ𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystyle R(b2)ZW(b)ZPsi 12Psi(b;mu,zeta), Ψμ,21,bare​(σ,b)subscriptΨ𝜇21bare𝜎𝑏displaystylePsimu,21,textbare(sigma,b) =displaystyle= R​(b2)​ZW​(b)​ZΨ​2​ZΨ​1⊗Ψμ,21​(σ,b;μ,ζ),tensor-product𝑅superscript𝑏2subscript𝑍𝑊𝑏subscript𝑍Ψ2subscript𝑍Ψ1subscriptΨ𝜇21𝜎𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystyle R(b2)ZW(b)ZPsi 2ZPsi 1otimesPsimu,21(sigma,b;mu,zeta), Ψμ,12,bare​(σ,b)subscriptΨ𝜇12bare𝜎𝑏displaystylePsimu,12,textbare(sigma,b) =displaystyle= R​(b2)​ZW​(b)​ZΨ​1​ZΨ​2⊗Ψμ,12​(σ,b;μ,ζ),tensor-product𝑅superscript𝑏2subscript𝑍𝑊𝑏subscript𝑍Ψ1subscript𝑍Ψ2subscriptΨ𝜇12𝜎𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystyle R(b2)ZW(b)ZPsi 1ZPsi 2otimesPsimu,12(sigma,b;mu,zeta), where factor R𝑅R is the rapidity renormalization factor same as in the TMD distribution case . The factors ZΨ​1subscript𝑍Ψ1ZPsi 1 and ZΨ​2subscript𝑍Ψ2ZPsi 2 are the UV renormalization (in the light-cone gauge) of operators H𝐻H and H​Fμ−𝐻subscript𝐹limit-from𝜇HFmu- correspondingly. The factor ZWsubscript𝑍𝑊ZW represents the renormalization of the staple finite-size contour in the direction v𝑣v. This factor is the same for ΨΨPsi-function and for qTMD correlator , because this part passes intact from the initial definition to the factorized form. In our computation of the coefficient function we did not include the computation of self-energies for v𝑣v-directed Wilson lines, which are totally absorbed into the factor ZWsubscript𝑍𝑊ZW. All power-unsuppressed differences between the finite (but large) and infinite L𝐿L are accumulated in this factor. The special rapidity divergences cancel in the sum of term in the factorized expression. The cancellation is not traceless but leaves a term responsible for the restoration of boost-invariance of the whole expression. This important mechanism is not yet discussed in the literature, and thus we present it here with extra details. The special rapidity divergences cancel in-between genuine NLP terms and do not require any additional “soft-factor” contribution (note that such a soft factor should carry an index μ𝜇mu, and thus be a NNLP). This can be seen already from the Dirac structures of genuine and kinematic terms, which are richer for the genuine terms. Due to it, the number of Lorenz-invariant components of genuine terms is larger that those of kinematic terms. The explicit decomposition can be found in sec. . Each independent Lorentz-invariant component has a special rapidity divergences, but only some of them have contribution of smaller-twist functions that could be accompanied by some soft-factor to cancel it. Thus, the cancellation of special rapidity divergences between genuine terms is the only possible mechanism which would work for all polarization cases. The cancellation involves terms from several lines in the factorization formula . To make it more explicit, we extract the terms of interest here. The expression for the factorization theorem is not yet ready for a practical application. Since qTMD are real-valued functions, we expect that all the complex terms can be simplified into some real combinations. The resolution of the complex structure of NLP factorization is a straightforward but tedious procedure. Both TMD distributions and coefficient functions have complex parts. The TMD distributions 𝚽μ,12subscript𝚽𝜇12Phimu,12 and 𝚽μ,21subscript𝚽𝜇21Phimu,21 are complex-valued functions with indefinite T-parity. A better choice of basis was suggested in ref. citebib.bib33: 𝚽μ,⊕[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b;μ,ζ)subscriptsuperscript𝚽delimited-Γ𝜇direct-sumsubscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystylePhi[Gamma]mu,oplus(x1,x2,x3,b;mu,zeta) =displaystyle= 𝚽μ,21[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b;μ,ζ)+𝚽μ,12[Γ]​(−x3,−x2,−x1,b;μ,ζ)2,subscriptsuperscript𝚽delimited-Γ𝜇21subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏𝜇𝜁subscriptsuperscript𝚽delimited-Γ𝜇12subscript𝑥3subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥1𝑏𝜇𝜁2displaystylefracPhi[Gamma]mu,21(x1,x2,x3,b;mu,zeta)+Phi[Gamma]mu,12(-x3,-x2,-x1,b;mu,zeta)2, 𝚽μ,⊖[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b;μ,ζ)subscriptsuperscript𝚽delimited-Γ𝜇symmetric-differencesubscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystylePhi[Gamma]mu,ominus(x1,x2,x3,b;mu,zeta) =displaystyle= i​𝚽μ,21[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b;μ,ζ)−𝚽μ,12[Γ]​(−x3,−x2,−x1,b;μ,ζ)2. 𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝚽delimited-Γ𝜇21subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏𝜇𝜁subscriptsuperscript𝚽delimited-Γ𝜇12subscript𝑥3subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥1𝑏𝜇𝜁2displaystyle ifracPhi[Gamma]mu,21(x1,x2,x3,b;mu,zeta)-Phi[Gamma]mu,12(-x3,-x2,-x1,b;mu,zeta)2. These functions have definite complexity and T-parity. For this reason they are called TMD distributions with definite parity. The drawback is that such functions do not have partonic interpretation, and mix during the evolution. Nonetheless, the basis 𝚽μ,⊕,𝚽μ,⊖subscript𝚽𝜇direct-sumsubscript𝚽𝜇symmetric-differencePhimu,oplus,Phimu,ominus is advantageous in comparison to 𝚽μ,12,𝚽μ,21subscript𝚽𝜇12subscript𝚽𝜇21Phimu,12,Phimu,21. Here and everywhere, we use the convention that the logarithm has a branch cut for the real negative argument. For example ln⁡(x−i​s​0)=ln⁡|x|−i​s​π​θ​(−x),𝑥𝑖𝑠0𝑥𝑖𝑠𝜋𝜃𝑥displaystyleln(x-is0)=ln|x|-ispitheta(-x), where θ​(x)𝜃𝑥theta(x) is the Heaviside function. • The integration in the vicinity of x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0 point. Using these rules, and definition , we rewrite the factorized expression in the explicitly real form.

The final expression for the factorization of the qTMD correlator is Ω[Γ]​(x,b,μ)superscriptΩdelimited-Γ𝑥𝑏𝜇displaystyleOmega[Gamma](x,b,mu) =displaystyle= Ψ​(b;μ,ζ¯)​ℂ11​Φ11⟦Γ⟧​(x,b;μ,ζ)Ψ𝑏𝜇¯𝜁subscriptℂ11superscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-⟦⟧Γ𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystylePsi(b;mu,barzeta)C11Phi11llbracketGammarrbracket(x,b;mu,zeta) where the integral over x2subscript𝑥2x2 is restricted by the arguments of TMD distributions as: −10𝑥0x>0 and −1−x<x<11𝑥𝑥1-1-x<x<1 for x<0𝑥0x<0. The argument of twist-three TMD distributions is x=(−x−x2,x2,x)𝑥𝑥subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥2𝑥tildex=(-x-x2,x2,x) The coefficient functions are ℂ11subscriptℂ11displaystyleC11 =displaystyle= 1+as​CF​(−𝐋p2−2​𝐋p−4+π26)+𝒪​(as2),1subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹superscriptsubscript𝐋𝑝22subscript𝐋𝑝4superscript𝜋26𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠2displaystyle 1+asCFleft(-Lp2-2Lp-4+fracpi26right)+O(as2), ℂR​(x,x2)subscriptℂ𝑅𝑥subscript𝑥2displaystyleCR(x,x2) =displaystyle= 1(x2)++asCF−𝐋p2+π26(x2)+displaystylefrac1(x2)++asBiggCFfrac-Lp2+fracpi26(x2)+ ℂI​(x,x2)subscriptℂ𝐼𝑥subscript𝑥2displaystyleCI(x,x2) =displaystyle= δ(x2)+asCF[δ(x2)(−𝐋p2+π26)+2​sx(x2)+]displaystyledelta(x2)+asBiggCFBig[delta(x2)left(-Lp2+fracpi26right)+frac2sx(x2)+Big] The integrals with these coefficient functions are regular at all points of integration. The NLP correction, denoted by 𝒪​(λ2)𝒪superscript𝜆2O(lambda2), includes the corrections NLP corrections in P+subscript𝑃P+ and NLP corrections in 1/L1𝐿1/L. As we see here, the power corrections scale with x​P+𝑥subscript𝑃xP+ rather than just P+subscript𝑃P+. It agrees with our power counting , defined for components of parton’s momentum. Summarizing the factorization assumptions made in secs. and , we specify 𝒪​(λ2)=𝒪​(M2x2​(v​P)2,1b2​(v​P)2,bL,1M​L). 𝒪superscript𝜆2𝒪superscript𝑀2superscript𝑥2superscript𝑣𝑃21superscript𝑏2superscript𝑣𝑃2𝑏𝐿1𝑀𝐿displaystyleO(lambda2)=Oleft(fracM2x2(vP)2,frac1b2(vP)2,fracbL,frac1MLright). The expression is the complete NLP/NLO expression for the factorization of the qTMD correlator. It is apparently complicated and contains all possible combinations of factors and terms. Not all of these terms contribute to particular components of the qTMD correlator, as discussed in the next section. There are several interesting features of the expression that are specific to the TMD factorization at NLP. The first feature is that NLP factorization mixes T-odd and T-even terms. The T-odd(even) TMD distributions (do not) change their global sign under the rotation of staple contour to a different-sign infinity citebib.bib52. The T-parity of Φ⊕[Γ]superscriptsubscriptΦdirect-sumdelimited-ΓPhioplus[Gamma] is opposite to the T-parity of Φ⊖[Γ]superscriptsubscriptΦsymmetric-differencedelimited-ΓPhiominus[Gamma]. However, the terms with opposite parity in always have relative factor s𝑠s. Thanks to it, the relative sign between T-odd and T-even terms remains the same under the T-conjugation. Thus, NLP TMD factorization mixes distributions with different parity but preserves the global T-parity, which is required by the T-invariance of QCD. The second feature is the presence of multiple θ𝜃theta functions. This is an unhealthy property of NLP TMD distributions. Their evolution equation also contains step functions, and due to it, the distributions are discontinuous at xi=0subscript𝑥𝑖0xi=0. In particular, it leads to the appearance of the special rapidity divergences, discussed in sec. . Nonetheless, the integrals are well-defined in . The structure of discontinuities could probably be simplified, but at the moment, such a procedure is unknown. Finally, we observe that TMD factorization also incorporates the Qiu-Sterman-like contributions citebib.bib53, namely the contributions of twist-three distributions with the zero-momentum gluon 𝚽​(−x,0,x)𝚽𝑥0𝑥Phi(-x,0,x). Generally, twist-3 TMD distributions are discontinuous at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0, but we have checked that, for all physically accessible cases of Ω[Γ]superscriptΩdelimited-ΓOmega[Gamma], the contributions 𝚽​(−x,0,x)𝚽𝑥0𝑥Phi(-x,0,x) are well-defined for the known cases (see appendix C in ref. citebib.bib33). The distributions 𝚽𝚽Phi are either continuous or zero at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0, or contribute starting from 𝒪​(as2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠2O(as2). The expression is the first example of NLP TMD factorization at NLO written explicitly. The previous computations were either at LO citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib32, or written in the abstract operator form citebib.bib31. It gives a taste of what one can expect from the NLP TMD factorization for other observables.
content_sections.5 On practical application of NLP factor: There are two main motivations to study the qTMD correlators. The first one is to determine the Collins-Soper kernel – the nonperturbative function that governs the evolution in the rapidity scale of TMD distributions. The second one is to determine actual TMD distributions. In this section, we discuss the different possibilities of using NLP factorization theorem to improve our knowledge of TMD physics.

Our starting point is the assumptions that the qTMD correlator can be computed on the lattice, as a function of (v​P)𝑣𝑃(vP), ℓℓell and b2superscript𝑏2b2. Depending on ΓΓGamma, it has different number of tensor components written in terms of the vectors Pμsuperscript𝑃𝜇Pmu, vμsuperscript𝑣𝜇vmu, Sμsuperscript𝑆𝜇Smu, bμsuperscript𝑏𝜇bmu, and the tensors gμ​νsuperscript𝑔𝜇𝜈gmunu and ϵTμ​νsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝑇epsilonmunuT citebib.bib21. These components can be extracted individually. There are already several examples of such computations, see, f. i. , citebib.bib21, bib.bib54, bib.bib26, bib.bib55, bib.bib22. The factorization theorem provides the theoretical description for each component. Generally speaking, the comparison can be made for any component of Ω[Γ]superscriptΩdelimited-ΓOmega[Gamma]. However, some combination have a cleaner interpretation from the view-point of the factorization theorem. For example, the quasi-TMD correlators Ω[γ0]superscriptΩdelimited-superscript𝛾0Omega[gamma0] and Ω[v̸]superscriptΩdelimited-italic-v̸Omega[notv] both give access to the unpolarized TMD distribution f1subscript𝑓1f1. However, their sum is 𝒪​(λ2)𝒪superscript𝜆2O(lambda2). Therefore, their difference has numerically smaller power-suppressed contribution, and is better suited to the study of leading nonperturbative physics. Comparing components ( - ) with the factorization theorem we find the factorization for each individual component. There are 8 qTMD distributions that obey the LP factorization, and 16 qTMD distributions that obey NLP factorization.

The LP factorization theorem for qTMD distributions is well-understood and already applied in practice. The eight qTMD distributions that obey the LP factorization are those given in the lines (, , ). This part of our computation coincides with the known results. For a review of the current state, see citebib.bib19 and references within. In this subsection, we would like to provide a sketch of possible applications of the LP factorization theorem to contrast the problems with the application of the NLP factorization discussed in the following section. It has been used in ref. citebib.bib26. The ΨΨPsi-function can be computed independently citebib.bib10, bib.bib58. In this case, the factorization formula can be used to determine the TMD distribution itself. For a more extended discussion of applications, we refer to recent reviews citebib.bib19, bib.bib59. Note that the ΨΨPsi-function can be used as an independent source for the determination of Collins-Soper kernel citebib.bib60, bib.bib61.

The combinations of the TMD distributions that are present in C𝐶C and D𝐷D are not random. These combinations form autonomous pairs that mixes through the evolution citebib.bib33. The evolution equations for TMD distributions of twist-three have an integral-differential form similar to the evolution of ordinary parton distributions but with an additional double-logarithmic term. The full set of TMD distributions of twist-three splits into subsets that evolve with the integral kernel ℙAsubscriptℙ𝐴PA or ℙBsubscriptℙ𝐵PB (see sec.4.3 in ref. citebib.bib33). All combinations present in eqn. evolve with ℙAsubscriptℙ𝐴PA only. In comparison to the LP factorization formula the NLP factorization formula is awkward. First, none of the qTMD distributions is proportional to a single TMD distribution of twist-three but always to a pair. Both coefficient functions ℂRsubscriptℂ𝑅CR and ℂIsubscriptℂ𝐼CI have tree-order contribution, and thus there is no perturbative suppression for one element of a pair. Second, many of the qTMD distributions contain twist-two terms, which could not be easily removed. Third, the function 𝚿2subscript𝚿2Psi2 is a new nonperturbative component that cannot be determined solely from measurements of qTMD distributions. Thus, a direct determination of TMD distributions of twist-three from eqn. is cumbersome. Inspecting the table , we observe that each qTMD correlator has a counterpart with the same twist-three content. Therefore, by combining several qTMD correlators, one could disentangle individual components and determine the TMD distribution of twist-three. Here one should also account for the contamination by the twist-two terms. Concluding, the direct application of NLP factorization theorem does not seem practical for the moment, due to its involved content that entangles several TMD distributions in a single qTMD distributions. Nonetheless, the ratios of qTMD distributions E,ET⟂,EL,ET,FL⟂,G⟂𝐸superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑇perpendicular-tosubscript𝐸𝐿subscript𝐸𝑇superscriptsubscript𝐹𝐿perpendicular-tosuperscript𝐺perpendicular-toE,ETperp,EL,ET,FLperp,Gperp can provide access to the Collins-Soper kernel, in a way similar to the LP case. Such ratios can be considered both in position and in momentum spaces. In both cases, ratios are not pure functions of 𝒟𝒟D but contain contamination from twist-three TMD distributions. However, this contamination is small ∼as/Ncsimilar-toabsentsubscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝑁𝑐sim as/Nc. In both cases, one could not improve the precision of the approach systematically (contrary to the LP case). We are hoping that further progress in studies of twist-three TMD distributions will open opportunities to use more precisely.
content_sections.6 Conclusions: In this work, we study a particular class of lattice observables known as quasi-transverse momentum-dependent (qTMD) distributions. These are the diagonal matrix elements between hadron states of a quark-quark correlator whose Wilson line is staple-like and equal-time. At large hadron’s momentum, the qTMD correlator can be factorized in terms of physical TMD distributions and some unknown TMD-like functions. The form of the factorization theorem crucially depends on the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma that contracts quark-fields’ spinor indices. In this work, we consider two cases: Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+ that projects both quark fields to their good components, and Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT that projects a good and a bad components of the quark field. The first case Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+ obeys the leading-power (LP) factorization theorem and has already been studied in several works. The case Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT requires the next-to-leading power (NLP) factorization and is addressed in this work for the first time. We derive the factorization theorem for qTMDs with Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT and compute the corresponding coefficient functions at NLO. For the first time, we present the outcome of TMD factorization at NLP/NLO in a directly usable form. In this sense, the expression derived in this work can serve as an example of a structure expected for more involved observables, such as differential cross-sections. We explicitly demonstrate that by combining all elements of NLP TMD factorization, one obtains a valid and well-defined expression. It is not a trivial statement due to the different singularity structures between NLP and LP cases. The computation is done for a general Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT, which includes 16 different qTMD distributions measurable on the lattice. As a by-product, we also obtain the LP factorization and confirm previous computations with a different method. We explicitly check that the NLP factorization theorem does not contribute to the case Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+, and thus any power correction to them actually starts at NLP. Note, that in this work, we operate with massless quarks ignoring power corrections of the type mq/(v​P)subscript𝑚𝑞𝑣𝑃mq/(vP). Along the work, we made several observations related to NLP TMD factorization that are general and important beyond the physics of qTMD distributions. • We observed that, at the bare NLO level, the NLP coefficient functions exactly reproduce the LP coefficient function in the limit of vanishing gluon momentum. We have checked that the same observation holds for the bare coefficient functions in Drell-Yan/SIDIS citebib.bib31. Since at NLP v𝑣v-collinear gluons carry vanishing light-cone momentum, the NLP coefficient function Cvsubscript𝐶𝑣Cv is equal to the LP coefficient C1subscript𝐶1C1. We argue that these relations could be a consequence of soft-gluon theorems and valid at all perturbative orders, but we do not have general proof of this statement beyond NLO. • For the first time, we explicitly demonstrate the cancellation of special rapidity divergences. The special rapidity divergences appear in the integral convolutions of twist-three TMD distributions and were observed in ref. citebib.bib33. The cancellation takes place in-between different collinear sectors and restores the boost invariance of the NLP factorization theorem. This mechanism is an essential part of the proof of the TMD factorization at NLP. • In the NLP factorization theorem, a non-trivial interplay occurs between the real and imaginary parts of the coefficient functions and the TMD parametrizations. Consequently, TMD factorization also incorporates Qiu-Sterman-like contributions, i. e. , contributions of twist-three distributions with vanishing gluon momentum. Such contributions appear already at LO and were missed in many previous considerations. The expression for the TMD factorization theorem at NLP is rather more complex than its LP counterpart. It mixes TMD distributions of twist-two (and their derivatives), twist-three, and derivatives of Collins-Soper kernels. Due to it, the direct application of the theorem is involved and requires several measurements to disentangle individual elements. However, even in this case, the determination will be incomplete because qTMD distribution depends on a single momentum fraction, whereas a twist-three distribution depends on two momentum fractions. Yet we demonstrate that a subset of observables (6 out of 16) can be used individually to determine the Collins-Soper kernel using a simplified procedure. The procedure is valid in the large-Ncsubscript𝑁𝑐Nc approximation and assumes that some integral weakly depends on the transverse distance. Both assumptions are accurate at the current precision of lattice simulations. Acknowledgements. We thank Andreas Schäfer for numerous discussions and the motivation to study this case. A. V. is funded by the Atracción de Talento Investigador program of the Comunidad de Madrid (Spain) No. 2020-T1/TIC-20204. A. V. is also supported by the Spanish Ministry grant PID2019-106080GB-C21. This work was partially supported by DFG FOR 2926 “Next Generation pQCD for Hadron Structure: Preparing for the EIC”, project number 430824754. S. R. acknowledge the financial support from the physics department of Ecole Polytechnique.

sorted_papers.title:
Unpolarized Transverse Momentum Distributions from a global fit of
  Drell-Yan and Semi-Inclusive Deep-Inelastic Scattering data
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present an extraction of unpolarized transverse-momentum-dependent parton
distribution and fragmentation functions based on more than two thousands data
points from several experiments for two different processes: semi-inclusive
deep-inelastic scattering and Drell-Yan production of lepton pairs. The
baseline analysis is performed with a Monte Carlo replica method resumming
large logarithms at N3LL accuracy. The resulting description of the data is
very good ($\chi^2/N_{\rm dat} = 1.06$). For semi-inclusive deep-inelastic
scattering, predictions for multiplicities are normalized by factors that cure
the discrepancy with data introduced by higher-order perturbative corrections.

qualitative: TMD factorization theorems provide a rigorous framework for defining Transverse Momentum Distributions (TMDs), establishing their universality and evolution properties, which are essential for extracting TMDs from experimental data.

Collins–Soper (CS) evolution governs the scale dependence of TMDs, describing how they evolve with the factorization scale, and is a key component in the theoretical description of TMDs in both SIDIS and Drell–Yan processes.

The extraction of TMDs relies on the universality of parton distributions across different processes, enabling a global fit that combines data from SIDIS and Drell–Yan to constrain both TMD parton distribution functions (PDFs) and fragmentation functions (FFs).

The inclusion of higher-order perturbative corrections, particularly resummation of large logarithms up to N3LL accuracy, significantly improves the theoretical precision and the description of experimental data.

Normalization issues in SIDIS data, especially from fixed-target experiments at moderate to low scales, are attributed to O(αs) corrections to the hard factor, which are challenging to describe within the current framework.

To address normalization discrepancies in SIDIS, the authors introduce a data-driven normalization correction by matching the integrated TMD cross section to the corresponding collinear result, effectively fixing the normalization a priori and independently of the fit.
quantitative: The baseline fit achieves a very good description of the data with χ²/N_dat = 1.06, based on 2031 data points from SIDIS and Drell–Yan processes.
The analysis is performed at N3LL accuracy for the resummation of large logarithms, with the only missing component being NNLO collinear fragmentation functions to reach full NLL accuracy.
The TMD evolution is implemented via the Collins–Soper equation, which governs the scale dependence of TMDs and is essential for consistent predictions across different kinematic regions.

The normalization correction procedure involves matching the integral over transverse momentum of the TMD cross section to the collinear result, effectively adjusting the TMD prediction to reproduce the correct normalization without altering the fit parameters.
