content_sections.I Introduction: In this paper, we present an extraction of Transverse Momentum Distributions (TMDs)citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4 using more than two thousand data points from several experiments for two different kinds of processes: Semi-Inclusive Deep Inelastic Scattering (SIDIS) and production of Drell–Yan (DY) lepton pairs, significantly improving our previous analysiscitebib.bib5. Building maps of the internal partonic structure of nucleons is a crucial step towards understanding the interactions between quarks and gluons and the phenomenon of confinement. A steady progress in the last decades has led to more and more refined versions of such maps. The TMDs extracted in this article encode information about the three-dimensional distributions of quarks in momentum space. The level of sophistication of a TMD extraction essentially depends on two ingredients: the amount of analyzed data from different processes, namely how “global” the experimental information from which TMDs are extracted, and the perturbative accuracy reached in the theoretical formalism. The extraction of TMDs is based on TMD factorization theorems, which provide a precise definition of the objects to be extracted, establishing their universality and evolution equations. In this case, the accuracy of the calculation is defined by the amount of large logarithms being resummed, that in turn defines the powers of the strong coupling αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas to be included in the perturbative quantitiescitebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9. Of particular relevance is also the combination of analyzed data from different processes. Similarly to the extraction of collinear Parton Distribution Functions (PDFs), we can talk about a global fit, i. e. , a fit that leverages the universality of the parton distributions and uses different processes to constrain them. However, in SIDIS two types of TMDs enter the cross section: the TMD PDFs, describing how partons are arranged in the nucleon, and TMD Fragmentation Functions (FFs), describing how a parton produces a final-state hadron. The knowledge of TMDs, in particular of TMD FFs, would be greatly improved by using data from electron-positron annihilations into two almost back-to-back hadronscitebib.bib10. Unfortunately, this data is presently not available. There are measurements for the inclusive production of single hadronscitebib.bib11 but, in this case, transverse momenta need to be defined with respect to the thrust axis: a careful description of the latter is non trivial, and a rigorous factorization theorem for this process has been discussed only recently (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14 and references therein). Therefore, for TMD extractions we currently talk about a global fit when data from SIDIS and DY processes are included. In the last ten years, several extractions of TMDs have been presentedcitebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib5, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib7, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. Most of them suffered some shortcomings: they were either obtained in a parton-model framework without QCD corrections, or they took into account only a limited set of data, or did not perform a full fit. TMDs were also studied in a different framework, the so-called parton-branching approachcitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27. At present, only two works have reached the stage of combining SIDIS and DY data in a full-fledged global TMD fit: the above mentioned extraction of Ref. citebib.bib5, henceforth named PV17, and the extraction of Ref. citebib.bib22, henceforth named SV19. The PV17 extraction reached the NLL accuracy, was based on the calculation of observables at mean kinematics in each bin, and did not manage to describe the normalization of all datasets. In this work, we push the accuracy of the analysis to what we will refer to as NLL (only NNLO collinear FFs are currently missing in order to reach full NLL accuracy).2While this work was being completed, further efforts, including a work by the MAP collaboration, were made to include part of the NNLO correctionscitebib.bib28, bib.bib29 in the extraction of FFs. Apart from the increase in perturbative accuracy, in this work we also include many measurements published after 2017. The SV19 extraction reached the same perturbative accuracy and included essentially the same datasets. Our work has crucial differences in the selection of specific data points (we include a much larger number of points), the implementation of TMD evolution, the choice of nonperturbative components, and the handling of normalization for SIDIS data. As we will describe in detail, we found it particularly difficult to describe in a satisfactory way the normalization of SIDIS data obtained in fixed-target experiments at moderate to low scales. When the analysis is performed at NLL accuracy, we can describe well shape and normalization of SIDIS data, but the description of high-energy DY data is very poor. Going to NLL and NLL, the description of DY data significantly improves, but we fail to reproduce the normalization of SIDIS data, mainly due to the 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠cal O(alphas) corrections to the hard factor. As a possible solution, we choose to adjust the normalization of the TMD predictions by comparing their integral upon transverse momentum and the corresponding collinear formula. With this procedure, we fix the normalization a priori, in a way that is independent of the results of the fit. Our baseline fit is performed at NLL, using 2031 data points and obtaining χ2/Ndat=1.06superscript𝜒2subscript𝑁dat1.06chi2/Nrm dat=1.06. We also discuss variations of this baseline fit by changing the theoretical accuracy and the selected data. The paper is organized as follows. In Sec. we describe the theoretical framework used in the analysis. In Sec. we explain how experimental data have been selected. Sec. presents the results of our extraction. Finally, in Sec. we draw our conclusions.
content_sections.II Formalism II.1 Drell–Yan: In the Drell–Yan (DY) process hA​(PA)+hB​(PB)⟶γ∗/Z​(q)+X⟶ℓ+​(l)+ℓ−​(l′)+X,⟶subscriptℎ𝐴subscript𝑃𝐴subscriptℎ𝐵subscript𝑃𝐵superscript𝛾𝑍𝑞𝑋⟶superscriptℓ𝑙superscriptℓsuperscript𝑙′𝑋hA(PA)+hB(PB) longrightarrow gamma*/Z(q)+Xlongrightarrowell+(l)+ell-(lprime)+X;, two hadrons hAsubscriptℎ𝐴hA and hBsubscriptℎ𝐵hB with four-momenta PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA and PBsubscript𝑃𝐵PB, respectively, collide with center-of-mass energy squared s=(PA+PB)2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴subscript𝑃𝐵2s=(PA+PB)2, producing a neutral vector boson γ∗/Zsuperscript𝛾𝑍gamma*/Z with four-momentum q𝑞q and large invariant mass Q=q2𝑄superscript𝑞2Q=sqrtq2. The vector boson eventually decays into a lepton and an antilepton with four-momenta constrained by momentum conservation, q=l+l′𝑞𝑙superscript𝑙′q=l+lprime. The involved momenta and the respective transverse components are summarized in Fig. . The rapidity of the neutral boson (or, equivalently, of the lepton pair) is defined as y=12​ln⁡(q0+qzq0−qz). 𝑦12subscript𝑞0subscript𝑞𝑧subscript𝑞0subscript𝑞𝑧y=frac12lnleft(fracq0+qzq0-qzright),. content_sections.II Formalism II.2 Semi-Inclusive Deep-In: In SIDIS, a lepton with momentum l𝑙l scatters off a hadron target N𝑁N with mass M𝑀M and four momentum P𝑃P. In the final state, the scattered lepton momentum l′superscript𝑙′lprime is measured together with one hadron hℎh with mass Mhsubscript𝑀ℎMh and four momentum Phsubscript𝑃ℎPh. The other products of the scattering are undetected. Thus the reaction reads ℓ​(l)+N​(P)→ℓ​(l′)+h​(Ph)+X. →ℓ𝑙𝑁𝑃ℓsuperscript𝑙′ℎsubscript𝑃ℎ𝑋ell(l)+N(P)rightarrow ell(lprime)+h(Ph)+X,. The (space-like) four-momentum transfer is q=l−l′𝑞𝑙superscript𝑙′q=l-lprime, with Q2≡−q2>0superscript𝑄2superscript𝑞20Q2equiv-q2>0. We use the standard SIDIS variablescitebib.bib37, bib.bib38: x𝑥displaystyle x =Q22​P⋅q,absentsuperscript𝑄2⋅2𝑃𝑞displaystyle=fracQ22,Pcdot q,, y𝑦displaystyle y =P⋅qP⋅l,absent⋅𝑃𝑞⋅𝑃𝑙displaystyle=fracPcdot qPcdot l,, z𝑧displaystyle z =P⋅PhP⋅q,absent⋅𝑃subscript𝑃ℎ⋅𝑃𝑞displaystyle=fracPcdot PhPcdot q,, γ𝛾displaystylegamma =2​M​xQ. absent2𝑀𝑥𝑄displaystyle=frac2MxQ,. For transverse momenta, we will follow the definitions and notations discussed in Ref. citebib.bib39, bib.bib40 (see also Fig. ). In particular, we define 𝑷h​Tsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇PhT as the hadron transverse momentum in the Breit frame, where P𝑃P and q𝑞q form a light-cone basis; as a consequence, Phsubscript𝑃ℎPh has only transverse components and |𝑷h​T|2=−Ph​T2superscriptsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑃ℎ𝑇2|PhT|2=-PhT2, which is frame independent. Similarly, we define 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT as the photon transverse momentum in the hadron frame, where P𝑃P and Phsubscript𝑃ℎPh form a light-cone basis; as a consequence, qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT has only transverse components and |𝒒T|2=−qT2superscriptsubscript𝒒𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇2|qT|2=-qT2, which is also frame independent. The two momenta are related bycitebib.bib41, bib.bib42 qTμ=−Ph​Tμz−2​x​|𝒒T|2Q2​Pμ. superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝑃𝜇ℎ𝑇𝑧2𝑥superscriptsubscript𝒒𝑇2superscript𝑄2superscript𝑃𝜇qTmu=-fracPmuhTz-2xfrac|qT|2Q2Pmu,. In the following, we will always work assuming that the invariant mass of the photon is large compared to the target and hadron masses (M2,Mh2≪Q2much-less-thansuperscript𝑀2superscriptsubscript𝑀ℎ2superscript𝑄2M2,Mh2ll Q2) and to the transverse momenta 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT and 𝑷h​Tsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇PhT (𝒒T2,𝑷h​T2≪Q2much-less-thansuperscriptsubscript𝒒𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇2superscript𝑄2qT2,,PhT2ll Q2). We neglect any power corrections that vanish in this limit, both kinematic and dynamical (higher twist), apart from some modifications to the normalization of the SIDIS observables (that could be seen as the effect of power corrections, see Sec. for more details). In this limit, Eq. reduces to 𝒒T≈−𝑷h​Tz. subscript𝒒𝑇subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑧qTapprox-fracPhTz,. Several studies have been made concerning higher-twist corrections of various origin (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib38, bib.bib44 for recent works). A careful study of the impact of power corrections to the case of unpolarized TMDs has been discussed in Ref. citebib.bib22. Lately, important advances in the study of higher-twist TMD factorization have been publishedcitebib.bib42, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, but they do not directly affect the observables we consider here. The differential cross section for SIDIS can be witten in terms of two structure functions, FU​U,Tsubscript𝐹𝑈𝑈𝑇FUU,T and FU​U,Lsubscript𝐹𝑈𝑈𝐿FUU,Lcitebib.bib37. The subscripts refer to the lepton, the target, and the photon polarization, respectively. The second structure function is formally a twist four contribution and is suppressed in the limit considered here, thus we neglect it. The differential cross section at small transverse momentumcitebib.bib37, bib.bib5 reads d​σSIDISd​x​d​z​d​|𝒒T|​d​Q=8​π2​α2​z2​|𝒒T|x​Q3​y22​(1−ϵ)​(1+γ22​x)​FU​U,T​(x,z,|𝒒T|,Q),𝑑superscript𝜎SIDIS𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑧𝑑subscript𝒒𝑇𝑑𝑄8superscript𝜋2superscript𝛼2superscript𝑧2subscript𝒒𝑇𝑥superscript𝑄3superscript𝑦221italic-ϵ1superscript𝛾22𝑥subscript𝐹𝑈𝑈𝑇𝑥𝑧subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄fracdsigmatextSIDISdx,dz,d|qT|,dQ=frac8pi2,alpha2,z2,|qT|x,Q3,fracy22(1-epsilon),bigg(1+fracgamma22xbigg),FUU,Tbig(x,z,|qT|,Qbig),, where α𝛼alpha is the QED coupling constant and ϵitalic-ϵepsilon is the photon flux factorcitebib.bib37. The anomalous dimension γ𝛾gamma for the renormalization-group evolution in μ𝜇mu reads: γ​(μ,ζ)=γF​(αs​(μ))−γK​(αs​(μ))​ln⁡ζμ,𝛾𝜇𝜁subscript𝛾𝐹subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇subscript𝛾𝐾subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝜁𝜇gammabig(mu,zetabig)=gammaFbig(alphas(mu)big)-gammaKbig(alphas(mu)big),ln,fracsqrtzetamu,, where γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK is the cusp anomalous dimension and γF​(αs​(μ))=γ​(μ,μ2)subscript𝛾𝐹subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝛾𝜇superscript𝜇2gammaFbig(alphas(mu)big)=gammabig(mu,mu2big) is the boundary conditioncitebib.bib7. Since μfsubscript𝜇𝑓muf is of the order of the boson virtuality Q𝑄Q, this introduces subleading power corrections scaling like (|𝒒T|/Q)nsuperscriptsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄𝑛(|qT|/Q)n, with n>0𝑛0n>0. Such a procedure has the advantage of facilitating a possible matching of the TMD formula, valid for |𝒒T|≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|ll Q, onto the fixed-order calculation valid for |𝒒T|≃Qsimilar-to-or-equalssubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|simeq Qcitebib.bib8, bib.bib56, bib.bib57. To be more precise, given a set of quark thresholds m1,m2,m3,…subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚3…m1,m2,m3,dots, the nfsubscript𝑛𝑓nf associated to each of the three scales above is computed by requiring that the scale lies between mnfsubscript𝑚subscript𝑛𝑓mnf and mnf+1subscript𝑚subscript𝑛𝑓1mnf+1. In total, the default configuration for the fit involves 21 free parameters: one associated to the nonperturbative part of the Collins–Soper kernel (Eq. ), 11 related to the nonperturbative part of the TMD PDF (Eqs. ,), and 9 for the nonperturbative part of the TMD FF (Eqs. ,). The functional forms in Eqs. - are largely arbitrary. However, an important feature is that they are the Fourier transforms of the sum of a Gaussian, a weighted Gaussian (multiplied by 𝒌⟂2superscriptsubscript𝒌perpendicular-to2kperp2) and, in the case of the TMD PDFs, a third Gaussian. They are therefore positive definite for all values of 𝒌⟂2superscriptsubscript𝒌perpendicular-to2kperp2.8Note, however, that the evolved TMD PDF, Eq. , can become negative at large values of transverse momentum. The same holds true for TMD FFs. The parameters λ𝜆lambda and λ2subscript𝜆2lambda2 in Eq. are squared in order to avoid negative contributions (there is no need to square the parameter λFsubscript𝜆𝐹lambdaF in Eq. because the fit always selects positive values for this parameter). The widths of the Gaussians, expressed by Eqs. ,, are x𝑥x (or z𝑧z) dependent and vanish as x𝑥x (or z𝑧z) approaches 1. Our choice of the functional form is also inspired by model calculations of TMD PDFs (see, e. g. , citebib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65) and TMD FFs (see, e. g, citebib.bib66, bib.bib67). Many of these models predict the existence of terms that behave similarly to Gaussians and weighted Gaussians. The details of the functional dependence predicted by the models are related to the correlation between the spin of the quarks and their transverse momentum. In the case of fragmentaton functions, a different role can be played by different producton channels (e. g. , direct production vs. production through the decay of hadronic resonances). Finally, for the logarithmic ordering we use the same convention adopted in Ref. citebib.bib7. In particular, the orders of truncation of the perturbative ingredients relevant to the present analysis are summarized in Tab. . At the time of this analysis, the full NLL accuracy could not be achieved because NNLO collinear FFs were not available. Very recently, two analyses of collinear FFs were presented in Refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib29 making an extraction of TMDs at full NLL possible. We leave this study to a future pubblication.
content_sections.II Formalism II.4 Normalization factors: In Ref. citebib.bib5 it was demonstrated that TMD factorization at NLL accuracy is able to successfully reproduce the normalization and shape of HERMESHERMESrm HERMES SIDIS multiplicities and the shape of the available COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities. More recently, the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS collaboration published a reanalysis of their datacitebib.bib68. The NLL TMD predictions correctly reproduce normalization and shape of the new datacitebib.bib69. However, when increasing the accuracy to NLL or higher, the TMD formula severely underestimates the measurementscitebib.bib70, bib.bib69 by nearly constant factors in each bin. Note that tensions between the TMD cross sections and the associated measurements exist also at large transverse momentum in SIDIScitebib.bib48, DYcitebib.bib71, and electron-positron annihilation into two hadronscitebib.bib72. In the present study, as will be shown in Sec. , we confirm that we obtain an excellent description of both normalization and shape of the SIDIS multiplicities at NLL and that the NLL results are much smaller. At NLL the results increase slightly, but they are still far from the NLL ones and, therefore, from data. At average kinematics of the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS measurements, we obtain the following ratios of multiplicities: MNLLMN2​LLsubscript𝑀NLLsubscript𝑀superscriptN2LLdisplaystylefracMrm NLLMrm N2LL ≳2,greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent2displaystylegtrsim 2, MNLLMN3​LLsubscript𝑀NLLsubscript𝑀superscriptN3LLdisplaystylefracMrm NLLMrm N3LL ≳1.5. greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent1.5displaystylegtrsim 1.5. The reason for the difference between the logarithmic orders is almost entirely due to the hard factor in Eq. citebib.bib34. If we look at the explicit expression for the hard factor9There are different definitions for the hard factor in the literature, which are compensated by different definitions of the matching coefficients C𝐶C in Eq. . Here we follow the definition of Ref. citebib.bib34. with the standard choice μ=Q𝜇𝑄mu=Q ℋSIDIS​(Q,Q)=1+αs​(Q)4​π​CF​(−16+π23),superscriptℋSIDIS𝑄𝑄1subscript𝛼𝑠𝑄4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐹16superscript𝜋23cal Hrm SIDIS(Q,Q)=1+fracalphas(Q)4piCFbigg(-16+fracpi23bigg), we can immediately see that just by introducing 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠cal O(alphas) corrections, at Q=2𝑄2Q=2 GeV with αs≈0.3subscript𝛼𝑠0.3alphasapprox 0.3, we reduce the structure function to about 60% of its original value. This change is not compensated by a similar reduction in the denominator of Eq. : the differences between the LO and NLO expressions of the inclusive DIS cross section are typically below 5% and the NLO results are actually larger than the LO ones. If the NLL expression is much larger than the NLL and NLL ones, we may suspect that it should overshoot the data by a factor 1.5 at least. However, several works before the present one have shown a good agreement with data using a parton-model approachcitebib.bib15, bib.bib16 or at NLLcitebib.bib17, bib.bib18. Moreover, the integral of the structure function over 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT is equal to the value of its Fourier transform at 𝒃T=0subscript𝒃𝑇0bT=0. Therefore, the integral of our NLL expression is in good agreement with data, which also indicates the absence of a large normalization error. If the NLL predictions describe the data well and are a factor 2 or 1.5 above the NLL and NLL predictions, we propose to modify the normalization of the latter to recover a good agreement with data. An extended discussion of this issue can be found in Ref. citebib.bib69. We observe that the integral of the TMD formula, valid at low 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT, should reproduce only part of the full collinear cross section. The only exception is the order 𝒪​(αs0)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠0cal O(alphas0) case, as we have seen above, since at that order there is no contribution from gluon radiation at high transverse momentum, beyond the TMD region. However, at NLL or higher, in the kinematics of fixed-target SIDIS experiments, the integral of the TMD region (i. e. , the integral of the so-called W𝑊W term in the language of Ref. citebib.bib6) is much smaller than the corresponding collinear cross section. The missing contribution to the integral should be recovered by the terms in the fixed-order calculation that are not included in the TMD resummed expression (the so-called Y𝑌Y term). Ideally, the Y𝑌Y term should be negligible in the low-𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT region. This is not the case in the experimental regions under consideration: the Y𝑌Y term is finite but relatively large, even at 𝒒T=0subscript𝒒𝑇0qT=0. We stress that these normalization factors depend only on the collinear PDFs and FFs, are independent of the parametrization of the nonperturbative part of the TMDs, and can be computed before performing a fit of the latter. At NLL, ω​(x,z,Q)=1𝜔𝑥𝑧𝑄1omega(x,z,Q)=1. Beyond NLL, the prefactor becomes larger than one and guarantees that the integral of the TMD part of the cross section reproduces most of the collinear cross section, as suggested by the data. On the contrary, without the enhancement due to the normalization factor, the integral of the TMD part of the cross section would be too small, requiring a large role of the high-transverse-momentum tail, which is not observed in the data. The impact of the normalization factor defined in Eq. will be addressed in detail in Sec. . As a consequence of our procedure, the theoretical expression for the SIDIS cross section in Eq. becomes d​σωSIDISd​x​d​z​d​|𝒒T|​d​Q=ω​(x,z,Q)​d​σSIDISd​x​d​z​d​|𝒒T|​d​Q. 𝑑superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜔SIDIS𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑧𝑑subscript𝒒𝑇𝑑𝑄𝜔𝑥𝑧𝑄𝑑superscript𝜎SIDIS𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑧𝑑subscript𝒒𝑇𝑑𝑄fracdsigmaomegatextSIDISdx,dz,d|qT|,dQ=omega(x,z,Q),fracdsigmatextSIDISdx,dz,d|qT|,dQ,.
content_sections.III Data selection: In this Section we describe the experimental data included in our global analysis. We consider a large number of datasets related to DY lepton pair production and SIDIS, for the observables discussed in Sec. and Sec. . The coverage in the x𝑥x-Q2superscript𝑄2Q2 plane spanned by these datasets is illustrated in Fig. . The majority of datasets analyzed in the present work was already included in the global analysis of SIDIS and DY data in Ref. citebib.bib5 and in the fit of DY data discussed in Ref. citebib.bib7. For more details, we refer the reader to those references. The new datasets included in the present analysis are: • DY di-muon production from the collision of a proton beam with an energy of 800 GeV on a H2superscript𝐻22H fixed target from E772 (s=38.8𝑠38.8sqrts=38.8 GeV)citebib.bib76; • DY di-muon production from the PHENIX Collaborationcitebib.bib77; • DY data at 13 TeV from the CMS Collaborationcitebib.bib78 and the ATLAS Collaborationcitebib.bib79.
content_sections.III Data selection III.1 Drell-Yan: Our analysis is based on TMD factorization, which is applicable only in the region |𝒒T|≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|ll Q. Therefore, in agreement with the choices of Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib22 we impose the following cut |𝒒T|<0.2​Q. subscript𝒒𝑇0.2𝑄|qT|<0.2,Q,. Table summarizes all the DY datasets included in our analysis. For some DY datasets the experimental observable is given within a fiducial region. This means that kinematic cuts on transverse momentum pT​ℓsubscript𝑝𝑇ℓpTell and pseudo–rapidity ηℓsubscript𝜂ℓetaell of the single final-state leptons are enforced (values reported in the next–to–last column of Tab. ). For more details we refer the reader to Ref. citebib.bib7. The second column of Tab. reports, for each experiment, the number of data points (Ndatsubscript𝑁datNtextdat) that survive the kinematic cuts. The total number of DY data points considered in this work is 484. Note that for E605 and E288 at 400 GeV we have excluded the bin in Q𝑄Q containing the ΥΥUpsilon resonance (Q≃9.5similar-to-or-equals𝑄9.5Qsimeq 9.5GeV). As can be seen in Tab. , the cross sections are released in different forms: some of them are normalized to the total (fiducial) cross section while others are not. When necessary, the required total cross section σ𝜎sigma is computed using the code 𝙳𝚈𝙽𝙽𝙻𝙾𝙳𝚈𝙽𝙽𝙻𝙾tt DYNNLOcitebib.bib35, bib.bib36 with the MMHT14 collinear PDF set, consistently with the perturbative order of the differential cross section (see also Tab. ). More precisely, the total cross section is computed at NLO for NNLL accuracy, and NNLO for NLL accuracy. The values of the total cross sections at different orders can be found in Table 3 of Ref. citebib.bib7. For the ATLAS dataset at 13 TeV, the value of the fiducial cross section is 694.3 pb at NLO and 707.3 pb at NNLO.
content_sections.III Data selection III.2 SIDIS: The identification of the TMD region in SIDIS is not a trivial task and may be subject to revision as new data appears and the theoretical description is improved, as discussed in dedicated studiescitebib.bib94, bib.bib38, bib.bib95. First of all, a cut in the virtuality Q𝑄Q of the exchanged photon is necessary to respect the condition Q≫ΛQCDmuch-greater-than𝑄subscriptΛQCDQggLambdatextQCD needed for perturbation theory to be applicable. In this way also mass corrections and higher twist corrections can be neglected. In this work, we require that Q>1.4𝑄1.4Q>1.4GeV. Studies of SIDIS in collinear kinematics employ similar cutscitebib.bib96, bib.bib29. In order to restrict ourselves to the SIDIS current fragmentation region and interpret the observables in terms of parton distribution and fragmentation functions, we apply a cut in the kinematic variable z𝑧z by requiring 0.2<z<0.70.2𝑧0.70.2<z<0.7. The lower limit is the same used in the study of collinear fragmentation functionscitebib.bib96, bib.bib29. We used a slightly more restrictive upper limit, to avoid contributions from exclusive channels and to focus on a region where the collinear fragmentation functions have small relative uncertainties. For what concerns the cut on transverse momentum, our baseline choice is |𝑷h​T|<min​[min​[c1​Q,c2​z​Q]+c3​GeV,z​Q],subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇minminsubscript𝑐1𝑄subscript𝑐2𝑧𝑄subscript𝑐3GeV𝑧𝑄|PhT|<textminbig[,textmin[c1,Q,c2,zQ]+c3,textGeV,,zQbig],, with fixed parameters c1=0.2subscript𝑐10.2c1=0.2, c2=0.5subscript𝑐20.5c2=0.5 and c3=0.3subscript𝑐30.3c3=0.3. This choice is more restrictive than a similar one made in Ref. citebib.bib5, but less restrictive than the one made in Ref. citebib.bib22. It allows for many data points with |𝑷h​T|≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑄|PhT|ll Q but also with 0.2​Q<|𝒒T|<Q0.2𝑄subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄0.2,Q<|qT|<Q. In Sec. , we will discuss variations of the baseline SIDIS cut in Eq. that give phenomenological support to our choice. As for the datasets included in the present analysis, the main difference with Ref. citebib.bib5 is that we include the new release of COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS datacitebib.bib68. In this dataset, the vector–boson contributions have been subtracted. For the HERMESHERMESrm HERMES dataset we consistently select the vector–meson–subtracted dataset (. vmsub set). Moreover, we select the zxpt-3D-binning for HERMESHERMESrm HERMES multiplicities, since it presents a finer binning in |𝑷h​T|subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇|PhT|. The breakdown of the entire SIDIS dataset included in the present analysis is reported in Tab. . The second column of Tab. shows the number of data points (Ndatsubscript𝑁datNtextdat) that respect the kinematic cuts for each dataset, with a total number of 1547 data points. In conclusion, the total number of DY and SIDIS data points surviving our kinematic cuts is 2031.
content_sections.III Data selection III.3 Error treatment: The considered experimental datasets are released with a set of systematic and statistical uncertainties. As already pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib7, a proper treatment of the experimental uncertainties is extremely important in order to obtain a reliable extraction of TMDs. Thus, we choose to treat systematic uncertainties as fully correlated only if, in the corresponding publication, it is explicitly specified that they are correlated. The statistical uncertainties, instead, are always considered as uncorrelated. At variance with Ref. citebib.bib7, in this analysis we do not make use of the iterative t0subscript𝑡0t0-prescriptioncitebib.bib97 for the treatment of correlated normalization uncertainties. This prescription is usually introduced to avoid the underestimation of the predictions caused by the so–called D’Agostini biascitebib.bib98. After performing the fit with and without the t0subscript𝑡0t0-prescription, we found that our analysis is not affected by the D’Agostini bias and therefore we saw no reason to introduce the t0subscript𝑡0t0-prescription in the computation of the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2. On top of experimental systematic uncertainties, there can be several sources of systematic theoretical errors. The first one comes from the choice of the underlying collinear parton distribution and fragmentation functions. In our case we choose the MMHT2014citebib.bib99 collinear PDFs and the DSS collinear FFs. Since the HERMESHERMESrm HERMES collaboration provides multiplicities for pions and kaons separately (see Tab. ), we use DSS14citebib.bib100 for π±superscript𝜋plus-or-minuspipm and DSS17citebib.bib96 for K±superscript𝐾plus-or-minusKpm. Given the nature of the PDF and FF set used in this analysis, their uncertainties are computed using the Hessian methodcitebib.bib101, bib.bib102, bib.bib100. We observed that PDF and FF uncertainties are significantly correlated across bins. In order to account for this correlation, we decomposed the corresponding uncertainties into a fully correlated part that amounts to 80% of the total, while we treated the remaining 60% as uncorrelated.11Notice that, using this decomposition, the sum in quadrature of correlated and uncorrelated parts reproduce the original uncertainties. Moreover, for the observables measured by COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS one needs a collinear set of FFs for unidentified charged hadrons. Since the dedicated DSS07 FF set for unidentified charged hadronscitebib.bib103 does not provide an estimate of the uncertainties, we computed the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities by using the sum of the DSS14 and DSS17 sets for pions and kaons.12We thus assumed that the yield due to other hadronic species, such as protons, ΛΛLambda, etc. , is negligible as compared to the sum of kaons and pions. As argued in Ref. citebib.bib104, the contribution to the total yield due to hadrons heavier that pions and kaons is indeed marginal. The associated uncertainty is calculated by propagating to the multiplicity the Hessian errors associated to each of the two hadronic component. As pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib22, the choice of specific sets for the collinear distributions may have a sizeable impact on the final result. In our analysis, we did not consider alternative collinear sets and postpone this study to a future publication. Likewise, we leave for future work the study of other sources of theoretical uncertainties such as higher-twist corrections, TMD flavor dependence and the choice of the perturbative scales.
content_sections.IV Results: In this section, we present the results obtained for the extraction of unpolarized quark TMDs from a global analysis including both DY and SIDIS data (see Sec. ). This work represents an important upgrade with respect to Ref. citebib.bib7. In fact, in the SIDIS process a single hadron is measured in the final state, allowing us to extract information about fragmentation functions. Therefore, the final result of this work is the simultaneous extraction of both TMD PDFs and FFs at NLL. At the moment, this is the most precise extraction of TMDs that has been achieved on more than two thousand data points. In Sec. we present the quality of the fit, in Sec. we discuss the extracted TMD distributions, and in Sec. we investigate the effect of variations on the baseline fit configuration.
content_sections.IV Results IV.1 Fit quality: In this section, we discuss the quality of the baseline fit performed at NLL imposing the cuts on |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q as discussed in Sec. . The error analysis is performed with the so-called bootstrap method, which consists in fitting an ensemble of 250 Monte Carlo replicas of the experimental data (see Ref. citebib.bib5 for more details). The most complete statistical information about the TMDs is given by the full ensemble of 250 replicas. For some purposes, however, it is useful to define a single, representative result instead of the full replica ensemble. In order to estimate the quality of our fit, the most appropriate indicator is the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 value of the best fit to the central (not fluctuated) experimental data (χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20). We refer to this fit as the “central replica. ” It is possible to analyze also the average of the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 over all replicas (⟨χ2⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝜒2chi2) as well as the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 of the mean replica (χm2superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑚2chim2), constructed as the average of all replicascitebib.bib7. These values should be very close to each other. Tab. reports the breakdown of the χ02superscriptsubscript𝜒02chi02 values normalized to the number of data points (Ndatsubscript𝑁datNtextdat) for DY and SIDIS datasets. As already discussed in Ref. citebib.bib7, in the presence of bin-by-bin correlated uncertainties the total χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 can be expressed as the sum of two contributions χ2=∑iN(expi−th¯iσi)2+χλ2=χD2+χλ2,superscript𝜒2superscriptsubscript𝑖𝑁superscriptsubscriptexp𝑖subscript¯th𝑖subscript𝜎𝑖2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝐷subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆displaystylechi2=sumiNleft(fractextexpi-overlinetextthisigmairight)2+chi2lambda=chi2D+chi2lambda;, where χD2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝐷chi2D is given by the standard formula for N𝑁N experimental data points expi and statistical and uncorrelated uncertainties σi2=σi,stat2+σi,uncor2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖stat2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖uncor2sigmai2=sigmai,rm stat2+sigmai,rm uncor2, but involving theoretical predictions shifted by the correlated uncertainties, th¯i=thi+∑α=1kλα​σi,corr(α),subscript¯th𝑖subscriptth𝑖superscriptsubscript𝛼1𝑘subscript𝜆𝛼superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖corr𝛼overlinetextthi=textthi+sumalpha=1klambdaalpha,sigmai,rm corr(alpha);, where σi,corr(α)superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖corr𝛼sigmai,rm corr(alpha) is the α𝛼alpha-th (100%) correlated uncertainty associated with the i𝑖i-th experimental data point and λαsubscript𝜆𝛼lambdaalpha is the so-called nuisance parameter. In Eq. , the χλ2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆chi2lambda is a penalty term due to the presence of correlated uncertainties and is completely determined by the nuisance parameters, χλ2=∑α=1kλα2. subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆superscriptsubscript𝛼1𝑘superscriptsubscript𝜆𝛼2chi2lambda=sumalpha=1klambdaalpha2;. The optimal value of the nuisance parameters is obtained by minimizing the total χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 in Eq. with respect to them. Because the shifted predictions in Eq. provide a better visual assessment of the fit quality, we will consistently display them for all observables used in the fit. From Tab. , the global χ02superscriptsubscript𝜒02chi02 value is 1.06, indicating that the description of the whole dataset is very good.13We also obtain ⟨χ2⟩=1.08±0.01delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝜒2plus-or-minus1.080.01chi2=1.08pm 0.01 and χm2=1.07superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑚21.07chim2=1.07. This means that the fit is able to simultaneously describe experimental data coming from two different processes over a wide kinematic range. As can be seen in Tabs. and , the low-energy dataset comes from fixed-target DY experiments and SIDIS observables, while the high-energy dataset comes from collider experiments at the LHC and Tevatron at energies higher by more than two orders of magnitude. It is important to notice that the correlated penalty term χλ2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆chi2lambda gives a significant contribution to the total χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 value. This means that the shifts induced by correlated uncertainties are often large. The χλ2/Ndat=0.29subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆subscript𝑁dat0.29chi2lambda/Ntextdat=0.29 obtained in this analysis is larger than the one in Ref. citebib.bib7, mainly because of the different treatment of the theoretical uncertainties related to collinear PDFs and FFs, which are considered here as 80% correlated.For HERMESHERMESrm HERMES and COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities, which represent about 75% of the total number of data points considered in this work, the description obtained by our global analysis is very good. From Tab. , the values of χ02/Ndatsubscriptsuperscript𝜒20subscript𝑁datchi20/Ntextdat are almost always smaller than 1. In the case of HERMESHERMESrm HERMES multiplicities, we note that the largest contribution to the χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 comes from the π+superscript𝜋pi+ channel, for both proton and deuteron targets. This result is consistent with the findings in both Refs. citebib.bib15, bib.bib5 andcitebib.bib22. Since kaon multiplicities are affected by larger statistical errors, and the collinear FFs for kaons display large uncertainties, the corresponding χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 value is lower. The comparison between theoretical results for the SIDIS multiplicities of Eq. and HERMESHERMESrm HERMES data for the production of charged pions and kaons off a deuteron target is shown in Fig. . Each column corresponds to a specific x𝑥x bin. Each row corresponds to a specific final-state channel. The results are displayed as functions of the transverse momentum |𝑷h​T|subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇|PhT| of the measured final-state hadron. Points with different markers and colors correspond to different representative z𝑧z bins, and are offset for a better visualization as indicated in the plot legend. The light blue rectangles are the theoretical results and correspond to the 68% Confidence-Level (CL) band (namely excluding the largest and smallest 16% of the replicas). We note that for K−superscript𝐾K- production the central column, corresponding to the 0.2<x<0.350.2𝑥0.350.2<x<0.35 bin, does not include the magenta points for the highest 0.6<z<0.80.6𝑧0.80.6<z<0.8 bin because of the kinematic cut in z𝑧z. Similarly, in all panels there are only three green points (for the lowest z𝑧z bin) because of the z𝑧z-dependent cut in Eq. , which leads to the exclusion of a larger number of |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT| bins at lower values of z𝑧z. The theoretical results display larger uncertainties for K−superscript𝐾K- production (second row) because of the combined effect of larger experimental errors and larger uncertainties in the kaon collinear FFs. Fig. refers to the same HERMESHERMESrm HERMES multiplicities with same conventions and notation as in Fig. but off a proton target. We remark that for K−superscript𝐾K- production the kinematic cuts have a more drastic effect because the magenta points for the |𝑷h​T|subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇|PhT| distributions at the largest z𝑧z bin are excluded for the two largest x𝑥x bins considered (central and rightmost panels of second row from top). In Fig. , we show the result of our fit for the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS SIDIS multiplicities for the production of unidentified negatively charged hadrons off a deuteron target. For each Q𝑄Q and x𝑥x bin, each panel displays the multiplicity on a logarithmic scale as a function of 𝑷h​T2/Q2superscriptsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇2superscript𝑄2PhT2/Q2. Again, points with different markers and colors correspond to different representative z𝑧z bins, as indicated in the plot legend. As before, the light-blue rectangles correspond to the 68% CL theoretical results. The results for unidentified negatively charged hadrons h−superscriptℎh- are obtained by simply adding the results for negatively charged pions and kaons, h−∼π−+K−similar-tosuperscriptℎsuperscript𝜋superscript𝐾h-simpi-+K-. We note that the agreement is good for almost all bins, which is reflected in small χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 values in Tab. . The situation worsens for the lowest Q𝑄Q bin (1.3<Q<1.731.3𝑄1.731.3<Q<1.73 GeV), particularly for x≳0.02greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑥0.02xgtrsim 0.02. We also remark that for some Q𝑄Q and x𝑥x bins the theoretical uncertainties for the largest z𝑧z bin compatible with our kinematic cuts (0.6<z<0.80.6𝑧0.80.6<z<0.8) are significantly larger than for other z𝑧z bins because of much larger uncertainties in the collinear FFs. Finally, looking at the table of panels from top to bottom, we realize that the lowest z𝑧z-bin distributions (red diamonds) are present only for the largest Q𝑄Q bins, and vice-versa, because of the kinematic cut in Eq. Fig. refers to the same COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities with same conventions and notation as in Fig. but for unidentified positively charged hadrons h+superscriptℎh+. Again, the light-blue rectangles correspond to the 68% CL theoretical results, and are obtained by adding the results for positively charged pions and kaons, h+∼π++K+similar-tosuperscriptℎsuperscript𝜋superscript𝐾h+simpi++K+. Comments similar to Fig. can be made about the agreement between data and theory.DY data represents approximately 25% of the full set of analyzed data. From Tab. it is evident that most of low-energy DY data from fixed-target experiments (E605, E288, E772), but also from PHENIX and STAR, can be fitted with low χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values, much lower than high-energy DY data from collider experiments like, e. g. , those at the LHC. As already pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib7, this most likely originates from the fact that low-energy DY data are affected by larger errors and collinear PDFs at these kinematics have larger uncertainties. From Tab. , we also note that the quality of our fit for the ATLAS datasets is poor. In particular, the description worsens for the first two low-rapidity bins of both ATLAS 7 TeV and ATLAS 8 TeV datasets, the worst case being at |y|<1𝑦1|y|<1 for ATLAS 7 TeV. Several effects might be responsible for this result. Since the experimental observable is a normalized cross section, systematic errors cancel in the ratio producing measurements with very small error bars. Fitting these data is very difficult, also because small theoretical effects can give significant contributions to the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2. Moreover, different implementations of phase-space cuts on the final-state leptons could lead to modifications in both the shape and the normalization of the theoretical observable (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib32, bib.bib105, bib.bib106). We leave this issue for future studies. At variance with Ref. citebib.bib22, we obtain our results without excluding any extra data points on top of the ones exceeding the maximum value of |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q in Eq. . It is interesting to comment the results of the fit for those datasets that were not included in the previous analysis of Ref. citebib.bib7 (see Sec. ). For E772, we are able to obtain a good description only for data points above the peak of the ΥΥUpsilon resonance. For Q<9𝑄9Q<9 GeV, the quality of the fit worsens. At variance with Ref. citebib.bib22, we keep the Q<9𝑄9Q<9 GeV bins because there is no evident motivation to exclude them. The new CMS dataset at s=13𝑠13sqrts=13 TeV is important because it extends the kinematic coverage considered in Ref. citebib.bib7. This dataset is very nicely described, even better than the ones at the smaller center-of-mass energies s=7,8𝑠78sqrts=7,8 TeV. This is probably due to the fact that the CMS 13 TeV dataset is densely binned in rapidity. In order to visualize the quality of our fit, in Figs. - we present the comparison between experimental data and theoretical results for a representative selection of the DY dataset. In the upper panels of each plot, we display the cross section differential in |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT|, while in the lower panels we show the ratio of data to theory. As for the SIDIS case, the light-blue bands are the 68% CL theoretical results. Fig. displays the DY cross section for the E288 dataset at beam energy Eb​e​a​m=200subscript𝐸𝑏𝑒𝑎𝑚200Ebeam=200 GeV for different bins in Q𝑄Q. For DY fixed-target observables, we calculate the cross section at mean values of |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT| (not integrating upon the bin limits). Hence, we display the 68% CL uncertainty as a band rather than a series of rectangles. We remark that the uncertainty band is larger for lower Q𝑄Q bins; this trend is induced by larger correlated uncertainties for smaller invariant masses of the lepton pair. In Fig. , we compare the theoretical results for the cross section for DY in p​p¯𝑝¯𝑝pbarp collisions at the Tevatron. Black data points in the left panel correspond to the results of Run I of the CDF experiment, while in the right panel the results for Run II are reported. The lower panels show the corresponding ratio of experimental data to theoretical results. The latter are displayed as light-blue rectangles, each one corresponding to the integral of the cross section within the corresponding bin limits. The size of the rectangle is given by the 68% CL. The quality of the fit for CDF data is comparable to the one in Ref. citebib.bib7. In Fig. , we compare the theoretical results for the DY cross section in p​p𝑝𝑝pp collisions at the LHC. From left to right, the black data points refer to the measurements by the CMS Collaboration at increasing s=𝑠absentsqrts= 7, 8, 13 TeV. For s=𝑠absentsqrts= 7, 8 TeV, the results are normalized to the fiducial cross section. As in previous figures, the lower panels display the ratio of experimental data to the 68% CL theoretical results. For s=𝑠absentsqrts= 7, 8 TeV, the quality of the fit is comparable to that in Ref. citebib.bib7. For the new dataset at s=13𝑠13sqrts=13 TeV, the agreement in the displayed rapidity bin is excellent, but remains very good also for higher rapidities. In Fig. , we compare the theoretical results for the DY cross section in p​p𝑝𝑝pp collisions normalized to the fiducial cross section for the ATLAS data at s=7𝑠7sqrts=7 TeV. From left to right, we consider three representative bins at increasing rapidity |y|𝑦|y|. The leftmost one corresponds to the worst described bin in our global fit, with χ2/Ndata=13.5superscript𝜒2subscript𝑁data13.5chi2/Ntextdata=13.5. As in previous figures, the lower part of each panel displays the ratio of experimental data to the 68% CL theoretical results. The quality of the fit increases at more forward rapidities (from left to right). The same trend is observed at s=8𝑠8sqrts=8 TeV, but not for CMS at 13 TeV.
content_sections.IV Results IV.2 TMD distributions: We now discuss the TMD distributions extracted from our baseline fit with NLL accuracy. Tab. displays the list of our 21 fitting parameters with their mean value and standard deviation. The majority of the parameters is well constrained. The only parameter that is compatible with zero is γ2subscript𝛾2gamma2. The λ𝜆lambda parameter measures the relative weight of the first Gaussian and the weighted-Gaussian in the nonperturbative part of the TMD PDF in Eq. . The value of this parameter is close to 2, indicating that the contribution of the weighted-Gaussian component is important. In Eq. , the parameter λ2subscript𝜆2lambda2 measures the relative weight of the first Gaussian and the third Gaussian; this parameter is small but not compatible with zero, which means that also this component of the TMD PDF is important to reach a good description of experimental data. Our parametrization of the nonperturbative part of TMD FFs in Eq. contains just the combination of a Gaussian and a weighted Gaussian: this is sufficient to describe the data in an accurate way. The λFsubscript𝜆𝐹lambdaF parameter measures the relative weight of the two components; its value is close to 0.1, indicating that the contribution of the weighted Gaussian is small. Nevertheless, it has non-trivial consequences on the tail of the TMD FF, as we will show below. The g2subscript𝑔2g2 parameter is a key ingredient to the extraction of the Collins–Soper kernel, discussed in Sec. . The same parameter was used in the analysis of Ref. citebib.bib5. It is interesting to observe that the value obtained in the present global fit is smaller by almost a factor of 4 with respect to Ref. citebib.bib5. This may be due to the higher theoretical accuracy of the present analysis and to the role of the very precise high-energy Drell–Yan measurements, which also determine the very small standard deviation of g2subscript𝑔2g2. In Fig. , we show a graphical representation of the correlations among the 21 fitting parameters. Using the color code indicated in the legend, it is easy to realize that the nondiagonal elements are very small except for some (anti–)correlation among the β1subscript𝛽1beta1, δ1subscript𝛿1delta1 and γ1subscript𝛾1gamma1 parameters that control the z𝑧z–dependent width of the Gaussians in the TMD FF (see Eqs. ,). The overall absence of large correlations suggests that the model parametrization of the non perturbative parts of TMDs is appropriate. In Fig. , we show the unpolarized TMD PDF for the up quark in the proton at μ=ζ=Q=2𝜇𝜁𝑄2mu=sqrtzeta=Q=2 GeV (left panel) and 10 GeV (right panel) as a function of the quark transverse momentum |𝒌⟂|subscript𝒌perpendicular-to|kperp| for three different values of x𝑥x, namely x𝑥x = 0.001, 0.01, and 0.1. The bands correspond to the 68%percent% CL. The TMD seems to be wider at intermediate x=0.01𝑥0.01x=0.01, but has also a high tail at x=0.001𝑥0.001x=0.001. As already mentioned, a significant role is played by the weighted Gaussian and by the second Gaussian in Eq. . This may be a sign of the presence of contributions from different quark flavors and/or from different spin configurations (see Sec. ). It is worth noticing that in both left and right panels the TMD PDF at x=0.001𝑥0.001x=0.001 shows the largest error band, particularly at low |𝒌⟂|subscript𝒌perpendicular-to|kperp|. This is due to the lack of experimental points in that kinematic region (see Fig. ). Future data from the Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) are expected to play an important role in getting a better description of the TMD PDFs at low x𝑥xcitebib.bib107, bib.bib108. In Fig. , we show the TMD FF for the up quark fragmenting into a π+superscript𝜋pi+ at μ=ζ=Q=2𝜇𝜁𝑄2mu=sqrtzeta=Q=2 GeV (left panel) and 10 GeV (right panel) as a function of the pion transverse momentum |𝑷⟂|subscript𝑷perpendicular-to|Pperp| (with respect to the fragmenting quark axis) for two different values of z=𝑧absentz= 0.3 and 0.6. As in the previous figure, the uncertainty bands correspond to the 68%percent% CL. In both left and right panels, an additional structure clearly emerges at intermediate P⟂subscript𝑃perpendicular-toPperp, especially at z=0.3𝑧0.3z=0.3, which is induced by the weighted Gaussian in Eq. . Further investigations on this topic are needed, and data from electron-positron annihilations would be valuable to better explore these features. We stress that the error bands displayed in Figs. - reflect the uncertainty on the fitted parameters (see Eqs. -) that are determined by taking into account the uncertainty on the collinear PDFs and FFs as discussed in Sec. . However, since the fits are performed using the central set of the collinear distributions, all TMD replicas have the same integral in 𝒌⟂subscript𝒌perpendicular-tokperp (i. e. , their values at 𝒃T=0subscript𝒃𝑇0bT=0 are the same). As a consequence, the plots in Figs. - only partially account for the error of the collinear distributions.It is interesting to study the Collins–Soper kernelcitebib.bib109, bib.bib6 that drives the evolution of TMDs in terms of the rapidity scale ζ𝜁zeta. Recent discussions of this crucial component of the TMD formalism have been presented in Refs. citebib.bib110, bib.bib111 and estimates based on lattice QCD have been proposed in Refs. citebib.bib112, bib.bib113, bib.bib114. The Collins–Soper kernel, as written in Eq. , is composed of two parts. The first part can be calculated perturbatively at NLL accuracy, and is computed at b∗subscript𝑏∗bast: K​(b∗​(|𝐛T|),μ)=∑n=0k−1(αs​(μb∗)4​π)n+1​K(n,0)−∑n=0kγK(n)​∫μb∗μd​μ′μ′​(αs​(μ′)4​π)n+1,𝐾subscript𝑏subscript𝐛𝑇𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑛0𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝜇subscript𝑏4𝜋𝑛1superscript𝐾𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝑛0𝑘superscriptsubscript𝛾𝐾𝑛superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝜇subscript𝑏𝜇𝑑superscript𝜇′superscript𝜇′superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠superscript𝜇′4𝜋𝑛1K(b*(|bT|),mu)=sumn=0k-1left(fracalphas(mub*)4piright)n+1K(n,0)-sumn=0kgammaK(n)intmub*mufracdmuprimemuprimeleft(fracalphas(muprime)4piright)n+1,, where K(n,0)superscript𝐾𝑛0K(n,0) and γK(n)superscriptsubscript𝛾𝐾𝑛gammaK(n) are coefficients of the perturbative expansion (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib34). This implies that at low |𝒃T|subscript𝒃𝑇|bT| the Collins–Soper kernel saturates to a finite value, as indicated by the dashed lines. content_sections.IV Results IV.3 Variations on the fit co: In this subsection we discuss the results obtained by modifying some of the baseline settings. In Sects. and we present fits at NNLL and NLL accuracy, respectively, comparing them to the baseline NLL. Finally, in Sec. we study the impact of adopting different cuts in |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT| on the SIDIS dataset.The baseline fit presented in the previous section is performed at NLL (see Tab. ). As already emphasized in Ref. citebib.bib7, the inclusion of perturbative corrections up to NLL is crucial to achieve an optimal description of some of the most recent experimental measurements, such as those by the LHC. However, it might be useful to extract unpolarized TMDs at lower perturbative orders. One of the reasons is that such sets can be used in global analyses of polarized TMDs where it is not possible to reach the same level of accuracy. This is the case of the Sivers TMDs where the computation of the polarized cross section for the Sivers effect presently cannot go beyond the NNLL levelcitebib.bib117, bib.bib118, bib.bib119, hence demanding unpolarised TMDs at the same level of accuracy. To this aim, we perform a new global fit at NNLL. However, when lowering the perturbative accuracy, it is possible to obtain acceptably good fits only by excluding those datasets whose precision requires the highest theoretical accuracy. Specifically, we found that only by removing the ATLAS dataset we were able to achieve an acceptable global description at NNLL accuracy. As a matter of fact, in Tab. the value of χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 in this configuration, namely for fixed-target DY and SIDIS, is lower than at NLL where ATLAS data is included. Because of the difference in the perturbative accuracy as well as in the dataset, we do not expect to get compatible values for the best fit parameters between the NNLL and NLL fits. For instance, we obtain λ=12±10𝜆plus-or-minus1210lambda=12pm 10 GeV and λF=340±280subscript𝜆𝐹plus-or-minus340280lambdaF=340pm 280 GeV at NNLL, to be compared to λ=1.8±0.3𝜆plus-or-minus1.80.3lambda=1.8pm 0.3 GeV and λF=0.08±0.01subscript𝜆𝐹plus-or-minus0.080.01lambdaF=0.08pm 0.01 GeV at NLL. The λ𝜆lambda and λFsubscript𝜆𝐹lambdaF parameters control the relative weight of the weighted Gaussian in the non perturbative part of the TMD PDF and FF, respectively, and control the size of the DY and SIDIS spectrum at middle to large values of |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT|. The large values obtained in the NNLL imply that the weighted Gaussian dominates for both TMD PDF and FF parametrizations. This behavior may be partially induced by the lack of perturbative corrections of the NNLL fit with respect to the NLL one, which are compensated by nonperturbative effects.We performed also a global analysis at NLL accuracy. Similarly to the NNLL fit discussed above, it might be useful to have also a global fit at NLL accuracy for contexts where it is not possible to reach the highest accuracy. However, in order to obtain acceptable χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values at this order, we had to exclude all collider DY data and the E772 fixed-target DY dataset. Thus, we reduced the dataset to the SIDIS data and the remaining fixed-target DY data, i. e. , E605 and E288. We point out that in our approach the integral of the W𝑊W-term at NLL is equal by construction to the SIDIS 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT-integrated collinear cross section. As a consequence, the value of the prefactor ω𝜔omega in Eq. is automatically equal to 1. Moreover, for this fit we consistently used MMHT2014 at LO for the collinear PDFs and we used DSS at NLO for collinear FFs. As shown in Tab. , at NLL both collinear PDFs and FFs should be evaluated at LO, but we choose FFs at NLO because no recent extractions at LO are currently available. As can be seen in Tab. , we obtain low χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values for all included datasets. It is interesting to compare this result with that of the PV17 analysiscitebib.bib5. In that work, the normalization of COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS data was fixed by the first bin in 𝑷h​T2superscriptsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇2PhT2. Here we demonstrate that we can obtain an excellent description of the most recent COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS data without any adjustment of the normalization.A crucial ingredient of any phenomenological analysis of TMDs is the introduction of the kinematic cut |𝒒T|/Q≪1much-less-thansubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄1|qT|/Qll 1 on the dataset to ensure that TMD factorization is valid. Our default choices are discussed in Sec. . It is interesting to study how the global quality of our fit changes upon variations of this cut in order to get quantitative information on the range of validity of TMD factorization. To this purpose, we changed the parameters c1subscript𝑐1c1, c2subscript𝑐2c2, and c3subscript𝑐3c3 in Eq. devised for the SIDIS data, while keeping the constraint |𝒒T|/Q<0.2subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄0.2|qT|/Q<0.2 for the DY data. We refer the reader to Ref. citebib.bib7 for an analogous study of the effect of the |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q cut on the DY data. We consider five different configurations for the cut on SIDIS data: (a) A first and most conservative cut is performed by fixing the z-independent upper value |𝒒T|/Q<0.4subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄0.4|qT|/Q<0.4 which can be obtained by setting c1=c2=0.4subscript𝑐1subscript𝑐20.4c1=c2=0.4 and c3=0subscript𝑐30c3=0 in Eq. ; (b) A second cut by setting c1=0.15subscript𝑐10.15c1=0.15, c2=0.4subscript𝑐20.4c2=0.4 and c3=0.2subscript𝑐30.2c3=0.2 in Eq. ; (c) The cut of our baseline fit with c1=0.2subscript𝑐10.2c1=0.2, c2=0.5subscript𝑐20.5c2=0.5 and c3=0.3subscript𝑐30.3c3=0.3; (d) A fourth cut with c1=0.2subscript𝑐10.2c1=0.2, c2=0.6subscript𝑐20.6c2=0.6 and c3=0.4subscript𝑐30.4c3=0.4 but without imposing |𝒒T|<Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|<Q (i. e. removing the outermost “min” in Eq. ); (e) A fifth cut inspired by the PV17 analysiscitebib.bib5, namely the same as in the previous case but with c1=0.2subscript𝑐10.2c1=0.2, c2=0.7subscript𝑐20.7c2=0.7 and c3=0.5subscript𝑐30.5c3=0.5; this is the least conservative choice. In Fig. , we show how the global χ2/Ndatsuperscript𝜒2subscript𝑁datchi2/Ntextdat changes when considering the five configurations described above. By observing that more conservative choices do not necessarily correspond to better χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values, we conclude that the TMD formalism is able to describe SIDIS data that fails to fulfil the formal requirement |𝒒T|/Q≪1much-less-thansubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄1|qT|/Qll 1. In this respect, we notice that the global χ2/Ndatsuperscript𝜒2subscript𝑁datchi2/Ntextdat of cut (d) is smaller than the baseline fit, despite including a larger amount of data, some of which at |𝒒T|≳Qgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|gtrsim Q, i. e. , well outside the region where TMD factorization is valid. In Fig. , we better illustrate the situation by showing the comparison between COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS data and theoretical predictions from our baseline fit for the SIDIS multiplicity for positively charged hadrons as a function of |𝑷h​T|/Qsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑄|PhT|/Q in the bin 1.3<Q<1.731.3𝑄1.731.3<Q<1.73 GeV, 0.02<x<0.0320.02𝑥0.0320.02<x<0.032, 0.3<z<0.40.3𝑧0.40.3<z<0.4. The upper panel of the plot displays the multiplicity while the lower panel shows the ratio of experimental data to the theoretical predictions. The solid circles indicate data points included in the fit while empty squares refer to those that do not survive the cut. Remarkably, the agreement remains very good up to |𝑷h​T|/Q≃0.5similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑄0.5|PhT|/Qsimeq 0.5, well beyond the largest |𝑷h​T|subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇|PhT| allowed by the cut. We also stress that this behavior is not specific of the considered bin but is a general feature also of other bins, as well as of the HERMESHERMESrm HERMES dataset. In conclusion, from our analysis it emerges that the validity of the TMD formalism in the kinematic region covered by COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS and HERMESHERMESrm HERMES seems to extend well beyond the customary cut |𝒒T|/Q≪1much-less-thansubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄1|qT|/Qll 1. This evidence justifies in a quantitative way our choice for the cut |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q in Eq. for the baseline fit, and explains why we obtain values of χ2/Ndatsuperscript𝜒2subscript𝑁datchi2/Ntextdat close to one also with less conservative cuts. Moreover, it suggests that the applicability of TMD factorization in SIDIS might be defined in terms of |𝑷h​T|subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇|PhT| rather than |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT|, calling for more extensive studies in this direction.
content_sections.V Conclusions and outlook: In this article, we presented an extraction of unpolarized Transverse-Momentum Dependent Parton Distribution Functions and Fragmentation Functions (TMD PDFs and TMD FFs, respectively), which we refer to as MAPTMD22. We analyzed 2031 data points collected by several experiments: 251 data points from Drell–Yan (DY) production measured at Tevatron, LHC and RHIC, 233 points from fixed-target DY (see Tab. ) and 1547 data points from Semi-Inclusive Deep Inelastic Scattering (SIDIS) measured by the HERMESand COMPASScollaborations (see Tab. ). Our description of the experimental observables is based on TMD factorization at a perturbative accuracy defined as NLL, in the sense that we use TMD evolution at the NLL level, hard factor and matching coefficients at order αs2subscriptsuperscript𝛼2𝑠alpha2s, collinear PDFs at NNLO, and collinear FFs at NLO (see Tab. ). We constructed the full TMDs by combining the perturbative components, regularized by means of the b∗subscript𝑏∗bast prescription defined in Eq. , and nonperturbative parts described as a sum of Gaussians and weighted Gaussians (see Eqs. and ). We used 21 free parameters: 11 related to the TMD PDF, 9 for the TMD FF, and one for the Collins–Soper kernel driving the TMD evolution. We assumed these parameters to be the same for all quark flavors. The TMD formalism is applicable only if observed transverse momenta are much smaller than the hard scale Q𝑄Q of the considered process. The details of our data selection are explained in Sec. . In the DY case, for the final lepton pair transverse momentum 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT we adopted a simple criterion, |𝒒T|<0.2​Qsubscript𝒒𝑇0.2𝑄|qT|<0.2,Q, which is common to other works in the literature. In the SIDIS case, our selection criterion is more restrictive than the one made in Ref. citebib.bib5, but less restrictive than the one made in Ref. citebib.bib22. For SIDIS data, where the detected hadron momentum is 𝑷h​T≈−z​𝒒Tsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑧subscript𝒒𝑇PhTapprox-zqT, our cut includes many data points with |𝑷h​T|≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑄|PhT|ll Q but also with 0.2​Q<|𝒒T|<Q0.2𝑄subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄0.2,Q<|qT|<Q. We also tested the quality of our fit by exploring other criteria for the |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q cut. Surprisingly, we find that fits of quality comparable to the baseline result can be obtained with less conservative cuts such that |𝒒T|≳Qgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|gtrsim Q for several data points. We believe that the definition of the range of applicability of the TMD factorization formula deserves further studies. We found it difficult to reproduce the normalization of SIDIS data measured in fixed-target experiments at moderate to low scales. The formalism works well at NLL order, but severely underestimates the data at the NLL order and above. The reason can be traced back to the fact that the integral upon transverse momentum of the TMD cross section coincides with the known collinear result only at NLL, while it misses other contributions at higher orders. A rigorous solution of the problem would imply the calculation of all these contributions (including the so-called Y𝑌Y-term in the language of Ref. citebib.bib6), which are currently not under control, and are beyond the scope of this work. We decided to modify our predictions by including pre-computed normalization factors that are independent of the fit, and that are identified by comparing the integral upon transverse momenta of the TMD formula to the corresponding collinear calculation, as explained in Sec. . The fit is performed with the bootstrap method, leading to an ensemble of 250 replicas. We reach a very good overall agreement with data, with χ2/Ndat=1.06superscript𝜒2subscript𝑁dat1.06chi2/Nrm dat=1.06 for the central replica (defined in Sec. ). The description of all individual datasets is satisfactory, except for a few cases, in particular for the ATLAS data (see Tab. and Fig. ). The discrepancy with the ATLAS data may be due to corrections not fully included in our analysis (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib32) which, in spite of being small, can have a large impact in the comparison with very high precision data. The resulting TMDs are shown in Figs. and. It is interesting to note that they deviate from a simple Gaussian shape (especially for the FFs) and the TMD shape changes in a nontrivial way as a function of x𝑥x or z𝑧z. Tables with grids of the obtained TMDs will be made publicly available at the NangaParbat website14 and the TMDlib website15citebib.bib120. The availability of TMD analyses at increasing precision will be essential in guiding detector design and feasibility studies for the future Electron Ion Collider, and will also have a strong impact on precision measurements of observables particularly sensitive to the hadron structure, such as W𝑊W mass measurements at hadron colliders (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib121, bib.bib122, bib.bib123). Our work can be extended by improving the perturbative accuracy of the analysis (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib124, bib.bib32), including theoretical uncertainties in terms of suitable scale variations (along the lines of Ref. citebib.bib125), improving the treatment of correlated uncertainties, and investigating possible flavor dependence of TMDscitebib.bib15, bib.bib24.

sorted_papers.title:
Nonperturbative Collins-Soper Kernel from Chiral Quarks with Physical
  Masses
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice QCD calculation of the rapidity anomalous dimension of
quark transverse-momentum-dependent distributions, i.e., the Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, up to transverse separations of about 1 fm. This unitary lattice
calculation is conducted, for the first time, employing the
chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermion discretization and physical
values of light and strange quark masses. The CS kernel is extracted from the
ratios of pion quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions
(quasi-TMDWFs) at next-to-leading logarithmic perturbative accuracy. Also for
the first time, we utilize the recently proposed Coulomb-gauge-fixed
quasi-TMDWF correlator without a Wilson line. We observe significantly slower
signal decay with increasing quark separations compared to the established
gauge-invariant method with a staple-shaped Wilson line. This enables us to
determine the CS kernel at large nonperturbative transverse separations and
find its near-linear dependence on the latter. Our result is consistent with
the recent lattice calculation using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs, and agrees
with various recent phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data.

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "The Collins-Soper (CS) kernel governs the rapidity scale evolution of transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs), enabling consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales.",
    "TMDs provide a three-dimensional picture of parton motion within hadrons, including transverse momentum and spin correlations, crucial for understanding nucleon structure and precision measurements in high-energy physics.",
    "Direct lattice QCD computation of TMDs is not feasible due to their light-cone definition, but they can be accessed via quasi-TMDs within the Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) framework.",
    "The conventional approach uses gauge-invariant (GI) quasi-TMDs with staple-shaped Wilson lines, which suffer from exponential signal decay and renormalization complications such as linear divergences and pinch-pole singularities.",
    "The Coulomb-gauge-fixed (CG) approach eliminates Wilson lines, leading to multiplicatively renormalizable correlators free from linear divergences, pinch singularities, and Wilson-line-induced operator mixings.",
    "The CG quasi-TMDWFs exhibit significantly slower signal decay with increasing quark separation compared to the GI method, enabling reliable extraction of nonperturbative physics at large transverse separations (up to ~1 fm).",
    "The CS kernel extracted from CG quasi-TMDWFs shows a near-linear dependence on transverse separation, consistent with phenomenological models and previous GI lattice results.",
    "The CG approach is in the same universality class as the GI case under LaMET, with factorization established at one-loop order in SCET, validating its use for nonperturbative TMD studies."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The CS kernel is extracted from ratios of pion quasi-TMD wave functions at next-to-leading logarithmic (NLL) perturbative accuracy.",
    "The calculation is performed using physical light and strange quark masses and chiral-symmetry-preserving domain wall fermions.",
    "The transverse separation range extends up to approximately 1 fm, which is significantly larger than previous lattice studies due to improved signal behavior in the CG approach.",
    "The CS kernel exhibits near-linear dependence on the transverse separation $ b_{\perp} $, consistent with the expected behavior in the nonperturbative regime.",
    "The result is consistent with recent lattice calculations using gauge-invariant quasi-TMDWFs and agrees with various phenomenological parametrizations of experimental data."
  ]
}
