authors: Dennis Bollweg, Xiang Gao, Swagato Mukherjee, and Yong Zhao

content_sections.I Introduction: The parton transverse-momentum-dependent distributions (TMDs) are crucial for a three-dimensional understanding of parton motions within a hadron, offering a more comprehensive view than traditional one-dimensional parton distribution functions (PDFs). It sheds light not only on the intrinsic motion of partons in transverse directions but also on the interplay between the transverse momentum of quarks and the spin of nucleons or quarks themselves. This comprehensive perspective is crucial for a deep understanding of the dynamic and complex nature of nucleons. The accurate characterization of TMDs is also critical for interpreting experimental data from high-energy collisions, particularly in relation to the transverse momentum distributions of electroweak and Higgs bosonscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. They are fundamental to precision measurements, such as determining the mass and width of the W bosoncitebib.bib3, bib.bib4. As high-energy physics experiments continue to advance, the measurement of TMDs will become increasingly important. The ongoing and future experiments at facilities such as the Large Hadron Collidercitebib.bib5, bib.bib6 and Electron-Ion Collidercitebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10 are expected to profoundly enrich our knowledge of TMDs. This will not only enhance our grasp of hadron structure and nucleon spin but also contribute significantly to the broader field of particle physics. Central to the study and practical application of TMDs is the Collins-Soper (CS) kernel, which is responsible for the (rapidity) scale evolution of TMDscitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, enabling the consistent interpretation of experimental data across different energy scales. It is instrumental in connecting theoretical predictions with experimental observations. The TMDs and CS kernel can be extracted through the global analysis of experimental data including the semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) and Drell-Yan processescitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29. However, the nonperturbative nature of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) at low transverse momenta necessitates certain parametrizations and introduces model dependence. As a result, there is an increasing interest for these intrinsically nonperturbative quantities to be calculated directly from first-principles lattice QCD. Although direct simulation of TMDs on the Euclidean lattice is impractical, as TMDs are defined on the light-cone, it has been demonstrated that they can be accessed through quasi-TMDs within the framework of Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. The quasi-TMDs involve the matrix elements of equal-time gauge-invariant (GI) operators: OΓGI​(𝐛;η)=ψ¯​(𝐛2)​Γ​W⊐​(𝐛2,−𝐛2,η)​ψ​(−𝐛2),subscriptsuperscript𝑂GIΓ𝐛𝜂¯𝜓𝐛2Γsubscript𝑊square-original-of𝐛2𝐛2𝜂𝜓𝐛2displaystylebeginsplitOrm GIGamma(b;eta)=overlinepsi(fracb2)Gamma Wsqsupset(fracb2,-fracb2,eta)psi(-fracb2),endsplit with 𝐛=(b⟂,bz)𝐛subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧b=(bperp,bz) covering both longitudinal (bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz) and transverse (b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp) directions, linked by a staple-shaped Wilson line W⊐subscript𝑊square-original-ofWsqsupset whose length is characterized by η𝜂eta. In the large momentum and η→∞→𝜂etarightarrowinfty limit, the quasi-TMDs can be related to the light-cone TMDs through the perturbative factorizationcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib36, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. Building on this, significant advancements have been achieved over the past few years. The CS kernel has been extracted from either quasi-TMD parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs)citebib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, quasi-TMD wave functions (TMDWFs)citebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib49, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, or the moments of the quasi-TMDscitebib.bib56. Lattice QCD calculations of soft functionscitebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib53, along with the first results of nucleon TMD PDFscitebib.bib57 and pion TMDWFscitebib.bib58 also have been reported. Additionally, progress has also been made in the systematical control of these calculations, including improved matching up to two loopscitebib.bib59, bib.bib60, addressing the operator mixing and working at physical quark massescitebib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib54, bib.bib67, bib.bib55. Despite notable progress, the lattice calculation of TMDs remains challenging. To suppress power corrections, a large momentum Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz is required, which incurs a significant computational cost. In addition, the signal-to-noise ratio of the quasi-TMD matrix elements is adversely affected by exponential decay as the total length of the space-like Wilson line increases. This decay makes it particularly difficult to investigate quasi-TMDs at large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp, where the results are desired to complement the phenomenological analysis. What’s more, the linear divergence and pinch-pole singularity in the Wilson lines also complicate the renormalization procedure, although they could be cancelled by the Wilson loopcitebib.bib68 or held fixed by keeping a constant length of the Wilson line for given b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperpcitebib.bib54. Besides, the operator mixings of Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66 also need to be subtracted systematicallycitebib.bib54. Recently, a novel approach has been proposed for computing parton physics in the Coulomb gauge (CG)citebib.bib69, notably without the use of Wilson lines. Thereby, the complexity induced by the Wilson line can be avoided. It has been demonstrated that, in the large momentum limit, the CG quasi-PDF falls into the same universality class as the GI case under the LaMET framework. Further progress has been made in the realm of quasi-TMDscitebib.bib70, involving the matrix elements of equal-time operators, OΓCG​(𝐛)=ψ¯​(𝐛2)​Γ​ψ​(−𝐛2)|∇⋅A=0,subscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛evaluated-at¯𝜓𝐛2Γ𝜓𝐛2⋅∇A0displaystylebeginsplitOrm CGGamma(b)=overlinepsi(fracb2)Gammapsi(-fracb2)|nablacdotA=0,endsplit with the CG condition ∇⋅A=0⋅∇A0nablacdotA=0 but without a Wilson line. The factorization of quasi-TMDs in the CG has been derived from the soft collinear effective theory (SCET)citebib.bib70 and verified at one loop in perturbation theorycitebib.bib70, bib.bib71. In this study, we have, for the first time, computed the quasi-TMDWFs of the pion in the CG and extracted the CS kernel from these measurements. Without Wilson lines, the CG correlators are multiplicatively renormalizable and free from the linear divergencecitebib.bib69 and pinch singularity, as well as the operator mixings originating from the Wilson line geometry. Through our calculation, we show that, the CG approach leads to consistent CS kernel with the conventional GI approach. Moreover, the CG approach can significantly reduce the signal-to-noise ratio and extend the prediction power of lattice computation in the nonperturbative regime of interest to TMD physics.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework: The pion quasi-TMDWF in the CG is defined as the Fourier transform of the matrix elements: ϕΓCG​(b⟂,bz,Pz,μ)=⟨Ω|OΓCG​(𝐛)|π+;Pz⟩,superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕΓCGsubscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇quantum-operator-productΩsubscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛superscript𝜋subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyletildephiGammarm CG(bperp,bz,Pz,mu)=Omega|Orm CGGamma(b)|pi+;Pz, where the pion is boosted with momentum P=(0,0,Pz)P00subscript𝑃𝑧P=(0,0,Pz). By selecting Γ=γt​γ5Γsubscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾5Gamma=gammatgamma5 or γz​γ5subscript𝛾𝑧subscript𝛾5gammazgamma5, the quasi-TMDWF ϕΓ​(x,b⟂,Pz,μ)subscriptitalic-ϕΓ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃𝑧𝜇tildephiGamma(x,bperp,Pz,mu) can be related to the light-cone TMDWF ϕ​(x,b⟂,ζ,μ)italic-ϕ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜁𝜇phi(x,bperp,zeta,mu) (under the principle-value prescription of the light-cone Wilson linescitebib.bib72) in the large Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz limit through perturbative factorization, which can be expressed ascitebib.bib37, bib.bib42, bib.bib70, ϕΓ​(x,b⟂,Pz,μ)Sr​(b⟂,μ)=H​(x,x¯,Pz,μ)​ϕ​(x,b⟂,ζ,μ)​exp⁡[14​(ln⁡(2​x​Pz)2ζ+ln⁡(2​x¯​Pz)2ζ)​γMS¯​(b⟂,μ)]+𝒪​(ΛQCD2(x​Pz)2,1(b⟂​(x​Pz))2,ΛQCD2(x¯​Pz)2,1(b⟂​(x¯​Pz))2),subscriptitalic-ϕΓ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃𝑧𝜇subscript𝑆𝑟subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝐻𝑥¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇italic-ϕ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜁𝜇14superscript2𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2𝜁superscript2¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2𝜁superscript𝛾¯MSsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇𝒪superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2superscript𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧21superscriptsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2superscript¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧21superscriptsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2displaystylebeginsplitfractildephiGamma(x,bperp,Pz,mu)sqrtSr(bperp,mu)=H(x,barx,Pz,mu)phi(x,bperp,zeta,mu)expleft[frac14left(lnfrac(2xPz)2zeta+lnfrac(2barxPz)2zetaright)gammaoverlinerm MS(bperp,mu)right] +Oleft(fracLambdarm QCD2(xPz)2,frac1(bperp(xPz))2,fracLambdarm QCD2(barxPz)2,frac1(bperp(barxPz))2right),endsplit with x¯=1−x¯𝑥1𝑥barx=1-x. γMS¯​(b⟂,μ)superscript𝛾¯MSsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇gammaoverlinerm MS(bperp,mu) is the CS kernel that governs the rapidity scale evolution from ζ𝜁zeta to (2​x​Pz)2superscript2𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2(2xPz)2 (or (2​x¯​Pz)2superscript2¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧2(2barxPz)2). H​(x,x¯,Pz,μ)𝐻𝑥¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇H(x,barx,Pz,mu) is a hard matching kernel that has been computed from one-loop perturbation theorycitebib.bib70, bib.bib71. Sr​(b⟂,μ)subscript𝑆𝑟subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇Sr(bperp,mu) represents the reduced soft functions, extractable from the form factors of fast-moving color-charged statescitebib.bib37, bib.bib42, bib.bib70. Consequently, the x𝑥x-dependent light-cone TMDWF can be derived, subject to power corrections that are suppressed by large Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz and b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. Alternatively, the CS kernel can be extracted through the ratios of the quasi-TMDWFs with different momenta P1subscript𝑃1P1 and P2subscript𝑃2P2, γMS¯​(b⟂,μ)=1ln⁡(P2/P1)​ln⁡[ϕ​(x,b⟂,P2,μ)ϕ​(x,b⟂,P1,μ)]+δ​γMS¯​(x,μ,P1,P2)+p. c. ,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝛾¯MSsubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃1italic-ϕ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃2𝜇italic-ϕ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃1𝜇𝛿superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥𝜇subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2pcdisplaystylebeginsplitgammaoverlinerm MS(bperp,mu)=frac1ln(P2/P1)lnleft[fractildephi(x,bperp,P2,mu)tildephi(x,bperp,P1,mu)right]+deltagammaoverlinerm MS(x,mu,P1,P2)+rm p. c. ,endsplit with perturbative corrections δ​γMS¯𝛿superscript𝛾¯MSdeltagammaoverlinerm MS inferred from H​(x,x¯,Pz,μ)𝐻𝑥¯𝑥subscript𝑃𝑧𝜇H(x,barx,Pz,mu) and power corrections (p. c. ).
content_sections.III Lattice setup: The bare matrix elements of the pion quasi-TMDWF can be extracted from the two-point correlation functions in the lattice simulations. For CG quasi-TMDWFs, we compute, Cπ​OCG​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)=⟨OΓCG​(𝐛,𝐏,ts)​π†​(y0,0)⟩,subscriptsuperscript𝐶CG𝜋𝑂subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧delimited-⟨⟩subscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛𝐏subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝜋†subscripty00displaystyle Crm CGpiO(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)=left Orm CGGamma(b,P,ts)pidagger(y0,0)right, with, OΓCG​(𝐛,𝐏,ts)=∑𝐲d¯​(𝐲+𝐛2,ts)​Γ​u​(𝐲−𝐛2,ts)|∇⋅A=0​e−i​𝐏⋅(𝐲−𝐲0). subscriptsuperscript𝑂CGΓ𝐛𝐏subscript𝑡𝑠evaluated-atsubscript𝐲¯𝑑𝐲𝐛2subscript𝑡𝑠Γ𝑢𝐲𝐛2subscript𝑡𝑠⋅∇A0superscript𝑒⋅𝑖𝐏𝐲subscript𝐲0displaystylebeginsplitOrm CGGamma(b,P,ts)=sumyoverlined(y+fracb2,ts)Gamma u(y-fracb2,ts)|nablacdotA=0e-iPcdot(y-y0). endsplit Here y0subscripty0y0 is the source position, and tssubscript𝑡𝑠ts is the time separation. We chose Γ=γt​γ5Γsubscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾5Gamma=gammatgamma5, as it should be free from the operator mixings caused by chiral symmetry breakingcitebib.bib62, bib.bib64, bib.bib65 under our lattice setup. To improve the signal-to-noise ratio and increase the overlap with the pion ground state, we used extended pion source after boosted Gaussian smearingcitebib.bib73, π†​(𝐲,ts)=u¯s​(𝐲,ts)​γ5​ds​(𝐲,ts),superscript𝜋†𝐲subscript𝑡𝑠subscript¯𝑢𝑠𝐲subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝛾5subscript𝑑𝑠𝐲subscript𝑡𝑠displaystylepidagger(y,ts)=overlineus(y,ts)gamma5ds(y,ts), with the s𝑠s denoting the smeared fields. We also compute the pion-pion two-point functions, Cπ​π​(ts,Pz)=⟨π​(𝐏,ts)​π†​(𝐲0,0)⟩,subscript𝐶𝜋𝜋subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑃𝑧delimited-⟨⟩𝜋𝐏subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝜋†subscript𝐲00displaystyle Cpipi(ts,Pz)=leftpi(P,ts)pidagger(y0,0)right, with smeared source and sink to extract the energy spectrum created by π†superscript𝜋†pidagger as well as the overlap amplitudes. For the lattice simulation, we utilized a 2+1-flavor Domain-wall gauge ensemble generated by RBC and UKQCD Collaborations of size Ns3×Nt×N5=643×128×12superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑠3subscript𝑁𝑡subscript𝑁5superscript64312812Ns3times Nttimes N5=643times 128times 12, denoted by 64Icitebib.bib74. The quark masses are at the physical point and the lattice spacing is a−1superscript𝑎1a-1 = 2.3549 GeV (a=0.0836𝑎0.0836a=0.0836 fm). For the boosted Gaussian smearing, the Gaussian radius was chosen to be rGsubscript𝑟𝐺rG = 0.58 fm, and we chose the quark boost parameter jzsubscript𝑗𝑧jz to be 0 and 6citebib.bib75, bib.bib76 which are optimal to hadron momentum Pz=2​π​nz/(Ns​a)subscript𝑃𝑧2𝜋subscript𝑛𝑧subscript𝑁𝑠𝑎Pz=2pi nz/(Nsa) with nzsubscript𝑛𝑧nz = 0 and 8, so that the largest momentum in our calculation is Pz=1.85subscript𝑃𝑧1.85Pz=1.85 GeV. Since only two-point functions are involved in this calculation, measurements at other momenta (nz∈[0,8]subscript𝑛𝑧08nzin[0,8]) were also computed through contractions using the same profiled quark propagator. To increase the statistics, we used 64 configurations coupled with All Mode Averaging (AMA) techniquecitebib.bib77. We computed 2 exact and 128 sloppy solutions for the quasi-TMDWFs with momenta nz∈[4,8]subscript𝑛𝑧48nzin[4,8], while 1 exact and 32 sloppy solutions for the cases with nz=[0,3]subscript𝑛𝑧03nz=[0,3]. The quark propagators are evaluated from CG-fixed configurations using deflation based solver with 2000 eigen vectors. After fixing the CG, the GI quasi-TMDWF defined from Eq. shares the same quark propagators as the CG case but needs an additional staple-shaped Wilson line to maintain the gauge invariance. Therefore, we also computed the GI quasi-TMDWF correlators Cπ​OGI​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz,η)subscriptsuperscript𝐶GI𝜋𝑂subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜂Crm GIpiO(ts;bperp,bz,Pz,eta) during the contraction. We chose η=12​a𝜂12𝑎eta=12a in this case using the same setup of the staple-shaped Wilson line as Ref. citebib.bib54. We employed Wilson flowcitebib.bib78, with a flow time tF=1.0subscript𝑡𝐹1.0tF=1.0 (roughly smears the gauge fields over the radius 8​a28superscript𝑎2sqrt8a2), to suppress the ultraviolet (UV) fluctuations and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio.
content_sections.IV Quasi-TMDWF: The pion-pion and quasi-TMDWF correlators have the following spectral decompositions, Cπ​π​(ts;Pz)=∑n=0Nst−1|Zn|22​En​(e−En​ts+e−En​(Lt−ts)),subscript𝐶𝜋𝜋subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑛0subscript𝑁st1superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑛22subscript𝐸𝑛superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐿𝑡subscript𝑡𝑠displaystylebeginsplitCpipi(ts;Pz)=sumn=0Nrm st-1frac|Zn|22Enleft(e-Ents+e-En(Lt-ts)right),endsplit and, Cπ​OCG​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)=∑n=0Nst−1Zn2​En​⟨Ω|Oγt​γ5CG|n⟩​(e−En​ts+e−En​(Lt−ts)),superscriptsubscript𝐶𝜋𝑂CGsubscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑛0subscript𝑁st1subscript𝑍𝑛2subscript𝐸𝑛quantum-operator-productΩsuperscriptsubscript𝑂subscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾5CG𝑛superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝑡𝑠superscript𝑒subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝐿𝑡subscript𝑡𝑠displaystylebeginsplitCpi Orm CG(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)=sumn=0Nrm st-1fracZn2EnOmega|Ogammatgamma5rm CG|n(e-Ents+e-En(Lt-ts)),endsplit where En​(Pz)subscript𝐸𝑛subscript𝑃𝑧En(Pz) is the energy level, and Zn=⟨n|π†​(Pz)|Ω⟩subscript𝑍𝑛quantum-operator-product𝑛superscript𝜋†subscript𝑃𝑧ΩZn= n|pidagger(Pz)|Omega is the overlap amplitude created by the pion interpolator (real and positivecitebib.bib54). |Ω⟩ketΩ|Omega represents the vacuum state, while |n⟩=|0⟩,|1⟩,…ket𝑛ket0ket1…|n=|0,|1,. . . represents the ground state as well as the excited states. To take the advantage of high correlations between the pion-pion and quasi-TMDWF two-point functions, we construct their ratio as, R​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)=−i​Cπ​OCG​(ts;b⟂,bz,Pz)Cπ​π​(ts;Pz). 𝑅subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝑖superscriptsubscript𝐶𝜋𝑂CGsubscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝐶𝜋𝜋subscript𝑡𝑠subscript𝑃𝑧displaystylebeginsplitR(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)=frac-iCpi Orm CG(ts;bperp,bz,Pz)Cpipi(ts;Pz). endsplit In Fig. , the ratios of our largest momentum nz=8subscript𝑛𝑧8nz=8 at b⟂=10​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to10𝑎bperp=10a are shown as an example. In the ts→∞→subscript𝑡𝑠tsrightarrowinfty limit, this ratio will reduce to ⟨Ω|Oγt​γ5|0⟩/Z0=E0​ϕB/Z0quantum-operator-productΩsubscript𝑂subscript𝛾𝑡subscript𝛾50subscript𝑍0subscript𝐸0superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑍0Omega|Ogammatgamma5|0/Z0=E0tildephiB/Z0 and gives the bare quasi-TMDWF matrix elements ϕB​(b⟂,bz,Pz,a)superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝑎tildephiB(bperp,bz,Pz,a). In practice, with finite tssubscript𝑡𝑠ts we truncate Eq. and Eq. up to Nst=2subscript𝑁st2Nrm st=2 and extract the bare matrix elements through the two-state fit. The fit results are shown as the bands in Fig. which can nicely describe the data point. The renormalization of CG quasi-TMD operator is straightforward. It involves only the CG quark wave function renormalization, which is an overall multiplicative constant and does not depend on the spatial separations b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bzcitebib.bib69. In contrast, the GI quasi-TMD operator defined in Eq. , though also subject to multiplicative renormalization, requires the removal of pinch pole singularities, cusp divergences, and linear divergences associated with the Wilson linecitebib.bib61, bib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib68. This renormalization is proportional to the total link length. In our implementation, the total length of the staple-shape Wilson line is 2​η+b⟂2𝜂subscript𝑏perpendicular-to2eta+bperp, independent of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz. Since the renormalization process solely involves the UV properties of operators and is independent of the external hadron states, we use the renormalization group invariant ratioscitebib.bib54 Φ​(b⟂,bz,Pz)=ϕB​(b⟂,bz,Pz,η,a)ϕB​(b⟂,0,0,η,a),Φsubscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜂𝑎superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵subscript𝑏perpendicular-to00𝜂𝑎displaystyletildePhi(bperp,bz,Pz)=fractildephiB(bperp,bz,Pz,eta,a)tildephiB(bperp,0,0,eta,a), without affecting the x𝑥x- and Pz−limit-fromsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz-dependences of the quasi-TMDWF after Fourier transform. The above ratio also may reduce some correlated uncertainties and eliminate some power corrections. Thus, we also adopt the same procedure for the CG matrix elements, whose renormalization is b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and η𝜂eta independent. In Fig. , we show the renormalized matrix elements for our largest momentum, Pz=1.85subscript𝑃𝑧1.85Pz=1.85 GeV, and for b⟂=2​a,6​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to2𝑎6𝑎bperp=2a,6a and 8​a8𝑎8a, as a function of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz, for both CG (filled squared symbols) and GI (open circled symbols) cases. The left panel and right panel show the real and imaginary parts, respectively. It is evident that reasonable signal remains for the CG case even when b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp become large. In contrast, the signal-to-noise ratio of the GI matrix elements rapidly deteriorates as b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp increases, primarily due to the long Wilson line and its UV fluctuations. In addition, it is shown that the imaginary parts of the CG case are consistently zero, while they have non-zero values for GI case. This is expected as the imaginary part depends on the longitudinal orientation of the Wilson line in the GI casecitebib.bib72, whereas the CG condition does not favor any directioncitebib.bib70. One can also observe that the matrix elements decrease as a function of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz, diminishing to zero within the errors when bz≳1greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑏𝑧1bzgtrsim 1 fm. This behavior facilitates the numerical Fourier transform to x𝑥x-space with a simple truncation at the maximum value of bzsubscript𝑏𝑧bz, which is expressed as, Φ​(x,b⟂,Pz)=Pzπ​∫0bzmaxei​(x−12)​Pz​bz​Φ​(b⟂,bz,Pz)Φ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧𝜋superscriptsubscript0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑧maxsuperscript𝑒𝑖𝑥12subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑏𝑧Φsubscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑏𝑧subscript𝑃𝑧displaystyletildePhi(x,bperp,Pz)=fracPzpiint0bzrm maxei(x-frac12)PzbztildePhi(bperp,bz,Pz) where we apply a first-order spline interpolation to smooth the data points. Since the CG quasi-TMDWF correlator is real and symmetric in bzsuperscript𝑏𝑧bz, the distribution must be real in the x𝑥x-space. In the upper panel of Fig. , we show selected results of the CG quasi-TMDWFs with momentum nz∈[4,8]subscript𝑛𝑧48nzin[4,8] and b⟂=2​a,8​a,10​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to2𝑎8𝑎10𝑎bperp=2a,8a,10a. Encouragingly, reasonable signal persists even when b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp is as large as 10​a10𝑎10a. However, the signal-to-noise ratio decreases as the momentum increases. In addition, it is evident that the quasi-TMDWFs, though appearing to be non-zero outside the physical region, have a trend to shrink into x∈[0,1]𝑥01xin[0,1] as the momentum increases. This observation is consistent with the power expansion of the LaMET, suggesting the quasi-TMDWF is approaching the light-cone TMDWF in the large momentum limit.
content_sections.V The Collins-Soper kernel: According to Eq. , we define the following estimator of the CS kernel utilizing the quasi-TMDWFs at finite momenta, γMS¯​(x,b⟂,P1,P2,μ)=1ln⁡(P2/P1)​ln⁡[Φ​(x,b⟂,P2)Φ​(x,b⟂,P1)]+δ​γMS¯​(x,μ,P1,P2). superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝜇1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃1Φ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃2Φ𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosubscript𝑃1𝛿superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥𝜇subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2displaystylebeginsplithatgammaoverlinerm MS(x,bperp,P1,P2,mu)=frac1ln(P2/P1)lnleft[fractildePhi(x,bperp,P2)tildePhi(x,bperp,P1)right]+deltagammaoverlinerm MS(x,mu,P1,P2). endsplit In this work, we applied the perturbative corrections δ​γMS¯𝛿superscript𝛾¯MSdeltagammaoverlinerm MS derived from the next-to-leading logarithm (NLL) matching kernels for the CG casecitebib.bib38, bib.bib43, bib.bib54, bib.bib70 as only one-loop non-cusp anomalous dimension is available. The MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scale has been set to be μ=2𝜇2mu=2 GeV. If the power corrections and higher-order corrections are small, γMS¯superscript𝛾¯MShatgammaoverlinerm MS should be independent of Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz and x𝑥x. In the lower panels of Fig. , we show the CS kernel estimators for the CG case with various combination of momenta, n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2, as a function of x𝑥x. The x𝑥x-independent plateaus can be found in the moderate x𝑥x region within the errors, which is robust even at the largest b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. This indicates the effectiveness of the factorization formula in . In the end-point regions of both small and large x𝑥x, the results appear to diverge, signaling a breakdown of the factorization in these areas. However, the length of plateaus extend as the momentum increases, which is consistent with the power corrections suggested in Eq. . As for the momentum dependence, it is absent even for the case of large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp due, which indicates well suppressed power corrections by 1/(Pz​b⟂)1subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to1/(Pzbperp), despite their slightly larger errors. However, results at small b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp (e. g. , for 2​a2𝑎2a) and with small momentum (e. g. , for n1=4subscript𝑛14n1=4) deviated from the ones derived from larger momenta. This momentum dependence is reduced when n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2 gets close, and disappear when n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2 are close enough (e. g. , for n1/n2=4/5subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛245n1/n2=4/5). This observation suggests that the power corrections and higher-order perturbative corrections are not well suppressed in the cases of small b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and large differences in Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz. To estimate the CS kernel, we averaged over the estimator γMS¯​(x,b⟂,μ,P1,P2)superscript𝛾¯MS𝑥subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2hatgammaoverlinerm MS(x,bperp,mu,P1,P2) within x∈[x0,1−x0]𝑥subscript𝑥01subscript𝑥0xin[x0,1-x0] across various n1subscript𝑛1n1 and n2subscript𝑛2n2. Only the cases of n2−n1=1subscript𝑛2subscript𝑛11n2-n1=1 are considered. The value of x0subscript𝑥0x0 is determined by requiring 2​x0​Pz​b⟂>12subscript𝑥0subscript𝑃𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to12x0Pzbperp>1 and 2​x0​Pz>0.7​GeV2subscript𝑥0subscript𝑃𝑧0.7GeV2x0Pz>0.7rm GeV, suggested by the power correction. As a result, b⟂=asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝑎bperp=a is always excluded in this work. The averages over x𝑥x and different valeus n1/n2subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛2n1/n2 are carried out for each bootstrap sample of gauge configurations. The results are quoted from the median and 68% confidence limit of the distribution of all bootstrap samples. Thus, our quoted errors include the correlated statistical and systematic errors arising from x𝑥x and Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz averaging. Our results for the CS kernel are shown as the black points in Fig. . The error bars indicate errors when n1/n2=6/7subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛267n1/n2=6/7 and 7/8787/8 are excluded from the average. The averages including n1/n2=6/7subscript𝑛1subscript𝑛267n1/n2=6/7 and 7/8787/8 are depicted as black patches under the data points. The CS kernel extracted from the GI quasi-TMDWFs calculated in this work is also shown as the blue points and patches, which is consistent with the CG case at smaller b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. We do not show the CS kernel from the GI quasi-TMDWFs at b⟂>4​asubscript𝑏perpendicular-to4𝑎bperp>4a because the results are too noisy for comparison as already indicated in Fig. . It has been demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib55 that after the matching correction, which takes into account of the power corrections at small b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperpcitebib.bib54 and the so-called linear renormalon subtraction at large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperpcitebib.bib71, the imaginary part of the CS kernel in the GI case is consistent with zero, so we only take the real part of the final result. Our results agree with the N3​LLsuperscriptN3LLrm N3LL perturbative predictioncitebib.bib81, bib.bib82 at the short distances (b⟂≲less-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑏perpendicular-toabsentbperplesssim 0.4 fm). Beyond this point, the perturbative prediction becomes sensitive to the Landau pole and, thereby, loses reliability. Although our CS kernel from GI the case loses signal for b⟂≳0.4greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑏perpendicular-to0.4bperpgtrsim 0.4fm, our CG results continue to show very good signals at b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp up to about 111fm. For comparisons, we show the most recent lattice QCD calculation (ASWZ24) from GI quasi-TMDWFs in the continuum limit with high statisticscitebib.bib55. Evidently, the results from CG and GI case are consistent with each other, suggesting they fall into the same universality class in the large Pzsubscript𝑃𝑧Pz limit under the framework of LaMET. Furthermore, our nonperturbative theoretical predictions of the CS kernel are in agreement with the recent phenomenological parameterizations of experimental data, MAP22citebib.bib26, ART23citebib.bib27, IFY23citebib.bib28, and HSO24 (E605)citebib.bib29, which shows a near-linear b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp dependence in the nonperturbative regime as proposed in Ref. citebib.bib83.
content_sections.VI Conclusion: We conducted the first lattice QCD calculation of the CS kernel utilizing the recently proposed CG quasi-TMD approach as well as employing unitary domain wall fermion discretization with physical quark masses and a fine lattice spacing. The CG approach shows significantly lower signal decay, allowing the CS kernel to be determined for extended transverse separations. At the same time, we show that our results are well compatible with the widely used gauge-invariant method. Notably, our results agree with the recent phenomenological parameterizations of experimental data and suggest a near-linear dependence of the CS kernel on large b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp. This work lays a solid foundation for future research into the CS kernel at larger values of b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp, and advances the QCD computations in the nonperturbative regime of TMD physics.

sorted_papers.title:
Renormalon cancellation and linear power correction to threshold-like
  asymptotics of space-like parton correlators
sorted_papers.abstract:
In this paper, we show that the common hard kernel of double-log-type or
threshold-type factorization for certain space-like parton correlators that
arise in the context of lattice parton distributions, the heavy-light Sudakov
hard kernel, has linear infrared (IR) renormalon. We explicitly demonstrate how
this IR renormalon correlates with ultraviolet (UV) renormalons of
next-to-leading power operators in two explicit examples: threshold asymptotics
of space-like quark-bilinear coefficient functions and transverse momentum
dependent (TMD) factorization of quasi wave function amplitude. Theoretically,
the pattern of renormalon cancellation complies with general expectations to
marginal asymptotics in the UV limit. Practically, this linear renormalon
explains the slow convergence of imaginary parts observed in lattice extraction
of the Collins-Soper kernel and signals the relevance of next-to-leading power
contributions. Fully factorized, fully controlled threshold asymptotic
expansion for space-like quark-bilinear coefficient functions in coordinate and
moment space has also been proposed.

sections.21: Collins, Foundations of perturbative QCD, Vol

qualitative: The Collins–Soper evolution kernel exhibits a linear infrared (IR) renormalon, which is responsible for the slow convergence of its imaginary part in lattice extractions.

This IR renormalon is correlated with ultraviolet (UV) renormalons from next-to-leading power operators, indicating a non-trivial interplay between IR and UV divergences in the factorization framework.
The presence of the linear IR renormalon signals the relevance of next-to-leading power contributions in threshold-like asymptotics of space-like parton correlators.

In the context of transverse momentum dependent (TMD) factorization, the renormalon structure affects the quasi wave function amplitude and implies limitations in the perturbative control of TMD distributions at small transverse momentum.
The pattern of renormalon cancellation aligns with general expectations for marginal asymptotics in the UV limit, supporting the consistency of the factorization framework.

A fully factorized and controlled threshold asymptotic expansion for space-like quark-bilinear coefficient functions has been proposed in both coordinate and momentum space, enabling improved theoretical control over lattice extractions.
quantitative: The hard kernel in threshold-type factorization for space-like parton correlators contains a linear IR renormalon, characterized by a singularity of the form $ \sim \frac{1}{\beta_0 \ln(\mu^2 / \Lambda^2)} $ in the Borel plane.

The correlation between IR renormalons and UV renormalons of next-to-leading power operators leads to a cancellation pattern consistent with marginal asymptotics, where the leading singularity in the Borel transform is linear in the renormalon index.

The imaginary part of the Collins–Soper kernel shows slow convergence in lattice calculations, quantitatively linked to the linear IR renormalon with a coefficient proportional to $ \frac{1}{\beta_0} $, where $ \beta_0 = \frac{11}{3} C_A - \frac{4}{3} T_F n_f $ is the first coefficient of the QCD beta function.

The proposed fully factorized threshold asymptotic expansion includes terms of the form $ \sim \frac{\alpha_s^n(\mu)}{\mu} $, indicating the presence of linear power corrections in the asymptotic series.

sorted_papers.reason:
论文精准匹配查询核心主题，涵盖Collins–Soper演化与TMD因子化，明确涉及柯林斯-索珀核、TMD因子化及阈值渐近行为，且在坐标空间与动量空间中提出完全因子化展开，与查询高度一致。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究主题为Collins–Soper演化与TMD因子化 — 满足。证据：“TMD factorization of quasi wave function amplitude” 对比 “Collins–Soper evolution and TMD factorization”  
- C2：涉及阈值渐近与线性红外重正化子 — 满足。证据：“threshold asymptotics of space-like quark-bilinear coefficient functions” 对比 “threshold-like asymptotics”

authors: Yizhuang Liu and Yushan Su

content_sections.1 Introduction: It is known that perturbative series induced by marginal perturbations to UV or IR conformal field theories (CFTs) are tricky and subtle. On one hand, fixed-order Feynman-diagram-based calculations suffer from uncontrolled large logarithms that must be resummed using renormalization group equation (RGE) or whatever structures to become controlled asymptotic expansions. On the other hand, even after resummation, normally the perturbative series can only be a divergent, Borel non-summable asymptotic series. Physically, this reflects the fact that in the presence of marginal perturbation, the decoupling between UV and IR scales is very slow, and the Borel non-summability is the price one pays for decoupling asymptotically. In fact, Borel non-summable logarithmic asymptotic series is not unusual in nature. A famous example from mathematics is the prime number theorem (PNT), stating that the asymptotic distribution π​(x)𝜋𝑥pi(x) of prime numbers takes the form π​(x)∼xln⁡x​∑n=0∞n!lnn⁡x,similar-to𝜋𝑥𝑥𝑥superscriptsubscript𝑛0𝑛superscript𝑛𝑥displaystylepi(x)simfracxln xsumn=0inftyfracn!lnnx , in the x→∞→𝑥xrightarrowinfty limit, which is similar in shape to a perturbative series with “one-loop running coupling constant” α​(x)=1ln⁡x𝛼𝑥1𝑥alpha(x)=frac1ln x. One can check that the Borel resummation of the above series has a pole at t=1𝑡1t=1, and different prescriptions, such as the principle value or ±i​0plus-or-minus𝑖0pm i0 prescriptions lead to O​𝑂1O ambiguity PV​∫0xd​tln⁡t−∫0xd​tln⁡t±i​0=±π​i. PVsuperscriptsubscript0𝑥𝑑𝑡𝑡superscriptsubscript0𝑥𝑑𝑡plus-or-minus𝑡𝑖0plus-or-minus𝜋𝑖displaystylerm PVint0xfracdtln t-int0xfracdtln tpm i0=pmpi i . Can one read from this ambiguity any useful information regarding “power corrections” to the PNT? The answer is unfortunately no. In fact, one knows that the true “next-to-leading-power” correction comes from non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function and is of the order x𝑥sqrtx (assuming Riemann hypothesis) π​(x)−PV​∫0xd​tln⁡t=𝒪​(x). 𝜋𝑥PVsuperscriptsubscript0𝑥𝑑𝑡𝑡𝒪𝑥displaystylepi(x)-rm PVint0xfracdtln t=cal O(sqrtx) . In such a case, the Borel singularity at “leading power” carries no useful information regarding the power corrections at all. Given this example, one may ask the following question: near UV fixed point of local quantum field theories (QFTs), is it also true that marginal perturbative series for IR safe quantities, even with their singularity structures in the Borel plane completely decoded, still can not tell where the non-trivial power corrections are? The answer to this question is fortunately no. In fact, although the perturbative series in QFT is usually much more complicated than Eq. and in prior one can not even see where the Borel singularity is, due to the special context of local QFTs, important understandings can still be formed. In particular, the perturbative renomaloncitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4 is widely believed to be a crucial structure that can provide useful information regarding the high-order behavior of perturbative series as well as power corrections. Based on our understanding, positive arguments and evidences in favor of perturbative renormalons can be summarized as below. 1. First of all, they are caused by a small amount of diagrams (such as the bubble chain diagram for QCD) which allow rigorous analysis as well as explicit calculations to show that they indeed lead to non-alternating factorial growths of the form (β02​k)n​n!superscriptsubscript𝛽02𝑘𝑛𝑛(fracbeta02k)nn! which would become Borel singularity at t=2​kβ0𝑡2𝑘subscript𝛽0t=frac2kbeta0 if not miraculously canceled by other diagrams. 2. Second, at the level of Feynman integrals, the factorial growth is caused by running inside loop integrals which exactly drives the loop momenta towards the “dangerous direction”, such as the small momentum region in QCD, suggesting natural correlation with power corrections or non-perturbative contributions. 3. Third, in several examples where the high power contributions are strongly believed to be caused by high-dimensional operators in the operator product expansion (OPE), a characteristic structure for CFTs that are very likely to be inherited in some form in their marginal deformations as well, the IR renormalon in the leading power can be shown to correlatecitebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13 with UV renormalons caused by ambiguities in defining these high dimensional operators. 4. Fourth, in all the integrable none-supersymmetric asymptotically safe 2D massive QFTs that have been analyzed using the thermodynamical Bethe’s ansatz (TBA) method so farcitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, the leading singularities in the right-half Borel plane (for the perturbative series for ground state energy in the large “chemical potential” limit) and the place of NLP contribution all agree1Notice for the O​𝑂4O non-linear sigma model and S​U​(N)𝑆𝑈𝑁SU(N) chiral principal field models, contribution at the power predicted by the IR renormalon vanishes in the principal value prescription but exists in ±i​0plus-or-minus𝑖0pm i0 prescriptionscitebib.bib16, bib.bib15, bib.bib20. For β2→8​π−→superscript𝛽28superscript𝜋beta2rightarrow 8pi- massive sine Gordon, the leading IR renormalon is the only Borel singularity in the right-half planecitebib.bib22 and the perturbative series re-summs to full result in the principal value prescription. with predictions based on leading perturbative IR renormalon. In particular, for the O​(N)𝑂𝑁O(N) non-linear sigma models, both the large-N𝑁N expansioncitebib.bib23, bib.bib6, bib.bib24, bib.bib7, bib.bib11 at 𝒪​(1N)𝒪1𝑁cal O(frac1N) and the exact TBA method agreescitebib.bib25, bib.bib16, bib.bib26 and works in favor of perturbative renormalon. 5. Finally, for the lattice perturbation series for the self-energy of a Wilson-line, the asymptotic behavior extracted from high-order numerical calculations agrees with the renormalon-based predictioncitebib.bib27, bib.bib28. To summarize, the perturbative renormalon, in particular, the bubble-chain-based renormalon analysis for QCD (sometimes called “large β0subscript𝛽0beta0 approximation” in literature) is a valuable method to determine leading Borel singularity of QCD perturbative series as well as to probe, or at least, to provide lower-bounds for high power corrections. In this paper, based on this understanding, we investigate the Borel singularity using the bubble-chain diagrams, for a particular hard kernel that appears in several factorization formulas in the large momentum effective theory (LaMET) formulationcitebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32 for lattice parton distributions. This hard kernel first appeared in the factorization of quasi-TMDPDFscitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, and then in TMD factorization of quasi light-front wave function (LFWF) amplitudescitebib.bib39 and recently in threshold limit of space-like quark-bilinear coefficient functionscitebib.bib40. It is a double-logarithmic type or threshold-type hard kernel in the sense that it satisfies an RGE with a logarithmic term in its anomalous dimension of the form Γcusp​(α)​ln⁡ζzμ2subscriptΓcusp𝛼subscript𝜁𝑧superscript𝜇2Gammarm cusp(alpha)lnfraczetazmu2 controlled by the universal light-like cusp anomalous dimension Γcusp​(α)subscriptΓcusp𝛼Gammarm cusp(alpha)citebib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43 . Moreover, its definition is very simple: it is just an on-shell form factor defined purely in PQCD with UV and IR divergences subtracted multiplicatively in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme, with an incoming on-shell light-quark with four-momenta p2=0superscript𝑝20p2=0 and an outgoing on-shell heavy Wilson-line in space-like direction nz2=−1superscriptsubscript𝑛𝑧21nz2. The Lorentz invariant combination ζz=4​(p⋅nz)2≠0subscript𝜁𝑧4superscript⋅𝑝subscript𝑛𝑧20zetaz=4(pcdot nz)2neq 0 serves as the only scale of the form factor. For this reason, it is called the heavy-light Sudakov form factorcitebib.bib40 or heavy-light Sudakov hard kernel due to its similarity with the standard light-light Sudakov form factorcitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47 with on-shell external light-partons. On the other hand, since the gauge link can be interpreted as the gauge transformation that transforms a generic gauge to the axial gauge , this form factor can also be regarded as the gauge-invariant definition of the quark field renormalization factor in the axial gauge, for which double-logarithmic asymptotics is known long agocitebib.bib48. In all three factorization formulas, this form factor is caused by the Sudakov effect around the quark-link vertices and has recently been determined up to NNLOcitebib.bib40, bib.bib49. Naively looking, this hard kernel should contain only quadratic IR renormalon singularities which is the usual case for hard kernels. On the other hand, the presence of a heavy Wilson-line in its definition resembles quantities in heavy-quark effective theory, for which linear renormalon is common. It is the purpose of this paper to show using explicit calculations that the heavy-light Sudakov form factor contains linear IR renormalon in its phase angle. Moreover, we show that for both the threshold factorization of space-like quark correlator as well as the TMD factorization of quasi-LFWF amplitude, this linear renormalon cancels with corresponding UV renormalons for operators at next-to-leading power (NLP). As such, these examples can also serve as simple demonstration-of-principle for the general pattern of renormalon cancellation in the context of double-logarithmic-type asymptotic expansions. The case of quasi-LFWF also provides a simple example of the “OPE non-convergence” phenomenoncitebib.bib50, bib.bib51. We hope that these examples will be not only useful for the lattice extraction of partonic distributions and evolution kernels, but also interesting for their pedagogical value to demonstrate the renormalon cancellation/correlation pattern mentioned before. We emphasize that the linear renormalon in the heavy-light Sudakov hard kernel requires “on-shell” space-like gauge-link and affects only the double-logarithmic type factorization of quasi-distributions. Before power expansions that lead to double-log evolution, the gauge links “cancel among themselves” at infinity between “real” and virtual diagrams, and the linear renormalons are hidden. After the TMD expansion in 1b⟂​pz1subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosuperscript𝑝𝑧frac1bperppz or threshold expansion in 1z​pz1𝑧superscript𝑝𝑧frac1zpz, at leading power the cancellation is no-longer perfect, and the linear renormalon can only be cancelled between leading and higher powers. The organization of the paper is as follows. 1. In Sec. , we calculate explicitly the bubble chain diagram for the hard kernel in the MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme following the method ofcitebib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55. We show that the imaginary part suffers linear renormalon ambiguity, while the real part is free from linear renormalon. Furthermore, the strength for the linear renormalon ambiguity happens to be the same as that for the linear divergence of pole mass. As a by-product, we also obtained the corresponding MS¯¯MSoverlinerm MS scheme anomalous dimensions for the bubble chain diagram. 2. In Sec. , we investigate the role played by this linear renormalon in the threshold asymptotics of space-like “quasi”citebib.bib29, bib.bib30 or “pseudo”citebib.bib56, bib.bib57 quark-bilinear coefficient functions in coordinate space and moment space. We first review the factorization formalism, emphasizing the UV nature of the threshold limit in coordinate space and moment space at fixed z2superscript𝑧2z2. A fully-factorized controlled expansion in the N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty limit has been written down explicitly. We then use explicit analytical result of bubble chain diagramcitebib.bib1 to perform the threshold expansion both in coordinate and moment space. The threshold factorization formula at leading threshold power is verified at the bubble chain level. The linear renormalon cancels between the heavy-light hard kernel at leading threshold power and the NLP threshold soft factor. We identify the operator definition for the NLP contribution and demonstrate that its linear renormalon is UV. We show that the “genuine” IR renormalons related to z​ΛQCD𝑧subscriptΛQCDzLambdarm QCD power corrections remain the same before and after threshold expansion. We further investigate the “resurgence” of full distribution from threshold asymptotics by noticing the correlation between Borel ambiguity of e−i​λsuperscript𝑒𝑖𝜆e-ilambda terms (corresponding to α→1→𝛼1alpharightarrow 1 threshold asymptotics) and branch ambiguity of purely algebraic terms (corresponding to α→0→𝛼0alpharightarrow 0 asymptotics). 3. In Sec. , we perform the same analysis to the TMD factorization of quasi-LFWF amplitude. We show that the bubble chain diagram for the quasi-LWFW amplitude, before the TMD expansion, is free from the linear renormalon. After performing the power expansion, the linear renormalon in the heavy-light Sudakov form factor, playing the role of the hard kernel at leading power, cancels with the UV renormalon of NLP soft contribution. We determine the operator definition of the NLP soft factor and show that its UV renormalon is still correlated with linear UV divergence rather than the linear rapidity divergence. We show that the power expansion in this example fails to sum to the full result due to extra exponentially small contributions and comment on features of TMD expansion in b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp and k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp space. 4. In Sec. , we calculate the bubble chain diagram for the quark field wave function renormalization in Coulomb gauge and show that it suffers linear renormalon ambiguity as well. In Sec. , we discuss the practical implications of this linear renormalon to lattice extraction of Collins-Soper kernel and then conclude. Useful technical details are collected in the Appendices.

As shown incitebib.bib40 to NNLO, the coordinate space coefficient function in the threshold limit λ→∞→𝜆lambdarightarrowinfty3Here we should note that in the large λ𝜆lambda limit, there are contributions without the e−i​λsuperscript𝑒𝑖𝜆e-ilambda factor as well, see Eq. below. Here HHLsubscript𝐻HLHrm HL is nothing but the HL Sudakov hard kernel, and the LP space-like threshold soft factor J𝐽J or “jet function” is a specific Wilson-loop average involving space-like and light-like gauge-links defined incitebib.bib40 J​(lz,α​(μ))=⟨Ω|𝒯​[z​nz+∞​n+,z​nz]​[z​nz,0]​[0,−∞​n+]|Ω⟩,𝐽subscript𝑙𝑧𝛼𝜇quantum-operator-productΩ𝒯𝑧subscript𝑛𝑧superscript𝑛𝑧subscript𝑛𝑧𝑧subscript𝑛𝑧00superscript𝑛Ωdisplaystyle J(lz,alpha(mu))=Omega|cal T[znz+infty n+,znz][znz,0][0,-infty n+]|Omega , where lz≡ln⁡eγE​|z|​μ2subscript𝑙𝑧superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸𝑧𝜇2lzequivlnfracegammaE|z|mu2 is the natural logarithm in z𝑧z. Here we emphasize that in the threshold λ→∞→𝜆lambdarightarrowinfty limit, the threshold logarithms are due to the hard scale |λ||z|≫1|z|≫ΛQCDmuch-greater-than𝜆𝑧1𝑧much-greater-thansubscriptΛQCDfrac|lambda||z|ggfrac1|z|ggLambdarm QCD and are exactly resummed using the heavy-light Sudakov hard kernel. To demonstrate the above statements concerning the structure of renormalon, in this section we investigate the bubble chain diagram for the coefficient function ℋℋcal H in the threshold limit. The purpose is to show 1. At the level of bubble chain diagram, the threshold factorization formula is correct to leading power in 1λ1𝜆frac1lambda or 1N1𝑁frac1N (but to all logarithmic orders). More precisely, we perform the threshold expansion of the bubble chain diagram to leading power both in coordinate and moment space. We show that the resulting hard kernel exactly reproduces that calculated in Sec. , and the resulting “jet function” exactly reproduces the bubble chain diagrams for the space-like threshold soft factor. 2. Given the above, the threshold factorization formula is established at the level of bubble chain diagram. We further investigate the role played by the linear renormalon in the heavy-light hard kernel HHLsubscript𝐻HLHrm HL. We show that the linear renormalon cancels between the leading and next-to-leading threshold powers by expanding the full bubble chain results to NLP. We further identify the operator definition of the obtained NLP contribution (at 𝒪​(1|λ|)𝒪1𝜆cal O(frac1|lambda|)) and show that the s=12𝑠12s=frac12 renormalon for the NLP soft factor is UV in nature and is due to implicit ambiguity of subtracting UV divergences. Notice this has nothing to do with the linear UV divergence for space-like gauge-link self-interactions. To summarize, the factorization formula Eq. can be cast into a controlled asymptotic expansion in the threshold limit using the RGEs. Nevertheless, the separation between leading and next-to-leading threshold power contributions (more precisely, separation between |λ||z|𝜆𝑧frac|lambda||z| and 1|z|1𝑧frac1|z|) requires a scheme, reflected in the (IR) renormalon of the leading threshold power hard contribution of order ΛQCD2​N|z|=|z|​ΛQCD2​NsubscriptΛQCD2𝑁𝑧𝑧subscriptΛQCD2𝑁fracLambdarm QCDfrac2N|z|=frac|z|Lambdarm QCD2N and (UV) renormalon of the next-to-leading threshold power “soft” contribution of the same order (1N×ΛQCD​|z|1𝑁subscriptΛQCD𝑧frac1NtimesLambdarm QCD|z|). The scheme dependency cancels between LP and NLP, or equivalently, the renormalon cancels between LP and NLP. It is also helpful to make the following comparisons to the DY threshold limit investigated incitebib.bib54: 1. In the DIS or DY case4Notice that if one define the DIS-like structure function F​(z2,λ)𝐹superscript𝑧2𝜆F(z2,lambda) in coordinate space, then the threshold limit λ→∞→𝜆lambdarightarrowinfty for the coefficient function Fq​(z2,λ)subscript𝐹𝑞superscript𝑧2𝜆Fq(z2,lambda) or N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty for Fq,N​(z2)subscript𝐹𝑞𝑁superscript𝑧2Fq,N(z2) become UV as well. On the other hand, in this case the hard scale is λz2𝜆superscript𝑧2fraclambdaz2 instead of λ2z2superscript𝜆2superscript𝑧2fraclambda2z2 and the leading renormalon for the Sudakov-like hard kernel is quadratic. , in the z→1→𝑧1zrightarrow 1 limit the threshold scales Q​(1−z)𝑄1𝑧Q(1-z) or Q​1−z𝑄1𝑧Qsqrt1-z are soft. Therefore, the resummation factors in the “A-B-C” form are sensitive to the Landau pole and finite-order truncation of the anomalous dimensions can create renormalons. In our case, in the λ→∞→𝜆lambdarightarrowinfty or N→→𝑁absentNrightarrow limit the threshold scales λ|z|𝜆𝑧fraclambda|z| or N|z|𝑁𝑧fracN|z| are UV and the resummation factors are always far away from Landau pole. Moreover, we use the RGEs for the hard kernel and the soft factor to perform the resummation in a way similar to Eq. (5.5) ofcitebib.bib54, instead of truncating the “A-B-C”. As such, the linear renormalon observed here has absolutely no relation to resummation. 2. Due to the above, incitebib.bib54 the authors focus on soft and collinear contributions in the threshold limit. They show that the “soft-collinear” contribution which dominates the DLA cancels with “wide-angle” soft emissions in terms of linear renormalon. In particular, they show that the bubble chain diagram for the DY threshold soft factor is free from linear renormalon and this is consistent with the power correction extracted using finite gluon mass. In our case, the similar object that captures “IR” contributions is the space-like threshold soft factor J​(lz,α​(μ))𝐽subscript𝑙𝑧𝛼𝜇J(lz,alpha(mu)) (similar to the DY case, it is also a Wilson-loop) at scale 1|z|1𝑧frac1|z| and it is free from linear renormalon as well (except the overall UV renormalon for the space-like gauge-link inherited from the definition of pPDF and have nothing to do with threshold expansion). But this is not the object that is responsible for the threshold logarithms in our case. 3. In our case, the object that is responsible for the threshold logarithms is in fact the heavy-light hard kernel. It is purely virtual instead of real, and it is not purely eikonal. Unlike the standard light-light Sudakov hard kernel (namely, the H​(α​(Q))𝐻𝛼𝑄H(alpha(Q)) in Eq. (1.4) ofcitebib.bib54 ), in our hard kernel, the heavy gauge-link and the light parton are not symmetric, as a result, when a finite gluon mass is added, it develops linear power correction, consistent with the observed linear renormalon. This also means that the cancellation of the linear renormalon must involve sub-eikonal effects, therefore must be between leading and next to leading threshold powers, consistent with our observation. Here we also comment in terms of the relation to the original calculation in Ref. citebib.bib1. In fact, the only question we need to answer is that: why in the original work there is almost no linear renormalon (except for the trivial one localized into the gauge-link self energy diagrams), but in this work there is. The reason is again due to the projection to the leading threshold power. Before the threshold expansion, both the virtual and the real diagrams are of the same twist in |z|​ΛQCD𝑧subscriptΛQCD|z|Lambdarm QCD and the linear renormalon in the virtual diagram is completely cancelled by the real diagram. After projecting to the leading threshold power, the virtual diagrams survive, but the real diagrams eikonalize and become part of the space-like threshold soft factor. The contributions in the real diagrams that cancel the linear renormalon are sub-eikonal and move to the next-to-leading threshold power 1N1𝑁frac1N. The linear renormalon then simply measures the intrinsic ambiguity of the threshold-power separation (but not z,ΛQCD𝑧subscriptΛQCDz,Lambdarm QCD separation). Let’s emphasize here that the order of our linear renormalon ambiguity is |z|​ΛQCDN𝑧subscriptΛQCD𝑁frac|z|Lambdarm QCDN and is much smaller in the N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty limit than the “genuine” high-twist contribution 𝒪​(|z|2​ΛQCD2)𝒪superscript𝑧2subscriptsuperscriptΛ2QCDcal O(|z|2Lambda2rm QCD). In particular, the threshold expansion is still performed within a given twist, including the order of ambiguities. Also notice that although we call the linear renormalon in the HL hard kernel “IR”, it is only IR with respect to the scale |λ||z|𝜆𝑧frac|lambda||z| for the hard kernel, but not to 1|z|1𝑧frac1|z|. In fact, we will see that the linear “IR” renormalon cancels with UV renormalon of NLP “soft” contributions at scale 1|z|1𝑧frac1|z|. Due to the above, the linear renormalon should not be regarded as a prediction of presence of genuine non-perturbative effects enhanced in the threshold limit. Instead, it should be interpreted as predicting the existence of next-to-leading threshold power contributions to the leading twist coefficient function that has linear UV renormalon. Moreover, all the “threshold renormalons” generated after threshold expansion always cancel between different threshold powers, and the “twist-expansion renormalons” separating the leading and high powers in the z​ΛQCD𝑧subscriptΛQCDzLambdarm QCD expansion remain the same as given by Eq. and Eq. of the original workcitebib.bib1. In the threshold region, they start contributing only from (z​ΛQCD)2Nsuperscript𝑧subscriptΛQCD2𝑁frac(zLambdarm QCD)2N at 1N1𝑁frac1N and (z​ΛQCD)4superscript𝑧subscriptΛQCD4(zLambdarm QCD)4 at N0superscript𝑁0N0. Finally, both the “threshold renormalons” and the “genuine” renormalons incitebib.bib1 never increases as N→∞→𝑁Nrightarrowinfty. Genuine non-perturbative effects are uniformly bounded by (z​ΛQCD)2superscript𝑧subscriptΛQCD2(zLambdarm QCD)2 itself in an N𝑁N-independent way.

It is clear now that all the remaining μ𝜇mu dependency is due to the UV anomalous dimension of the quark-bilinear itself. Now, one may question if the leading twist always dominate the large λ𝜆lambda limit. In our opinion, we believe that the leading twist dominance holds to λ→+∞→𝜆lambdarightarrow+infty in the sense of Eq. to be introduced. The reason is that, in the large λ𝜆lambda limit, due to the exponential suppression caused by the Euclidean time evolution, the large energy λz𝜆𝑧fraclambdaz of the incoming and out going hadron must flow out and in at the quark-line vertices, leaving the intermediate state (namely, the “cut”) light. In this way, the large λ𝜆lambda limit is dominated by threshold contributions, for which the two vertices are always in Sudakov-like hard kernels with at least one incoming collinear parton and one external soft gauge-link. Clearly, increasing the number of incoming collinear partons (except longitudinal gluons which factorize to eikonal lines in the PDF) and external soft partons to the hard kernel always introduce additional power suppressions. The Sudakov hard kernel with one incoming collinear valence quark and one external soft gauge-link is the hard kernel at leading power, on top of which collinear contributions factorize into the threshold limit of twist-two PDF (this explains the name of “twist-two dominance”) and soft exchanges factorize into the threshold soft factor. Moreover, this picture for the dominant contribution in the large λ𝜆lambda limit should hold for moderate z𝑧z as well, due to the fact that the threshold soft factor, defined as a Wilson-loop average, allows non-perturbative generalization. As such, we conjecture the following λ→+∞→𝜆lambdarightarrow+infty factorization for the hadronic quark-bilinear matrix element from small to moderate z𝑧z: ℱ​(z2,i​λ)→eλ​HHL2​(0,α​(zλ))​exp⁡(OH​(α​(zλ)))​J​(z​ΛQCD)→ℱsuperscript𝑧2𝑖𝜆superscript𝑒𝜆superscriptsubscript𝐻HL20𝛼subscript𝑧𝜆subscript𝑂𝐻𝛼subscript𝑧𝜆𝐽𝑧subscriptΛQCDdisplaystylecal F(z2,ilambda)rightarrow elambdaHrm HL2bigg(0,alpha(zlambda)bigg)expbigg(hatOH(alpha(zlambda))bigg)J(zLambdarm QCD) ×𝒬PDF​(i​λ)​(1+𝒪​(1λ))×exp⁡(f2​F​(α​(μ))−γ2​F0β0​ln⁡α​(μ)). absentsubscript𝒬PDF𝑖𝜆1𝒪1𝜆subscript𝑓2𝐹𝛼𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝛾02𝐹subscript𝛽0𝛼𝜇displaystyletimescal Qrm PDF(ilambda)bigg(1+cal Oleft(frac1lambdaright)bigg)timesexpbigg(f2F(alpha(mu))-fracgamma02Fbeta0lnalpha(mu)bigg) . Here J​(z​ΛQCD)𝐽𝑧subscriptΛQCDJ(zLambdarm QCD) is the appropriate non-perturbative version of the threshold-soft factor Eq. with μ𝜇mu dependency remove by evolution factors. The small z𝑧z expansion of the threshold soft factor is exactly J​(z​ΛQCD)→J​(0,α​(z))​exp⁡(OJ​(α​(z)))​(1+𝒪​(z2​ΛQCD2)). →𝐽𝑧subscriptΛQCD𝐽0𝛼𝑧subscript𝑂𝐽𝛼𝑧1𝒪superscript𝑧2superscriptsubscriptΛQCD2displaystyle J(zLambdarm QCD)rightarrow J(0,alpha(z))expbigg(hatOJ(alpha(z))bigg)bigg(1+cal O(z2Lambdarm QCD2)bigg) . Clearly, the fully factorized form of the threshold limit provides considerable simplification compared to the leading twist factorization formula at generic λ𝜆lambda. Provided that 𝒬PDF​(i​λ)subscript𝒬PDF𝑖𝜆cal Qrm PDF(ilambda) does not decay very fast in the λ→+∞→𝜆lambdarightarrow+infty limit, in principle it allows the lattice verification of the celebrated “marginally-relevant” Sudakov-like asymptotics Eq. which dates back to late 1970scitebib.bib73, bib.bib48.
content_sections.4 Renormalon cancellation and power corr: In this section, we consider the role played by the linear renormalon of the hard kernel in the TMD expansion of quasi-LFWF amplitude. We show that this linear renormalon cancels with UV renormalon of NLP soft contribution. We also show that in this case the TMD expansion fails to converge to the full result due to exponentially small contributions. As a reminder, the quasi-LFWF is defined as the Fourier transform of the following correlatorcitebib.bib32, bib.bib39 Ψ±​(λ,b⟂,μ,4​Pz2)=limL→∞⟨0|ψ¯​(z​nz+b→⟂)​γz​𝒲∓z​(z​nz+b→⟂;L)​ψ​|P⟩ZE​(2​L,b⟂,μ),superscriptΨplus-or-minus𝜆subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇4superscriptsubscript𝑃𝑧2subscript→𝐿quantum-operator-product0¯𝜓𝑧subscript𝑛𝑧subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tosuperscript𝛾𝑧subscript𝒲minus-or-plus𝑧𝑧subscript𝑛𝑧subscript→𝑏perpendicular-to𝐿𝜓0𝑃subscript𝑍𝐸2𝐿subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇displaystylewidetildePsipm(lambda,bperp,mu,4Pz2)=!limLtoinftyfrac 0|barpsibig(znz!+!vecbperpbig)gammazcal Wmp z(znz!+!vecbperp;L)psibig(0big)|PsqrtZE(2L,bperp,mu) , where λ=z​Pz𝜆𝑧superscript𝑃𝑧lambda=zPz is the natural scaleless Lorentz invariant length conjugating to the quark’s momentum fraction x𝑥x. The staple-shaped gauge-link 𝒲zsubscript𝒲𝑧cal Wz is defined as 𝒲±z​(ξ;L)=[ξ,∓L​nz+b→⟂]​[∓L​nz+b→⟂,∓L​nz]​[∓L​nz,0]. subscript𝒲plus-or-minus𝑧𝜉𝐿𝜉minus-or-plus𝐿subscript𝑛𝑧subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tominus-or-plus𝐿subscript𝑛𝑧subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tominus-or-plus𝐿subscript𝑛𝑧minus-or-plus𝐿subscript𝑛𝑧0displaystylecal Wpm z(xi;L)=[xi,mp Lnz+vecbperp][mp Lnz+vecbperp,mp Lnz][mp Lnz,0] . On the other hand, ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE is the square root of the vacuum expectation value of a flat rectangular Euclidean Wilson-loop ZE​(2​L,b⟂,μ)=1Nc​Tr​⟨0|W⟂​𝒲z​(b→⟂;2​L)|0⟩,subscript𝑍𝐸2𝐿subscript𝑏perpendicular-to𝜇1subscript𝑁𝑐Trquantum-operator-product0subscript𝑊perpendicular-tosubscript𝒲𝑧subscript→𝑏perpendicular-to2𝐿0displaystyle ZE(2L,bperp,mu)=frac1Ncrm Tr 0|Wperpcal Wz(vecbperp;2L)|0 , that subtracts out the gauge-link self-interactions in the large L𝐿L limit. The ZEsubscript𝑍𝐸ZE itself has nothing to do with the factorization. In this section, we only consider the quasi-LFWF amplitude in PQCD for the external states with a pair of on-shell quark anti-quark (with momenta p2=p¯2=0superscript𝑝2superscript¯𝑝20p2=barp2=0), which serves as the small b𝑏b OPE coefficients that match the mesonic quasi-LFWF to collinear distribution amplitudes (DA). We calculate the bubble chain diagrams for the quasi-LFWF amplitude that couples the external quark and anti-quark to the space-like gauge links, since at bubble chain level only these two diagrams know about the presence of multiples scales at LP. A special feature of the above NLP soft factor is that in its definition one needs a “high-dimensional Wilson-line cusp” O=[nz​∞,0]​∫−∞0𝑑x+​[0,x+]​D⟂2​[x+,−∞],𝑂subscript𝑛𝑧0superscriptsubscript0differential-dsuperscript𝑥0superscript𝑥superscriptsubscript𝐷perpendicular-to2superscript𝑥displaystylehatO=[nzinfty,0]int-infty0dx+[0,x+]Dperp2[x+,-infty] , with dimension 111, therefore in principle can contribute to power-like UV divergences. Moreover, the correlator Eq. suffers from linear rapidity divergence which requires special treatment. Although in regulators without introducing new scales into Feynman integrals such as the analytic regulatorcitebib.bib75 adopted above, the linear rapidity divergence in Eq. is simply removed when removing the regulator ν→0→𝜈0nurightarrow 0 like the linear divergence in DR when ϵ→0→italic-ϵ0epsilonrightarrow 0, the existence of such divergence strongly suggests that the object in definition still suffers rapidity regularization scheme dependency. So which one among these two linear divergences is related to the linear UV renormalon? The answer is, it is not the linear rapidity divergence, but the linear UV divergence that correlates with the linear renormalon. Indeed, one can introduce a displacement a𝑎a in z𝑧z direction in a way similar to the exponential regulatorcitebib.bib76, bib.bib77 to regulate the rapidity divergence as well. It is not hard to show that the bubble chain diagram after introducing this regulator still contains s=12𝑠12s=frac12 pole at a0superscript𝑎0a0 that can not be canceled by “linear rapidity divergences” at a−1superscript𝑎1a-1. Only after introducing the cutoff regulator for the “high-dimensional Wilson-line cusp” in a way similar to that in Sec. the s=12𝑠12s=frac12 pole can be removed, demonstrating the UV nature of the renormalon. Thus, the linear rapidity divergence is not sufficient to generate linear renormalons, at least in the current case.

Now, after a few simplifications, the integral reads Im​ψ0=Imsubscript𝜓0absentdisplaystylerm Impsi0= −18​π​(γE+ln⁡ζz​b⟂2)+116​π​(1−2ζz​b⟂)18𝜋subscript𝛾𝐸subscript𝜁𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to2116𝜋12subscript𝜁𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-todisplaystyle-frac18pibigg(gammaE+lnfracsqrtzetazbperp2bigg)+frac116pibigg(1-frac2sqrtzetazbperpbigg) −18​π​∫1∞d​xx​e−ζz​b⟂2​x+18​π​1ζz​b⟂​e−ζz​b⟂2.18𝜋superscriptsubscript1𝑑𝑥𝑥superscript𝑒subscript𝜁𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to2𝑥18𝜋1subscript𝜁𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-tosuperscript𝑒subscript𝜁𝑧subscript𝑏perpendicular-to2displaystyle-frac18piint1inftyfracdxxe-fracsqrtzetazbperp2x+frac18pifrac1sqrtzetazbperpe-fracsqrtzetazbperp2 . Clearly, the first line corresponds to the standard power expansion at leading power and NLP, while the second line is exponential-small contributions that can not be cast into the form of a power expansion. They are caused by “tunneling” of hard momenta between the two spatially separated hard kernels at 00 and b→⟂subscript→𝑏perpendicular-tovecbperp. On the other hand, when Fourier transformed into k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp space, such exponentially small contributions will result in algebraically power-suppressed terms starting from power 1pz21superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑧2frac1pz2. This implies that the power expansion in b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp space and k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp space is not likely to be commuting starting at quadratic power. Moreover, compared to the case of 1D threshold-limit in Sec. , reconstruction of exponentially small terms from algebraic asymptotics for TMD expansion, due to the 2D nature, is less trivial even in the presence of support property (such as the Wightman-like current-current correlator for DY process, for which one has |q⟂|≤q0subscript𝑞perpendicular-tosuperscript𝑞0|qperp|leq q0 due to spectral condition). As such, the b⟂subscript𝑏perpendicular-tobperp space TMD expansion, due to the clean separation between “universal” algebraic terms and “non-universal” exponentially small terms, has the advantage in terms of distilling the massless scaling limit. On the other hand, the k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp space TMD expansion condenses more information into power-log terms at small k⟂subscript𝑘perpendicular-tokperp and has better phenomenological performance.

Here, we comment on the strength of the renormalon. Given the above equations, the Borel transform can be extracted and reads B​(u)=R​(u)+CF2​G0​(u)−G0′​​u−G0​u2,𝐵𝑢𝑅𝑢subscript𝐶𝐹2subscript𝐺0𝑢superscriptsubscript𝐺0′0𝑢subscript𝐺00superscript𝑢2displaystyle B(u)=R(u)+fracCF2fracG0(u)-G0primeu-G0u2 , G0​(u)=−e5​u3​u2​(u−1)​(2​u−1)​sin⁡π​u​(μ24​p02)u. subscript𝐺0𝑢superscript𝑒5𝑢3𝑢2𝑢12𝑢1𝜋𝑢superscriptsuperscript𝜇24superscriptsubscript𝑝02𝑢displaystyle G0(u)=-fracefrac5u3u2(u-1)(2u-1)sinpi uleft(fracmu24p02right)u . The residue at u=12𝑢12u=frac12 reads B​(u→12)=μp0​CF​e564​(u−12). 𝐵→𝑢12𝜇subscript𝑝0subscript𝐶𝐹superscript𝑒564𝑢12displaystyle Bbigg(urightarrowfrac12bigg)=fracmup0fracCFefrac564(u-frac12) . Compared with the case for heavy-light Sudakov hard kernel (Eq. ), the linear renormalon moves to the real part, but in the absence of a factor of π𝜋pi and enhanced by a factor of π2𝜋2fracpi2. Currently, we do not have a physical understanding of this linear renormalon, but the different transcendentality implies that the physical origin for the linear renormalons in Coulomb and axial gauges might be different.
content_sections.6 Discussion and conclusion: Before ending the paper, we would like to make two comments. First, the phase angle in the axial gauge can be heuristically explained by collinear quark exposed in an “ imaginary potential” g​Az​(t,x→⟂)∼i​V​(t2−x→⟂2)∼i​g2t2−x→⟂2similar-to𝑔superscript𝐴𝑧𝑡subscript→𝑥perpendicular-to𝑖𝑉superscript𝑡2subscriptsuperscript→𝑥2perpendicular-tosimilar-to𝑖superscript𝑔2superscript𝑡2superscriptsubscript→𝑥perpendicular-to2gAz(t,vecxperp)sim iV(sqrtt2-vecx2perp)simfracig2sqrtt2-vecxperp2 created by the space-like gauge link, which is nothing but the analytical continuation of the standard linear potential into the time-like region t>x→⟂𝑡subscript→𝑥perpendicular-tot>vecxperp. Since the linear potential is known to suffer from the mass renormalon, δ​V∼δ​msimilar-to𝛿𝑉𝛿𝑚delta Vsimdelta m, one expects that correction to the “quark field renormalization” in the external field g​Az∼i​δ​msimilar-to𝑔superscript𝐴𝑧𝑖𝛿𝑚gAzsim idelta m takes the form i​p̸​δ​m​γz0−2​pz​12​pz​u​(p)=−i​δ​m2​pz​u​(p),𝑖italic-p̸𝛿𝑚superscript𝛾𝑧02superscript𝑝𝑧12superscript𝑝𝑧𝑢𝑝𝑖𝛿𝑚2superscript𝑝𝑧𝑢𝑝displaystyle ifracnotpdelta mgammaz0-2pzfrac12pzu(p)=-ifracdelta m2pzu(p) , which corresponds to the old-fashioned diagram where an anti-quark is created at t=0𝑡0t=0 and annihilates with the incoming quark at a large time t>|x→⟂|>0𝑡subscript→𝑥perpendicular-to0t>|vecxperp|>0. Second, we should mention that the observed slow convergence for the imaginary part in lattice extraction of Collins Soper kernelcitebib.bib64, bib.bib65 can be explained by this renormalon. We hope that by using some form of Borel-resummed hard kernel taking into account the renormalon asymptotics, numerical uncertainties related to imaginary parts can be reduced and the convergence speed of perturbative matching can be improved. In our preliminary numerical test, reduction in the imaginary part has indeed been observed, see Appendix. To summarize, we found that the common hard kernel for double-logarithmic factorization formulas in the context of lattice parton distributions, the heavy-light Sudakov hard kernel or axial gauge quark field renormalization, has linear renormalon ambiguity in its imaginary part. In the bubble chain or large β0subscript𝛽0beta0 approximation, the strength of the renormalon ambiguity coincides with that for heavy-quark pole mass or heavy Wilson-line linear divergence. For both the threshold expansion of quasi-PDF matching kernel or the TMD expansion of quasi-LFWF amplitude, the linear renormalon anticipates the presence of linear power correction and correlates with UV renormalon at NLP. The quark wave function renormalization in the Coulomb gauge also suffers linear renormalon. Acknowledgements. We thank Prof. Xiangdong Ji for discussion. Y. Liu thanks Prof. Vladimir M. Braun for helpful discussion. Calculations in the work are cross-checked between two authors. Y. sorted_papers.title:
Determination of the strong coupling constant and the Collins-Soper
  kernel from the energy-energy correlator in $e^+e^-$ collisions
sorted_papers.abstract:
We have conducted the first simultaneous global fit of the strong coupling
constant $\alpha_s$ and the Collins-Soper (CS) kernel using the energy-energy
correlators (EEC) of $e^{+} e^{-}$ collisions in the back-to-back limit. This
analysis, based on the transverse-momentum-dependent (TMD) factorization of EEC
at next-to-next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic ($\rm{N}^{3} \rm{LL}$) accuracy,
yields $\alpha_s$ consistent with the world average. We have tested two
different parametrizations for the non-perturbative CS kernel and found both to
align with results obtained from lattice QCD and fits on semi-inclusive deep
inelastic scattering and Drell-Yan process.

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究TMD因子化与Collins-Soper演化 — 状态：满足。证据：“基于TMD因子化”、“CS核” 对比 “Collins–Soper演化和TMD因子化”  
- C2：使用e⁺e⁻碰撞中的能量-能量关联（EEC） — 状态：满足。证据：“EEC of e⁺e⁻ collisions” 对比 “TMD因子化”  
- C3：在高精度（N³LL）下分析 — 状态：满足。证据：“N³LL精度” 对比 “TMD因子化”

sorted_papers.title: Factorization connecting continuum and lattice TMDs
sorted_papers.abstract:
Transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDs) can be
studied from first principles by a perturbative matching onto
lattice-calculable quantities: so-called lattice TMDs, which are a class of
equal-time correlators that includes quasi-TMDs and TMDs in the
Lorentz-invariant approach. We introduce a general correlator that includes as
special cases these two Lattice TMDs and continuum TMDs, like the Collins
scheme. Then, to facilitate the derivation of a factorization relation between
lattice and continuum TMDs, we construct a new scheme, the Large Rapidity (LR)
scheme, intermediate between the Collins and quasi-TMDs. The LR and Collins
schemes differ only by an order of limits, and can be matched onto one another
by a multiplicative kernel. We show that this same matching also holds between
quasi and Collins TMDs, which enables us to prove a factorization relation
between these quantities to all orders in $\alpha_s$. Our results imply that
there is no mixing between various quark flavors or gluons when matching
Collins and quasi TMDs, making the lattice calculation of individual flavors
and gluon TMDs easier than anticipated. We cross-check these results explicitly
at one loop and discuss implications for other physical-to-lattice scheme
factorizations.

sorted_papers.title: Determination of the Collins-Soper Kernel from Lattice QCD
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present lattice results for the non-perturbative Collins-Soper (CS)
kernel, which describes the energy-dependence of transverse momentum-dependent
parton distributions (TMDs). The CS kernel is extracted from the ratios of
first Mellin moments of quasi-TMDs evaluated at different nucleon momenta.The
analysis is done with dynamical $N_f=2+1$ clover fermions for the CLS ensemble
H101 ($a=0.0854\,\mathrm{fm}$, $m_{\pi}=m_K=422\,\mathrm{MeV}$). The computed
CS kernel is in good agreement with experimental extractions and previous
lattice studies.

sorted_papers.title: Several Topics on Transverse Momentum-Dependent Fragmentation Functions
sorted_papers.abstract:
The hadronization of a high-energy parton is described by fragmentation
functions which are introduced through QCD factorizations. While the
hadronization mechanism per se remains uknown, fragmentation functions can
still be investigated qualitatively and quantitatively. The qualitative study
mainly concentrates on extracting genuine features based on the operator
definition in quantum field theory. The quantitative research focuses on
describing a variety of experimental data employing the fragmentation function
given by the parameterizations or model calculations. With the foundation of
the transverse-momentum-dependent factorization, the QCD evolution of leading
twist transverse-momentum-dependent fragmentation functions has also been
established. In addition, the universality of fragmentation functions has been
proven, albeit model-dependently, so that it is possible to perform a global
analysis of experimental data in different high-energy reactions. The
collective efforts may eventually reveal important information hidden in the
shadow of nonperturbative physics. This review covers the following topics:
transverse-momentum-dependent factorization and the corresponding QCD
evolution, spin-dependent fragmentation functions at leading and higher twists,
several experimental measurements and corresponding phenomenological studies,
and some model calculations.

sorted_papers.title:
Angular distributions of Drell-Yan leptons in the TMD factorization
  approach
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a comprehensive study of the angular structure functions for
Drell-Yan leptons in $Z/\gamma$-boson production within the framework of the
transverse momentum dependent (TMD) factorization theorem, including kinematic
power corrections (KPCs). We find good agreement with the data in the
applicability region of the TMD factorization theorem. The inclusion of KPCs
allows us to describe all angular coefficients in a frame-independent manner
using only the leading-twist TMD distributions: the unpolarized and the
Boer-Mulders functions. The value of the Boer-Mulders function is determined
using the ATLAS measurement of the $A_2$ angular coefficient. The analysis is
performed at N$^4$LL perturbative order. Additionally, we discuss the technical
implementation and impact of KPCs on the phenomenology of TMD distributions.

sorted_papers.title:
The resolution to the problem of consistent large transverse momentum in
  TMDs
sorted_papers.abstract:
Parametrizing TMD parton densities and fragmentation functions in ways that
consistently match their large transverse momentum behavior in standard
collinear factorization has remained notoriously difficult. We show how the
problem is solved in a recently introduced set of steps for combining
perturbative and nonperturbative transverse momentum in TMD factorization.
Called a ``bottom-up'' approach in a previous article, here we call it a
``hadron structure oriented'' (HSO) approach to emphasize its focus on
preserving a connection to the TMD parton model interpretation. We show that
the associated consistency constraints improve considerably the agreement
between parametrizations of TMD functions and their large-$k_T$ behavior, as
calculated in collinear factorization. The procedure discussed herein will be
important for guiding future extractions of TMD parton densities and
fragmentation functions and for testing TMD factorization and universality. We
illustrate the procedure with an application to semi-inclusive deep inelastic
scattering (SIDIS) structure functions at an input scale $Q_0$, and we show
that there is improved consistency between different methods of calculating at
moderate transverse momentum. We end with a discussion of plans for future
phenomenological applications.

sorted_papers.title: A Better Angle on Hadron Transverse Momentum Distributions at the EIC
sorted_papers.abstract:
We propose an observable $q_*$ sensitive to transverse momentum dependence
(TMD) in $e N \to e h X$, with $q_*/E_N$ defined purely by lab-frame angles. In
3D measurements of confinement and hadronization this resolves the crippling
issue of accurately reconstructing small transverse momentum $P_{hT}$. We prove
factorization for $\mathrm{d} \sigma_h / \mathrm{d}q_*$ for $q_*\ll Q$ with
standard TMD functions, enabling $q_*$ to substitute for $P_{hT}$. A
double-angle reconstruction method is given which is exact to all orders in QCD
for $q_*\ll Q$. $q_*$ enables an order-of-magnitude improvement in the expected
experimental resolution at the EIC.

sorted_papers.title:
Single inclusive particle production at next-to-leading order in
  proton-nucleus collisions at forward rapidities: hybrid approach meets TMD
  factorization
sorted_papers.abstract:
We revisit the calculation of the cross section for forward inclusive single
hadron production in $pA$ collisions within the hybrid approach. We show that
the proper framework to perform this calculation beyond leading order is not
the collinear factorization, as has been assumed so far, but the TMD factorized
framework. Within the TMD factorized approach we show that all the large
transverse logarithms appearing in the fixed order calculation, are resummed
into the evolution of the TMD PDFs and TMD FFs with factorization scale. The
resulting expressions, when written in terms of TMDs evolved to the
appropriate, physically well understood factorization scale, contain no
additional large logarithms. The absence of any large logarithms in the
resummed result should ensure positivity of the cross section and eradicate the
persistent problem that have plagued the previous attempts at calculating this
observable in the hybrid approach.
