Also, as mentioned earlier, a new fitting including the threshold effect is required for a higher precision theoretical result. Thus, the all-order resummation formula reads d4​σSIDISd​y​d2​𝒒T​d​τSIDIS=σ0DIS​∫CNd​N2​π​i​(τSIDIS)−Nsuperscriptd4superscript𝜎SIDISd𝑦superscriptd2subscript𝒒𝑇dsuperscript𝜏SIDISsuperscriptsubscript𝜎0DISsubscriptsubscript𝐶𝑁d𝑁2𝜋𝑖superscriptsuperscript𝜏SIDIS𝑁displaystylefracd4sigmaSIDISdyd2qTdtauSIDIS=sigma0DISintCNfracdN2pi ileft(tauSIDISright)-N ∫d​bT​bT2​π​J0​(qT​bT)​HSIDIS​(Q,Q)dsubscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇2𝜋subscript𝐽0subscript𝑞𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝐻SIDIS𝑄𝑄displaystyleintfracdbT,bT2piJ0(qTbT)HSIDISleft(Q,Qright) ×∑qeq2fq/h1TTMD(N,bT,Q)Dh2/qTTMD(N,bT,Q). displaystyletimessumqeq2tildefq/h1,TTMD(N,bT,Q)tildeDh2/qTTMD(N,bT,Q). Finally, we move on to the factorization and resummation for the inclusive back-to-back two hadron production in e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collision, i. e. e++e−→γ∗​(q)→h1​(P1)+h2​(P2)+X,→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscript𝛾𝑞→subscriptℎ1subscript𝑃1subscriptℎ2subscript𝑃2𝑋e++e-rightarrowgamma*(q)rightarrow h1(P1)+h2(P2)+X, where Piμsuperscriptsubscript𝑃𝑖𝜇Pimu is the momentum of the hadron hisubscriptℎ𝑖hi, and the threshold variable in this process is defined by τe+​e−≡(P1+P2)2Q2,superscript𝜏superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝑃1subscript𝑃22superscript𝑄2taue+e-equivfrac(P1+P2)2Q2, with Q2=q2superscript𝑄2superscript𝑞2Q2=q2. As expected, in the joint limit, the cross section is factorized as the product of hard factor and two TMD FFs that describe final-state di-hadron production. Besides, the RG consistence also implies the Collins-Soper scale is the same as that in the Drell-Yan and SIDIS processes. Therefore, we obtain the following all-order resummation formula. d3​σe+​e−d2​𝒒T​d​τe+​e−=σ0e+​e−​∫CNd​N2​π​i​(τe+​e−)−Nsuperscriptd3superscript𝜎superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscriptd2subscript𝒒𝑇dsuperscript𝜏superscript𝑒superscript𝑒superscriptsubscript𝜎0superscript𝑒superscript𝑒subscriptsubscript𝐶𝑁d𝑁2𝜋𝑖superscriptsuperscript𝜏superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝑁displaystylefracd3sigmae+e-d2qTdtaue+e-=sigma0e+e-intCNfracdN2pi ileft(taue+e-right)-N ∫d​bT​bT2​π​J0​(qT​bT)​He+​e−​(Q,Q)dsubscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇2𝜋subscript𝐽0subscript𝑞𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝐻superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝑄𝑄displaystyleintfracdbT,bT2piJ0(qTbT)He+e-left(Q,Qright) ×∑qeq2Dh1/qTTMD(N,bT,Q)Dh2/q¯TTMD(N,bT,Q),displaystyletimessumqeq2tildeDh1/qTTMD(N,bT,Q)tildeDh2/barqTTMD(N,bT,Q), where σ0e+​e−=4​π​αem2/Q2superscriptsubscript𝜎0superscript𝑒superscript𝑒4𝜋superscriptsubscript𝛼em2superscript𝑄2sigma0e+e-=4pialpharm em2/Q2 is the Born cross section and He+​e−superscript𝐻superscript𝑒superscript𝑒He+e- is the corresponding hard factor, and the definition of the threshold-TMD FFs at the scale Q𝑄Q is given in . From now on, we have obtained the all-order resummation formula for Drell-Yan, SIDIS and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- processes, and also find a close correspondence between them. In the joint limit, the cross section is factorized as the product of the hard factor and threshold-TMD functions, including TMD PDFs and FFs. Such a property of universality is significant in the future global fitting analysis for threshold-TMD functions in different processes. To summarize this section, we give the following generic resummation structure for all three processes as d3​σAd2​𝒒T​d​τA=σ0A​∫CNd​N2​π​i​(τA)−Nsuperscriptd3superscript𝜎Asuperscriptd2subscript𝒒𝑇dsuperscript𝜏Asuperscriptsubscript𝜎0Asubscriptsubscript𝐶𝑁d𝑁2𝜋𝑖superscriptsuperscript𝜏A𝑁displaystylefracd3sigmaAd2qTdtauA=sigma0AintCNfracdN2pi ileft(tauAright)-N ∫d​bT​bT2​π​J0​(qT​bT)​HA​(Q,Q)dsubscript𝑏𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇2𝜋subscript𝐽0subscript𝑞𝑇subscript𝑏𝑇superscript𝐻A𝑄𝑄displaystyleintfracdbT,bT2piJ0(qTbT)Hrm Aleft(Q,Qright) ×∑qeq2ℱi,h1TTMD(N,bT,Q)ℱi,h2TTMD(N,bT,Q),displaystyletimessumqeq2tildeFi,h1TTMD(N,bT,Q)tildeFi,h2TTMD(N,bT,Q), where for A=DYADYrm A=rm DY; ℱi,h1TTMD=fq/h1TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ1TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑓𝑞subscriptℎ1TTMDtildeFi,h1TTMD=tildefq/h1,TTMD, ℱi,h2TTMD=fq¯/h2TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ2TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑓¯𝑞subscriptℎ2TTMDtildeFi,h2TTMD=tildefbarq/h2,TTMD, for A=SIDISASIDISrm A=SIDIS; ℱi,h1TTMD=fq/h1TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ1TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑓𝑞subscriptℎ1TTMDtildeFi,h1TTMD=tildefq/h1,TTMD, ℱi,h2TTMD=Dh2/qTTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ2TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝐷subscriptℎ2𝑞TTMDtildeFi,h2TTMD=tildeDh2/q,TTMD, and for A=e+​e−Asuperscript𝑒superscript𝑒rm A=e+e-; ℱi,h1TTMD=Dh1/qTTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ1TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝐷subscriptℎ1𝑞TTMDtildeFi,h1TTMD=tildeDh1/q,TTMD, ℱi,h2TTMD=Dh2/q¯TTMDsuperscriptsubscriptℱ𝑖subscriptℎ2TTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝐷subscriptℎ2¯𝑞TTMDtildeFi,h2TTMD=tildeDh2/barq,TTMD and the Born cross sections σ0Asuperscriptsubscript𝜎0Asigma0rm A and hard function HAsuperscript𝐻AHrm A are defined accordingly. Here in addition to τ𝜏tau, the Born cross section σ0subscript𝜎0sigma0 can also depend on additional variables, for example, σ0SIDISsuperscriptsubscript𝜎0SIDISsigma0rm SIDIS depends on the inelasticity y𝑦y as shown in . This structure holds in the all-order QCD resummation formula.
content_sections.3 Numerical results: In this section, we numerically study the threshold effect in TMD PDF and TMD FF using the factorization formula of Drell-Yan, SIDIS, and the back-to-back di-hadron production via the e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- annihilation process. We apply these extracted TMD functions to provide transverse momentum distributions for these three processes for different experimental kinematics. Throughout our numerical analysis, we use one loop strong coupling constant (αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas). The fine structure constant (αemsubscript𝛼emalpharm em) is taken to be 1/13711371/137 and the number of active quark flavor nf=5subscript𝑛𝑓5nf=5 in the massless limit. For the TMD and collinear evolution, we choose our initial scale (μF)subscript𝜇𝐹(muF) to be 1.31.31.3 GeV. As mentioned earlier, threshold factorization has been done in the Mellin space because, in this space, the convolutions become a simple product. After achieving the factorization formula in Mellin space, we need to do the Mellin inversion to achieve results in the x𝑥x space. During our numerical study, we observe that one needs to modify the Collins-Soper scale in order to achieve results that are consistence throughout the allowed kinematic region of τ𝜏tau. This modified Collins-Soper scale will introduce new poles in the Mellin inversion formula. We discuss these issues in detail in the next subsection and present a prescription for Mellin inversion.
content_sections.3 Numerical results 3.1 Modified Collins: In the NLL resummation formula, the Collins-Soper scale in the non-perturbative factor SNPsubscript𝑆NPSrm NP is given by Q/N¯𝑄¯𝑁Q/barN. When the moment variable N𝑁N becomes very large, ζfTTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑓TTMDzetafrm TTMD goes down to the nonperturbative scale Q02superscriptsubscript𝑄02Q02, which violates the power counting, Q≫Q​(1−τ)≫qTmuch-greater-than𝑄𝑄1𝜏much-greater-thansubscript𝑞𝑇Qgg Q(1-hattau)gg qT, in the factorization theorem. Therefore, we need to freeze the Collins-Soper scale as ζN,f<Q02subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝑄02zetaN,f<Q02 ζ∗≡ζ∗​(ζN,f,Q0)=(QN¯)2​(1+Q02​N¯2Q2),subscript𝜁subscript𝜁subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓subscript𝑄0superscript𝑄¯𝑁21superscriptsubscript𝑄02superscript¯𝑁2superscript𝑄2displaystylezeta*equivzeta*(zetaN,f,Q0)=left(fracQbarNright)2left(1+fracQ02barN2Q2right), which reproduces the resummation formula in the moment variable N𝑁N as long as Q/N¯≫Q0much-greater-than𝑄¯𝑁subscript𝑄0Q/barNgg Q0. Besides, it also allows us to perform a straightforward numerical calculation in the complex-N𝑁N plane. We leave the investigation of different function forms and corresponding theoretical uncertainties for future studies. To implement the inverse Mellin transformation, one often rewrites the standard textbook Mellin inversion citebib.bib71 as an integration over a real variable with adjustable contour: φ​(x)=1π​∫0∞dz​Im​[exp​(i​ϕ)​x−c−z​exp⁡(i​ϕ)​φn=c+z​exp⁡(i​ϕ)]. 𝜑𝑥1𝜋superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑧Imdelimited-exp𝑖italic-ϕsuperscript𝑥𝑐𝑧𝑖italic-ϕsubscript𝜑𝑛𝑐𝑧𝑖italic-ϕvarphi(x)=frac1piintnolimits0inftydz Imleft[exp(iphi)x-c-zexp(iphi)varphin=c+zexp(iphi)right]. Then contour is characterized by a real number c𝑐c, which should be the right to the rightmost singularity of φnsubscript𝜑𝑛varphin, and an arbitrary angle ϕitalic-ϕphi as shown in Figure . The parametrization forms we chose are certain simple combinations of power functions, so they only have poles on the real axis. In this regard, one can always find a suitable c𝑐c and may try various ϕitalic-ϕphi to reach higher numerical efficiency. There are different Mellin inversion prescriptions available in the literature citebib.bib72, bib.bib71. However, when we modify the Collins-Soper scale term from ζfsubscript𝜁𝑓zetaf to ζ∗subscript𝜁zeta*, we find that this modified term would contribute two new poles n=−c±i​Q¯Q0,𝑛plus-or-minus𝑐𝑖¯𝑄subscript𝑄0n=-cpm ifracbarQQ0, with Q¯≡eγE​Q. ¯𝑄superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸𝑄barQequiv egammaEQ. These two singularities would cause numerical issues even when the contour might not exactly pass the poles for different Q¯/Q0¯𝑄subscript𝑄0barQ/Q0. For simplicity, we chose ϕ=π/2italic-ϕ𝜋2phi=pi/2 for our contour, which is safe no matter what the Q𝑄Q value is. Also, we choose c=1.6𝑐1.6c=1.6 to avoid all the other poles. Numerically, we vary the value of ϕitalic-ϕphi and c𝑐c and find the results are stable and do not depend, within errors, on the particular choice of input parameters.
content_sections.3 Numerical results 3.2 Threshold-TMD PD: In this subsection, we present the numerical results for threshold-improved TMD PDFs and FFs. To investigate the impact of threshold resummation, we compare the TMD PDFs with and without threshold resummation in Figure, which shows the transverse momentum distributions of TMD PDFs for the up (u𝑢u) quark in the proton. We vary the value of x𝑥x from 0.10.10.1 to 0.80.80.8 at 555 GeV. The green curves represent the results obtained without threshold resummation, corresponding to the case where ζN,f=Q2subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscript𝑄2zetaN,f=Q2 in . The red and blue curves correspond to the results obtained with threshold resummation using the ζ∗subscript𝜁zeta* scheme and naive replacement ζN,f=Q2/N¯2subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscript𝑄2superscript¯𝑁2zetaN,f=Q2/barN2, respectively. In the low x𝑥x region, all three curves exhibit consistent behavior since the threshold effect is mild. However, as we increase the value of x𝑥x, the threshold effects become evident. Especially, it is noteworthy that in the naive scheme the theoretical predictions for the distribution rapidly decrease to zero and then turn negative. Consequently, we conclude that the ζ∗subscript𝜁zeta* prescription is necessary to ensure reliable theoretical predictions. In Figure, we present the threshold improved TMD PDF for u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks. We make the use of CT18NNLO citebib.bib73 parameterized colinear PDF set at a given initial scale μF=1.3subscript𝜇𝐹1.3muF=1.3 GeV. The upper panel is for the u𝑢u quark and the bottom panel is for the d𝑑d quark. We present our results for three different choices of hard scales; Q=2.0,5.0,10.0𝑄2.05.010.0Q=2.0,5.0,10.0 GeV with three different choices of Bjorken scales x=0.4,0.6,0.8𝑥0.40.60.8x=0.4,0.6,0.8, from left to the right respectively. In the threshold region when the hadronic threshold variable is in the order of 111, the Bjorken scales are also in the order of 111. We present our results for a large Bjorken scale to highlight the threshold effect in this region. The uncertainty bands correspond to the 1-σ𝜎sigma variation from CT18NNLO PDFs using the Hessian method. The details of these uncertainties are given in appendix . In Figure, we present threshold improved TMD fragmentation function of Pion (π+superscript𝜋pi+ hadron). In this case, we use the parameterized JAM20 fragmentation function citebib.bib74 at the scale μF=1.3subscript𝜇𝐹1.3muF=1.3 GeV. Similar to Figure, the upper panel is for the up-type and bottom panel is for the down-type quark fragmentation functions and z𝑧z is the fragmentation variable. Here, we present our results for three different values of hard scale (Q𝑄Q) with three different values of fragmentation (z𝑧z) variables. The uncertainty bands correspond to 1-σ𝜎sigma variation of JAM FFs using the replica method. At this point, we would like to emphasize that the direct comparison between the usual TMD functions and threshold-improved TMD functions is not trivial. In our theoretical formalism, we evolve the TMD evolution factor from the initial scale μFsubscript𝜇𝐹muF to the desired final scale. On the other hand, in the standard TMD evolution, one starts from scale μb∗superscriptsubscript𝜇𝑏mub*. Besides, both perturbative and nonperturbative parts in the Collins-Soper evolution are also different in these two frameworks. Because of this different evolution scheme, it is not straightforward to compare them and we leave such a study for future investigation.
content_sections.3 Numerical results 3.3 Predictions for: Finally, we use previously determined threshold-improved TMD functions (PDFs and FFs) to predict some experimental results. For this, we investigate three different processes namely, DY, SIDIS, and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- annihilation process. For the DY process, we consider dilepton transverse momentum distribution from proton-proton collision. We choose the hadronic center of mass energy to be s=15.0𝑠15.0sqrts=15.0 GeV and Q=6.0𝑄6.0Q=6.0 GeV, which is relevant to the Drell-Yan production at typical Fermilab experiments. The results are presented in the left panel of Figure . For the e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- annihilation process, we consider Pion (π+superscript𝜋pi+) in the final hadronic state. We choose the virtuality of the photon to be Q=10.58𝑄10.58Q=10.58 GeV and the threshold variable τ=0.64𝜏0.64tau=0.64 for the BELLE citebib.bib75 kinematics. The results are presented in the right panel of Figure . The uncertainties in all the cross sections are coming from the threshold-TMD functions which are computed from the 1-σ𝜎sigma variation of PDF and FF using Hessian and Replica methods respectively. As shown in the previous subsection, the uncertainty from FFs is larger than that from PDFs at a similar value of the light-cone momentum fraction making a wider uncertainty band in the e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- process than in the Drell-Yan process. Finally, in Figure , we present the results for the SIDIS process. In this case, we make use of four experiments kinematics namely HERMES citebib.bib76, COMPASS citebib.bib77, the future Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) citebib.bib3 at Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the 121212 GeV programcitebib.bib78 currently underway at Jefferson Lab (JLab). The upper left panel is for HERMES kinematics of e​p𝑒𝑝ep collision with hard scale Q2=4superscript𝑄24Q2=4 GeV and threshold parameter τ=0.24𝜏0.24tau=0.24 and the upper right panel is for COMPASS kinematics with hard scale Q2=20superscript𝑄220Q2=20 GeV and threshold parameter τ=0.32𝜏0.32tau=0.32. In the lower panel, we present the results for EIC (left, Q2=50superscript𝑄250Q2=50 GeV and τ=0.4𝜏0.4tau=0.4) and JLab 12 GeV program (right, Q2=3superscript𝑄23Q2=3 GeV and τ=0.48𝜏0.48tau=0.48) kinematics. To conclude this section, we notice that although the future EIC will make the most precise SIDIS measurements at small x𝑥x (down to x∼10−4similar-to𝑥superscript104xsim 10-4), the EIC will also increase the precision of the data in the large-x𝑥x region up to x∼0.5similar-to𝑥0.5xsim 0.5. The JLab 12 GeV program will make precision measurements up to x∼0.6similar-to𝑥0.6xsim 0.6 and smaller values of Q2superscript𝑄2Q2. We expect both EIC and JLab 12 to make important constraints on these threshold TMD PDFs and TMD FFs in the near future.
content_sections.4 Conclusion: In this paper, we have introduced the threshold TMD functions namely, PDFs and FFs. To probe this threshold effect, we use three different processes: Drell-Yan, SIDIS and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- processes. Considering the extra degrees of freedom in this kinematical region known as collinear-soft degrees of freedom, we re-factorized the formula for these processes and defined threshold-improved TMD distribution functions. We observe that because of the phase space reduction in the threshold region, the Collins-Soper scale is not the same for the usual TMD and threshold-improved TMD distribution functions. We find that ζN,f=Q2/N¯2subscript𝜁𝑁𝑓superscript𝑄2superscript¯𝑁2zetaN,f=Q2/barN2 for threshold TMD whereas ζf=Q2subscript𝜁𝑓superscript𝑄2zetaf=Q2 for the usual TMD. During our numerical analysis, we observe that apart from the RG consistency, the Collins-Soper scale has to be chosen in such a way that it is always greater than the non-perturbative scale Q0subscript𝑄0Q0. Therefore, one needs to modify the Collins-Soper scale, and this modified scale introduces a new kind of pole in the integration contour. We provide a Mellin inversion prescription to avoid all kinds of poles in the integration contour. Finally, we provide the numerical predictions for transverse momentum distributions of Drell-Yan, SIDIS and e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- processes for different experimental kinematics using these threshold-improved TMD functions. Our theoretical formalism would open a new window into TMD physics. Future experimental analysis and global fitting analysis will certainly help in understanding the non-perturbative mechanism of the TMD functions in the threshold limit and unveiling the three-dimensional picture of a hadron in the large x𝑥x limit. Our formalism will be important to extract these TMD functions in the threshold region and will be reliable theoretical input to understand the experimental data. In this work, we only present the factorization and resummation formula for the unpolarized cross section, but it can be generalized to the process involving polarized hadron in both initial and final states. The corresponding theoretical predictions on the spin asymmetry in the threshold limit will be explored in future work. Acknowledgements. We thank Hui-Chun Chin, Jun Gao, Dianyu Liu, Zekun Lou and Yiyu Zhou for their useful discussions. Z. K. is supported by the National Science Foundation under grant No. PHY-1945471. K. S. and D. Y. S. are supported by the National Science Foundations of China under Grant No.12275052 and No.12147101 and the Shanghai Natural Science Foundation under Grant No.21ZR1406100. Y. Z. is supported by the Tsung-Dao Lee Chinese Undergraduate Research Endowment under Grant No.202210246155.

all_papers.title:
Transformation of transverse momentum distributions from Parton
  Branching to Collins-Soper-Sterman framework
all_papers.abstract:
Two main frameworks for defining transverse momentum dependent (TMD) parton
densities are the Collins-Soper-Sterman (CSS) formalism, and the Parton
Branching (PB) approach. While PB-TMDs have an explicit dependence on a single
scale which is used to evolve PB-TMDs in momentum space, TMDs defined in CSS
formalism present a double-scale evolution in renormalization and rapidity
scales, via a pair of coupled evolution equations. In this letter I leverage
the Collins-Soper kernel determined from simulated Drell Yan transverse
momentum spectra using PB-TMDs, and provide, for the first time, the
transformation of TMD parton distributions from the PB framework to the CSS
formalism. The evolved PB-TMDs in $b$-space are compared to the recently
released, unpolarized TMD distribution ART23.

all_papers.title: Factorization for quasi-TMD distributions of sub-leading power
all_papers.abstract:
The quasi-transverse-momentum dependent (qTMD) distributions are equal-time
correlators that can be computed within the lattice QCD approach. In the regime
of large hadron's momentum, qTMD distributions are expressed in terms of
standard TMD distributions via the factorization theorem. We derive the
corresponding factorization theorem at the next-leading power (NLP), and, for
the first time, we present the factorized expressions for a large class of qTMD
distributions of sub-leading power. The NLP expression contains TMD
distributions of twist-two, twist-three, and a new lattice-specific
nonperturbative function. We point out that some of the qTMD distributions
considered in this work can be employed to extract the Collins-Soper kernel
using the standard techniques of different-momenta-ratio. We provide NLO
expressions for all the elements of the factorization theorem. Also, for the
first time, we explicitly demonstrate the restoration of boost invariance in
NLP TMD factorization.

sections.7: Cichy, Overview of lattice calculations of the x-dependence of PDFs, GPDs and TMDs, EPJ Web Conf
sections.19: Collins, Foundations of perturbative QCD, vol
sections.22: Abbott, The Background Field Method Beyond One Loop, Nucl
sections.30: Jaffe, Parton Distribution Functions for Twist Four, Nucl
sections.48: Vladimirov, Structure of rapidity divergences in multi-parton scattering soft factors, JHEP 04 (2018) 045 [1707.07606].
sections.55: Collins, Leading twist single transverse-spin asymmetries: Drell-Yan and deep inelastic scattering, Phys

qualitative: The factorization theorem for quasi-TMD (qTMD) distributions at next-to-leading power (NLP) connects physical transverse-momentum dependent (TMD) distributions of twist-two and twist-three, along with a new lattice-specific nonperturbative function.
The NLP factorization theorem restores boost invariance through the cancellation of special rapidity divergences, which is explicitly demonstrated for the first time in this work.

qTMD distributions at sub-leading power can be used to extract the Collins-Soper kernel via ratios of observables at different momentum scales, leveraging the universality of the kernel and cancellation of hadronic components.
The Collins-Soper kernel parametrizes properties of the QCD vacuum and is a universal function, enabling combination of data from different experiments to improve statistical precision.

The TMD operator expansion method is employed as the primary tool for deriving the NLP factorization theorem, providing a systematic framework for handling nonperturbative and perturbative contributions.
At NLP, the singularity structure differs from leading power, requiring careful treatment of explicit poles and rapidity divergences to ensure consistency with physical expectations.

The NLP factorization theorem is applicable to a large class of qTMD distributions, including those with Dirac matrices projecting good and bad quark spinor components (Γ ∈ Γ_T), which are typically power-suppressed in collider experiments but accessible in lattice QCD.
quantitative: The qTMD correlator is defined as: Ω[Γ](y) ∼ ⟨P,S| q̄(y) [staple link] Γ q |P,S⟩, where y is a space-like distance and the staple link ensures gauge invariance.
Power counting: Ω[Γ₊] ∼ P⁰ (leading power), Ω[Γ_T] ∼ P⁻¹ (next-to-leading power), where P is the hadron momentum.
The NLP factorization theorem includes contributions from twist-two TMD distributions, twist-three TMD distributions, and a new lattice-specific nonperturbative function.
The factorization theorem is derived at next-to-leading order (NLO) in perturbation theory, with explicit NLO expressions provided for all coefficient functions and nonperturbative elements.
The cancellation of special rapidity divergences is demonstrated explicitly, which is essential for restoring boost invariance in the NLP TMD factorization framework.

The final factorized form of the qTMD correlator at NLP and NLO is presented in both position and momentum-fraction spaces, with renormalization and evolution properties of nonperturbative functions fully specified.

The Collins-Soper kernel can be extracted from ratios of qTMD distributions at different momentum scales, using the relation: K(Q₁, Q₂) = ln(Q₁/Q₂) × (d/dlnQ) ln[⟨Ω(Q₁)⟩ / ⟨Ω(Q₂)⟩], where Ω denotes the qTMD correlator.

content_sections.1 Introduction: The determination of parton distributions with lattice QCD simulations is a rapidly growing direction in the physics of strong iterations. Within the last decade, it has been raised from an abstract concept citebib.bib1, bib.bib2 to practical applications (see recent reviews citebib.bib3, bib.bib4). It is foreseeable that the lattice-parton studies will soon reach a similar precision level as the experimental fits. Most importantly, lattice simulations can access parton distributions that cannot be (or are too complicated to be) measured experimentally. This is especially true for higher-twist parton distributions citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7 and transverse-momentum dependent (TMD) distributions citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12. In this work, we push the formalism of factorization theorems for lattice correlators further and derive the factorization theorem for lattice TMD distributions (usually called quasi-TMD or qTMD distributions) at sub-leading power. The derived factorization theorem connects TMD distributions of twist-three and a large set of lattice observables. The qTMD correlator is a hadron matrix element of the form Ω[Γ]​(y)∼⟨P,S|q¯​(y)​[staple link]​Γ​q​|P,S⟩,similar-tosuperscriptΩdelimited-Γ𝑦quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆¯𝑞𝑦delimited-staple linkΓ𝑞0𝑃𝑆displaystyleOmega[Gamma](y)sim P,S|barq(y)[textstaple link]Gamma q|P,S, where y𝑦y is a space-like distance, and a staple-like gauge link connects the quark fields. The precise definition is given in sec. . At large P𝑃P, the qTMD correlator can be written in terms of physical TMD distributions. These relations are a particular form of the TMD factorization theorem widely used for the description of the TMD spectrum of semi-inclusive processes, for instance, citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. In the qTMD case, the form of the factorization theorem crucially depends on the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma that contracts the quark spinor indices. So, for Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+ that projects both spinors to their good components, one needs the leading-power (LP) TMD factorization theorem citebib.bib16, bib.bib17. This case is already well-developed theoretically citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. The next-difficulty case is Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT which projects a good and a bad components of quark spinor. Here one needs the next-to-leading power (NLP) TMD factorization theorem. The factorization theorem for this case is derived in this work for the first time. From the collider-experiment view-point, the ΓΓGamma-matrix is selected by the kinematic and the type of scattering process, and many polarized structures are accompanied by extra power-suppression factors making them especially difficult to access. In contrast, the lattice simulations could measure correlators with different ΓΓGamma’s without conceptual complications, and the power counting of different components of ΓΓGamma is plain (i. e. Ω[Γ+]∼P0similar-tosuperscriptΩdelimited-subscriptΓsuperscript𝑃0Omega[Gamma+]sim P0 and Ω[ΓT]∼P−1similar-tosuperscriptΩdelimited-subscriptΓ𝑇superscript𝑃1Omega[GammaT]sim P-1). As a matter of fact, the NLP components of qTMD correlators have been already computed. For example, within the Lorentz-invariant approaches citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23 one obtains qTMD correlator with all components of ΓΓGamma as a by-product of the computational technique. These components are usually discarded due to a lack of applicability. One of the primary motivations for this work was to find an application for these components. One can expect two possible applications for qTMD distributions. They can be used to determine physical TMD distributions or to extract the Collins-Soper kernel. The latter case is especially important since it is the simplest and yet very important. In contrast to TMD distributions that parametrize partons dynamics, the Collins-Soper kernel parametrizes properties of QCD vacuum citebib.bib24. Therefore, it can be accessed in the ratios of observables, where hadron components cancel entirely citebib.bib8, bib.bib25. Collins-Soper kernel is a universal function, and measurements from different sources can be combined together, multiplying the statistical precision citebib.bib26. As we demonstrate in this work, the direct determination of TMD distributions from qTMD distribution of sub-leading power is not feasible (at the present moment). Nonetheless, they allow for the extraction of the Collins-Soper kernel and thus provide a new source of information for this interesting observable. The NLP TMD factorization theorem is a relatively novel direction of research. The first steps were made in ref. citebib.bib27, but the systematic development started almost twenty years later citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33. Still, many aspects of the NLP TMD factorization beyond leading perturbative order (LO) are mysterious. Thus, another main motivation for this work was to develop the factorization for the qTMD correlator at NLO till the stage of application (that is not yet done for Drell-Yan, or Semi-Inclusive Deep-Inelastic Scattering (SIDIS), although NLO expressions at the operator level are known citebib.bib31, bib.bib33). Indeed, the expressions for the factorization theorem for the qTMD correlator are shorter but contain all principal structures. For the derivation of the factorization theorem, we use the TMD operator expansion method citebib.bib31, which is so far the most developed approach to NLP TMD factorization. For the first time, we explicitly demonstrate that by brining together all elements of NLP TMD factorization, one receives a valid expression satisfying all expected properties. It is not a trivial statement since the singularity structures of NLP and LP cases are different. The paper is organized as follows. In sec. we introduce the basic definitions and notation. In sec. we present the computation of the effective operator for the qTMD correlator in TMD factorization. Here we closely follow the method presented in ref. citebib.bib31. The NLO computation of coefficient functions is given in sec. . The main result of this section is the bare expression for TMD factorization at LP and NLP at NLO which is presented in secs. and in position and momentum-fraction spaces correspondingly. The bare expression is practically useless because it contains explicit and implicit singularities and unresolved complex structures. These problems are addressed one by one in sec. . In particular, in secs. , and , we provide the renormalization and evolution properties of the relevant nonperturbative functions and demonstrate the cancellation of explicit poles present in the bare version of the factorization theorem. In sec. we discuss so-called “special rapidity divergences” observed in ref. citebib.bib33. We explicitly demonstrate their cancellation and that this mechanism is responsible for restoring the boost invariance of TMD factorization at NLP. The complex structure of the expressions is discussed in sec. , in which the final form of the factorization theorem is presented. In sec. we discuss possible practical applications of the derived factorization formula. Specifically, we parametrize and write factorization theorems for individual measurable components of the qTMD correlator. The new and main results are collected in sec. . In appendix , we summarize for convenience the used parametrizations for physical TMD distributions of twist two and three.
content_sections.2 Definition of qTMD correlator: In this section we introduce the main definitions and conventions. Let us start with the defintion of the qTMD correlator: Ωq/hi​j​(y;μ)=ZW−1​(y,L,μ)​ZJ−2​(μ)​⟨P,S|q¯j​(y)​[y;y⟂+L​v]​[y⟂+L​v;L​v]​[L​v;0]​qi​|P,S⟩subscriptsuperscriptΩ𝑖𝑗𝑞ℎ𝑦𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝑍1𝑊𝑦𝐿𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝑍2𝐽𝜇quantum-operator-product𝑃𝑆superscript¯𝑞𝑗𝑦𝑦subscript𝑦perpendicular-to𝐿𝑣subscript𝑦perpendicular-to𝐿𝑣𝐿𝑣𝐿𝑣0superscript𝑞𝑖0𝑃𝑆displaystylewidetildeOmegaijq/h(y;mu)=Z-1W(y,L,mu)Z-2J(mu) P,S|barqj(y)[y;yperp+Lv][yperp+Lv;Lv][Lv;0]qi|P,S where |P,S⟩ket𝑃𝑆ketP,S is the hadron state with momentum P𝑃P and spin S𝑆S y⟂μ=yμ−vμ​(v​y)/v2superscriptsubscript𝑦perpendicular-to𝜇superscript𝑦𝜇superscript𝑣𝜇𝑣𝑦superscript𝑣2yperpmu=ymu-vmu(vy)/v2, and Z𝑍Z are the ultraviolet (UV) renormalization factors discussed below. In the convention of ref. citebib.bib19, the correlator ΩΩOmega is called the qTMD beam-function. The operator in eqn. is an equal-time operator, i. e. the time-components of vectors y𝑦y and v𝑣v are null. The indices i𝑖i and j𝑗j are the spinor indices of the quark fields. The notation [a​r,b​r]𝑎𝑟𝑏𝑟[ar,br] identifies a straight gauge link from b𝑏b to a𝑎a in the fundamental representation of S​U​(Nc)𝑆𝑈subscript𝑁𝑐SU(Nc): [a​r+x,b​r+x]=P​exp⁡(−i​g​∫ab𝑑s​rμ​Aμ,i​(s​r+x)​Ti),𝑎𝑟𝑥𝑏𝑟𝑥𝑃𝑖𝑔superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑏differential-d𝑠superscript𝑟𝜇subscript𝐴𝜇𝑖𝑠𝑟𝑥superscript𝑇𝑖displaystyle[ar+x,br+x]=Pexpleft(-igintabds rmuAmu,i(sr+x)Tiright), where r𝑟r is any vector and Tisuperscript𝑇𝑖Ti is the generator of S​U​(Nc)𝑆𝑈subscript𝑁𝑐SU(Nc) group. In eqn. , we do not specify the flavors of the quarks. These could be in a singlet or non-singlet combination. This choice does not modify the following computations and final results. For that reason we omit the subscript q/h𝑞ℎq/h in the following. It is important to mention that for the singlet quantum numbers we expect that the matrix element contains only the connected contribution, i. e. the contribution ∼⟨P,S|P,S⟩similar-toabsentinner-product𝑃𝑆𝑃𝑆sim P,S|P,S is subtracted. The qTMD correlator is equipped with renormalization factors that make it UV finite. For simplicity, we distinguish two UV renormalizations in eqn. . The factor ZW​(y,L,μ)subscript𝑍𝑊𝑦𝐿𝜇ZW(y,L,mu) is the renormalization of UV divergences associated with the gauge links. It includes the renormalization of linear divergences citebib.bib34 and remote cusps. It could also include the scheme factors for the transition from lattice to MS¯¯MSoverlinetextMS-scheme. The renormalization factors ZJsubscript𝑍𝐽ZJ renormalize quark fields (in axial gauge) . Let us stress that we distinguish the notions of qTMD correlator and qTMD distribution. The qTMD correlator is the object defined in eqn. , and it is the outcome of the lattice computation (up to renormalization factors). The qTMD distribution is defined in the sec. (eqn. ). It is defined such that it matches physical TMD distribution at LP/LO. From the perspective of lattice computation it involves and extra nonperturbative functions, which we refer to as ΨΨPsi-functions in following sections. These functions can be identified with quasi-TMD soft factors discussed in ref. citebib.bib19. In the limit of highly-energetic hadron and large L𝐿L, the qTMD correlator can be expressed in terms of standard TMD distributions citebib.bib35, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib19. In this work, we also consider the same limit. Specifically, we introduce light-cone directions n¯¯𝑛barn and n𝑛n identified with large and small components of the hadron’s momentum, correspondingly. One has Pμ=P+​n¯μ+M22​P+​nμ,superscript𝑃𝜇superscript𝑃superscript¯𝑛𝜇superscript𝑀22superscript𝑃superscript𝑛𝜇displaystyle Pmu=P+barnmu+fracM22P+nmu, where n2=n¯2=0superscript𝑛2superscript¯𝑛20n2=barn2=0, (n​n¯)=1𝑛¯𝑛1(nbarn)=1, and P2=M2superscript𝑃2superscript𝑀2P2=M2 is the mass of the hadron. The relative orientation of vectors n𝑛n and n¯¯𝑛barn is selected such that the vector v𝑣v belongs to the plane (n,n¯)𝑛¯𝑛(n,barn). content_sections.3 TMD operator expansion for qTMD operat: In this section, we derive the bare form of the factorization theorem for the qTMD correlator using the method of TMD operator expansion, and compute the (bare) coefficient functions at NLO. The derivation follows the one for the correlators of electro-magnetic currents presented in details in ref. citebib.bib31. Therefore, we skip most of the conceptual discussion and point out only the specific features of the qTMD case. The main result from the calculations presented in this section is the bare form of factorization theorem given in secs. and .

The procedure of the TMD operator expansion starts with the functional-integral formulation of the qTMD correlator. It can be easily done, since the operator in is an equal-time operator. We have Ωbare[Γ]​(y)superscriptsubscriptΩbaredelimited-Γ𝑦displaystylewidetildeOmegatextbare[Gamma](y) =displaystyle= ∫[D​q¯​D​q​D​A]​ei​SQCD​ϕ∗​(P,S)​J¯v​(y)​Γ2​Jv​​ϕ​(P,S),delimited-𝐷¯𝑞𝐷𝑞𝐷𝐴superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝑆QCDsuperscriptitalic-ϕ𝑃𝑆subscript¯𝐽𝑣𝑦Γ2subscript𝐽𝑣0italic-ϕ𝑃𝑆displaystyleint[DbarqDqDA]eiStextQCDphi*(P,S)barJv(y)fracGamma2Jvphi(P,S), where ϕitalic-ϕphi is the hadron’s wave function, and SQCDsubscript𝑆QCDStextQCD is the QCD action. Next, we declare the parton model for the hadrons. The parton model consists in the statement that the constituent fields of a fast hadron are almost free and that their traverse momentum is suppressed in comparison to their longitudinal momentum. In other words, the hadron consists of collinear fields, which we label by subscript n¯¯𝑛barn. They obey the counting ∂+,∂−,∂T​qn¯subscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑇subscript𝑞¯𝑛displaystylepartial+,partial-,partialTqbarn ≲less-than-or-similar-todisplaystylelesssim P+​1,λ2,λ​qn¯,subscript𝑃1superscript𝜆2𝜆subscript𝑞¯𝑛displaystyle P+1,lambda2,lambdaqbarn, ∂+,∂−,∂T​An¯μsubscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑇subscriptsuperscript𝐴𝜇¯𝑛displaystylepartial+,partial-,partialTAmubarn ≲less-than-or-similar-todisplaystylelesssim P+​1,λ2,λ​An¯μ,subscript𝑃1superscript𝜆2𝜆subscriptsuperscript𝐴𝜇¯𝑛displaystyle P+1,lambda2,lambdaAmubarn, where λ∼M/P+similar-to𝜆𝑀superscript𝑃lambdasim M/P+ is a small parameter. The sign ≲less-than-or-similar-tolesssim indicates that the partons’ momenta are not restricted from below and thus includes also lower-counting modes. The momentum counting rules and the QCD equation of motions (EOMs) fix the counting for the components of the fields. It is straightforward to demonstrate that ξn¯,ηn¯=γ−​γ+2​qn¯,γ+​γ−2​qn¯∼λ,λ2,subscript𝜉¯𝑛subscript𝜂¯𝑛superscript𝛾superscript𝛾2subscript𝑞¯𝑛superscript𝛾superscript𝛾2subscript𝑞¯𝑛similar-to𝜆superscript𝜆2displaystylexibarn,etabarn=Bigfracgamma-gamma+2qbarn,fracgamma+gamma-2qbarnBigsimlambda,lambda2, An¯+,An¯−,An¯​T∼1,λ2,λ. similar-tosubscript𝐴limit-from¯𝑛subscript𝐴limit-from¯𝑛subscript𝐴¯𝑛𝑇1superscript𝜆2𝜆displaystyleAbarn+,Abarn-,AbarnTsim1,lambda2,lambda. The main difference of the TMD factorization from collinear factorization is the counting rule for the distance y𝑦y. It has a large transverse component b∼(λ​P+)−1similar-to𝑏superscript𝜆subscript𝑃1bsim(lambda P+)-1. As a consequence, the transverse derivatives of collinear field accompanied by b𝑏b have a unity counting, bμ​∂μqn¯∼1similar-tosuperscript𝑏𝜇subscript𝜇subscript𝑞¯𝑛1bmupartialmuqbarnsim 1, which result in the TMD-type of operators. Simultaneously, we should guarantee that the anti-collinear momentum of partons remains suppressed, y+​∂−qn¯∼λsimilar-tosuperscript𝑦subscriptsubscript𝑞¯𝑛𝜆y+partial-qbarnsimlambda (at least), and that collinear derivatives are not magnified, i. e. y−​∂+qn¯∼1similar-tosuperscript𝑦subscriptsubscript𝑞¯𝑛1y-partial+qbarnsim 1 (at most). The latter is required in order to keep away of the small-x𝑥x effects. In our definition y+∼y−∼ℓsimilar-tosuperscript𝑦superscript𝑦similar-toℓy+sim y-simell. These requirements uniquely fix the scaling ℓ,b∼P+−1​1,λ−1. similar-toℓ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑃11superscript𝜆1displaystyleell,bsim P+-11,lambda-1. Note that these constraints imply that ℓ≪uch-less-thanℓ𝑏ellll b, which should be fulfilled in lattice simulations. The counting does not fully incorporate the nonperturbative components of eqn. . The extra source of nonperturbative corrections are the gluon fields present in the Wilson line. These fields can couple between Wilson links separated by large-b𝑏b. Such interactions are nonperturbative and to extract their contribution, we introduce the v𝑣v-collinear modes with the counting ∂+,∂−,∂T​qvsubscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑇subscript𝑞𝑣displaystylepartial+,partial-,partialTqv ≲less-than-or-similar-todisplaystylelesssim P+​λ2,λ2,λ​qv,subscript𝑃superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝜆subscript𝑞𝑣displaystyle P+lambda2,lambda2,lambdaqv, ∂+,∂−,∂T​Avμsubscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑇subscriptsuperscript𝐴𝜇𝑣displaystylepartial+,partial-,partialTAmuv ≲less-than-or-similar-todisplaystylelesssim P+​λ2,λ2,λ​Avμ. subscript𝑃superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝜆subscriptsuperscript𝐴𝜇𝑣displaystyle P+lambda2,lambda2,lambdaAmuv. There is no necessity to split the fields further and introduce extra modes, because collinear and v𝑣v-collinear modes accumulate all nonperturbative effects. To integrate the perturbative component, we split the fields in the functional integral as q=ψ+qn¯+qv,Aμ=Bμ+An¯μ+Avμ,formulae-sequence𝑞𝜓subscript𝑞¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑣superscript𝐴𝜇superscript𝐵𝜇subscriptsuperscript𝐴𝜇¯𝑛subscriptsuperscript𝐴𝜇𝑣displaystyle q=psi+qbarn+qv,qquad Amu=Bmu+Amubarn+Amuv, where ψ𝜓psi and B𝐵B are the dynamical fields, which do not satisfy the counting (, ). This decomposition covers all configurations within the functional integral. There is, however, a double counting, which takes place if the (anti)collinear momentum of the collinear field became too soft, ∂+,∂−,∂T​q∼λ2,λ2,λ​qsimilar-tosubscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑇𝑞superscript𝜆2superscript𝜆2𝜆𝑞partial+,partial-,partialTqsimlambda2,lambda2,lambdaq. There are two popular approaches to resolve the issue of double-counting. The first is to introduce an extra cutting rule in the overlap region, which eliminates the overlap on the level of the functional integral. This approach is used in refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib29. The second approach is to assume that hadrons do no contain the soft components (which is valid for the non-small-x approximation), and subtract double-counting contribution by division of the functional integral by corresponding vacuum contribution citebib.bib36, bib.bib16. This contribution is called the soft factor and denoted as S​(y)𝑆𝑦S(y). In this work, we utilize the second approach. The resulting functional integral reads Ωbare[Γ]​(y)=∫[D​q¯n¯​D​qn¯​D​An¯]​[D​q¯v​D​qv​D​Av]​ei​SQCD​[qn¯,An¯]+i​SQCD​[qv,Av]​ϕ∗​(P,S)​𝒲e​f​f[Γ]​(y)S​(y)​ϕ​(P,S),superscriptsubscriptΩbaredelimited-Γ𝑦delimited-𝐷subscript¯𝑞¯𝑛𝐷subscript𝑞¯𝑛𝐷subscript𝐴¯𝑛delimited-𝐷subscript¯𝑞𝑣𝐷subscript𝑞𝑣𝐷subscript𝐴𝑣superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝑆QCDsubscript𝑞¯𝑛subscript𝐴¯𝑛𝑖subscript𝑆QCDsubscript𝑞𝑣subscript𝐴𝑣superscriptitalic-ϕ𝑃𝑆subscriptsuperscript𝒲delimited-Γ𝑒𝑓𝑓𝑦𝑆𝑦italic-ϕ𝑃𝑆widetildeOmegatextbare[Gamma](y)=!int[DbarqbarnDqbarnDAbarn][DbarqvDqvDAv]eiStextQCD[qbarn,Abarn]+iStextQCD[qv,Av]phi*(P,S)fracW[Gamma]eff(y)S(y)phi(P,S), where 𝒲e​f​f[Γ]​(y)=∫[D​ψ¯​D​ψ​D​B]​ei​Sint. ​(J¯v​(y)​Γ2​Jv​)​[ψ+qn¯+qv,B+An¯+Av]. subscriptsuperscript𝒲delimited-Γ𝑒𝑓𝑓𝑦delimited-𝐷¯𝜓𝐷𝜓𝐷𝐵superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝑆int. subscript¯𝐽𝑣𝑦Γ2subscript𝐽𝑣0𝜓subscript𝑞¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑣𝐵subscript𝐴¯𝑛subscript𝐴𝑣displaystyleW[Gamma]eff(y)=int[DbarpsiDpsi DB]eiStextint. left(barJv(y)fracGamma2Jvright)[psi+qbarn+qv,B+Abarn+Av]. Here, Sint. =SQCD​[ψ+qn¯+qv,B+An¯+Av]−SQCD​[qn¯,An¯]−SQCD​[qv,Av]subscript𝑆int. subscript𝑆QCD𝜓subscript𝑞¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑣𝐵subscript𝐴¯𝑛subscript𝐴𝑣subscript𝑆QCDsubscript𝑞¯𝑛subscript𝐴¯𝑛subscript𝑆QCDsubscript𝑞𝑣subscript𝐴𝑣Stextint. =StextQCD[psi+qbarn+qv,B+Abarn+Av]-StextQCD[qbarn,Abarn]-StextQCD[qv,Av] is the background-field Lagrangian with two background fields. Explicit expression for Sint. subscript𝑆int. Stextint. can be found in appendix A of ref. citebib.bib31. The definition of the soft factor depends entirely on the shape of the overlap region, which is defined by the counting rules. Since in the present case the overlap region coincides with the case for the ordinary TMD factorization theorem citebib.bib16, bib.bib17, the soft factor is the usual TMD soft factor. In the form the factorization theorem does not require a proof. In the sense that the expression is already factorized. Indeed, the effective operator 𝒲e​f​f[Γ]subscriptsuperscript𝒲delimited-Γ𝑒𝑓𝑓W[Gamma]eff is a polynomial in background fields, and all interaction structure is already collected into action exponents. The signal of the factorization violation would be a mismatch of the singularity structures between poles of nonperturbative elements, coefficient functions, and S​(y)𝑆𝑦S(y). As we demonstrate in sec. , in the present case all singularities cancel in-between terms. This confirms the factorization statement. One of the advantages of the background-field method is the possibility of fixing different types of gauges for the dynamical and for each background sector. We use the standard choice of background gauge for the dynamical gluon citebib.bib37. For the background fields, we use the light-cone gauges, because this choice essentially simplifies the computation. We define the gauge-fixing conditions An¯+=0,Av−=0. formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝐴¯𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑣0displaystyle Abarn+=0,qquad Av-=0. The light-cone gauge is to be supplemented by the appropriate boundary conditions for the transverse component of the fields. We set limz−→s​∞An¯μT​(z)=0,limz+→−∞AvμT​(z)=0. formulae-sequencesubscript→superscript𝑧𝑠subscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝜇𝑇¯𝑛𝑧0subscript→superscript𝑧subscriptsuperscript𝐴subscript𝜇𝑇𝑣𝑧0displaystylelimz-to sinftyAmuTbarn(z)=0,qquadlimz+to-inftyAmuTv(z)=0. This choice follows from the analysis of the integrals at one-loop, which are presented below. Boundary conditions are fixed to nullify the gluon interaction at spatial infinity, which corresponds to the diagrams with interaction with transverse links. For a more detailed discussion we refer to sec.3 of ref. citebib.bib31. As consequence of and , the components of the gluon field can be expressed via the field-strength tensor An¯μ​(z)=−∫s​∞0𝑑s​Fμ+​(s​n+z),Avμ​(z)=−∫−∞0𝑑s​Fμ−​(s​n¯+z). formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝐴¯𝑛𝜇𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑠0differential-d𝑠superscript𝐹limit-from𝜇𝑠𝑛𝑧superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑣𝜇𝑧superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝑠superscript𝐹limit-from𝜇𝑠¯𝑛𝑧displaystyle Abarnmu(z)=-intsinfty0dsFmu+(sn+z),qquad Avmu(z)=-int-infty0dsFmu-(sbarn+z). Let us note that the gauge-fixation condition for v𝑣v-collinear field is somewhat redundant. Indeed, the counting rules for the components Avsubscript𝐴𝑣Av-field states that Av−∼Av+∼λ2similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐴𝑣superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑣similar-tosuperscript𝜆2Av-sim Av+simlambda2, and thus the sensitivity to Av−superscriptsubscript𝐴𝑣Av- is beyond our accuracy. In this case, one cannot justify the choice of gauge-fixing solely by counting arguments (as it could be done for factorization of cross-section). To fix it, one needs to perform one-loop computation and restore the Wilson line from the gluon interaction. Such computation was done in ref. citebib.bib12, and conditions (, ) corresponds to it. The integral for the effective operator 𝒲𝒲W is to be taken by means of the perturbative expansion. Throughout this process, the background fields are considered as external classical fields, which satisfy the QCD equation of motions (EOMs). The loop-coordinates have an effective counting ∼1/P+similar-toabsent1superscript𝑃sim 1/P+ for all components, despite the loop integrals span the whole space. Therefore, the power-unsuppressed interactions are effectively confined in small volumes around currents at 00 and y𝑦y. The diagrams that include an exchange between these volumes (see fig. ), contain propagators in the distance (x−y)𝑥𝑦(x-y) (with x𝑥x being a loop-coordinate). Such propagators lead to a suppression factor ∼b−2∼λ2similar-toabsentsuperscript𝑏2similar-tosuperscript𝜆2sim b-2simlambda2. In other words, such diagrams are NNLP at least. The more propagators connect the volumes the higher is the suppression. The main conclusion of this hierarchy is that LP and NLP contributions to the effective operator come from the diagrams without exchanges between 00 and y𝑦y positions. The whole set of this diagrams can be presented as the product 𝒲e​f​f[Γ]​(y)=𝒥¯​(y)​Γ2​𝒥​+𝒪​(λ2),subscriptsuperscript𝒲delimited-Γ𝑒𝑓𝑓𝑦¯𝒥𝑦Γ2𝒥0𝒪superscript𝜆2displaystyleW[Gamma]eff(y)=barJ(y)fracGamma2J+O(lambda2), where 𝒥​(y)=∫[D​ψ¯​D​ψ​D​B]​ei​Sint. ​Jv​(y)​[ψ+qn¯+qv,B+An¯+Av]. 𝒥𝑦delimited-𝐷¯𝜓𝐷𝜓𝐷𝐵superscript𝑒𝑖subscript𝑆int. subscript𝐽𝑣𝑦𝜓subscript𝑞¯𝑛subscript𝑞𝑣𝐵subscript𝐴¯𝑛subscript𝐴𝑣displaystyleJ(y)=int[DbarpsiDpsi DB]eiStextint. Jv(y)[psi+qbarn+qv,B+Abarn+Av]. This relation is straightforward to proof using the combinatorial formula for the disconnected diagrams. Thus, to receive LP and NLP expressions one needs to derive the NLP expansion for the effective current 𝒥𝒥J only. Note, that in eqn. we indicated the order of correction 𝒪​(λ2)𝒪superscript𝜆2O(lambda2) relatively to the leading term. We will do the same in all subsequent sections.

The LP and tree order of the effective current 𝒥𝒥J is given by diagram A in fig. . It reads 𝒥𝐀​subscript𝒥𝐀0displaystyleJA =displaystyle= P​exp⁡[−i​g​∫L0𝑑s​vμ​(Av,μ​(s​v)+An¯,μ​(s​v))]​qn¯​. The diagram C vanishes due to our choice of the gauge conditions. The form of expressions is not unique but could be modified using EOMs. This presents the problem of fixing the operator basis for the sub-leading power computation. It is known that, generally, sub-leading power distributions mix with the leading power distributions under the renormalization. Therefore, the best choice for the basis is the one that nullifies the mixing. This is accomplished by sorting the operators with respect to the twist, that is the Lorenz-invariant characteristic of operator. The twist of operator is computed by the usual dimension-minus-spin rule, where spin is projected to the collinear direction. Distribution with different twists do not mix. In the present case the twist-decomposition must be done for collinear operator only, because the v𝑣v-collinear operators already have the minimal twist at NLP. The operator ξn¯subscript𝜉¯𝑛xibarn is of twist-one, and it cannot be reduced. The twist of operator ηn¯subscript𝜂¯𝑛etabarn (that appear in 𝒥A/NLP)JtextA/NLP) is not defined. Applying EOMs in the massless quark approximation, the field ηn¯subscript𝜂¯𝑛etabarn can be expressed via ∂μξn¯subscript𝜇subscript𝜉¯𝑛partialmuxibarn (the total derivative of twist-one operator) and An¯,μ​ξn¯subscript𝐴¯𝑛𝜇subscript𝜉¯𝑛Abarn,muxibarn (the twist-two operator). These operators have independent renormalizations, and thus represent our go-to choice for the basis. We now combine the expressions for the effective currents to form the factorization theorem for the effective operator . The LP term is given by the product of LP currents , whereas the NLP terms are the product of one LP and one NLP currents. Let us schematically summarize the necessary steps to obtain the desired factorized expression: i) multiply effective currents into the effective operator ; ii) write expressions in a generic gauge by assigning the light-cone links and rewriting gluon fields via the gluon field-strength tensors ; iii) recouple the color indices such that products of collinear and v𝑣v-collinear fields are independently color-neutral. Finally, the hadron matrix element is taken, and each combination of fields turns into an independent matrix element. Since these are standard procedures (see e. g. citebib.bib27, bib.bib29, bib.bib31) we do not present them in details, and we write directly the final expression. All definitions implicitly contain transverse links that connect the far ends of the Wilson lines. These links are required to make the definition strictly gauge invariant. Let us stress that the TMD-distributions (, , ) are independent nonperturbative functions. Each of them obeys a separate closed evolution equation. The indexing 11, 12, and 21 refers to the TMD-twist of this operators citebib.bib31. So, the operator of TMD distribution Φ11subscriptΦ11Phi11 consists of product of two (so-called semi-compact) operators of twist-one (good component of quark field with an attached light-like Wilson line), and it has TMD-twist-(1,1). The operators for TMD distributions Φ12subscriptΦ12Phi12 and Φ21subscriptΦ21Phi21 consist of operators twist-one and twist-two (good component of quark field and good component of the gluon field with attached light-like Wilson line). Therefore, they have TMD-twist-(1,2) and TMD-twist-(2,1), respectively. In the following, we refer to TMD distributions of TMD-twist-(1,2) and TMD-twist-(2,1), simply as TMD distributions of twist-three. The ΨΨPsi-functions do not have definite twist, because the twist of the Wilson line Hvsubscript𝐻𝑣Hv is not defined. Nonetheless, their enumeration follows the same pattern for analogy. These function are nonperturbative objects with autonomous evolution.

We emphasize that the signs of momentum-fractions are not restricted. The TMD distributions and qTMD correlators are defined for positive and negative values of the momentum fractions. TMD distributions of twist-two with the negative values of x𝑥x are associated with the anti-parton distributions. TMD distributions of twist-three have a more involved interpretation. Three momentum fractions x1subscript𝑥1x1, x2subscript𝑥2x2 and x3subscript𝑥3x3 are related to each other by momentum conservation x1+x2+x3=0subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥30x1+x2+x3=0, which is reflected in the delta-function in the integral measure . There are six combinations of signs for x𝑥x’s. Each combination has a separate partonic interpretation for Φμ,12subscriptΦ𝜇12Phimu,12 and Φμ,21subscriptΦ𝜇21Phimu,21 citebib.bib33. The important point is that different ranges of x𝑥x’s are mixed in the integral convolutions with coefficient functions or with evolution kernel. content_sections.4 Factorization theorem in the physical: The derivation of the bare form of the factorization theorem is only the halfway point in the derivation of the final expression. To obtain a presentation suitable for practical applications, one needs to perform several manipulations and combine together different elements. Some of these iterations require additional computations. In this section, we collect the key points of this procedure and describe the process of deriving the factorization expression in the physical terms. The well-known elements (such as the recombination of rapidity divergences) are discussed very briefly, whereas the novel aspects are presented in some detail. Structurally, the TMD factorization at NLP is more involved than at LP. It contains a larger number of details to be treated. The first point to address is the cancellation of divergences in-between different elements of the formula. The NLP TMD factorization contains the following combinations of divergences • The infrared (IR) divergences of the coefficient function are presented as 1/ϵ1italic-ϵ1/epsilon-terms in the bare expressions (, ). These poles are canceled by the ultraviolet (UV) renormalization constants of TMD distributions and ΨΨPsi-functions. The cancellation of 1/ϵ1italic-ϵ1/epsilon-terms is, however, not complete. The leftover is the UV pole corresponding to the renormalization of currents Jvsubscript𝐽𝑣Jv. Since the TMD distributions of distinct TMD-twists are independent nonperturbative functions, such cancellation must happen individually for each term of the bare expression . Schematically, one should observe that each term of factorized expression satisfies ZJ−2​Ci†​Cj​Zi​jTMD​ZΨ=finite,superscriptsubscript𝑍𝐽2superscriptsubscript𝐶𝑖†subscript𝐶𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑖𝑗TMDsubscript𝑍Ψfinitedisplaystyle ZJ-2CidaggerCjZijtextTMDZPsi=textfinite, where ZJsubscript𝑍𝐽ZJ is the renormalization constant for Jvsubscript𝐽𝑣Jv, Zi​jTMDsuperscriptsubscript𝑍𝑖𝑗TMDZijtextTMD is the renormalization constant for Φi​jsubscriptΦ𝑖𝑗Phiij, and ZΨsubscript𝑍ΨZPsi is the renormalization constant for the corresponding ΨΨPsi-function. • The rapidity divergences of TMD operator and ΨΨPsi-functions are canceled by the soft factor S​(y)𝑆𝑦S(y) . At LP and NLP the soft factor is ordinary TMD soft factor citebib.bib32. The cancellation of rapidity divergences for NLP operators has been demonstrated explicitly at NLO in ref. citebib.bib31. In the present context, the only difference from computation in ref. citebib.bib31 are the ΨΨPsi-functions. The function ΨΨPsi has been studied in refs. citebib.bib35, bib.bib12, and its rapidity divergence is identical to Φ11subscriptΦ11Phi11. The rapidity divergences of Ψμ,12subscriptΨ𝜇12Psimu,12 and Ψμ,21subscriptΨ𝜇21Psimu,21 functions are the same as for ΨΨPsi at NLO, which can be checked by direct computation. Therefore, all rapidity divergent factors cancel at NLP just as they cancel at LP citebib.bib35, bib.bib11, bib.bib12. We do not provide a deeper discussion. • The integrals over σ𝜎sigma for NLP terms are divergent at σ→s​∞→𝜎𝑠sigmato sinfty. In momentum-fraction space , this divergence transforms to the divergence at x2→0→subscript𝑥20x2to 0. These divergences are called “special” rapidity divergences citebib.bib33. Special rapidity divergences are implicit and cancel between collinear and v𝑣v-collinear sectors (second and third lines in eqn. ). To make the factorization formula finite term-by-term, one defines physical TMD distributions by adding (and subtracting in the factorized formula) specific divergent pieces. Special rapidity divergences are specific for power corrections, i. e. , they are trivially absent at LP. The computation of rapidity divergences is performed in the δ𝛿delta-regularization defined in refs. citebib.bib42, bib.bib43. As a result of these procedures, one obtains the finite expression for the qTMD correlator with each nonperturbative element satisfying an evolution equation (given in sec. ). Even so, the expression is still not very practical. It contains a combination of terms with different imaginary parts. The last step is to resolve the complex structure and present the formula in a directly usable form. In the following sections, we discuss in particular detail these procedures. The final expression for the NLP factorization of the qTMD correlator is given in sec. .

The bare TMD distributions are defined in eqns. (, , ). Their renormalization and evolution properties are known. For the detailed description of twist-three TMD distribution we refer to ref. citebib.bib33. In this section, we briefly summarize the features that are important for the present work. All TMD distributions are renormalized by three factors. Two UV renormalization constant (one for each semi-compact operator), and the rapidity renormalization factor. For the present case, we have Φ11,bare[Γ]​(x,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ11bare𝑥𝑏displaystylePhi[Gamma]11,textbare(x,b) =displaystyle= R​(b2)​ZU​1​(−x)​ZU​1​(x)​Φ11[Γ]​(x,b;μ,ζ),𝑅superscript𝑏2subscript𝑍𝑈1𝑥subscript𝑍𝑈1𝑥subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ11𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystyle R(b2)ZU1(-x)ZU1(x)Phi[Gamma]11(x,b;mu,zeta), Φμ,21,bare[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝜇21baresubscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏displaystylePhi[Gamma]mu,21,textbare(x1,x2,x3,b) =displaystyle= R​(b2)​ZU​2​(x2,x1)​ZU​1​(x3)⊗Φμ,12[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b;μ,ζ),tensor-product𝑅superscript𝑏2subscript𝑍𝑈2subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥1subscript𝑍𝑈1subscript𝑥3subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝜇12subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystyle R(b2)ZU2(x2,x1)ZU1(x3)otimesPhi[Gamma]mu,12(x1,x2,x3,b;mu,zeta), Φμ,12,bare[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b)subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝜇12baresubscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏displaystylePhi[Gamma]mu,12,textbare(x1,x2,x3,b) =displaystyle= R​(b2)​ZU​1​(x1)​ZU​2​(x2,x3)⊗Φμ,12[Γ]​(x1,x2,x3,b;μ,ζ),tensor-product𝑅superscript𝑏2subscript𝑍𝑈1subscript𝑥1subscript𝑍𝑈2subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝜇12subscript𝑥1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystyle R(b2)ZU1(x1)ZU2(x2,x3)otimesPhi[Gamma]mu,12(x1,x2,x3,b;mu,zeta), where ZU​1subscript𝑍𝑈1ZU1 and ZU​2subscript𝑍𝑈2ZU2 are the UV renormalization constants for the twist-1 and twist-2 semi-compact constituents of the TMD operator citebib.bib33. The factor R𝑅R is the renormalization constant for the rapidity divergence citebib.bib44, bib.bib45. Loosely speaking, R=1/S𝑅1𝑆R=1/sqrtS citebib.bib46, bib.bib16, bib.bib45. The scales μ𝜇mu and ζ𝜁zeta are the scales of UV and rapidity renormalization, respectively. The symbol ⊗tensor-productotimes denotes the integral convolution in x𝑥x’s between ZU​2subscript𝑍𝑈2ZU2 and the TMD distribution. The rapidity divergences are associated with the light-cone directions, and thus their renormalization introduces the non-boost-invariant scales ν±superscript𝜈plus-or-minusnupm. The UV renormalization constant also have dependence on light-cone direction, which appear in the collinear-divergent part and scales with the common momentum q±superscript𝑞plus-or-minusqpm passing through the operator. The soft factor S​(y)𝑆𝑦S(y) cancels the rapidity and collinear divergences. It also restores the boost-invariance through the introduction of the boost-invariant scales for rapidity evolution: ζ=2​(q+)2​ν−ν+,ζ¯=2​(v−​μ)2​ν+ν−. formulae-sequence𝜁2superscriptsuperscript𝑞2superscript𝜈superscript𝜈¯𝜁2superscriptsuperscript𝑣𝜇2superscript𝜈superscript𝜈displaystylezeta=2(q+)2fracnu-nu+,qquadbarzeta=2(v-mu)2fracnu+nu-. where the ζ¯¯𝜁barzeta can only be proportional to μ2superscript𝜇2mu2, since no other hard scale is present in the v𝑣v-collinear sector. The resulting renormalization factors are called subtracted, and depend on ζ𝜁zeta. Details on the whole procedure can be found in refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib44, bib.bib16, bib.bib45. The NLO expression for the subtracted renormalization constant ZU​1sub. subscriptsuperscript𝑍sub. 𝑈1Ztextsub. U1 is well-known citebib.bib47, bib.bib17: ZU​1sub. subscriptsuperscript𝑍sub. The renormalized TMD distributions satisfy a pair of evolution equations (, ). The scaling with respect to μ𝜇mu reads μ2​dd​μ2​Φi​j[Γ]​(xi,xj,b;μ,ζ)=(γi†​(xi,μ,ζ)+γj​(xj,μ,ζ))⊗Φi​j[Γ]​(xi,xj,b;μ,ζ),superscript𝜇2𝑑𝑑superscript𝜇2subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝑖𝑗subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝑥𝑗𝑏𝜇𝜁tensor-productsubscriptsuperscript𝛾†𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖𝜇𝜁subscript𝛾𝑗subscript𝑥𝑗𝜇𝜁subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝑖𝑗subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝑥𝑗𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystylemu2fracddmu2Phi[Gamma]ij(xi,xj,b;mu,zeta)=left(gammadaggeri(xi,mu,zeta)+gammaj(xj,mu,zeta)right)otimesPhi[Gamma]ij(xi,xj,b;mu,zeta), where xnsubscript𝑥𝑛xn indicates a collection of n𝑛n momentum fractions, γisubscript𝛾𝑖gammai are anomalous dimensions, and ⊗tensor-productotimes is the integral convolution in x𝑥x’s. In the present work we need only anomalous dimensions γ1subscript𝛾1gamma1 and γ2subscript𝛾2gamma2. Both anomalous dimensions have complex parts. For anomalous dimension γ1subscript𝛾1gamma1, the complex phase accumulates the full dependence on the momentum fraction. In the case of TMD distributions of twist-two these complex parts cancel entirely in the sum and the result is the well-known expression, which at LO is γ1†​(x,μ,ζ)+γ1​(x,μ,ζ)subscriptsuperscript𝛾†1𝑥𝜇𝜁subscript𝛾1𝑥𝜇𝜁displaystylegammadagger1(x,mu,zeta)+gamma1(x,mu,zeta) =displaystyle= 2​as​CF​(ln⁡(μ2ζ)+3)+𝒪​(as2).2subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹superscript𝜇2𝜁3𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠2displaystyle 2asCFleft(lnleft(fracmu2zetaright)+3right)+O(as2). The anomalous dimension γ2subscript𝛾2gamma2 is cumbersome, which leads to an involved expression for the evolution equation already at LO citebib.bib33. The evolution with respect to scale ζ𝜁zeta reads ζ​dd​ζ​Φi​j[Γ]​(xi,xj,b;μ,ζ)=−𝒟​(b,μ)​Φi​j[Γ]​(xi,xj,b;μ,ζ),𝜁𝑑𝑑𝜁subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝑖𝑗subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝑥𝑗𝑏𝜇𝜁𝒟𝑏𝜇subscriptsuperscriptΦdelimited-Γ𝑖𝑗subscript𝑥𝑖subscript𝑥𝑗𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystylezetafracddzetaPhi[Gamma]ij(xi,xj,b;mu,zeta)=-D(b,mu)Phi[Gamma]ij(xi,xj,b;mu,zeta), where 𝒟𝒟D is the Collins-Soper kernel citebib.bib48. The equation is valid for i​j𝑖𝑗ij=11, 12, 21. The Collins-Soper kernel is a nonperturbative function. UV and rapidity anomalous dimensions satisfy the integrability condition citebib.bib44, bib.bib49 −ζ​dd​ζ​(γi​(xi,μ,ζ)+γj​(xj,μ,ζ))=μ2​dd​μ2​𝒟​(b,μ)=Γcusp​(μ)2,𝜁𝑑𝑑𝜁subscript𝛾𝑖subscript𝑥𝑖𝜇𝜁subscript𝛾𝑗subscript𝑥𝑗𝜇𝜁superscript𝜇2𝑑𝑑superscript𝜇2𝒟𝑏𝜇subscriptΓcusp𝜇2displaystyle-zetafracddzetaleft(gammai(xi,mu,zeta)+gammaj(xj,mu,zeta)right)=mu2fracddmu2D(b,mu)=fracGammatextcusp(mu)2, where ΓcuspsubscriptΓcuspGammatextcusp is the cusp anomalous dimension. At small values of b𝑏b the TMD distributions can be computed in the terms of collinear PDFs by means of operator product expansion (OPE). Herewith, there is no relation between the TMD-twist of TMD distribution and the collinear twist of PDF. So, for TMD-twist-two distributions, the leading term of OPE has the form Φ11​(x,b)∼C​(x,ln⁡(b))⊗f​(x)similar-tosubscriptΦ11𝑥𝑏tensor-product𝐶𝑥𝑏𝑓𝑥Phi11(x,b)sim C(x,ln(b))otimes f(x) where C𝐶C is a perturbative coefficient, and f​(x)𝑓𝑥f(x) is a collinear distribution of twist-two or -three, see examples in refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib50, and complete analysis in ref. citebib.bib30. In this way, the factorization formula has the 𝒪​(as/b​(v​P))𝒪subscript𝑎𝑠𝑏𝑣𝑃O(as/b(vP)) behaviour in the perturbative approximation. Partially, it comes from ∂μΦ11subscript𝜇subscriptΦ11partialmuPhi11 (due to the derivative of ln⁡(b)𝑏ln(b) at NLO), and partially, from twist-three terms . It also shows that in order to receive the NLP TMD factorization from the resummation approach, one must take into account collinear twist-two and twist-three operators (at least). Finally, we have to address the emergence of the special rapidity divergences. As it is discussed in sec. , the point x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0 is the singular point of the factorized expression. If the TMD distribution is continuous at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0, the integral around this point will only produce an imaginary part. However, twist-three distributions are generally discontinuous at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0. Therefore, the integrals of type are divergent. The divergences of integrals of type are rapidity divergences. They are a different type of rapidity divergences compared to the ordinary one of the TMD operator that are renormalized by the factor R𝑅R , and for that reason are called “special rapidity divergences”. Special rapidity divergences can be computed explicitly order-by-order in perturbation theory. A feature of the special rapidity divergence is that it is proportional to the TMD distributions of twist-two. This can be used to re-define TMD distributions in a controllable and systematic manner. One defines physical TMD distributions by subtracting a precomputed finite term such that the integrals of type are finite. The factorization theorem for qTMD correlator contains new objects – ΨΨPsi-functions. To our best knowledge these functions are specific to the factorization of the qTMD correlator. On the one hand they are similar to the ordinary TMD distributions, but with the parton field replaced by Wilson lines along direction v𝑣v (or equivalently by the field H𝐻H ). For that reason, some of the properties of ΨΨPsi-functions are analogous to the properties of TMD distribution (for example, double-scale evolution). On the other hand, they are similar to correlators of two heavy-quark fields, and some of their properties could be deduced by analytical continuation to v2<0superscript𝑣20v2<0. We summarize important properties of ΨΨPsi-functions in this section. This rule is universal for LP and NLP terms. The cancellation of IR and UV divergences for both the real and imaginary parts is a strong check of the computation of coefficient functions.

The special rapidity divergences cancel in the sum of term in the factorized expression. The cancellation is not traceless but leaves a term responsible for the restoration of boost-invariance of the whole expression. This important mechanism is not yet discussed in the literature, and thus we present it here with extra details. The special rapidity divergences cancel in-between genuine NLP terms and do not require any additional “soft-factor” contribution (note that such a soft factor should carry an index μ𝜇mu, and thus be a NNLP). This can be seen already from the Dirac structures of genuine and kinematic terms, which are richer for the genuine terms. Due to it, the number of Lorenz-invariant components of genuine terms is larger that those of kinematic terms. The explicit decomposition can be found in sec. . Each independent Lorentz-invariant component has a special rapidity divergences, but only some of them have contribution of smaller-twist functions that could be accompanied by some soft-factor to cancel it. Thus, the cancellation of special rapidity divergences between genuine terms is the only possible mechanism which would work for all polarization cases. The cancellation involves terms from several lines in the factorization formula . To make it more explicit, we extract the terms of interest here. The expression for the factorization theorem is not yet ready for a practical application. Using these rules, and definition , we rewrite the factorized expression in the explicitly real form.

The final expression for the factorization of the qTMD correlator is Ω[Γ]​(x,b,μ)superscriptΩdelimited-Γ𝑥𝑏𝜇displaystyleOmega[Gamma](x,b,mu) =displaystyle= Ψ​(b;μ,ζ¯)​ℂ11​Φ11⟦Γ⟧​(x,b;μ,ζ)Ψ𝑏𝜇¯𝜁subscriptℂ11superscriptsubscriptΦ11delimited-⟦⟧Γ𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁displaystylePsi(b;mu,barzeta)C11Phi11llbracketGammarrbracket(x,b;mu,zeta) where the integral over x2subscript𝑥2x2 is restricted by the arguments of TMD distributions as: −10𝑥0x>0 and −1−x<x<11𝑥𝑥1-1-x<x<1 for x<0𝑥0x<0. The argument of twist-three TMD distributions is x=(−x−x2,x2,x)𝑥𝑥subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥2𝑥tildex=(-x-x2,x2,x) The coefficient functions are ℂ11subscriptℂ11displaystyleC11 =displaystyle= 1+as​CF​(−𝐋p2−2​𝐋p−4+π26)+𝒪​(as2),1subscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹superscriptsubscript𝐋𝑝22subscript𝐋𝑝4superscript𝜋26𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠2displaystyle 1+asCFleft(-Lp2-2Lp-4+fracpi26right)+O(as2), ℂR​(x,x2)subscriptℂ𝑅𝑥subscript𝑥2displaystyleCR(x,x2) =displaystyle= 1(x2)++asCF−𝐋p2+π26(x2)+displaystylefrac1(x2)++asBiggCFfrac-Lp2+fracpi26(x2)+ ℂI​(x,x2)subscriptℂ𝐼𝑥subscript𝑥2displaystyleCI(x,x2) =displaystyle= δ(x2)+asCF[δ(x2)(−𝐋p2+π26)+2​sx(x2)+]displaystyledelta(x2)+asBiggCFBig[delta(x2)left(-Lp2+fracpi26right)+frac2sx(x2)+Big] The integrals with these coefficient functions are regular at all points of integration. The NLP correction, denoted by 𝒪​(λ2)𝒪superscript𝜆2O(lambda2), includes the corrections NLP corrections in P+subscript𝑃P+ and NLP corrections in 1/L1𝐿1/L. As we see here, the power corrections scale with x​P+𝑥subscript𝑃xP+ rather than just P+subscript𝑃P+. It agrees with our power counting , defined for components of parton’s momentum. Summarizing the factorization assumptions made in secs. and , we specify 𝒪​(λ2)=𝒪​(M2x2​(v​P)2,1b2​(v​P)2,bL,1M​L). 𝒪superscript𝜆2𝒪superscript𝑀2superscript𝑥2superscript𝑣𝑃21superscript𝑏2superscript𝑣𝑃2𝑏𝐿1𝑀𝐿displaystyleO(lambda2)=Oleft(fracM2x2(vP)2,frac1b2(vP)2,fracbL,frac1MLright). The expression is the complete NLP/NLO expression for the factorization of the qTMD correlator. It is apparently complicated and contains all possible combinations of factors and terms. Not all of these terms contribute to particular components of the qTMD correlator, as discussed in the next section. There are several interesting features of the expression that are specific to the TMD factorization at NLP. The first feature is that NLP factorization mixes T-odd and T-even terms. The T-odd(even) TMD distributions (do not) change their global sign under the rotation of staple contour to a different-sign infinity citebib.bib52. The T-parity of Φ⊕[Γ]superscriptsubscriptΦdirect-sumdelimited-ΓPhioplus[Gamma] is opposite to the T-parity of Φ⊖[Γ]superscriptsubscriptΦsymmetric-differencedelimited-ΓPhiominus[Gamma]. However, the terms with opposite parity in always have relative factor s𝑠s. Thanks to it, the relative sign between T-odd and T-even terms remains the same under the T-conjugation. Thus, NLP TMD factorization mixes distributions with different parity but preserves the global T-parity, which is required by the T-invariance of QCD. The second feature is the presence of multiple θ𝜃theta functions. This is an unhealthy property of NLP TMD distributions. Their evolution equation also contains step functions, and due to it, the distributions are discontinuous at xi=0subscript𝑥𝑖0xi=0. In particular, it leads to the appearance of the special rapidity divergences, discussed in sec. . Nonetheless, the integrals are well-defined in . The structure of discontinuities could probably be simplified, but at the moment, such a procedure is unknown. Finally, we observe that TMD factorization also incorporates the Qiu-Sterman-like contributions citebib.bib53, namely the contributions of twist-three distributions with the zero-momentum gluon 𝚽​(−x,0,x)𝚽𝑥0𝑥Phi(-x,0,x). Generally, twist-3 TMD distributions are discontinuous at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0, but we have checked that, for all physically accessible cases of Ω[Γ]superscriptΩdelimited-ΓOmega[Gamma], the contributions 𝚽​(−x,0,x)𝚽𝑥0𝑥Phi(-x,0,x) are well-defined for the known cases (see appendix C in ref. citebib.bib33). The distributions 𝚽𝚽Phi are either continuous or zero at x2=0subscript𝑥20x2=0, or contribute starting from 𝒪​(as2)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑠2O(as2). The expression is the first example of NLP TMD factorization at NLO written explicitly. The previous computations were either at LO citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib32, or written in the abstract operator form citebib.bib31. It gives a taste of what one can expect from the NLP TMD factorization for other observables.
content_sections.5 On practical application of NLP factor: There are two main motivations to study the qTMD correlators. The first one is to determine the Collins-Soper kernel – the nonperturbative function that governs the evolution in the rapidity scale of TMD distributions. The second one is to determine actual TMD distributions. In this section, we discuss the different possibilities of using NLP factorization theorem to improve our knowledge of TMD physics.

Our starting point is the assumptions that the qTMD correlator can be computed on the lattice, as a function of (v​P)𝑣𝑃(vP), ℓℓell and b2superscript𝑏2b2. Depending on ΓΓGamma, it has different number of tensor components written in terms of the vectors Pμsuperscript𝑃𝜇Pmu, vμsuperscript𝑣𝜇vmu, Sμsuperscript𝑆𝜇Smu, bμsuperscript𝑏𝜇bmu, and the tensors gμ​νsuperscript𝑔𝜇𝜈gmunu and ϵTμ​νsubscriptsuperscriptitalic-ϵ𝜇𝜈𝑇epsilonmunuT citebib.bib21. These components can be extracted individually. There are already several examples of such computations, see, f. i. , citebib.bib21, bib.bib54, bib.bib26, bib.bib55, bib.bib22. The factorization theorem provides the theoretical description for each component. Generally speaking, the comparison can be made for any component of Ω[Γ]superscriptΩdelimited-ΓOmega[Gamma]. However, some combination have a cleaner interpretation from the view-point of the factorization theorem. For example, the quasi-TMD correlators Ω[γ0]superscriptΩdelimited-superscript𝛾0Omega[gamma0] and Ω[v̸]superscriptΩdelimited-italic-v̸Omega[notv] both give access to the unpolarized TMD distribution f1subscript𝑓1f1. However, their sum is 𝒪​(λ2)𝒪superscript𝜆2O(lambda2). Therefore, their difference has numerically smaller power-suppressed contribution, and is better suited to the study of leading nonperturbative physics.
