Comparing components ( - ) with the factorization theorem we find the factorization for each individual component. There are 8 qTMD distributions that obey the LP factorization, and 16 qTMD distributions that obey NLP factorization.

The LP factorization theorem for qTMD distributions is well-understood and already applied in practice. The eight qTMD distributions that obey the LP factorization are those given in the lines (, , ). This part of our computation coincides with the known results. For a review of the current state, see citebib.bib19 and references within. In this subsection, we would like to provide a sketch of possible applications of the LP factorization theorem to contrast the problems with the application of the NLP factorization discussed in the following section. The LP factorization theorem reads F​(x,b;μ)𝐹𝑥𝑏𝜇displaystyle F(x,b;mu) =displaystyle= ((2​|x|​(v​P))2ζ)−𝒟​(b,μ)​ℂ11​(𝐋p,μ)​f​(x,b;μ,ζ)+𝒪​(λ2),superscriptsuperscript2𝑥𝑣𝑃2𝜁𝒟𝑏𝜇subscriptℂ11subscript𝐋𝑝𝜇𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇𝜁𝒪superscript𝜆2displaystyleleft(frac(2|x|(vP))2zetaright)-D(b,mu)C11(Lp,mu)f(x,b;mu,zeta)+O(lambda2), where F∈F1,F1​T⟂,G1,G1​T,H1,H1​L⟂,H1⟂,H1​T⟂𝐹subscript𝐹1superscriptsubscript𝐹1𝑇perpendicular-tosubscript𝐺1subscript𝐺1𝑇subscript𝐻1superscriptsubscript𝐻1𝐿perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐻1perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐻1𝑇perpendicular-toFinF1,F1Tperp,G1,G1T,H1,H1Lperp,H1perp,H1Tperp and f∈f1,f1​T⟂,g1,g1​T,h1,h1​L⟂,h1⟂,h1​T⟂𝑓subscript𝑓1superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑇perpendicular-tosubscript𝑔1subscript𝑔1𝑇subscriptℎ1superscriptsubscriptℎ1𝐿perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscriptℎ1perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscriptℎ1𝑇perpendicular-tofinf1,f1Tperp,g1,g1T,h1,h1Lperp,h1perp,h1Tperp in a natural one-to-one correspondence. The coefficient function ℂ11subscriptℂ11C11 is given in eqn. . This factorization theorem has been derived in refs. citebib.bib35, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib18 using different techniques. In this work, we have explicitly demonstrated that the correction to is 𝒪​(λ2)𝒪superscript𝜆2O(lambda2), not 𝒪​(λ)𝒪𝜆O(lambda). The expression is the simplest case among factorization formulas for qTMD distributions. The most direct application of is the determination of Collins-Soper kernel from the ratio of qTMDs measured at different momenta P𝑃P citebib.bib35, bib.bib12. One finds F​(x,b;μ;P1)F​(x,b;μ;P2)=((v​P1)(v​P2))−2​𝒟​(b,μ)​ℂ11​(𝐋P​1,μ)ℂ11​(𝐋P​2,μ)+𝒪​(λ2). 𝐹𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝑃1𝐹𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝑃2superscript𝑣subscript𝑃1𝑣subscript𝑃22𝒟𝑏𝜇subscriptℂ11subscript𝐋𝑃1𝜇subscriptℂ11subscript𝐋𝑃2𝜇𝒪superscript𝜆2displaystylefracF(x,b;mu;P1)F(x,b;mu;P2)=left(frac(vP1)(vP2)right)-2D(b,mu)fracC11(LP1,mu)C11(LP2,mu)+O(lambda2). All ingredients of this expression, except 𝒟𝒟D, are perturbative, and thus Collins-Soper kernel can be determined. The precision of Collins-Soper kernel determined in this way is systematically improvable by increasing the perturbative order of ℂ11subscriptℂ11C11 and the precision of lattice computation. This approach has been implemented in refs. citebib.bib54, bib.bib55. There is an alternative approach to determining the Collins-Soper kernel citebib.bib12, which is technically much simpler but has limited precision. In this alternative approach, one considers the ratio of qTMDs directly in the position space. Limiting ourself to the case ℓ=0ℓ0ell=0, we find F​(ℓ=0,b;μ;P1)F​(ℓ=0,b;μ;P2)=((v​P2)(v​P1))2​𝒟​(b,μ)​𝐫+𝒪​(λ2),𝐹ℓ0𝑏𝜇subscript𝑃1𝐹ℓ0𝑏𝜇subscript𝑃2superscript𝑣subscript𝑃2𝑣subscript𝑃12𝒟𝑏𝜇superscript𝐫0𝒪superscript𝜆2displaystylefracwidetildeF(ell=0,b;mu;P1)widetildeF(ell=0,b;mu;P2)=left(frac(vP2)(vP1)right)2D(b,mu)r+O(lambda2), where 𝐫=1+4​CF​as​ln⁡((v​P1)(v​P2))​[ln⁡(μ24​(v​P1)​(v​P2))+1−2​𝐌ln⁡|x|​f​(b,μ)]. superscript𝐫014subscript𝐶𝐹subscript𝑎𝑠𝑣subscript𝑃1𝑣subscript𝑃2delimited-superscript𝜇24𝑣subscript𝑃1𝑣subscript𝑃212subscriptsuperscript𝐌0𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇displaystyler=1+4CFaslnleft(frac(vP1)(vP2)right)left[lnleft(fracmu24(vP1)(vP2)right)+1-2Mfln|x|(b,mu)right]. The function 𝐌𝐌M is the ratio of integrals of TMD distributions 𝐌ln⁡|x|​f​(b,μ)=∫−11𝑑x​ln⁡|x|​|x|−2​𝒟​(b,μ)​f​(x,b;μ,ζ0)∫−11𝑑x​|x|−2​𝒟​(b,μ)​f​(x,b;μ,ζ0),subscriptsuperscript𝐌0𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇superscriptsubscript11differential-d𝑥𝑥superscript𝑥2𝒟𝑏𝜇𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁0superscriptsubscript11differential-d𝑥superscript𝑥2𝒟𝑏𝜇𝑓𝑥𝑏𝜇subscript𝜁0displaystyleMfln|x|(b,mu)=fracint-11dxln|x|,|x|-2D(b,mu)f(x,b;mu,zeta0)int-11dx|x|-2D(b,mu)f(x,b;mu,zeta0), where f𝑓f is the TMD distribution analogous to qTMD distribution F𝐹F (e. g. f1subscript𝑓1f1 corresponds to F1subscript𝐹1F1). The expression is independent on ζ0subscript𝜁0zeta0. In ref. citebib.bib12, it is argued that the nonperturbative function 𝐌𝐌M is almost a constant in a broad range of b𝑏b. This conjecture is supported by known phenomenological extractions. Therefore, the “constant” 𝐌𝐌M can be fixed by comparing one of the lattice points (at b≲1less-than-or-similar-to𝑏1blesssim 1GeV) to the perturbative value of 𝒟𝒟D. The method can be generalized to non-zero ℓℓell. The detailed discussion can be found ref. citebib.bib12. In this way, one avoids the decrease of precision due to the discrete Fourier transform over the lattice data and needs only a single ℓℓell-value measurement. For the same reason, the method is technically much simpler. However, it contains an assumption 𝐌=const. 𝐌const. M=textconst. with an unknown state, and its precision could not be improved beyond NLO (it requires an introduction of another unknown function analogous to 𝐌𝐌M). Nonetheless, the current systematic uncertainty of lattice simulations and the size of λ𝜆lambda are significant, and this method can be safely and reliably applied. It has been used in ref. citebib.bib26. The ΨΨPsi-function can be computed independently citebib.bib10, bib.bib58. In this case, the factorization formula can be used to determine the TMD distribution itself. For a more extended discussion of applications, we refer to recent reviews citebib.bib19, bib.bib59. Note that the ΨΨPsi-function can be used as an independent source for the determination of Collins-Soper kernel citebib.bib60, bib.bib61.

The remaining 16 components of the qTMD correlator (-) obey the NLP factorization. We write it in the following general form F​(x,b;μ)𝐹𝑥𝑏𝜇displaystyle F(x,b;mu) =displaystyle= 1x((2​|x|​(v​P))2ζ)−𝒟​(b,μ)ℂ11(𝐋p,μ)A(x,b;μ,ζ)displaystylefrac1xleft(frac(2|x|(vP))2zetaright)-D(b,mu)BigC11(Lp,mu)A(x,b;mu,zeta) where x=(−x−x2,x2,x)𝑥𝑥subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥2𝑥tildex=(-x-x2,x2,x). The letters A𝐴A, B𝐵B, C𝐶C and D𝐷D denote combinations of physical TMD distributions. They are listed in the table for each of NLP structure function. In general, A𝐴A and B𝐵B contain only twist-two distributions, and C𝐶C and D𝐷D contain only twist-three distributions. We introduced also 𝚿2​(b)=i​bμb2​M2​𝚿μ,21​(b;μ,μ2)Ψ​(b;μ,μ2),subscript𝚿2𝑏𝑖superscript𝑏𝜇superscript𝑏2superscript𝑀2superscriptsubscript𝚿𝜇210𝑏𝜇superscript𝜇2Ψ𝑏𝜇superscript𝜇2displaystylePsi2(b)=fracibmub2M2fracPsimu,21(b;mu,mu2)Psi(b;mu,mu2), which is dimensionless and scale-invariant (at least at NLO ). The notation f̊̊𝑓mathringf stands for f̊​(b)=2M2​∂f​(b,μ)∂b2,̊𝑓𝑏2superscript𝑀2𝑓𝑏𝜇superscript𝑏2displaystylemathringf(b)=frac2M2fracpartial f(b,mu)partial b2, for an arbitrary function f𝑓f. Note, that 𝒟̊​(b)̊𝒟𝑏mathringD(b) is independent on μ𝜇mu as a consequence of eqn. . We stress that, in this representation, the factorization theorem is explicitly real-valued. The combinations of the TMD distributions that are present in C𝐶C and D𝐷D are not random. These combinations form autonomous pairs that mixes through the evolution citebib.bib33. The evolution equations for TMD distributions of twist-three have an integral-differential form similar to the evolution of ordinary parton distributions but with an additional double-logarithmic term. The full set of TMD distributions of twist-three splits into subsets that evolve with the integral kernel ℙAsubscriptℙ𝐴PA or ℙBsubscriptℙ𝐵PB (see sec.4.3 in ref. citebib.bib33). All combinations present in eqn. evolve with ℙAsubscriptℙ𝐴PA only. In comparison to the LP factorization formula the NLP factorization formula is awkward. First, none of the qTMD distributions is proportional to a single TMD distribution of twist-three but always to a pair. Both coefficient functions ℂRsubscriptℂ𝑅CR and ℂIsubscriptℂ𝐼CI have tree-order contribution, and thus there is no perturbative suppression for one element of a pair. Second, many of the qTMD distributions contain twist-two terms, which could not be easily removed. Third, the function 𝚿2subscript𝚿2Psi2 is a new nonperturbative component that cannot be determined solely from measurements of qTMD distributions. Thus, a direct determination of TMD distributions of twist-three from eqn. is cumbersome. Inspecting the table , we observe that each qTMD correlator has a counterpart with the same twist-three content. Therefore, by combining several qTMD correlators, one could disentangle individual components and determine the TMD distribution of twist-three. Here one should also account for the contamination by the twist-two terms. We found the following groups that share the same nonperturbative content E,H;H1,HT⟂,ET⟂;H1,H1​T⟂,HL⟂,EL;H1​L⟂,HT,ET;H1,H1​T⟂,𝐸𝐻subscript𝐻1superscriptsubscript𝐻𝑇perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑇perpendicular-tosubscript𝐻1superscriptsubscript𝐻1𝑇perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐻𝐿perpendicular-tosubscript𝐸𝐿superscriptsubscript𝐻1𝐿perpendicular-tosubscript𝐻𝑇subscript𝐸𝑇subscript𝐻1superscriptsubscript𝐻1𝑇perpendicular-todisplaystyleE,H;H1,qquadHTperp,ETperp;H1,H1Tperp,qquadHLperp,EL;H1Lperp,qquadHT,ET;H1,H1Tperp, GT,FT,G1​T,F1​T⟂,GL⟂,FL⟂;G1,F⟂,G⟂;F1,GT⟂,FT⟂;G1​T,F1​T⟂. subscript𝐺𝑇subscript𝐹𝑇subscript𝐺1𝑇superscriptsubscript𝐹1𝑇perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐺𝐿perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐹𝐿perpendicular-tosubscript𝐺1superscript𝐹perpendicular-tosuperscript𝐺perpendicular-tosubscript𝐹1superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑇perpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscript𝐹𝑇perpendicular-tosubscript𝐺1𝑇superscriptsubscript𝐹1𝑇perpendicular-todisplaystyleGT,FT,G1T,F1Tperp,qquadGLperp,FLperp;G1,qquadFperp,Gperp;F1,qquadGTperp,FTperp;G1T,F1Tperp. However, even these combinations could not provide an unambiguous determination of twist-three distributions because the factorization formula projects twist-three functions to a single variable x𝑥x. A more immediate application can be made in the spirit of ref. citebib.bib26, which is briefly explained below eqn. . Concluding, the direct application of NLP factorization theorem does not seem practical for the moment, due to its involved content that entangles several TMD distributions in a single qTMD distributions. Nonetheless, the ratios of qTMD distributions E,ET⟂,EL,ET,FL⟂,G⟂𝐸superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑇perpendicular-tosubscript𝐸𝐿subscript𝐸𝑇superscriptsubscript𝐹𝐿perpendicular-tosuperscript𝐺perpendicular-toE,ETperp,EL,ET,FLperp,Gperp can provide access to the Collins-Soper kernel, in a way similar to the LP case. Such ratios can be considered both in position and in momentum spaces. In both cases, ratios are not pure functions of 𝒟𝒟D but contain contamination from twist-three TMD distributions. However, this contamination is small ∼as/Ncsimilar-toabsentsubscript𝑎𝑠subscript𝑁𝑐sim as/Nc. In both cases, one could not improve the precision of the approach systematically (contrary to the LP case). We are hoping that further progress in studies of twist-three TMD distributions will open opportunities to use more precisely.
content_sections.6 Conclusions: In this work, we study a particular class of lattice observables known as quasi-transverse momentum-dependent (qTMD) distributions. These are the diagonal matrix elements between hadron states of a quark-quark correlator whose Wilson line is staple-like and equal-time. At large hadron’s momentum, the qTMD correlator can be factorized in terms of physical TMD distributions and some unknown TMD-like functions. The form of the factorization theorem crucially depends on the Dirac matrix ΓΓGamma that contracts quark-fields’ spinor indices. In this work, we consider two cases: Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+ that projects both quark fields to their good components, and Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT that projects a good and a bad components of the quark field. The first case Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+ obeys the leading-power (LP) factorization theorem and has already been studied in several works. The case Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT requires the next-to-leading power (NLP) factorization and is addressed in this work for the first time. We derive the factorization theorem for qTMDs with Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT and compute the corresponding coefficient functions at NLO. For the first time, we present the outcome of TMD factorization at NLP/NLO in a directly usable form. In this sense, the expression derived in this work can serve as an example of a structure expected for more involved observables, such as differential cross-sections. We explicitly demonstrate that by combining all elements of NLP TMD factorization, one obtains a valid and well-defined expression. It is not a trivial statement due to the different singularity structures between NLP and LP cases. The computation is done for a general Γ∈ΓTΓsubscriptΓ𝑇GammainGammaT, which includes 16 different qTMD distributions measurable on the lattice. As a by-product, we also obtain the LP factorization and confirm previous computations with a different method. We explicitly check that the NLP factorization theorem does not contribute to the case Γ∈Γ+ΓsubscriptΓGammainGamma+, and thus any power correction to them actually starts at NLP. Note, that in this work, we operate with massless quarks ignoring power corrections of the type mq/(v​P)subscript𝑚𝑞𝑣𝑃mq/(vP). Along the work, we made several observations related to NLP TMD factorization that are general and important beyond the physics of qTMD distributions. • We observed that, at the bare NLO level, the NLP coefficient functions exactly reproduce the LP coefficient function in the limit of vanishing gluon momentum. We have checked that the same observation holds for the bare coefficient functions in Drell-Yan/SIDIS citebib.bib31. Since at NLP v𝑣v-collinear gluons carry vanishing light-cone momentum, the NLP coefficient function Cvsubscript𝐶𝑣Cv is equal to the LP coefficient C1subscript𝐶1C1. We argue that these relations could be a consequence of soft-gluon theorems and valid at all perturbative orders, but we do not have general proof of this statement beyond NLO. • For the first time, we explicitly demonstrate the cancellation of special rapidity divergences. The special rapidity divergences appear in the integral convolutions of twist-three TMD distributions and were observed in ref. citebib.bib33. The cancellation takes place in-between different collinear sectors and restores the boost invariance of the NLP factorization theorem. This mechanism is an essential part of the proof of the TMD factorization at NLP. • In the NLP factorization theorem, a non-trivial interplay occurs between the real and imaginary parts of the coefficient functions and the TMD parametrizations. Consequently, TMD factorization also incorporates Qiu-Sterman-like contributions, i. e. , contributions of twist-three distributions with vanishing gluon momentum. Such contributions appear already at LO and were missed in many previous considerations. The expression for the TMD factorization theorem at NLP is rather more complex than its LP counterpart. It mixes TMD distributions of twist-two (and their derivatives), twist-three, and derivatives of Collins-Soper kernels. Due to it, the direct application of the theorem is involved and requires several measurements to disentangle individual elements. However, even in this case, the determination will be incomplete because qTMD distribution depends on a single momentum fraction, whereas a twist-three distribution depends on two momentum fractions. Yet we demonstrate that a subset of observables (6 out of 16) can be used individually to determine the Collins-Soper kernel using a simplified procedure. The procedure is valid in the large-Ncsubscript𝑁𝑐Nc approximation and assumes that some integral weakly depends on the transverse distance. Both assumptions are accurate at the current precision of lattice simulations. Acknowledgements. We thank Andreas Schäfer for numerous discussions and the motivation to study this case. A. V. is funded by the Atracción de Talento Investigador program of the Comunidad de Madrid (Spain) No. 2020-T1/TIC-20204. A. V. is also supported by the Spanish Ministry grant PID2019-106080GB-C21. This work was partially supported by DFG FOR 2926 “Next Generation pQCD for Hadron Structure: Preparing for the EIC”, project number 430824754. S. R. acknowledge the financial support from the physics department of Ecole Polytechnique.

all_papers.title:
Unpolarized Transverse Momentum Distributions from a global fit of
  Drell-Yan and Semi-Inclusive Deep-Inelastic Scattering data
all_papers.abstract:
We present an extraction of unpolarized transverse-momentum-dependent parton
distribution and fragmentation functions based on more than two thousands data
points from several experiments for two different processes: semi-inclusive
deep-inelastic scattering and Drell-Yan production of lepton pairs. The
baseline analysis is performed with a Monte Carlo replica method resumming
large logarithms at N3LL accuracy. The resulting description of the data is
very good ($\chi^2/N_{\rm dat} = 1.06$). For semi-inclusive deep-inelastic
scattering, predictions for multiplicities are normalized by factors that cure
the discrepancy with data introduced by higher-order perturbative corrections.

qualitative: TMD factorization theorems provide a rigorous framework for defining Transverse Momentum Distributions (TMDs), establishing their universality and evolution properties, which are essential for extracting TMDs from experimental data.

Collins–Soper (CS) evolution governs the scale dependence of TMDs, describing how they evolve with the factorization scale, and is a key component in the theoretical description of TMDs in both SIDIS and Drell–Yan processes.

The extraction of TMDs relies on the universality of parton distributions across different processes, enabling a global fit that combines data from SIDIS and Drell–Yan to constrain both TMD parton distribution functions (PDFs) and fragmentation functions (FFs).

The inclusion of higher-order perturbative corrections, particularly resummation of large logarithms up to N3LL accuracy, significantly improves the theoretical precision and the description of experimental data.

Normalization issues in SIDIS data, especially from fixed-target experiments at moderate to low scales, are attributed to O(αs) corrections to the hard factor, which are challenging to describe within the current framework.

To address normalization discrepancies in SIDIS, the authors introduce a data-driven normalization correction by matching the integrated TMD cross section to the corresponding collinear result, effectively fixing the normalization a priori and independently of the fit.
quantitative: The baseline fit achieves a very good description of the data with χ²/N_dat = 1.06, based on 2031 data points from SIDIS and Drell–Yan processes.
The analysis is performed at N3LL accuracy for the resummation of large logarithms, with the only missing component being NNLO collinear fragmentation functions to reach full NLL accuracy.
The TMD evolution is implemented via the Collins–Soper equation, which governs the scale dependence of TMDs and is essential for consistent predictions across different kinematic regions.

The normalization correction procedure involves matching the integral over transverse momentum of the TMD cross section to the collinear result, effectively adjusting the TMD prediction to reproduce the correct normalization without altering the fit parameters.

content_sections.I Introduction: In this paper, we present an extraction of Transverse Momentum Distributions (TMDs)citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4 using more than two thousand data points from several experiments for two different kinds of processes: Semi-Inclusive Deep Inelastic Scattering (SIDIS) and production of Drell–Yan (DY) lepton pairs, significantly improving our previous analysiscitebib.bib5. Building maps of the internal partonic structure of nucleons is a crucial step towards understanding the interactions between quarks and gluons and the phenomenon of confinement. A steady progress in the last decades has led to more and more refined versions of such maps. The TMDs extracted in this article encode information about the three-dimensional distributions of quarks in momentum space. The level of sophistication of a TMD extraction essentially depends on two ingredients: the amount of analyzed data from different processes, namely how “global” the experimental information from which TMDs are extracted, and the perturbative accuracy reached in the theoretical formalism. The extraction of TMDs is based on TMD factorization theorems, which provide a precise definition of the objects to be extracted, establishing their universality and evolution equations. In this case, the accuracy of the calculation is defined by the amount of large logarithms being resummed, that in turn defines the powers of the strong coupling αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas to be included in the perturbative quantitiescitebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9. Of particular relevance is also the combination of analyzed data from different processes. Similarly to the extraction of collinear Parton Distribution Functions (PDFs), we can talk about a global fit, i. e. , a fit that leverages the universality of the parton distributions and uses different processes to constrain them. However, in SIDIS two types of TMDs enter the cross section: the TMD PDFs, describing how partons are arranged in the nucleon, and TMD Fragmentation Functions (FFs), describing how a parton produces a final-state hadron. The knowledge of TMDs, in particular of TMD FFs, would be greatly improved by using data from electron-positron annihilations into two almost back-to-back hadronscitebib.bib10. Unfortunately, this data is presently not available. There are measurements for the inclusive production of single hadronscitebib.bib11 but, in this case, transverse momenta need to be defined with respect to the thrust axis: a careful description of the latter is non trivial, and a rigorous factorization theorem for this process has been discussed only recently (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14 and references therein). Therefore, for TMD extractions we currently talk about a global fit when data from SIDIS and DY processes are included. In the last ten years, several extractions of TMDs have been presentedcitebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib5, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib7, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. Most of them suffered some shortcomings: they were either obtained in a parton-model framework without QCD corrections, or they took into account only a limited set of data, or did not perform a full fit. TMDs were also studied in a different framework, the so-called parton-branching approachcitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27. At present, only two works have reached the stage of combining SIDIS and DY data in a full-fledged global TMD fit: the above mentioned extraction of Ref. citebib.bib5, henceforth named PV17, and the extraction of Ref. citebib.bib22, henceforth named SV19. The PV17 extraction reached the NLL accuracy, was based on the calculation of observables at mean kinematics in each bin, and did not manage to describe the normalization of all datasets. In this work, we push the accuracy of the analysis to what we will refer to as NLL (only NNLO collinear FFs are currently missing in order to reach full NLL accuracy).2While this work was being completed, further efforts, including a work by the MAP collaboration, were made to include part of the NNLO correctionscitebib.bib28, bib.bib29 in the extraction of FFs. Apart from the increase in perturbative accuracy, in this work we also include many measurements published after 2017. The SV19 extraction reached the same perturbative accuracy and included essentially the same datasets. Our work has crucial differences in the selection of specific data points (we include a much larger number of points), the implementation of TMD evolution, the choice of nonperturbative components, and the handling of normalization for SIDIS data. As we will describe in detail, we found it particularly difficult to describe in a satisfactory way the normalization of SIDIS data obtained in fixed-target experiments at moderate to low scales. When the analysis is performed at NLL accuracy, we can describe well shape and normalization of SIDIS data, but the description of high-energy DY data is very poor. Going to NLL and NLL, the description of DY data significantly improves, but we fail to reproduce the normalization of SIDIS data, mainly due to the 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠cal O(alphas) corrections to the hard factor. As a possible solution, we choose to adjust the normalization of the TMD predictions by comparing their integral upon transverse momentum and the corresponding collinear formula. With this procedure, we fix the normalization a priori, in a way that is independent of the results of the fit. Our baseline fit is performed at NLL, using 2031 data points and obtaining χ2/Ndat=1.06superscript𝜒2subscript𝑁dat1.06chi2/Nrm dat=1.06. We also discuss variations of this baseline fit by changing the theoretical accuracy and the selected data. The paper is organized as follows. In Sec. we describe the theoretical framework used in the analysis. In Sec. we explain how experimental data have been selected. Sec. presents the results of our extraction. Finally, in Sec. we draw our conclusions.
content_sections.II Formalism II.1 Drell–Yan: In the Drell–Yan (DY) process hA​(PA)+hB​(PB)⟶γ∗/Z​(q)+X⟶ℓ+​(l)+ℓ−​(l′)+X,⟶subscriptℎ𝐴subscript𝑃𝐴subscriptℎ𝐵subscript𝑃𝐵superscript𝛾𝑍𝑞𝑋⟶superscriptℓ𝑙superscriptℓsuperscript𝑙′𝑋hA(PA)+hB(PB) longrightarrow gamma*/Z(q)+Xlongrightarrowell+(l)+ell-(lprime)+X;, two hadrons hAsubscriptℎ𝐴hA and hBsubscriptℎ𝐵hB with four-momenta PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA and PBsubscript𝑃𝐵PB, respectively, collide with center-of-mass energy squared s=(PA+PB)2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐴subscript𝑃𝐵2s=(PA+PB)2, producing a neutral vector boson γ∗/Zsuperscript𝛾𝑍gamma*/Z with four-momentum q𝑞q and large invariant mass Q=q2𝑄superscript𝑞2Q=sqrtq2. The vector boson eventually decays into a lepton and an antilepton with four-momenta constrained by momentum conservation, q=l+l′𝑞𝑙superscript𝑙′q=l+lprime. The involved momenta and the respective transverse components are summarized in Fig. . The hadronic four momenta PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA and PBsubscript𝑃𝐵PB can be chosen to identify the longitudinal direction z𝑧z and define the transverse momentum 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT of the γ∗/Zsuperscript𝛾𝑍gamma*/Z. The rapidity of the neutral boson (or, equivalently, of the lepton pair) is defined as y=12​ln⁡(q0+qzq0−qz). 𝑦12subscript𝑞0subscript𝑞𝑧subscript𝑞0subscript𝑞𝑧y=frac12lnleft(fracq0+qzq0-qzright),. For our purposes, we need the cross section for this process initiated by unpolarized hadrons and integrated over the azimuthal angle of the exchanged boson. content_sections.II Formalism II.2 Semi-Inclusive Deep-In: In SIDIS, a lepton with momentum l𝑙l scatters off a hadron target N𝑁N with mass M𝑀M and four momentum P𝑃P. In the final state, the scattered lepton momentum l′superscript𝑙′lprime is measured together with one hadron hℎh with mass Mhsubscript𝑀ℎMh and four momentum Phsubscript𝑃ℎPh. The other products of the scattering are undetected. Thus the reaction reads ℓ​(l)+N​(P)→ℓ​(l′)+h​(Ph)+X. →ℓ𝑙𝑁𝑃ℓsuperscript𝑙′ℎsubscript𝑃ℎ𝑋ell(l)+N(P)rightarrow ell(lprime)+h(Ph)+X,. The (space-like) four-momentum transfer is q=l−l′𝑞𝑙superscript𝑙′q=l-lprime, with Q2≡−q2>0superscript𝑄2superscript𝑞20Q2equiv-q2>0. For transverse momenta, we will follow the definitions and notations discussed in Ref. citebib.bib39, bib.bib40 (see also Fig. ). In particular, we define 𝑷h​Tsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇PhT as the hadron transverse momentum in the Breit frame, where P𝑃P and q𝑞q form a light-cone basis; as a consequence, Phsubscript𝑃ℎPh has only transverse components and |𝑷h​T|2=−Ph​T2superscriptsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑃ℎ𝑇2|PhT|2=-PhT2, which is frame independent. Similarly, we define 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT as the photon transverse momentum in the hadron frame, where P𝑃P and Phsubscript𝑃ℎPh form a light-cone basis; as a consequence, qTsubscript𝑞𝑇qT has only transverse components and |𝒒T|2=−qT2superscriptsubscript𝒒𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑇2|qT|2=-qT2, which is also frame independent. In the following, we will always work assuming that the invariant mass of the photon is large compared to the target and hadron masses (M2,Mh2≪Q2much-less-thansuperscript𝑀2superscriptsubscript𝑀ℎ2superscript𝑄2M2,Mh2ll Q2) and to the transverse momenta 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT and 𝑷h​Tsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇PhT (𝒒T2,𝑷h​T2≪Q2much-less-thansuperscriptsubscript𝒒𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇2superscript𝑄2qT2,,PhT2ll Q2). We neglect any power corrections that vanish in this limit, both kinematic and dynamical (higher twist), apart from some modifications to the normalization of the SIDIS observables (that could be seen as the effect of power corrections, see Sec. for more details). In this limit, Eq. reduces to 𝒒T≈−𝑷h​Tz. subscript𝒒𝑇subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑧qTapprox-fracPhTz,. Several studies have been made concerning higher-twist corrections of various origin (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib38, bib.bib44 for recent works). A careful study of the impact of power corrections to the case of unpolarized TMDs has been discussed in Ref. citebib.bib22. Lately, important advances in the study of higher-twist TMD factorization have been publishedcitebib.bib42, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, but they do not directly affect the observables we consider here. The differential cross section for SIDIS can be witten in terms of two structure functions, FU​U,Tsubscript𝐹𝑈𝑈𝑇FUU,T and FU​U,Lsubscript𝐹𝑈𝑈𝐿FUU,Lcitebib.bib37. The subscripts refer to the lepton, the target, and the photon polarization, respectively. The second structure function is formally a twist four contribution and is suppressed in the limit considered here, thus we neglect it. content_sections.II Formalism II.3 Transverse Momentum Di: As a consequence of the renormalization of ultraviolet and rapidity divergencescitebib.bib6, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, TMD PDFs and FFs acquire a dependence on the renormalization scale μ𝜇mu and on the rapidity scale ζ𝜁zeta. The evolution of TMDs from some initial values of the scales μisubscript𝜇𝑖mui, ζisubscript𝜁𝑖zetai, to some final values μf,ζfsubscript𝜇𝑓subscript𝜁𝑓muf,zetaf, is given by f1a​(x,𝒃T2;μf,ζf)=f1a​(x,𝒃T2;μi,ζi)​exp⁡∫μiμfd​μμ​γ​(μ,ζf)​(ζfζi)K​(|𝒃T|,μi)/2. superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑎𝑥superscriptsubscript𝒃𝑇2subscript𝜇𝑓subscript𝜁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑎𝑥superscriptsubscript𝒃𝑇2subscript𝜇𝑖subscript𝜁𝑖superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝜇𝑖subscript𝜇𝑓𝑑𝜇𝜇𝛾𝜇subscript𝜁𝑓superscriptsubscript𝜁𝑓subscript𝜁𝑖𝐾subscript𝒃𝑇subscript𝜇𝑖2hatf1a(x,bT2;muf,zetaf)=hatf1a(x,bT2;mui,zetai),expbiggintmuimuffracdmumu,gammabig(mu,zetafbig)bigg,bigg(fraczetafzetaibigg)K(|bT|,,mui)/2,. The anomalous dimension γ𝛾gamma for the renormalization-group evolution in μ𝜇mu reads: γ​(μ,ζ)=γF​(αs​(μ))−γK​(αs​(μ))​ln⁡ζμ,𝛾𝜇𝜁subscript𝛾𝐹subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇subscript𝛾𝐾subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝜁𝜇gammabig(mu,zetabig)=gammaFbig(alphas(mu)big)-gammaKbig(alphas(mu)big),ln,fracsqrtzetamu,, where γKsubscript𝛾𝐾gammaK is the cusp anomalous dimension and γF​(αs​(μ))=γ​(μ,μ2)subscript𝛾𝐹subscript𝛼𝑠𝜇𝛾𝜇superscript𝜇2gammaFbig(alphas(mu)big)=gammabig(mu,mu2big) is the boundary conditioncitebib.bib7. The Collins–Soper kernel K𝐾K, instead, is the anomalous dimension for the evolution in ζ𝜁zetacitebib.bib6. The same structure holds for the TMD FF. In total, the default configuration for the fit involves 21 free parameters: one associated to the nonperturbative part of the Collins–Soper kernel (Eq. ), 11 related to the nonperturbative part of the TMD PDF (Eqs. ,), and 9 for the nonperturbative part of the TMD FF (Eqs. ,). The functional forms in Eqs. - are largely arbitrary. However, an important feature is that they are the Fourier transforms of the sum of a Gaussian, a weighted Gaussian (multiplied by 𝒌⟂2superscriptsubscript𝒌perpendicular-to2kperp2) and, in the case of the TMD PDFs, a third Gaussian. They are therefore positive definite for all values of 𝒌⟂2superscriptsubscript𝒌perpendicular-to2kperp2.8Note, however, that the evolved TMD PDF, Eq. , can become negative at large values of transverse momentum. The same holds true for TMD FFs. The parameters λ𝜆lambda and λ2subscript𝜆2lambda2 in Eq. are squared in order to avoid negative contributions (there is no need to square the parameter λFsubscript𝜆𝐹lambdaF in Eq. because the fit always selects positive values for this parameter). The widths of the Gaussians, expressed by Eqs. ,, are x𝑥x (or z𝑧z) dependent and vanish as x𝑥x (or z𝑧z) approaches 1. Our choice of the functional form is also inspired by model calculations of TMD PDFs (see, e. g. , citebib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65) and TMD FFs (see, e. g, citebib.bib66, bib.bib67). Many of these models predict the existence of terms that behave similarly to Gaussians and weighted Gaussians. The details of the functional dependence predicted by the models are related to the correlation between the spin of the quarks and their transverse momentum. In the case of fragmentaton functions, a different role can be played by different producton channels (e. g. , direct production vs. production through the decay of hadronic resonances). Finally, for the logarithmic ordering we use the same convention adopted in Ref. citebib.bib7. In particular, the orders of truncation of the perturbative ingredients relevant to the present analysis are summarized in Tab. . At the time of this analysis, the full NLL accuracy could not be achieved because NNLO collinear FFs were not available. Very recently, two analyses of collinear FFs were presented in Refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib29 making an extraction of TMDs at full NLL possible. We leave this study to a future pubblication.
content_sections.II Formalism II.4 Normalization factors: In Ref. citebib.bib5 it was demonstrated that TMD factorization at NLL accuracy is able to successfully reproduce the normalization and shape of HERMESHERMESrm HERMES SIDIS multiplicities and the shape of the available COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities. More recently, the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS collaboration published a reanalysis of their datacitebib.bib68. The NLL TMD predictions correctly reproduce normalization and shape of the new datacitebib.bib69. However, when increasing the accuracy to NLL or higher, the TMD formula severely underestimates the measurementscitebib.bib70, bib.bib69 by nearly constant factors in each bin. Note that tensions between the TMD cross sections and the associated measurements exist also at large transverse momentum in SIDIScitebib.bib48, DYcitebib.bib71, and electron-positron annihilation into two hadronscitebib.bib72. In the present study, as will be shown in Sec. , we confirm that we obtain an excellent description of both normalization and shape of the SIDIS multiplicities at NLL and that the NLL results are much smaller. At NLL the results increase slightly, but they are still far from the NLL ones and, therefore, from data. At average kinematics of the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS measurements, we obtain the following ratios of multiplicities: MNLLMN2​LLsubscript𝑀NLLsubscript𝑀superscriptN2LLdisplaystylefracMrm NLLMrm N2LL ≳2,greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent2displaystylegtrsim 2, MNLLMN3​LLsubscript𝑀NLLsubscript𝑀superscriptN3LLdisplaystylefracMrm NLLMrm N3LL ≳1.5. greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent1.5displaystylegtrsim 1.5. The reason for the difference between the logarithmic orders is almost entirely due to the hard factor in Eq. citebib.bib34. If we look at the explicit expression for the hard factor9There are different definitions for the hard factor in the literature, which are compensated by different definitions of the matching coefficients C𝐶C in Eq. . Here we follow the definition of Ref. citebib.bib34. with the standard choice μ=Q𝜇𝑄mu=Q ℋSIDIS​(Q,Q)=1+αs​(Q)4​π​CF​(−16+π23),superscriptℋSIDIS𝑄𝑄1subscript𝛼𝑠𝑄4𝜋subscript𝐶𝐹16superscript𝜋23cal Hrm SIDIS(Q,Q)=1+fracalphas(Q)4piCFbigg(-16+fracpi23bigg), we can immediately see that just by introducing 𝒪​(αs)𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠cal O(alphas) corrections, at Q=2𝑄2Q=2 GeV with αs≈0.3subscript𝛼𝑠0.3alphasapprox 0.3, we reduce the structure function to about 60% of its original value. This change is not compensated by a similar reduction in the denominator of Eq. : the differences between the LO and NLO expressions of the inclusive DIS cross section are typically below 5% and the NLO results are actually larger than the LO ones. If the NLL expression is much larger than the NLL and NLL ones, we may suspect that it should overshoot the data by a factor 1.5 at least. However, several works before the present one have shown a good agreement with data using a parton-model approachcitebib.bib15, bib.bib16 or at NLLcitebib.bib17, bib.bib18. Moreover, the integral of the structure function over 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT is equal to the value of its Fourier transform at 𝒃T=0subscript𝒃𝑇0bT=0. Using any b∗superscript𝑏b* prescription with in=2​e−γE/Qsubscript𝑏min2superscript𝑒subscript𝛾𝐸𝑄brm min=2e-gammaE/Q, the integral of the NLL expression by construction corresponds to the LO expression of the collinear SIDIS structure function, independent of the TMD nonperturbative parameters:10Note that in the absence of a insubscript𝑏minbrm min prescription, the integral would vanish. ∫d2​𝒒T​FU​U,T​(x,z,|𝒒T|,Q)=x​∑aea2​ℋSIDIS​(Q,Q)​(f1a​(x,𝒃T2;Q,Q2)​D1a→h​(z,𝒃T2;Q,Q2))||𝒃T|=0=NLLx​∑aea2​f1a​(x;Q)​D1a→h​(z;Q). superscript𝑑2subscript𝒒𝑇subscript𝐹𝑈𝑈𝑇𝑥𝑧subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄evaluated-at𝑥subscript𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑒𝑎2superscriptℋSIDIS𝑄𝑄superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑎𝑥superscriptsubscript𝒃𝑇2𝑄superscript𝑄2superscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑎ℎ𝑧superscriptsubscript𝒃𝑇2𝑄superscript𝑄2subscript𝒃𝑇0superscriptNLL𝑥subscript𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑒𝑎2superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑎𝑥𝑄superscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑎ℎ𝑧𝑄beginsplitint d2qT,FUU,Tbig(x,z,|qT|,Qbig)=x,sumaea2,cal Hrm SIDIS(Q,Q)bigg(hatf1a(x,bT2;Q,Q2),hatD1ato h(z,bT2;Q,Q2)bigg)bigg||bT|=0 stackrelscriptstylerm NLL=x,sumaea2,f1a(x;Q),D1ato h(z;Q). endsplit The LO collinear SIDIS predictions are known to describe the data reasonably well and, if anything, they seem to be lower than the datacitebib.bib73, bib.bib74. Therefore, the integral of our NLL expression is in good agreement with data, which also indicates the absence of a large normalization error. If the NLL predictions describe the data well and are a factor 2 or 1.5 above the NLL and NLL predictions, we propose to modify the normalization of the latter to recover a good agreement with data. An extended discussion of this issue can be found in Ref. citebib.bib69. We observe that the integral of the TMD formula, valid at low 𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT, should reproduce only part of the full collinear cross section. The only exception is the order 𝒪​(αs0)𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠0cal O(alphas0) case, as we have seen above, since at that order there is no contribution from gluon radiation at high transverse momentum, beyond the TMD region. However, at NLL or higher, in the kinematics of fixed-target SIDIS experiments, the integral of the TMD region (i. e. , the integral of the so-called W𝑊W term in the language of Ref. citebib.bib6) is much smaller than the corresponding collinear cross section. The missing contribution to the integral should be recovered by the terms in the fixed-order calculation that are not included in the TMD resummed expression (the so-called Y𝑌Y term). Ideally, the Y𝑌Y term should be negligible in the low-𝒒Tsubscript𝒒𝑇qT region. This is not the case in the experimental regions under consideration: the Y𝑌Y term is finite but relatively large, even at 𝒒T=0subscript𝒒𝑇0qT=0. If we consider the contribution to the integral of the W𝑊W term (i. e. , the integral of Eq. ), with our insubscript𝑏minbrm min prescription, at order αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas we obtain, schematically ∫d2​𝒒T​W|𝒪​(αs)=σ0​αs4​π​∑qeq2​[D1q→h⊗CTMDq​q⊗f1q+D1q→h⊗CTMDq​g⊗f1g+D1g→h⊗CTMDg​q⊗f1q]​(x,z,Q),evaluated-atsuperscript𝑑2subscript𝒒𝑇𝑊𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝜎0subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑒𝑞2delimited-tensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑞ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝐶TMD𝑞𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑞tensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑞ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝐶TMD𝑞𝑔superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑔tensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑔ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝐶TMD𝑔𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑞𝑥𝑧𝑄int d2qT,Wbiggr|cal O(alphas)=sigma0fracalphas4pisumqeq2Bigl[D1qto hotimes Crm TMDqqotimes f1q+D1qto hotimes Crm TMDqgotimes f1g+D1gto hotimes Crm TMDgqotimes f1qBigr](x,z,Q), where σ0=4​π2​α2​z2​|𝒒T|x​Q3​Y+. subscript𝜎04superscript𝜋2superscript𝛼2superscript𝑧2subscript𝒒𝑇𝑥superscript𝑄3subscript𝑌sigma0=frac4pi2,alpha2,z2,|qT|x,Q3Y+. The former can be written as C1a​b​(x,z;Q,μ)superscriptsubscript𝐶1𝑎𝑏𝑥𝑧𝑄𝜇displaystyle C1ab(x,z;Q,mu) =Cnomixa​b​(x,z,Q,μ)+Cmixa​b​(x,z). absentsuperscriptsubscript𝐶nomix𝑎𝑏𝑥𝑧𝑄𝜇superscriptsubscript𝐶mix𝑎𝑏𝑥𝑧displaystyle=Crm nomixab(x,z,Q,mu)+Crm mixab(x,z). The coefficient Cnomixsubscript𝐶nomixCrm nomix is the sum of all those terms that contain either a δ​(1−x)𝛿1𝑥delta(1-x) or a δ​(1−z)𝛿1𝑧delta(1-z) (or both). Some of these terms are present in CTMDsubscript𝐶TMDCrm TMD, but not all. This definition holds at all orders in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. The CLsubscript𝐶𝐿CL matching coefficients, instead, only contain “mixed” contributions. For convenience, we reproduce all coefficients at order αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas in App. . In order to increase the size of the TMD component, we consider the contribution of all the “nonmixed” terms Cnomixsubscript𝐶nomixCrm nomix. The reason behind this choice is that it might be possible to include such terms into a redefinition of the individual TMDs. Hence, we define d​σnomixd​x​d​Q​d​z|𝒪​(αs)=σ0​αs4​π​∑qeq2​[D1q→h⊗Cnomixq​q⊗f1q+D1q→h⊗Cnomixq​g⊗f1g+D1g→h⊗Cnomixg​q⊗f1q]​(x,z,Q),evaluated-at𝑑superscript𝜎nomix𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑄𝑑𝑧𝒪subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝜎0subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋subscript𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑒𝑞2delimited-tensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑞ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝐶nomix𝑞𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑞tensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑞ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝐶nomix𝑞𝑔superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑔tensor-productsuperscriptsubscript𝐷1→𝑔ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝐶nomix𝑔𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑓1𝑞𝑥𝑧𝑄fracdsigmarm nomixdxdQdzbiggr|cal O(alphas)=sigma0fracalphas4pisumqeq2Bigl[D1qto hotimes Crm nomixqqotimes f1q+D1qto hotimes Crm nomixqgotimes f1g+D1gto hotimes Crm nomixgqotimes f1qBigr](x,z,Q), and similarly for higher orders, and we introduce the following normalization factor: ω​(x,z,Q)=d​σnomixd​x​d​z​d​Q/∫d2​𝒒T​W. 𝜔𝑥𝑧𝑄𝑑superscript𝜎nomix𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑧𝑑𝑄superscript𝑑2subscript𝒒𝑇𝑊omega(x,z,Q)=fracdsigmarm nomixdx,dz,dQbigg/int d2qT,W,. We stress that these normalization factors depend only on the collinear PDFs and FFs, are independent of the parametrization of the nonperturbative part of the TMDs, and can be computed before performing a fit of the latter. At NLL, ω​(x,z,Q)=1𝜔𝑥𝑧𝑄1omega(x,z,Q)=1. Beyond NLL, the prefactor becomes larger than one and guarantees that the integral of the TMD part of the cross section reproduces most of the collinear cross section, as suggested by the data. On the contrary, without the enhancement due to the normalization factor, the integral of the TMD part of the cross section would be too small, requiring a large role of the high-transverse-momentum tail, which is not observed in the data. The impact of the normalization factor defined in Eq. will be addressed in detail in Sec. . As a consequence of our procedure, the theoretical expression for the SIDIS cross section in Eq. becomes d​σωSIDISd​x​d​z​d​|𝒒T|​d​Q=ω​(x,z,Q)​d​σSIDISd​x​d​z​d​|𝒒T|​d​Q. 𝑑superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜔SIDIS𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑧𝑑subscript𝒒𝑇𝑑𝑄𝜔𝑥𝑧𝑄𝑑superscript𝜎SIDIS𝑑𝑥𝑑𝑧𝑑subscript𝒒𝑇𝑑𝑄fracdsigmaomegatextSIDISdx,dz,d|qT|,dQ=omega(x,z,Q),fracdsigmatextSIDISdx,dz,d|qT|,dQ,.
content_sections.III Data selection: In this Section we describe the experimental data included in our global analysis. We consider a large number of datasets related to DY lepton pair production and SIDIS, for the observables discussed in Sec. and Sec. . The coverage in the x𝑥x-Q2superscript𝑄2Q2 plane spanned by these datasets is illustrated in Fig. . The majority of datasets analyzed in the present work was already included in the global analysis of SIDIS and DY data in Ref. citebib.bib5 and in the fit of DY data discussed in Ref. citebib.bib7. For more details, we refer the reader to those references. The new datasets included in the present analysis are: • DY di-muon production from the collision of a proton beam with an energy of 800 GeV on a H2superscript𝐻22H fixed target from E772 (s=38.8𝑠38.8sqrts=38.8 GeV)citebib.bib76; • DY di-muon production from the PHENIX Collaborationcitebib.bib77; • DY data at 13 TeV from the CMS Collaborationcitebib.bib78 and the ATLAS Collaborationcitebib.bib79.
content_sections.III Data selection III.1 Drell-Yan: Our analysis is based on TMD factorization, which is applicable only in the region |𝒒T|≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|ll Q. Therefore, in agreement with the choices of Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib22 we impose the following cut |𝒒T|<0.2​Q. subscript𝒒𝑇0.2𝑄|qT|<0.2,Q,. Table summarizes all the DY datasets included in our analysis. For some DY datasets the experimental observable is given within a fiducial region. This means that kinematic cuts on transverse momentum pT​ℓsubscript𝑝𝑇ℓpTell and pseudo–rapidity ηℓsubscript𝜂ℓetaell of the single final-state leptons are enforced (values reported in the next–to–last column of Tab. ). For more details we refer the reader to Ref. citebib.bib7. The second column of Tab. reports, for each experiment, the number of data points (Ndatsubscript𝑁datNtextdat) that survive the kinematic cuts. The total number of DY data points considered in this work is 484. Note that for E605 and E288 at 400 GeV we have excluded the bin in Q𝑄Q containing the ΥΥUpsilon resonance (Q≃9.5similar-to-or-equals𝑄9.5Qsimeq 9.5GeV). As can be seen in Tab. , the cross sections are released in different forms: some of them are normalized to the total (fiducial) cross section while others are not. When necessary, the required total cross section σ𝜎sigma is computed using the code 𝙳𝚈𝙽𝙽𝙻𝙾𝙳𝚈𝙽𝙽𝙻𝙾tt DYNNLOcitebib.bib35, bib.bib36 with the MMHT14 collinear PDF set, consistently with the perturbative order of the differential cross section (see also Tab. ). More precisely, the total cross section is computed at NLO for NNLL accuracy, and NNLO for NLL accuracy. The values of the total cross sections at different orders can be found in Table 3 of Ref. citebib.bib7. For the ATLAS dataset at 13 TeV, the value of the fiducial cross section is 694.3 pb at NLO and 707.3 pb at NNLO.
content_sections.III Data selection III.2 SIDIS: The identification of the TMD region in SIDIS is not a trivial task and may be subject to revision as new data appears and the theoretical description is improved, as discussed in dedicated studiescitebib.bib94, bib.bib38, bib.bib95. First of all, a cut in the virtuality Q𝑄Q of the exchanged photon is necessary to respect the condition Q≫ΛQCDmuch-greater-than𝑄subscriptΛQCDQggLambdatextQCD needed for perturbation theory to be applicable. In this way also mass corrections and higher twist corrections can be neglected. In this work, we require that Q>1.4𝑄1.4Q>1.4GeV. Studies of SIDIS in collinear kinematics employ similar cutscitebib.bib96, bib.bib29. In order to restrict ourselves to the SIDIS current fragmentation region and interpret the observables in terms of parton distribution and fragmentation functions, we apply a cut in the kinematic variable z𝑧z by requiring 0.2<z<0.70.2𝑧0.70.2<z<0.7. The lower limit is the same used in the study of collinear fragmentation functionscitebib.bib96, bib.bib29. We used a slightly more restrictive upper limit, to avoid contributions from exclusive channels and to focus on a region where the collinear fragmentation functions have small relative uncertainties. For what concerns the cut on transverse momentum, our baseline choice is |𝑷h​T|<min​[min​[c1​Q,c2​z​Q]+c3​GeV,z​Q],subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇minminsubscript𝑐1𝑄subscript𝑐2𝑧𝑄subscript𝑐3GeV𝑧𝑄|PhT|<textminbig[,textmin[c1,Q,c2,zQ]+c3,textGeV,,zQbig],, with fixed parameters c1=0.2subscript𝑐10.2c1=0.2, c2=0.5subscript𝑐20.5c2=0.5 and c3=0.3subscript𝑐30.3c3=0.3. This choice is more restrictive than a similar one made in Ref. citebib.bib5, but less restrictive than the one made in Ref. citebib.bib22. It allows for many data points with |𝑷h​T|≪Qmuch-less-thansubscript𝑷ℎ𝑇𝑄|PhT|ll Q but also with 0.2​Q<|𝒒T|<Q0.2𝑄subscript𝒒𝑇𝑄0.2,Q<|qT|<Q. In Sec. , we will discuss variations of the baseline SIDIS cut in Eq. that give phenomenological support to our choice. As for the datasets included in the present analysis, the main difference with Ref. citebib.bib5 is that we include the new release of COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS datacitebib.bib68. In this dataset, the vector–boson contributions have been subtracted. For the HERMESHERMESrm HERMES dataset we consistently select the vector–meson–subtracted dataset (. vmsub set). Moreover, we select the zxpt-3D-binning for HERMESHERMESrm HERMES multiplicities, since it presents a finer binning in |𝑷h​T|subscript𝑷ℎ𝑇|PhT|. The breakdown of the entire SIDIS dataset included in the present analysis is reported in Tab. . The second column of Tab. shows the number of data points (Ndatsubscript𝑁datNtextdat) that respect the kinematic cuts for each dataset, with a total number of 1547 data points. In conclusion, the total number of DY and SIDIS data points surviving our kinematic cuts is 2031.
content_sections.III Data selection III.3 Error treatment: The considered experimental datasets are released with a set of systematic and statistical uncertainties. As already pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib7, a proper treatment of the experimental uncertainties is extremely important in order to obtain a reliable extraction of TMDs. Thus, we choose to treat systematic uncertainties as fully correlated only if, in the corresponding publication, it is explicitly specified that they are correlated. The statistical uncertainties, instead, are always considered as uncorrelated. At variance with Ref. citebib.bib7, in this analysis we do not make use of the iterative t0subscript𝑡0t0-prescriptioncitebib.bib97 for the treatment of correlated normalization uncertainties. This prescription is usually introduced to avoid the underestimation of the predictions caused by the so–called D’Agostini biascitebib.bib98. After performing the fit with and without the t0subscript𝑡0t0-prescription, we found that our analysis is not affected by the D’Agostini bias and therefore we saw no reason to introduce the t0subscript𝑡0t0-prescription in the computation of the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2. On top of experimental systematic uncertainties, there can be several sources of systematic theoretical errors. The first one comes from the choice of the underlying collinear parton distribution and fragmentation functions. In our case we choose the MMHT2014citebib.bib99 collinear PDFs and the DSS collinear FFs. Since the HERMESHERMESrm HERMES collaboration provides multiplicities for pions and kaons separately (see Tab. ), we use DSS14citebib.bib100 for π±superscript𝜋plus-or-minuspipm and DSS17citebib.bib96 for K±superscript𝐾plus-or-minusKpm. Given the nature of the PDF and FF set used in this analysis, their uncertainties are computed using the Hessian methodcitebib.bib101, bib.bib102, bib.bib100. We observed that PDF and FF uncertainties are significantly correlated across bins. In order to account for this correlation, we decomposed the corresponding uncertainties into a fully correlated part that amounts to 80% of the total, while we treated the remaining 60% as uncorrelated.11Notice that, using this decomposition, the sum in quadrature of correlated and uncorrelated parts reproduce the original uncertainties. Moreover, for the observables measured by COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS one needs a collinear set of FFs for unidentified charged hadrons. Since the dedicated DSS07 FF set for unidentified charged hadronscitebib.bib103 does not provide an estimate of the uncertainties, we computed the COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities by using the sum of the DSS14 and DSS17 sets for pions and kaons.12We thus assumed that the yield due to other hadronic species, such as protons, ΛΛLambda, etc. , is negligible as compared to the sum of kaons and pions. As argued in Ref. citebib.bib104, the contribution to the total yield due to hadrons heavier that pions and kaons is indeed marginal. The associated uncertainty is calculated by propagating to the multiplicity the Hessian errors associated to each of the two hadronic component. As pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib22, the choice of specific sets for the collinear distributions may have a sizeable impact on the final result. In our analysis, we did not consider alternative collinear sets and postpone this study to a future publication. Likewise, we leave for future work the study of other sources of theoretical uncertainties such as higher-twist corrections, TMD flavor dependence and the choice of the perturbative scales.
content_sections.IV Results: In this section, we present the results obtained for the extraction of unpolarized quark TMDs from a global analysis including both DY and SIDIS data (see Sec. ). This work represents an important upgrade with respect to Ref. citebib.bib7. In fact, in the SIDIS process a single hadron is measured in the final state, allowing us to extract information about fragmentation functions. Therefore, the final result of this work is the simultaneous extraction of both TMD PDFs and FFs at NLL. At the moment, this is the most precise extraction of TMDs that has been achieved on more than two thousand data points. In Sec. we present the quality of the fit, in Sec. we discuss the extracted TMD distributions, and in Sec. we investigate the effect of variations on the baseline fit configuration.
content_sections.IV Results IV.1 Fit quality: In this section, we discuss the quality of the baseline fit performed at NLL imposing the cuts on |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q as discussed in Sec. . The error analysis is performed with the so-called bootstrap method, which consists in fitting an ensemble of 250 Monte Carlo replicas of the experimental data (see Ref. citebib.bib5 for more details). The most complete statistical information about the TMDs is given by the full ensemble of 250 replicas. For some purposes, however, it is useful to define a single, representative result instead of the full replica ensemble. In order to estimate the quality of our fit, the most appropriate indicator is the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 value of the best fit to the central (not fluctuated) experimental data (χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20). We refer to this fit as the “central replica. ” It is possible to analyze also the average of the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 over all replicas (⟨χ2⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝜒2chi2) as well as the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 of the mean replica (χm2superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑚2chim2), constructed as the average of all replicascitebib.bib7. These values should be very close to each other. Tab. reports the breakdown of the χ02superscriptsubscript𝜒02chi02 values normalized to the number of data points (Ndatsubscript𝑁datNtextdat) for DY and SIDIS datasets. As already discussed in Ref. citebib.bib7, in the presence of bin-by-bin correlated uncertainties the total χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 can be expressed as the sum of two contributions χ2=∑iN(expi−th¯iσi)2+χλ2=χD2+χλ2,superscript𝜒2superscriptsubscript𝑖𝑁superscriptsubscriptexp𝑖subscript¯th𝑖subscript𝜎𝑖2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝐷subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆displaystylechi2=sumiNleft(fractextexpi-overlinetextthisigmairight)2+chi2lambda=chi2D+chi2lambda;, where χD2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝐷chi2D is given by the standard formula for N𝑁N experimental data points expi and statistical and uncorrelated uncertainties σi2=σi,stat2+σi,uncor2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖stat2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖uncor2sigmai2=sigmai,rm stat2+sigmai,rm uncor2, but involving theoretical predictions shifted by the correlated uncertainties, th¯i=thi+∑α=1kλα​σi,corr(α),subscript¯th𝑖subscriptth𝑖superscriptsubscript𝛼1𝑘subscript𝜆𝛼superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖corr𝛼overlinetextthi=textthi+sumalpha=1klambdaalpha,sigmai,rm corr(alpha);, where σi,corr(α)superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖corr𝛼sigmai,rm corr(alpha) is the α𝛼alpha-th (100%) correlated uncertainty associated with the i𝑖i-th experimental data point and λαsubscript𝜆𝛼lambdaalpha is the so-called nuisance parameter. In Eq. , the χλ2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆chi2lambda is a penalty term due to the presence of correlated uncertainties and is completely determined by the nuisance parameters, χλ2=∑α=1kλα2. subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆superscriptsubscript𝛼1𝑘superscriptsubscript𝜆𝛼2chi2lambda=sumalpha=1klambdaalpha2;. The optimal value of the nuisance parameters is obtained by minimizing the total χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 in Eq. with respect to them. Because the shifted predictions in Eq. provide a better visual assessment of the fit quality, we will consistently display them for all observables used in the fit. From Tab. , the global χ02superscriptsubscript𝜒02chi02 value is 1.06, indicating that the description of the whole dataset is very good.13We also obtain ⟨χ2⟩=1.08±0.01delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝜒2plus-or-minus1.080.01chi2=1.08pm 0.01 and χm2=1.07superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑚21.07chim2=1.07. This means that the fit is able to simultaneously describe experimental data coming from two different processes over a wide kinematic range. As can be seen in Tabs. and , the low-energy dataset comes from fixed-target DY experiments and SIDIS observables, while the high-energy dataset comes from collider experiments at the LHC and Tevatron at energies higher by more than two orders of magnitude. It is important to notice that the correlated penalty term χλ2subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆chi2lambda gives a significant contribution to the total χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 value. This means that the shifts induced by correlated uncertainties are often large. The χλ2/Ndat=0.29subscriptsuperscript𝜒2𝜆subscript𝑁dat0.29chi2lambda/Ntextdat=0.29 obtained in this analysis is larger than the one in Ref. citebib.bib7, mainly because of the different treatment of the theoretical uncertainties related to collinear PDFs and FFs, which are considered here as 80% correlated.For HERMESHERMESrm HERMES and COMPASSCOMPASSrm COMPASS multiplicities, which represent about 75% of the total number of data points considered in this work, the description obtained by our global analysis is very good. From Tab. , the values of χ02/Ndatsubscriptsuperscript𝜒20subscript𝑁datchi20/Ntextdat are almost always smaller than 1. In the case of HERMESHERMESrm HERMES multiplicities, we note that the largest contribution to the χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 comes from the π+superscript𝜋pi+ channel, for both proton and deuteron targets. This result is consistent with the findings in both Refs. citebib.bib15, bib.bib5 andcitebib.bib22. Since kaon multiplicities are affected by larger statistical errors, and the collinear FFs for kaons display large uncertainties, the corresponding χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 value is lower. The comparison between theoretical results for the SIDIS multiplicities of Eq. and HERMESHERMESrm HERMES data for the production of charged pions and kaons off a deuteron target is shown in Fig. . Each column corresponds to a specific x𝑥x bin. Each row corresponds to a specific final-state channel. Points with different markers and colors correspond to different representative z𝑧z bins, and are offset for a better visualization as indicated in the plot legend. The light blue rectangles are the theoretical results and correspond to the 68% Confidence-Level (CL) band (namely excluding the largest and smallest 16% of the replicas). We note that for K−superscript𝐾K- production the central column, corresponding to the 0.2<x<0.350.2𝑥0.350.2<x<0.35 bin, does not include the magenta points for the highest 0.6<z<0.80.6𝑧0.80.6<z<0.8 bin because of the kinematic cut in z𝑧z. Similarly, in all panels there are only three green points (for the lowest z𝑧z bin) because of the z𝑧z-dependent cut in Eq. , which leads to the exclusion of a larger number of |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT| bins at lower values of z𝑧z. The theoretical results display larger uncertainties for K−superscript𝐾K- production (second row) because of the combined effect of larger experimental errors and larger uncertainties in the kaon collinear FFs. We note that the agreement is good for almost all bins, which is reflected in small χ02subscriptsuperscript𝜒20chi20 values in Tab. . The situation worsens for the lowest Q𝑄Q bin (1.3<Q<1.731.3𝑄1.731.3<Q<1.73 GeV), particularly for x≳0.02greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑥0.02xgtrsim 0.02. We also remark that for some Q𝑄Q and x𝑥x bins the theoretical uncertainties for the largest z𝑧z bin compatible with our kinematic cuts (0.6<z<0.80.6𝑧0.80.6<z<0.8) are significantly larger than for other z𝑧z bins because of much larger uncertainties in the collinear FFs. Finally, looking at the table of panels from top to bottom, we realize that the lowest z𝑧z-bin distributions (red diamonds) are present only for the largest Q𝑄Q bins, and vice-versa, because of the kinematic cut in Eq. Again, the light-blue rectangles correspond to the 68% CL theoretical results, and are obtained by adding the results for positively charged pions and kaons, h+∼π++K+similar-tosuperscriptℎsuperscript𝜋superscript𝐾h+simpi++K+. Comments similar to Fig. can be made about the agreement between data and theory.DY data represents approximately 25% of the full set of analyzed data. From Tab. it is evident that most of low-energy DY data from fixed-target experiments (E605, E288, E772), but also from PHENIX and STAR, can be fitted with low χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values, much lower than high-energy DY data from collider experiments like, e. g. , those at the LHC. As already pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib7, this most likely originates from the fact that low-energy DY data are affected by larger errors and collinear PDFs at these kinematics have larger uncertainties. From Tab. , we also note that the quality of our fit for the ATLAS datasets is poor. In particular, the description worsens for the first two low-rapidity bins of both ATLAS 7 TeV and ATLAS 8 TeV datasets, the worst case being at |y|<1𝑦1|y|<1 for ATLAS 7 TeV. Several effects might be responsible for this result. Since the experimental observable is a normalized cross section, systematic errors cancel in the ratio producing measurements with very small error bars. Fitting these data is very difficult, also because small theoretical effects can give significant contributions to the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2. Moreover, different implementations of phase-space cuts on the final-state leptons could lead to modifications in both the shape and the normalization of the theoretical observable (see, e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib32, bib.bib105, bib.bib106). We leave this issue for future studies. At variance with Ref. citebib.bib22, we obtain our results without excluding any extra data points on top of the ones exceeding the maximum value of |𝒒T|/Qsubscript𝒒𝑇𝑄|qT|/Q in Eq. . It is interesting to comment the results of the fit for those datasets that were not included in the previous analysis of Ref. citebib.bib7 (see Sec. ). For E772, we are able to obtain a good description only for data points above the peak of the ΥΥUpsilon resonance. For Q<9𝑄9Q<9 GeV, the quality of the fit worsens. At variance with Ref. citebib.bib22, we keep the Q<9𝑄9Q<9 GeV bins because there is no evident motivation to exclude them. The new CMS dataset at s=13𝑠13sqrts=13 TeV is important because it extends the kinematic coverage considered in Ref. citebib.bib7. This dataset is very nicely described, even better than the ones at the smaller center-of-mass energies s=7,8𝑠78sqrts=7,8 TeV. This is probably due to the fact that the CMS 13 TeV dataset is densely binned in rapidity. In order to visualize the quality of our fit, in Figs. - we present the comparison between experimental data and theoretical results for a representative selection of the DY dataset. In the upper panels of each plot, we display the cross section differential in |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT|, while in the lower panels we show the ratio of data to theory. As for the SIDIS case, the light-blue bands are the 68% CL theoretical results. Fig. displays the DY cross section for the E288 dataset at beam energy Eb​e​a​m=200subscript𝐸𝑏𝑒𝑎𝑚200Ebeam=200 GeV for different bins in Q𝑄Q. For DY fixed-target observables, we calculate the cross section at mean values of |𝒒T|subscript𝒒𝑇|qT| (not integrating upon the bin limits). Hence, we display the 68% CL uncertainty as a band rather than a series of rectangles. We remark that the uncertainty band is larger for lower Q𝑄Q bins; this trend is induced by larger correlated uncertainties for smaller invariant masses of the lepton pair. In Fig. , we compare the theoretical results for the cross section for DY in p​p¯𝑝¯𝑝pbarp collisions at the Tevatron. Black data points in the left panel correspond to the results of Run I of the CDF experiment, while in the right panel the results for Run II are reported. The lower panels show the corresponding ratio of experimental data to theoretical results. The latter are displayed as light-blue rectangles, each one corresponding to the integral of the cross section within the corresponding bin limits. The size of the rectangle is given by the 68% CL. The quality of the fit for CDF data is comparable to the one in Ref. citebib.bib7. In Fig. , we compare the theoretical results for the DY cross section in p​p𝑝𝑝pp collisions at the LHC. From left to right, the black data points refer to the measurements by the CMS Collaboration at increasing s=𝑠absentsqrts= 7, 8, 13 TeV. For s=𝑠absentsqrts= 7, 8 TeV, the results are normalized to the fiducial cross section. As in previous figures, the lower panels display the ratio of experimental data to the 68% CL theoretical results. For s=𝑠absentsqrts= 7, 8 TeV, the quality of the fit is comparable to that in Ref. citebib.bib7. For the new dataset at s=13𝑠13sqrts=13 TeV, the agreement in the displayed rapidity bin is excellent, but remains very good also for higher rapidities. In Fig. , we compare the theoretical results for the DY cross section in p​p𝑝𝑝pp collisions normalized to the fiducial cross section for the ATLAS data at s=7𝑠7sqrts=7 TeV. From left to right, we consider three representative bins at increasing rapidity |y|𝑦|y|. The leftmost one corresponds to the worst described bin in our global fit, with χ2/Ndata=13.5superscript𝜒2subscript𝑁data13.5chi2/Ntextdata=13.5. As in previous figures, the lower part of each panel displays the ratio of experimental data to the 68% CL theoretical results. The quality of the fit increases at more forward rapidities (from left to right). The same trend is observed at s=8𝑠8sqrts=8 TeV, but not for CMS at 13 TeV.
content_sections.IV Results IV.2 TMD distributions: We now discuss the TMD distributions extracted from our baseline fit with NLL accuracy. Tab. displays the list of our 21 fitting parameters with their mean value and standard deviation. The majority of the parameters is well constrained. The only parameter that is compatible with zero is γ2subscript𝛾2gamma2. The λ𝜆lambda parameter measures the relative weight of the first Gaussian and the weighted-Gaussian in the nonperturbative part of the TMD PDF in Eq. . The value of this parameter is close to 2, indicating that the contribution of the weighted-Gaussian component is important. In Eq. , the parameter λ2subscript𝜆2lambda2 measures the relative weight of the first Gaussian and the third Gaussian; this parameter is small but not compatible with zero, which means that also this component of the TMD PDF is important to reach a good description of experimental data. Our parametrization of the nonperturbative part of TMD FFs in Eq. contains just the combination of a Gaussian and a weighted Gaussian: this is sufficient to describe the data in an accurate way. The λFsubscript𝜆𝐹lambdaF parameter measures the relative weight of the two components; its value is close to 0.1, indicating that the contribution of the weighted Gaussian is small. Nevertheless, it has non-trivial consequences on the tail of the TMD FF, as we will show below. The g2subscript𝑔2g2 parameter is a key ingredient to the extraction of the Collins–Soper kernel, discussed in Sec. . The same parameter was used in the analysis of Ref. citebib.bib5. It is interesting to observe that the value obtained in the present global fit is smaller by almost a factor of 4 with respect to Ref. citebib.bib5. This may be due to the higher theoretical accuracy of the present analysis and to the role of the very precise high-energy Drell–Yan measurements, which also determine the very small standard deviation of g2subscript𝑔2g2. In Fig. , we show a graphical representation of the correlations among the 21 fitting parameters. Using the color code indicated in the legend, it is easy to realize that the nondiagonal elements are very small except for some (anti–)correlation among the β1subscript𝛽1beta1, δ1subscript𝛿1delta1 and γ1subscript𝛾1gamma1 parameters that control the z𝑧z–dependent width of the Gaussians in the TMD FF (see Eqs. ,). The overall absence of large correlations suggests that the model parametrization of the non perturbative parts of TMDs is appropriate. In Fig. , we show the unpolarized TMD PDF for the up quark in the proton at μ=ζ=Q=2𝜇𝜁𝑄2mu=sqrtzeta=Q=2 GeV (left panel) and 10 GeV (right panel) as a function of the quark transverse momentum |𝒌⟂|subscript𝒌perpendicular-to|kperp| for three different values of x𝑥x, namely x𝑥x = 0.001, 0.01, and 0.1. The bands correspond to the 68%percent% CL. The TMD seems to be wider at intermediate x=0.01𝑥0.01x=0.01, but has also a high tail at x=0.001𝑥0.001x=0.001. As already mentioned, a significant role is played by the weighted Gaussian and by the second Gaussian in Eq. . This may be a sign of the presence of contributions from different quark flavors and/or from different spin configurations (see Sec. ). It is worth noticing that in both left and right panels the TMD PDF at x=0.001𝑥0.001x=0.001 shows the largest error band, particularly at low |𝒌⟂|subscript𝒌perpendicular-to|kperp|. This is due to the lack of experimental points in that kinematic region (see Fig. ). Future data from the Electron-Ion Collider (EIC) are expected to play an important role in getting a better description of the TMD PDFs at low x𝑥xcitebib.bib107, bib.bib108. In Fig. , we show the TMD FF for the up quark fragmenting into a π+superscript𝜋pi+ at μ=ζ=Q=2𝜇𝜁𝑄2mu=sqrtzeta=Q=2 GeV (left panel) and 10 GeV (right panel) as a function of the pion transverse momentum |𝑷⟂|subscript𝑷perpendicular-to|Pperp| (with respect to the fragmenting quark axis) for two different values of z=𝑧absentz= 0.3 and 0.6. As in the previous figure, the uncertainty bands correspond to the 68%percent% CL. In both left and right panels, an additional structure clearly emerges at intermediate P⟂subscript𝑃perpendicular-toPperp, especially at z=0.3𝑧0.3z=0.3, which is induced by the weighted Gaussian in Eq. . Further investigations on this topic are needed, and data from electron-positron annihilations would be valuable to better explore these features. We stress that the error bands displayed in Figs. - reflect the uncertainty on the fitted parameters (see Eqs. -) that are determined by taking into account the uncertainty on the collinear PDFs and FFs as discussed in Sec. . However, since the fits are performed using the central set of the collinear distributions, all TMD replicas have the same integral in 𝒌⟂subscript𝒌perpendicular-tokperp (i. e. , their values at 𝒃T=0subscript𝒃𝑇0bT=0 are the same). As a consequence, the plots in Figs. - only partially account for the error of the collinear distributions.It is interesting to study the Collins–Soper kernelcitebib.bib109, bib.bib6 that drives the evolution of TMDs in terms of the rapidity scale ζ𝜁zeta. Recent discussions of this crucial component of the TMD formalism have been presented in Refs. citebib.bib110, bib.bib111 and estimates based on lattice QCD have been proposed in Refs. citebib.bib112, bib.bib113, bib.bib114. The Collins–Soper kernel, as written in Eq. , is composed of two parts.
